title: Minimum-Weight Steiner Triangulation of Convex Polygons Requires Interior Steiner Points
summary: We construct a convex polygon for which the minimum-weight Steiner triangulation requires an interior Steiner point. This provides a counterexample to a 1994 conjecture of Eppstein that minimum-weight Steiner triangulation of convex polygons needs only Steiner points on the boundary of the polygon.

AI.tldr: Provides a counterexample to a 1994 conjecture: the minimum-weight Steiner triangulation of a convex polygon can require an interior Steiner point, not only boundary points.
AI.motivation: To test the boundary-only conjecture for minimum-weight Steiner triangulations of convex polygons and understand the structure of optimal triangulations.
AI.method: Explicit construction of a convex polygon and analysis of its minimum-weight Steiner triangulations, showing that any optimal triangulation must include an interior Steiner point.
AI.result: A convex polygon is produced for which the minimal Steiner triangulation uses at least one interior Steiner point, disproving Eppstein's conjecture.
AI.conclusion: The conjecture that all minimum-weight Steiner triangulations of convex polygons can be achieved with Steiner points only on the polygon boundary is false; interior Steiner points may be necessary.

title: Segment Watchman Routes
comment: 19 pages, 10 figures, accepted to the 51st International Symposium on Mathematical Foundations of Computer Science (MFCS 2026)
summary:
Motivated by applications for robust guarding, we consider a variant of the multiple-watchmen problem that ensures that every point within a polygon $P$ is seen from more than one direction: we search for two routes $W_1,W_2$, such that every point $p\in P$ is contained in a segment $\overline{w_1w_2}\subseteq P$ such that $w_1\in W_1$ and $w_2\in W_2$. We call such routes segment watchman routes.
  We show that finding the two routes that are optimal with respect to the min-max criterion is weakly NP-hard even in simple polygons, and that finding the routes that are optimal with respect to the min-sum criterion is NP-hard in polygons with holes. Moreover, we present sufficient conditions for routes to be segment watchman routes, and provide a polynomial-time $2$-approximation under both the min-max criterion and the min-sum criterion, both in simple polygons. Finally, we show how to generalize our results for $k$ watchmen.

AI.tldr: Introduces segment watchman routes for dual-route visibility; establishes hardness results (weak NP-hard for min-max in simple polygons; NP-hard for min-sum with holes); provides sufficient conditions and a polynomial-time 2-approximation for both criteria in simple polygons; generalizes to k watchmen.
AI.motivation: Motivated by robust guarding needs, the work enforces that every point is visible from at least two directions via a segment whose endpoints lie on two routes W1 and W2; this extends the classic watchman route problem to a segment-based, multi-directional visibility setting.
AI.method: Shows hardness via complexity reductions for min-max and min-sum criteria; derives sufficient conditions for segment watchman routes; designs a polynomial-time 2-approximation algorithm in simple polygons for both criteria; discusses generalization to k watchmen.
AI.result: Hardness results (weak NP-hardness for min-max in simple polygons; NP-hardness for min-sum in polygons with holes); constructive sufficient conditions; a 2-approximation algorithm in simple polygons for both criteria; generalization to k watchmen.
AI.conclusion: The problem is computationally challenging but admits efficient constant-factor approximations in simple polygons; holes amplify complexity; the framework extends to any number of watchmen, suggesting directions for tighter approximations and exact algorithms in restricted classes.

title: Sharp approximate Carathéodory theorem and application to iterated Delaunay refinement
comment: Extended, corrected, and substantially strengthened version of Chapter 2 of arXiv:2112.07573. 61 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables
summary: We analyze the decrease of simplex diameters under iterated refinement of spherical Delaunay complexes. Unlike in ordinary subdivision, the refined Delaunay complex need not be a subdivision of the previous one, so mesh contraction is not automatic. We derive explicit contraction bounds for several families of Steiner points, including Delaunay analogues of barycentric and edgewise subdivision. The proof reduces the problem to sharp covering estimates for Euclidean simplices. These estimates are obtained through a strengthening of Maurey's empirical method via pivotal sampling and a dimension-dependent version of the approximate Carathéodory theorem. Theoretical results and numerical experiments show that Delaunay refinements achieve stronger contraction than their subdivision counterparts.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes explicit contraction bounds for simplex diameters under iterated Delaunay refinements on spherical complexes, showing stronger contraction than ordinary subdivision by using probabilistic covering bounds for Euclidean simplices, via a strengthened Maurey empirical method with pivotal sampling and a dimension-aware Carathéodory approach.
AI.motivation: Mesh quality control in Delaunay refinements is nontrivial because the refined complex need not be a subdivision of the previous one. Understanding diameter contraction is key to ensuring convergence and mesh quality, especially for Steiner point insertions that mimic barycentric and edgewise schemes.
AI.method: Translate contraction analysis into sharp covering estimates for Euclidean simplices. Develop bounds using a strengthened Maurey empirical method with pivotal sampling and an adaptive, dimension-dependent approximate Carathéodory theorem. Apply these to Delaunay analogues of barycentric and edgewise subdivision and derive explicit contraction constants for several Steiner-point families.
AI.result: Derivation of explicit contraction bounds for simplex diameters under iterated Delaunay refinements. Demonstration that Delaunay refinements achieve stronger contraction than standard subdivision. Theoretical results are complemented by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Delaunay refinement strategies can outperform subdivision in achieving mesh contraction, informing design choices for mesh-generation algorithms and providing novel probabilistic/tools-based techniques for analyzing geometric refinement processes. The results also connect empirical sampling methods with geometric covering theory and Carathéodory-type representations.

title: Furthest Pair Requires Quadratic Time in Superconstant Dimension under SETH
summary:
Several fundamental problems in computational geometry admit algorithms with running time $f(d) \cdot n^{2-Θ(1/d)}$ for $n$ points in $d$ dimensions, making them among the most prominent examples of barely subquadratic computation. Notable members of this class include Furthest Pair, Bichromatic Closest Pair, (Bichromatic) Maximum Innter Product, and Hopcroft's Problem. Chen [Theory Comput. 2020] proved that, assuming the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis (SETH), these problems require $n^{2-o(1)}$ time when the dimension satisfies $d=2^{Θ(\log^* n)}$. We extend this lower bound to all efficiently constructible dimensions $d=ω(1)$. Thus, assuming SETH, the dependence of the best known algorithms on the dimension is essentially unavoidable. The proof utilizes techniques in OpenAI's recent disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture.
  The proof was initially discovered by ChatGPT 5.5 Pro. The authors have validated and substantially edited the proof to improve the presentation.

AI.tldr: SETH-based lower bounds for barely subquadratic algorithms in high dimensions extend to all efficiently constructible dimensions d=ω(1), making dimension dependence essentially unavoidable; uses techniques related to OpenAI's disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture; initial discovery attributed to ChatGPT 5.5 Pro and refined by authors.
AI.motivation: Clarify how dimension d affects the runtime of fundamental computational-geometry problems and establish that, under SETH, the known dimension-dependent barrier persists beyond the previously known regimes.
AI.method: Provide SETH-based reductions extending Chen 2020 to all efficiently constructible dimensions d=ω(1), leveraging ideas from the Erdos unit-distance debate and AI-assisted proof generation, followed by authors' rigorous polishing.
AI.result: For all efficiently constructible dimensions d=ω(1), the problems (Furthest Pair, Bichromatic Closest Pair, (Bichromatic) Maximum Inner Product, Hopcroft's Problem) require n^{2−o(1)} time under SETH; the dimension dependence in the best algorithms is essentially tight.
AI.conclusion: The dimension barrier for these barely-subquadratic problems under SETH is effectively settled for all ω(1) dimensions; the work also highlights a provocative use of AI-generated proof steps, subsequently refined for mathematical rigor.

title: SEMIR: Topology-Preserving Graph Minors for Thin-Structure Segmentation
comment: Accepted to the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) 2026
summary: Thin-structure segmentation--power lines, cracks, lane markings at 1-3 pixel width--requires preserving connectivity that standard representations preclude: patching severs continuous structures and conventional superpixels merge thin targets into background before classification. Topology-aware losses penalize connectivity breaks at the objective level but cannot recover what the representation has already destroyed. We propose SEMIR, a framework that replaces the pixel lattice with a parameterized graph minor whose contraction map preserves thin-structure connectivity under the contraction criterion. The minor collapses millions of pixels into tens or hundreds of boundary-aligned supernodes, enabling full-resolution inference without patching at scales demonstrated up to 21 MP in this paper; a lightweight GNN classifies the reduced graph and an exact map lifts predictions to pixel resolution. One pipeline--identical architecture, features, loss, and GNN hyperparameters across all dataset--matches or exceeds domain-specific baselines on TTPLA (power lines), CrackSeg9k (pavement cracks), and SkyScapes Lane (aerial markings) on Dice, IoU, and Boundary F1 while reducing mask fragmentation by at least 4.6x relative to SLIC at matched inference.

AI.tldr: SEMIR uses a parameterized graph minor in place of the pixel lattice to preserve thin-structure connectivity during segmentation, enabling full-resolution inference without patching and yielding strong, generalizable performance on power lines, cracks, and lane markings.
AI.motivation: Conventional representations disrupt thin structures when patching or with standard superpixels; topology-aware losses can't restore connectivity that has already been destroyed, necessitating a representation that preserves connectivity.
AI.method: Construct a parameterized graph minor whose contraction map preserves thin-structure connectivity under a contraction criterion. The minor collapses millions of pixels into boundary-aligned supernodes; a lightweight GNN classifies the reduced graph, and an exact map lifts predictions back to pixel resolution. The same architecture, features, loss, and GNN hyperparameters are used across datasets.
AI.result: Matches or exceeds domain-specific baselines on TTPLA, CrackSeg9k, and SkyScapes Lane in Dice, IoU, and Boundary F1; reduces mask fragmentation by at least 4.6x relative to SLIC at matched inference; scalable to 21 MP images with full-resolution inference without patching.
AI.conclusion: The graph-minor framework provides connectivity-preserving, high-resolution segmentation for thin structures with a single, generalizable pipeline, combining efficient graph-based inference with precise pixel-level outputs.

title: BFMTrack: Latent Sequence Optimization for Physics-Based Motion Tracking with Behavioral Foundation Models
summary: Behavioral Foundation Models (BFMs) offer a promising path toward universal physics-based character control by organizing a rich repertoire of physically plausible behaviors into a latent space, guided by a large-scale motion dataset. While these models excel at time-invariant tasks, such as goal-reaching and state-based reward optimization, their latent space does not directly support time-varying objectives, such as tracking a motion sequence. For tracking, existing heuristics rely on moving-window-averaging that fails to capture the nuances of highly dynamic motions. In this work, we propose a novel Latent Sequence Optimization (LSO) to address these shortcomings. Our approach combines simulation rollouts with a policy gradient update to optimize over a sequence of latents, extending the capabilities of BFMs toward precise motion tracking without requiring reward engineering and tuning. To guide the optimization toward smooth, coherent latent trajectories, we model the latent sequence using temporally correlated noise. We validate our approach across dense tracking, sparse keyframing, and direct deployment onto a real humanoid robot.

AI.tldr: Proposes Latent Sequence Optimization (LSO) to extend Behavioral Foundation Models for time-varying motion tracking by optimizing a latent sequence via policy-gradient with correlated noise, validated on dense/sparse tracking and real robot.
AI.motivation: BFMs excel at time-invariant tasks but lack direct support for dynamic motion tracking; current moving-average heuristics fail to capture fast dynamics; need end-to-end latent-sequence optimization without reward engineering.
AI.method: Combine simulation rollouts with policy gradient to optimize a sequence of latents; model latent sequence as temporally correlated noise for smooth trajectories; apply to dense tracking, sparse keyframing, real humanoid deployment.
AI.result: Validated across dense tracking, sparse keyframing, and real humanoid deployment; improved tracking precision and coherence; avoids reward tuning.
AI.conclusion: LSO extends BFMs to track dynamic motions, enabling precise time-varying objectives; potential broader applicability to tasks requiring sequence-level latent control.

title: Dense Supervision Is Not Enough: The Readout Blind Spot in Looped Language Models
summary: Looped language models turn hidden states into runtime state: each state is decoded for prediction and fed back into future computation. This creates a basic supervision question: which state variables does cross-entropy actually control? We show that dense per-loop cross-entropy controls the variables exposed by the readout, not every variable active in the recurrent transition. Hidden-state scale gives a concrete failure mode. Scale-invariant readouts such as RMSNorm and LayerNorm hide radial scale from the immediate cross-entropy loss, while pre-norm residual recurrence continues to carry and update that same scale. Thus per-loop loss can make early exits usable without controlling recurrent scale. In 44M and 129M looped transformers without inter-loop normalization, per-loop cross-entropy through RMSNorm readouts still drives final hidden-state norms into the thousands or tens of thousands. Scale-visible readouts and explicit norm penalties keep norms in the tens, and scale-removing recurrence is the complementary architectural fix. The resulting design rule is simple: dense supervision trains exits; recurrent scale control requires either making scale visible to a loss or removing it from the loop. Consistent with this rule, scale-controlled variants achieve lower perplexity at matched inference-depth operating points in our variable-depth benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Dense per-loop supervision controls only readout-exposed variables in looped LMs; hidden-state scale can diverge under RMSNorm/LayerNorm with pre-norm recurrence, undermining recurrence-scale control. Use scale-visible readouts or norm penalties, or remove scale from the loop. This yields lower perplexity at fixed inference depth.
AI.motivation: Investigate which state variables cross-entropy supervision actually influences in looped language models and why hidden-state scale undermines control over recurrence. Derive actionable architecture/design principles.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of per-loop supervision; empirical evaluation on looped transformers with 44M and 129M parameters, varying normalization schemes (no inter-loop norm, RMSNorm/LayerNorm, pre-norm residuals), and with/without explicit norm penalties. Compare dense exit training vs recurrent-scale control mechanisms across variable-depth benchmarks.
AI.result: Per-loop cross-entropy primarily constrains readout-exposed variables; the recurrence’s hidden-state variables can remain uncontrolled and their scale can explode under RMSNorm/LayerNorm without explicit penalties. Scale-visible readouts and norm penalties keep hidden-state norms manageable; scale-removing recurrence is a viable architectural fix. Dense supervision improves exit training; controlling recurrence scale requires making scale loss-visible or removing scale from the loop. Scale-controlled variants achieve lower perplexity at matched inference-depth points.
AI.conclusion: To train effective early exits and control recurrent scale, either expose scale to the loss (scale-visible readouts or explicit penalties) or remove scale from the loop. The proposed rule is supported by experiments and improves perplexity under fixed depth.

title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems
summary: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI is a comprehensive practitioner's reference for building autonomous AI systems. The book covers the full stack from first principles to production deployment, organized around a central thesis: building great agentic systems requires understanding every layer of the pipeline, not just one. The book opens with the LLM substrate -- transformer architecture, GPU systems, training and fine-tuning (SFT,LoRA, MoE), model compression, and inference optimization -- treated as essential foundations rather than the primary focus. It then develops the alignment and reasoning layer: reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), PPO, DPO and its variants, GRPO, reward modeling, and RL for large reasoning models including chain-of-thought and test-time scaling. The second half is devoted to agentic AI proper. Topics include agentic training and trajectory-based RL, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG and Agentic RAG), memory systems (in-context, external, episodic, and semantic), agent harness design and context management, and a taxonomy of agent design patterns. Inter-agent coordination is covered in depth: the Model Context Protocol (MCP), agent skills and tool use, the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) communication protocol, and multi-agent architectures spanning centralized, decentralized, and hierarchical topologies. The book concludes with agent development frameworks, agentic UI design, evaluation methodology for agentic tasks, and production deployment. Each chapter pairs rigorous theoretical foundations with implementation guidance, code examples, and references to the primary literature.

AI.tldr: A full-stack, theory-to-practice guide for building agentic AI systems, from LLM foundations to multi-agent deployment, with protocols, memory models, and evaluation frameworks.
AI.motivation: To address fragmentation in agentic AI development by providing a cohesive, standards-based reference that couples rigorous theory with actionable guidance across the entire pipeline.
AI.method: Book structure that spans foundational substrate (transformers, training, compression, inference), alignment and reasoning (RLHF, PPO/DPO, reward modeling, reasoning in LLMs), agentic AI development (training, RAG, memories, context/agent design), inter-agent coordination (MCP, A2A, architectures), and tooling (frameworks, UI, evaluation, deployment), pairing theory with code examples and literature references.
AI.result: A comprehensive, practitioner-oriented blueprint and taxonomy enabling end-to-end development of agentic AI systems, including coordination protocols, design patterns, and deployment guidance.
AI.conclusion: Adoption of the guide could standardize best practices across the field, improve robustness and scalability of agentic AI, and align engineering efforts with solid theoretical foundations; as a didactic resource, it bridges research and production.

title: Curvature-Guided Mixing for MLLM Adaptation
summary: Fine-tuning Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) on specialized tasks often leads to catastrophic forgetting of their general capabilities. Existing model merging methods to combat this are often heuristic or use sub-optimal objectives. We propose CurvatureGuided Mixing (CGM), a theoretically grounded framework that merges pre-trained and fine-tuned models. CGM formulates a joint optimization objective and uses a second-order (Hessian) approximation of the loss landscapes to analytically derive an optimal, closed-form "soft mixing" ratio. This ratio intelligently blends parameters based on their relative task-specific curvatures. We also introduce CGM$\dagger$, a robust "hard mixing" variant that performs sparse parameter selection guided by a novel, curvature-aware score. Experiments on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2.5VL across multiple downstream tasks show that CGM and CGM$\dagger$ consistently improve the trade-off between task specialization and general knowledge retention over existing methods. Code is available at github.com/zzsyjl/CGM-ECCV-2026.

AI.tldr: A curvature-guided model merging framework (CGM) with soft mixing and a sparse hard-mixing variant (CGMdagger) to fuse pre-trained and fine-tuned multimodal LLMs, improving general-knowledge retention while preserving task specialization, validated on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2.5VL.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning multimodal LLMs on specialized tasks often causes catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities. Existing model-merging methods are heuristic or sub-optimal; a principled, theoretically grounded approach is needed to merge models while balancing specialization and generalization.
AI.method: CGM derives a joint optimization objective and uses a second-order (Hessian) approximation of the loss landscapes to analytically obtain a closed-form, soft mixing ratio for parameter fusion. CGMdagger introduces a robust, sparse hard-mixing variant guided by a curvature-aware score for parameter selection.
AI.result: Empirical experiments on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2.5VL across multiple downstream tasks show that CGM and CGMdagger improve the trade-off between task-specific performance and preservation of general knowledge compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: A theoretically grounded, curvature-informed model-merging framework that yields principled soft and hard mixing strategies, with demonstrated gains over prior approaches and publicly available code.

title: Invariant Kalman filtering for extended pose estimation in multi-IMU articulated rigid-body systems
summary: Accurate extended pose estimation (orientation, velocity, and position) for IMU-instrumented articulated rigid-body systems is a key challenge in robotics and human motion analysis. The invariant extended Kalman filter (IEKF) addresses this problem for a single rigid body with convergence guarantees and consistency under unobservability, but extending these properties to articulated systems is nontrivial: inter-body pose coupling prevents a direct application, and incorporating joint kinematic constraints within the invariant framework remains an open problem. To address this gap, we introduce the relative L-extended pose, a Lie group representation for kinematic-tree systems. With one IMU per body, it yields group-affine dynamics and allows joint constraints to be expressed in invariant form. We incorporate these constraints as noise-free pseudo-measurements within an iterated IEKF (IterIEKF), thereby preserving the convergence and consistency guarantees of invariant filtering. Validated on both a UR5e robot and a human leg, the proposed IterIEKF outperforms all EKF, IterEKF, and absolute-pose IterIEKF baselines. It converges faster, exhibits lower run-to-run variability, and consistently achieves the lowest RMSE, with reductions of at least 50% compared to the second-best filter across all scenarios considered in this work.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Lie-group based representation (relative L-extended pose) for articulated rigid-body systems with one IMU per body, enabling invariant filtering (IterIEKF) that enforces joint constraints as noise-free pseudo-measurements; empirically outperforms EKF/IterEKF baselines and absolute-pose IterIEKF on a UR5e and a human leg, with faster convergence, lower variance, and at least 50% RMSE reduction.
AI.motivation: Accurate extended pose estimation for articulated systems (pose, velocity, position) is essential in robotics and human motion analysis. IEKF provides convergence guarantees for a single rigid body but cannot be directly applied to kinematic-tree structures due to inter-body coupling and joint constraints. A representation and filtering framework that preserves invariance while handling constraints is needed.
AI.method: Formulate a relative Lie-group representation (relative L-extended pose) for kinematic-tree systems with one IMU per body, ensuring group-affine dynamics. Express joint constraints in invariant form and incorporate them as noise-free pseudo-measurements within an iterated IEKF (IterIEKF), preserving convergence and consistency.
AI.result: Validated on a UR5e robot and a human leg. The IterIEKF outperforms all EKF, IterEKF, and absolute-pose IterIEKF baselines, converging faster, showing lower run-to-run variability, and achieving the lowest RMSE across scenarios, with reductions of at least 50% compared to the second-best filter.
AI.conclusion: The approach extends invariant filtering guarantees to articulated systems by a Lie-group representation and constraint integration, enabling reliable and accurate extended pose estimation. This paves the way for robust real-time tracking of complex kinematic trees; future work could explore scalability to larger trees, robustness to sensor dropout, and integration with higher-level estimation tasks.

title: From Meta Idea to Advanced Mathematical Discovery -- Human-AI Co-Discovery of Sign-Embedding Quantum Algorithms
summary: AI-assisted mathematics is often evaluated on solving predefined problems. In practice, however, many important advances begin earlier, when a vague research intuition is transformed into a concrete problem, a promising route, and a theorem family worth proving. This report studies that stage through a case study that led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms for matrix equations and matrix functions, foundational primitives in quantum linear algebra and operator-output quantum algorithms. The project began with a human-originated intuition that rational approximation is especially effective for jump-type functions such as the sign function, and might therefore serve as a design principle for quantum algorithms. Rather than merely assisting after the problem was fixed, AI-assisted exploration, including workflows later integrated into the agentic AI-mathematician system AIM, played a key role in expanding this intuition into a route map, comparing candidate formulations, and converging toward sign embedding as the central framework. AIM then helped connect a known matrix-sign identity to wider classes of matrix equations and matrix functions, and drafted proof and complexity calculations. The decisive scientific judgments remained human: selecting which human-AI-expanded routes were worth pursuing, rejecting a Cayley-trapezoidal approximation when its validity required a hidden condition, and refining the Sylvester implementation from a coarse quadratic-gap query route to the final factorized and scaled analysis. The report argues that human-AI co-discovery workflows, with systems such as AIM as important components, are most valuable not as standalone theorem provers, but as research partners for problem formation, connection discovery, derivation, and skeptical review inside a human-gated research loop.

AI.tldr: AI-assisted mathematics research benefits from human-AI co-discovery during problem formation, with sign-embedding as a central framework for quantum algorithms in matrix equations/functions; AIM-like systems assist exploration but humans make decisive judgments within a gated loop.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between AI-assisted problem solving and AI-facilitated problem formation; investigate how AI can transform vague intuition into concrete research directions, using sign embedding in quantum linear algebra as a case study.
AI.method: A case study tracing the transformation of a human intuition about rational approximation for jump-type functions into a concrete research route. It maps candidate formulations, connects a matrix-sign identity to broader classes, integrates AI agent AIM for exploration and drafting proofs/complexity analyses, and examines human judgments in route selection and refinement (e.g., rejecting a Cayley-trapezoidal approach, refining the Sylvester implementation).
AI.result: Sign embedding emerges as the central framework enabling a broader class of matrix equations and functions, with AI-assisted exploration expanding intuition into a route map and aiding proof/concept development. Ultimately, decisive scientific judgments remain human, and AIM functions as a key research partner within a human-gated loop.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI co-discovery workflows, exemplified by AIM-like systems, are most valuable for problem formation, connection discovery, derivation, and skeptical review, rather than as standalone theorem provers.

title: Project Auto-World: Towards Automated Benchmarking of Neural Relational Reasoners
comment: Submitted to NeurIPS 2026 E&D track. Code is available at https://github.com/autoworldrules/auto-world-rules
summary: Reasoning about relational structures remains a significant challenge for neural models, particularly when they must systematically apply learned knowledge to problem instances that are harder than those seen in training. Progress is hampered by the difficulty of evaluating such generalization, since a priori, it is rarely clear what makes an instance hard. We study how this issue can be addressed by using large language models (LLMs) to automate benchmark generation, learning to produce increasingly challenging instances in an end-to-end manner. Concretely, given a world parametrized by Datalog rules, and an Edge Transformer as the reasoning evaluator, we use LLM-driven evolutionary search (based on FunSearch) and autonomous agentic search to discover sampling functions that yield hard problem instances. We also show that the Edge Transformer can be improved using this data such that it generalizes well to further data perturbations. Finally, we show that the same machinery can be applied to novel worlds proposed by LLMs, opening the door to autonomous research on neural relational reasoning.

AI.tldr: LLM-driven generation of hard relational-reasoning benchmarks via evolutionary and agentic search, using Edge Transformer as evaluator; improves generalization and enables autonomous, novel-world benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Generalization in neural relational reasoning is challenging and hard to benchmark reliably. Automating benchmark generation with LLMs can produce progressively harder, diverse, and configurable problem instances to evaluate and train models.
AI.method: A world parametrized by Datalog rules is evaluated by an Edge Transformer. An LLM-driven evolutionary search (FunSearch) and autonomous agentic search discover sampling functions that generate hard problem instances. Generated data then improves the Edge Transformer, enhancing generalization to perturbations. The framework also allows applying the same pipeline to novel worlds proposed by LLMs, enabling autonomous research on neural relational reasoning.
AI.result: Edge Transformer performance improves with data discovered by the proposed pipeline, achieving better generalization to data perturbations. The approach enables generating novel, LLMed worlds for benchmarking and autonomous exploration of neural relational reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided benchmark generation combined with evolutionary and agentic search provides a scalable path for autonomous research in neural relational reasoning, yielding harder benchmarks and more robust evaluators that can adapt to new worlds proposed by LLMs.

title: Noise-Aware Boundary-Enhanced Generative Learning for Ultrasound Speckle Reduction
summary: Ultrasound is a non-invasive, real-time, and cost-effective imaging technique widely used in clinical diagnosis. However, its diagnostic efficacy is often compromised by inherent speckle noise that degrades image quality and obscures underlying anatomical structures. Existing speckle reduction methods tend to over-smooth tissue boundaries and generalize poorly to heterogeneous noise levels. To address these limitations, we propose a Noise-Aware Boundary-Enhanced Generative Learning (NBGL) framework for ultrasound speckle reduction, which simultaneously preserves annotated anatomical boundaries and adapts to varying noise levels. The NBGL framework consists of a speckle reduction branch and a boundary enhancement branch. The former leverages generative learning to suppress speckle noise, while the latter learns boundary-sensitive representations to preserve target anatomical structures. Furthermore, a noise-aware interaction weight generation (NIWG) module estimates the speckle noise level via 3D Laplacian filtering and a median absolute deviation estimator, and translates it into an adaptive interaction weight. This weight is incorporated into a weighted feature-wise linear modulation (wFiLM) module to adaptively modulate cross-branch feature coupling, thereby improving robustness to varying noise levels. Extensive evaluations on 141 3D transvaginal ultrasound volumes demonstrate that NBGL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in speckle reduction and structural preservation across six noise levels, while maintaining consistency with annotated anatomical boundaries.

AI.tldr: Proposes Noise-Aware Boundary-Enhanced Generative Learning (NBGL) for ultrasound speckle reduction, featuring a speckle-reduction generative branch, a boundary-enhancement branch, and a noise-aware interaction weight (NIWG) module that informs cross-branch modulation via a weighted FiLM (wFiLM); validated on 141 3D transvaginal ultrasound volumes across six noise levels with improved denoising and boundary preservation.
AI.motivation: Speckle noise in ultrasound degrades image quality and obscures anatomy; existing despeckling methods over-smooth boundaries and poorly adapt to varying noise levels, necessitating a framework that preserves boundaries and adapts to noise.
AI.method: Two-branch NBGL framework: speckle reduction branch using generative learning; boundary enhancement branch to preserve boundaries; NIWG module estimates noise via 3D Laplacian filter and MAD, converts to adaptive interaction weight used in wFiLM to modulate cross-branch features, enabling noise-adaptive fusion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 141 3D transvaginal ultrasound volumes show NBGL outperforms state-of-the-art despeckling methods across six noise levels, with superior speckle suppression and boundary preservation while maintaining anatomical boundary consistency.
AI.conclusion: NBGL provides robust, boundary-preserving despeckling across heterogeneous noise and demonstrates strong generalization on 3D clinical ultrasound data; the NIWG-wFiLM mechanism enables adaptive cross-branch interactions responsive to noise level.

title: ADM-Fusion: Adaptive Deep Multi-Sensor Fusion for Robust Ego-Motion Estimation in Diverse Conditions
summary: Robust multi-sensor fusion is essential for reliable autonomy in diverse and degraded environments, where sensor reliability can fluctuate rapidly. Because different modalities fail in distinct ways, effective fusion should adaptively balance complementary cues rather than rely on fixed weighting. This adaptability is particularly important for ego-motion estimation, since accurate updates depend on the consistent integration of complementary sensor information. We propose ADM-Fusion, an end-to-end deep learning based multi-sensor fusion method designed to adapt to environmental changes and sensor degradation. ADM-Fusion employs an adaptive sensor mixture-of-experts framework with content-aware routing to dynamically assign weights to sensor inputs in real time. The system further incorporates separate translation and rotation branches, coupled through a cross-task attention mechanism to preserve task-specific specialization while enabling information sharing. ADM-Fusion is trained on the CARLA-LOC simulated dataset and subsequently fine-tuned on KITTI real-world data, demonstrating effective simulation-to-real transfer. Experiments show that ADM-Fusion remains robust under degraded conditions while maintaining competitive performance against existing methods.

AI.tldr: Adaptive robust multi-sensor fusion using an adaptive mixture-of-experts with content-aware routing for ego-motion, enabling real-time weighting of sensor inputs and cross-task attention between translation and rotation branches; trained on CARLA-LOC and fine-tuned on KITTI, showing simulation-to-real transfer and robustness under degraded sensors.
AI.motivation: Sensor reliability varies across environments and modalities fail differently. For ego-motion, robust fusion requires dynamically balancing cues rather than fixed weights to maintain accurate updates in degraded conditions.
AI.method: End-to-end deep learning framework with adaptive sensor mixture-of-experts and content-aware routing to dynamically weight sensor inputs. Separate translation and rotation branches are coupled via cross-task attention to preserve specialization while enabling information sharing. Trained on CARLA-LOC and fine-tuned on KITTI for sim-to-real transfer.
AI.result: The method remains robust under degraded conditions and achieves competitive performance compared to existing methods, demonstrating effective simulation-to-real transfer.
AI.conclusion: ADM-Fusion offers an adaptive, robust fusion solution for ego-motion estimation in degraded environments, leveraging cross-task attention to balance specialization and information sharing across translation and rotation. Further work could include ablations, robustness analyses, and real-time deployment considerations.

title: On-Device Neural Architecture Search
summary: This paper proposes a new approach to near-sensor computing, in which a lightweight Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is performed directly on the deployment device to find the best tiny neural architecture for analyzing the real-time data acquired through sensors. This new adaptation capability can be particularly useful in the case of human-machine interfaces for which the neural network analyzing the biometrical data can be re-designed each time the user changes, after a guided data collection procedure, fighting the typical data variations between individuals on a new level. To implement the proposed approach a new NAS has been designed and then validated on the Italian Sign Language dataset (ISL), a collection of surface electromyography (sEMG) signals of the signs of the Italian alphabet, using several embedded systems. Moreover, further validation on the Case Western Reserve University dataset (CWRU), a benchmark for intelligent fault diagnosis, is presented to suggest another possible application of the proposed approach. When run on a Raspberry Pi 4, the proposed NAS performs beyond the state of the art proposing a tiny neural architecture having 0.63 times less RAM occupancy and 5.96 percentage points of more accuracy in the case of the ISL dataset; and 0.44 times less RAM occupancy and 0.2 percentage points of more accuracy in the case of the CWRU dataset.

AI.tldr: On-device lightweight NAS for real-time biometrics analysis; achieves compact architectures with improved accuracy on ISL and CWRU datasets, enabling personalized near-sensor computing.
AI.motivation: To enable reconfigurable, user-specific neural architectures directly on deployment devices to handle inter-user data variation in biometrics and other sensor-driven tasks.
AI.method: Design and validate a neural architecture search algorithm that runs on the deployment device (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4), applied to ISL sEMG data and CWRU fault diagnosis data, yielding tiny architectures with reduced RAM and higher accuracy.
AI.result: ISL: RAM occupancy reduced to 0.63x with 5.96 percentage points accuracy gain; CWRU: RAM occupancy reduced to 0.44x with 0.2 percentage points accuracy gain; validation on embedded systems demonstrates potential for near-sensor computing and flexible user-specific models.
AI.conclusion: On-device NAS enables efficient, personalized neural models for real-time biometrics and fault diagnosis, reducing memory footprint while preserving or improving accuracy, suitable for embedded HMI and early fault detection.

title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Compounding Failures in Agentic Persuasion via Taxonomic Strategy Retrieval
summary:
Foundation-model agents in multi-step, open-ended environments frequently suffer from compounding errors, where early mistakes contaminate long-horizon trajectories. While Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) succeeds in deterministic domains, agents in subjective tasks like persuasion experience severe problem drift and sycophantic conformity. We identify semantic leakage in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) as a reproducible trigger for these failures, as standard RAG prioritizes vocabulary overlap over logical necessity.
  To eliminate this leakage, we introduce Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), a systems intervention that routes strategies through a discrete categorical bottleneck to decouple argumentative structure from topical content. Zero-shot, cross-domain evaluations demonstrate that TS-RAG significantly improves the transfer of abstract logic where standard semantic retrieval collapses. Crucially, TS-RAG acts as a "capability bridge" in asymmetric deployments, empowering lightweight persuaders to consistently defeat parametrically superior opponents (improving win rates from 70.5 to 78.5) and accelerating argumentative efficiency. Finally, we introduce trace-level diagnostics via a turn-by-turn Debate State Representation (DSR), demonstrating the necessity of strict constraints to prevent evaluation collapse via default agentic sycophancy.

AI.tldr: TS-RAG uses a discrete taxonomic bottleneck to decouple argumentative structure from topical content in retrieval-augmented generation, mitigating semantic leakage and improving cross-domain abstract reasoning and debate efficiency.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model agents in open-ended, multi-step environments suffer from compounding errors and semantic leakage where RAG favors vocabulary overlap over logical necessity, leading to drift and conformity in subjective tasks such as persuasion.
AI.method: Introduce Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG) with a discrete categorical bottleneck routing strategies; evaluate zero-shot cross-domain performance; develop Debate State Representation (DSR) for turn-by-turn trace diagnostics; demonstrate TS-RAG as a capability bridge in asymmetric deployments.
AI.result: TS-RAG significantly improves the transfer of abstract logic where standard RAG collapses; win rates rise (e.g., from 70.5 to 78.5); reduces evaluation collapse due to sycophancy; accelerates argumentative efficiency.
AI.conclusion: TS-RAG serves as a generalizable capability bridge enabling lightweight persuaders to defeat stronger opponents; trace diagnostics via DSR are necessary to prevent evaluation collapse and guide strict constraint enforcement.

title: Yuvion VL: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Adversarial Content and AI Safety
summary: General-purpose models often struggle to reliably identify and understand real-world multimodal risks, largely due to the inherent multimodal adversarial nature of content and AI safety. We present Yuvion VL, a family of multimodal large language models purpose-built for content and AI safety, with both instruction-tuned and reasoning-oriented variants. Yuvion VL addresses this gap by treating safety as an inherently adversarial and multimodal problem and designing the entire pipeline around adversarial robustness. For data construction, we develop an automated pipeline integrating adversarial-aware data synthesis with multi-stage quality control, producing large-scale, high-quality multimodal samples augmented with domain knowledge and reasoning annotations. For training, we adopt a three-stage pipeline that includes continued pretraining for risk-concept cross-modal alignment, instruct post-training for production-grade safety tasks, and reasoning post-training for enhanced interpretability and performance in complex tasks. We further introduce Confuse-then-Contrast Fine-Tuning, a contrastive framework that mines model-specific confusions and constructs multi-image contrastive groups to enforce explicit discrimination of fine-grained visual-semantic elements, enabling the model to distinguish between visually similar cases with different safety implications in adversarial safety tasks. To support rigorous evaluation, we further introduce Yuvion VL RiskEval (YVRE), a collection of benchmarks covering diverse open and internal evaluations, with a focus on content and AI safety, adversarial robustness, and real-world capability requirements. Experiments show that Yuvion VL-32B achieves industry-leading safety performance, surpassing comparably sized open-source models and best closed-source commercial models, while maintaining comparable general capabilities.

AI.tldr: Yuvion VL is a safety-focused multimodal large language model family designed for adversarial robustness in content and AI safety, featuring automated adversarial-aware data synthesis, a three-stage training pipeline, Confuse-then-Contrast Fine-Tuning, and the Yuvion VL RiskEval benchmark suite; results claim industry-leading safety performance for Yuvion VL-32B with maintained general capabilities.
AI.motivation: The core problem is that general-purpose multimodal models struggle to reliably identify and manage real-world multimodal risks due to their adversarial and multimodal nature. The work aims to build a safety-aligned, robust multimodal LLM and an end-to-end pipeline.
AI.method: Automated adversarial-aware data construction with multi-stage quality control; three-stage training (continued pretraining for cross-modal risk alignment, instruct post-training for safety tasks, reasoning post-training for interpretability); Confuse-then-Contrast Fine-Tuning with model-specific confusion mining and multi-image contrastive groups; development of Yuvion VL RiskEval (YVRE) benchmarks for safety, robustness, and real-world capabilities.
AI.result: Yuvion VL-32B achieves industry-leading safety performance, outperforming comparably sized open models and best closed-source models, while maintaining competitive general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields substantial improvements in multimodal safety and adversarial robustness, supported by a dedicated evaluation suite (YVRE) and a structured training pipeline that targets risk concepts, instruction safety, and reasoning.

title: SurveilNav: Collaborative Object Goal Navigation with Robot and Surveillance System
summary: With the growing deployment of surveillance systems in factories, offices, and homes, integrating them with robots offers a promising direction for collaborative and efficient task execution. However, existing approaches largely focus on single-robot scenarios and struggle with multi-view collaboration in large-scale environments. In this paper, we present a novel indoor collaborative object navigation dataset built on Habitat-Sim, featuring 206 cameras across 74 floors. The dataset enables systematic evaluation of an agent's ability to exploit multi-view surveillance information. To address the limitations of single-robot perception, we propose SurveilNav, a collaborative navigation framework that integrates active camera scheduling, joint 2D/3D mapping, VLM-based value estimation, and collaborative target verification. By synergizing the robot's dynamic local perception with the static global view of surveillance, this architecture effectively overcomes both the limited perception range of single agents and the inherent blind spots of fixed cameras, resolving inefficient exploration. Experimental results on the HM3D dataset demonstrate that SurveilNav substantially outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both exploration efficiency and navigation success rate. Moreover, the system shows strong potential for applications in large-scale search, home environments, and rescue missions.

AI.tldr: A collaborative, multi-view indoor object navigation framework (SurveilNav) with a surveillance-camera dataset, combining multi-view perception, 2D/3D mapping, and VLM-based value estimation to improve exploration and navigation in large-scale environments.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of single-robot perception and blind spots in fixed camera networks; enable efficient exploration and robust navigation in large-scale indoor settings by leveraging diverse surveillance viewpoints.
AI.method: Dataset: Habitat-Sim based with 206 cameras across 74 floors. SurveilNav architecture: active camera scheduling, joint 2D/3D mapping, VLM-based value estimation, collaborative target verification; coordination between robot local perception and static global surveillance.
AI.result: On HM3D dataset, SurveilNav achieves state-of-the-art exploration efficiency and navigation success rate, outperforming existing methods; demonstrates strong potential for large-scale search, home environments, rescue missions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating dynamic robot perception with static surveillance views via multi-view collaboration yields significant gains in indoor navigation tasks; dataset enables systematic evaluation of multi-view exploitation.

title: LLM Evolution as an Industry-Scale Ecosystem: A Lifecycle Perspective on Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning capability is critical for Industrial LLMs, as deployed models must be continuously updated to meet evolving requirements and environments, rather than repeatedly retrained from scratch. However, most existing research focuses on improvements on static benchmarks, failing to capture real industrial needs. In this survey, we reformulate Industrial Continual Learning (ICL) for LLMs as a closed-loop update-and-release problem in a versioned ecosystem, where updates propagate hierarchically to industrial, application-specific models and LLM-powered applications, with capability inheritance and transfer across versions and model families. From this ecosystem perspective, we identify three core challenges: repeated adaptation erodes model plasticity, foundation-model upgrades break capability inheritance, and long-term sustainability is constrained by deployment requirements. We then organize the technical landscape of ICL around five lifecycle design principles: preserving plasticity headroom, treating upgrades as capability transfer, enabling trustworthy continual reinforcement learning, making training recipes self-optimizing, and building accountability as a base layer for long-term iteration. For each principle, we synthesize representative technical directions. Finally, we evaluate the maturity of each principle and its technical components via an evidence-based lens, identify key gaps hindering real-world deployment, and outline a practical ICL deployment blueprint and a pathway for feeding industrial realities back into academic research.

AI.tldr: Proposes Industrial Continual Learning (ICL) for LLMs as a closed-loop, versioned update-and-release ecosystem; identifies three core challenges; offers five lifecycle design principles; provides maturity assessment and deployment blueprint.
AI.motivation: Industrial deployments require ongoing updates, capability inheritance across models and versions, and accountability; static benchmarks fail to capture real-world needs.
AI.method: Conceptual survey that reframes ICL, analyzes ecosystem-driven challenges, and derives design principles and evaluation/deployment guidance; synthesizes technical directions per principle.
AI.result: A structured five-principle framework with maturity assessment and a practical deployment blueprint, plus a roadmap linking industry realities back to research.
AI.conclusion: Treat upgrades as capability transfer, preserve plasticity headroom, enable trustworthy RL, automate training, and embed accountability; address gaps in plasticity retention, inheritance across base-model upgrades, and long-term sustainability; outline steps to close academia-industry loop.

title: Do vision-language models search like humans? Reasoning tokens as a reaction-time analog in classic visual-search paradigms
summary: Visual search has been one of the most productive paradigms in the study of visual attention: the way reaction time scales with the number of items distinguishes parallel, "pop-out" search from serial, attention-demanding search. I ask whether vision-language models (VLMs) exhibit the same behavioral signatures. I adapt four classic paradigms: feature versus conjunction search, spatial-configuration (T-vs-L) search, enumeration, and the tilted/vertical search asymmetry; and present them to current frontier and mid-tier models. Because a single model call has no reaction time, I use the number of reasoning ("thinking") tokens a model spends per trial as a within-model analog of search effort, and I compare against a large public human benchmark (Wolfe et al., 2010). The models reproduce several human signatures: feature search costs flat effort while conjunction effort climbs with set size; frontier models hold accuracy where mid-tier models collapse to chance; and a resolution control shows the conjunction cost is genuine search rather than difficulty resolving small shapes. They also diverge from humans in informative ways. The target-present effort slope exceeds the target-absent slope, reversing the human ordering; enumeration remains accurate where humans would lose count; and a reasoning model with adaptive deliberation declines to deliberate on detection tasks altogether, so that a single search expresses itself as an effort gradient in one model and as an accuracy cliff in another. I argue that psychophysical paradigms, applied behaviorally, are a sharp and inexpensive probe of machine visual cognition, and that the points of divergence are as informative as the points of agreement.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: Chorus II: Cross-Request Sparsity Reuse for Efficient Image-to-Video Generation
summary: Serving diffusion models for image-to-video generation is computationally expensive, posing significant challenges for large-scale deployment. Real I2V workloads often contain similar requests, such as repeated effect templates, related subjects, and recurring shot layouts. Existing cross-request acceleration methods mainly exploit this redundancy through feature reuse. We observe that similar I2V requests also share highly consistent sparse attention patterns, enabling historical sparse masks to serve as request-conditioned priors with almost no online mask-prediction overhead. We propose a cross-request reuse framework centered on \textbf{sparsity reuse}, with \textbf{feature reuse} as an optional extension safeguarded by a lightweight \textbf{guidance enhancement}. Our sparsity reuse is implemented as shared sparse mask reuse, which reuses high-quality sparse masks from similar historical requests to avoid per-request online mask prediction. Optional feature reuse applies downsampled computation to highly redundant spatiotemporal regions, mitigating boundary artifacts while preserving efficiency gains. Guidance enhancement reinforces image/text conditioning after reuse, mitigating semantic drift and condition-adherence issues. Experiments show that default sparsity reuse configuration preserves generation quality with a \textbf{2.16$\times$} speedup.

AI.tldr: Cross-request sparsity reuse for accelerating image-to-video diffusion; reuses historical sparse attention masks to avoid online mask prediction, with optional feature reuse and guidance enhancement, achieving 2.16x speedup while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost in serving diffusion-based image-to-video generation by exploiting cross-request redundancy in attention sparsity patterns.
AI.method: Shared sparse mask reuse across requests; optional feature reuse via downsampled computation in redundant spatiotemporal regions; guidance enhancement to reinforce conditioning after reuse; no online mask prediction, relies on high-quality masks from similar historical requests.
AI.result: 2.16x speedup reported; default sparsity reuse preserves generation quality; feature reuse helps mitigate boundary artifacts; guidance enhancement reduces semantic drift and improves condition adherence.
AI.conclusion: Sparsity reuse is an effective cross-request acceleration strategy for I2V diffusion models; combining with feature reuse and guidance enhancement offers additional robustness and efficiency, contingent on historical mask relevance and request similarity.

title: AeroCast: Probabilistic 3D Trajectory Prediction for Non-Cooperative Aerial Obstacles via Transformer-MDN Architecture
summary: Autonomous aerial vehicles operating in shared airspace must predict the future positions of non-cooperative obstacles to plan evasive maneuvers before a collision becomes unavoidable. Unlike cooperative systems that share intent, non-cooperative obstacles such as birds, uncontrolled drones, or debris exhibit multi-modal motion that deterministic predictors cannot adequately represent. Existing methods either rely on recurrent encoders that propagate temporal information sequentially, limiting their ability to capture long-range kinematic precursors of maneuver initiation, or produce point forecasts that provide no distributional information to downstream planners. This paper presents AeroCast, a probabilistic trajectory prediction framework that combines a Transformer encoder with a Mixture Density Network output head to predict per-timestep Gaussian mixture distributions over future three-dimensional displacements. A translation-invariant consecutive displacement encoding and a calibration-oriented training objective address the input design and mode-degeneracy challenges specific to mixture-based aerial trajectory prediction. On a hybrid real-and-synthetic quadrotor corpus spanning nine motion categories, AeroCast reduces Average Displacement Error and Final Displacement Error by approximately 50% relative to the baselines over a five-second horizon, and achieves the lowest negative log-likelihood and Continuous Ranked Probability Score among all compared methods. Ablation analysis identifies velocity input and model capacity as the primary contributors to prediction quality, and positional encoding as essential for long-horizon trajectory coherence. AeroCast inference completes in 0.1ms per sample, compatible with real-time onboard deployment at 100Hz.

AI.tldr: AeroCast is a probabilistic trajectory predictor for non-cooperative aerial obstacles that uses a Transformer encoder and a Mixture Density Network head to output per-timestep Gaussian mixtures of 3D displacements. It achieves ≈50% reductions in ADE/FDE over 5s against baselines, yields favorable NLL/CRPS, and runs in real-time (0.1 ms per sample) on hardware, with ablations highlighting velocity input, model capacity, and positional encoding as key factors.
AI.motivation: Non-cooperative obstacles (birds, debris, rogue drones) exhibit multi-modal, long-range, and uncertain motion that deterministic models struggle to capture. Existing recurrent encoders propagate information sequentially and struggle with long-horizon precursors; point forecasts lack uncertainty information critical for planning. A probabilistic, long-horizon-capable, real-time predictor is needed for safe autonomous flight.
AI.method: AeroCast combines a Transformer encoder with a Mixture Density Network (MDN) output head to produce per-timestep Gaussian mixture distributions over 3D displacements. It uses a translation-invariant consecutive displacement encoding and a calibration-oriented training objective to address input design and mode-degeneracy in mixture predictions. The model inputs include velocity; positional encoding is used for long-horizon coherence. Training and evaluation on a hybrid real-and-synthetic quadrotor corpus spanning nine motion categories. Inference runs at 100 Hz with 0.1 ms per sample.
AI.result: AeroCast reduces Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Final Displacement Error (FDE) by ~50% over a 5-second horizon versus baselines. It achieves the lowest negative log-likelihood (NLL) and Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) among compared methods. Ablation shows velocity input and model capacity as primary predictors of performance; positional encoding is essential for long-horizon coherence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Transformer+MDN framework provides accurate, multi-modal probabilistic trajectory predictions for non-cooperative aerial obstacles, enabling real-time onboard deployment. Key contributions include translation-invariant displacement encoding and calibration-focused MDN training to mitigate mode degeneracy, with ablations confirming critical design choices.

title: A Spectral Phase Diagram for Binary Few-Shot Classification: Intrinsic Dimensionality, Geometric Saturation, and Representational Diagnosis
summary:
Deciding when to stop collecting labeled examples is a fundamental but undertheorized problem in applied machine learning. The saturation index $S(K) = \operatorname{erank}(\widehatΣ_W^{(K)}) / K$ measures the ratio of the effective rank of the pooled within-class sample covariance to the shot count; we prove it falls below a threshold precisely when the covariance estimator is well-concentrated around the population covariance and the linear discriminant has stabilized. The index is computable in $O(d^3)$ time from support features alone, requiring no test labels or trained classifier.
  Evaluated across $N = 246$ doubling-pair observations from seventeen binary tasks and six datasets, sixteen of seventeen tasks have a positive within-task Spearman correlation between $S(K)$ and marginal accuracy gain (median $ρ= 0.811$). The pooled Spearman correlation is $ρ= 0.548$ ($p = 1.1 \times 10^{-20}$, $N = 246$). A three-phase diagram (exploration, transition, saturation) with mean marginal gains of $3.48\%$, $2.40\%$, and $0.82\%$ is supported by all pairwise significance tests ($p \leq 0.008$). As a binary stopping rule, the index achieves AUC $= 0.752$, providing meaningful probabilistic guidance for annotation decisions. Asymptotic effective rank and peak accuracy show no significant monotone relationship across tasks (Spearman $r_s = 0.380$, $p = 0.133$, $N = 17$). A small saturation index paired with low accuracy diagnoses representational inadequacy. All results are for binary classification with a fixed linear classifier; extensions to $N$-way settings and pretrained backbone representations are discussed as future work.

AI.tldr: Introduces the saturation index S(K) = erank(Σ̂_W^{(K)})/K as a data-efficient stopping criterion for labeling; computable from support features without labels or a trained classifier; empirically correlates with marginal gains and yields a probabilistic stopping rule (AUC=0.752) in binary tasks; applicable limitations include restriction to binary linear classifiers and future extensions to multiclass/backbones.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of principled stopping criteria for labeling in supervised learning. Propose a statistic that captures when the pooled within-class covariance is well-estimated (concentrated) and the linear discriminant has stabilized, enabling a data-driven stop independent of test labels.
AI.method: Define S(K) = erank(Σ̂_W^{(K)}) / K, estimated from support features only. Evaluate on 246 doubling-pair observations across 17 binary tasks and 6 datasets. Assess within-task Spearman correlations between S(K) and marginal accuracy gains, compute pooled correlation, and examine a three-phase exploration–transition–saturation diagram. Use S(K) as a binary stopping rule and compute AUC. Discuss asymptotic relationships between erank and peak accuracy and note lack of monotone relation across tasks.
AI.result: S(K) crosses a threshold when the covariance estimator concentrates and the LDA stabilizes. 16 of 17 tasks show positive within-task Spearman correlation between S(K) and marginal gains (median ρ=0.811); pooled Spearman ρ=0.548 (p=1.1×10^-20, N=246). Three-phase diagram: mean marginal gains 3.48%, 2.40%, 0.82% across exploration, transition, saturation with p≤0.008 for pairwise tests. As binary stopping rule, AUC = 0.752. Asymptotic effective rank and peak accuracy show no significant monotone relationship across tasks (r_s=0.380, p=0.133). Low S(K) paired with low accuracy signals representational inadequacy.
AI.conclusion: The saturation index S(K) is computable from features alone and provides meaningful probabilistic guidance for annotation decisions in binary, fixed-linear-classifier settings. It captures when the covariance estimator is well-concentrated and the discriminant has stabilized, supporting a three-phase labeling workflow. Limitations include applicability to multi-class settings and to pretrained backbones; future work covers N-way tasks and non-linear representations.

title: Beyond Shapley: Efficient Computation of Asymmetric Shapley Values
summary: We address the problem of explainability in machine learning models through feature attribution methods. In particular, we consider a variant of Shapley values known as Asymmetric Shapley Values (ASV), which enables the incorporation of causal knowledge into model-agnostic explanations through the use of a causal graph. We show that in certain contexts in which the computation of SHAP is $\#P$-hard, the exact computation of ASV can be done in polynomial time. To extend this algorithmic result, we introduce a notion of equivalence classes over the topological orderings of the underlying causal graph, which is useful to reduce the time to compute ASV. In particular, we present a polynomial-time algorithm (in the number of equivalence classes) to compute it whenever the causal graph is a rooted directed tree. Finally, we develop an algorithm for approximating ASV in arbitrary causal DAGs which relies on a procedure to sample topological orderings uniformly at random. To implement this sampling mechanism we leverage known algorithms as well as simpler alternatives. Our experimental results demonstrate the practical viability of the proposed approach in realistic causal structures.

AI.tldr: ASV (Asymmetric Shapley Values) uses causal graphs to produce model-agnostic explanations; exact ASV can be computed in polynomial time in some SHAP-hard settings; equivalence classes of topological orderings reduce computation; there's a polynomial-time method for rooted trees; an approximation method via random topological ordering sampling for general DAGs; experiments show practical viability.
AI.motivation: To improve reliable feature attribution by embedding causal knowledge into explanations, overcoming SHAP's computational hardness and enabling scalable, causally-consistent attributions.
AI.method: Formalize ASV with a causal graph; prove exact polynomial-time computation in certain graph families; define equivalence classes over topological orderings to reduce work; give a polynomial-time algorithm for rooted directed trees based on these classes; develop an approximation algorithm for arbitrary DAGs by sampling topological orders uniformly; implement sampling via known algorithms and practical heuristics; validate on realistic causal structures.
AI.result: Demonstrates that exact ASV computation is tractable in contexts where SHAP is #P-hard; equivalence-class reduction makes computation faster; rooted-tree case admits a polynomial-time solution in the number of equivalence classes; provides a sampling-based approximation method for general DAGs with practical efficiency; experiments confirm viability on realistic causal structures.
AI.conclusion: ASV provides a causally informed, computationally efficient alternative to SHAP under a broad set of causal graphs; offers exact solutions for specific structures and scalable approximations for general DAGs, supported by empirical results encouraging real-world applicability.

title: Wan-Streamer v0.1: End-to-end Real-time Interactive Foundation Models
summary: We present Wan-Streamer, a native-streaming, end-to-end interactive foundation model designed from the ground up for real-time, low-latency, full-duplex audio-visual interaction. Wan-Streamer seamlessly models language, audio, and video as both input and output within a single Transformer, where the sequence is represented as interleaved visual, audio, and text input tokens together with visual, audio, and text output tokens, coordinated by block-causal attention for incremental streaming. Unlike cascaded interactive systems that rely on separate VAD, ASR, language, TTS, audio-driven animation, or video-generation modules, Wan-Streamer does not rely on external language, speech, avatar, or video-generation modules: perception, reasoning, generation, response timing, turn management, and cross-modal synchronization are learned jointly within one unified model, reducing pipeline latency and error accumulation. To support natural audio-visual responsiveness, we redesign the entire stack around streamability, including causal encoders, causal decoders, block-causal attention, and low-latency multimodal token scheduling, enabling streaming units as short as 160 ms at 25 fps. Wan-Streamer achieves approximately 200 ms model-side response latency and approximately 550 ms total interaction latency when combined with 350 ms bidirectional network latency, supporting sub-second duplex audio-visual communication. These results position Wan-Streamer as a unified, end-to-end, multimodal interactive foundation model for low-latency streaming interaction.

AI.tldr: Wan-Streamer is a native-streaming, end-to-end interactive multimodal foundation model enabling real-time, low-latency, full-duplex audio-visual interaction with interleaved tokens and block-causal attention, achieving sub-second latency.
AI.motivation: To reduce latency and error accumulation in cascaded pipelines by learning perception, reasoning, and generation jointly in a single model; improve streaming responsiveness across language, audio, and video.
AI.method: A single Transformer treats language, audio, and video as both input and output. The sequence uses interleaved visual, audio, and text input tokens with corresponding output tokens; employs causal encoders/decoders, block-causal attention, and low-latency streaming token scheduling; streaming units as short as 160 ms at 25 fps; designed to operate without external modules like VAD, ASR, TTS, or video generation modules; end-to-end learning of perception, reasoning, generation, response timing, and cross-modal synchronization.
AI.result: Model-side latency ≈200 ms; total interaction latency ≈550 ms when combined with 350 ms bidirectional network latency; supports sub-second duplex audio-visual communication.
AI.conclusion: Wan-Streamer positions itself as a unified, end-to-end multimodal interactive foundation model optimized for low-latency streaming interaction.

title: RGB: RL Guided Whole-Body MPPI for Humanoid Control
summary: Humanoid robots require whole-body controllers that are both robust and precise in contact-rich environments. While deep reinforcement learning (RL) achieves robust stability, its behavior is tightly coupled to the training objective and command interface, making it difficult to add new feedback objectives without retraining. In this study, we propose an RL guided whole-body model predictive path integral (MPPI) framework that acts as an add-on feedback controller on top of a pretrained RL policy. Instead of using RL policy as the final controller, we use it as a sampling prior that biases MPPI rollouts toward dynamically feasible behaviors. Task objectives are specified through modular MPPI cost terms, and MPPI closes the loop by continuously correcting the RL prior online to satisfy these objectives without retraining the policy. Simulations on a 29-DoF Unitree G1 humanoid in MuJoCo demonstrate stable high-rate control (average 280~Hz). The proposed method improves task-level precision over a pure RL baseline under the same command interface. This is achieved by correcting systematic drift during straight walking and tracking additional whole-body reference signals imposed through the cost.

AI.tldr: An RL-guided MPPI add-on controller combines a pretrained policy as a sampling prior with modular MPPI costs to achieve high-rate, robust whole-body control for humanoids, enabling objective changes without retraining; demonstrates ~280 Hz in simulation and improved task accuracy vs pure RL.
AI.motivation: RL alone fixes behavior to training objectives; need ability to incorporate new feedback objectives online and achieve precise, stable control in contact-rich humanoid scenarios.
AI.method: Use pretrained RL policy as sampling prior to MPPI rollouts; MPPI costs encode task objectives; online correction of RL prior; no policy retraining; simulated on 29-DoF humanoid in MuJoCo; ~280 Hz control.
AI.result: Improved task-level precision over pure RL baseline; stable high-rate control; corrected drift; tracked additional reference signals via cost; shows feasibility of add-on approach.
AI.conclusion: An effective, retraining-free augmentation to RL for flexible, precise whole-body control in humanoids; MPPI module complements RL to meet new objectives online.

title: When Do Conservation Laws Survive Learned Representations? Certified Horizons for Latent World Models
summary: We ask a representation-learning question about physical world models: when does a conservation law remain certifiable after a model learns a latent representation? A certified horizon bounds -- in advance, from measurable model defects -- how many steps a rollout provably stays on a physical invariant's level set. The key design choice is what is certified: not a learned latent Hamiltonian or a learned scalar witness (a model can conserve either while drifting in true energy), but the decoded physical invariant obtained by decoding the latent state and evaluating the known invariant. Around this object we derive shell-horizon certificates whose budget decomposes into representation, readout, and latent-dynamics defects, with a monotone alignment bridge through which a soft learned witness yields a certified horizon for the decoded invariant, and test them across state, learned-lift, and pixel observations on conservative systems. Conservation certificates can survive learned representation, but not all geometric priors survive equally: hard canonical symplectic structure yields the longest horizons in known phase coordinates yet does not cross a learned chart, whereas a controlled-Lipschitz-aligned soft invariant survives in the learned-representation settings we test; pixel certification is recovered on a readout-stable sub-tube; and the Kepler problem exposes a geometric boundary. The central object is therefore not a latent Hamiltonian, but a decoded physical invariant whose robustness to representation learning can be measured, certified, and falsified.

AI.tldr: A framework to certify that a decoded physical invariant remains on its level set despite learning latent representations, via shell-horizon certificates decomposed into representation, readout, and latent-dynamics defects; evaluated across state, lifted, and pixel observations; highlights robustness of decoded invariants and differential robustness of geometric priors (symplectic vs Lipschitz-soft) with Kepler as a boundary case.
AI.motivation: To quantify and guarantee when a conservation law remains certifiable after learning a latent representation. By certifying a decoded invariant (evaluated from the latent state) rather than a latent Hamiltonian or scalar witness, the work provides principled, model-agnostic bounds on how far a rollout can deviate before violating the invariant.
AI.method: Introduce shell-horizon certificates that bound the steps a rollout can stay on the invariant's level set. Decompose the certifiable budget into representation, readout, and latent-dynamics defects, with a monotone alignment bridge enabling a soft learned witness to yield a certified horizon for the decoded invariant. Evaluate across state, lifted (learned latent), and pixel observations, on conservative systems; compare hard geometric priors (canonical symplectic structure) versus controlled-Lipschitz-aligned soft invariants; analyze pixel-level certification on readout-stable sub-tubes; test on Kepler problem to identify geometric boundaries.
AI.result: Conservation certificates can endure learned representations, but the robustness depends on the geometric prior: hard symplectic structure yields longer horizons in true phase coordinates but does not transfer across learned charts; a controlled-Lipschitz-aligned soft invariant remains robust in learned representations; pixel-level certification is achievable on readout-stable sub-tubes; the Kepler problem reveals a geometric boundary that constrains certifiability.
AI.conclusion: The central object is the decoded physical invariant, not a latent Hamiltonian. Its robustness to representation learning can be measured, certified, and falsified, enabling principled guarantees for physics-informed learning. The framework reveals trade-offs between priors and learnability and suggests design guidelines for constructing invariants and readouts that survive representation learning.

title: The Clinician's Veto: Navigating Trust, Liability, and Uncertainty in Autonomous AI Prescribing
summary: Autonomous AI systems are transitioning from advisory to autonomous roles for medication prescriptions. Recent United States bill H.R. 238 and Utah's prescription-renewal pilot both authorize AI to prescribe medications in an agentic capacity.
