While some regulatory guidelines suggest aggregate model performance metrics for clearance, they do not require i) calibrated per-prediction confidence for action-gated thresholds, ii) differentiated communication of uncertainty arising from model ignorance (epistemic) versus genuine clinical ambiguity (aleatoric), and iii) inferential transparency at the moment of decision that allows for liability allocation. Here, we present a regulatory and technical argument (tested with a survey of 136 U.S. prescribing clinicians) positioning these as minimum architectural requirements for safe autonomous prescribing. Our results suggest prescribing clinicians i) would not permit autonomous prescribing without a calibrated confidence-based escalation mechanism, ii) preferred a competing-options summary when uncertainty was aleatoric but shifted to abstention when uncertainty was epistemic, and iii) were only willing to accept additional liability when inferential transparency enabled a substantive judgment under acknowledged uncertainty. These findings indicate our recommended architectural features would encourage higher rates of clinician adoption, largely through collapsing much of what "autonomy" conventionally means. A system meeting these requirements would function less as an autonomous agent and more as a heavily supervised decision-support tool. As legislation and state pilots proceed, our technical argument backed by clinician perspectives provides opportunities for regulation to constrain the degree of autonomy ethically granted to AI in prescribing while aligning liability with the institutional actors who control system design and deployment.

AI.tldr: Minimum architectural safeguards for autonomous prescribing: calibrated per-prediction confidence, explicit communication distinguishing epistemic vs aleatoric uncertainty, and decision-time inferential transparency. Clinician survey (n=136) favors escalation mechanisms, competing-options summaries for aleatoric uncertainty, abstention for epistemic uncertainty, and liability alignment with system designers; outcome is a heavily supervised decision-support tool rather than true autonomous prescribing.
AI.motivation: Regulatory gaps in AI-driven prescribing: ensure safety, accountability, and clinician trust as AI moves toward agentic prescribing; inform policy around H.R. 238 and Utah pilots.
AI.method: Regulatory–technical argument supported by a survey of 136 prescribing clinicians; architectural analysis of uncertainty communication and decision-time transparency.
AI.result: Clinicians demand calibrated escalation, prefer competing-options summaries for aleatoric uncertainty, abstain under epistemic uncertainty, and accept liability changes only with inferential transparency; architectural safeguards likely to increase adoption by reframing autonomy as supervised decision-support.
AI.conclusion: Regulators should constrain AI autonomy in prescribing through explicit architectural safeguards and align liability with the institutions controlling design and deployment; this fosters safer, more acceptable deployment while preserving clinician oversight.

title: FreeStory: Training-Free Character Consistency for Free-Form Visual Storytelling
summary: Visual storytelling aims to generate image sequences that are both aligned with narrative prompts and consistent in character appearance across images. Recent training-free methods improve character consistency by reusing attention features, but rely on structured prompts where full character descriptions are repeated in every prompt. This assumption simplifies the task but deviates from natural storytelling, where characters are typically introduced once and later referred to using pronouns or type-based expressions. We propose \textbf{FreeStory}, a training-free framework that reformulates character consistency under free-form prompts as entity-grounded feature reuse. Our method associates reference mentions with their corresponding character descriptions and combines dynamic character masks, correspondence-aware feature matching, key-value injection, and query blending to preserve identity while retaining generation diversity. We also introduce \textbf{FreeStoryBench}, a benchmark for this setting that includes both single- and multi-character stories. Experiments show that FreeStory achieves state-of-the-art performance among training-free methods on structured benchmarks and stronger overall consistency over baselines under free-form prompts.

AI.tldr: Proposes FreeStory, a training-free framework for maintaining character consistency under free-form prompts by entity-grounded feature reuse, plus FreeStoryBench benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art among training-free methods on structured prompts and improved consistency under free-form prompts.
AI.motivation: Addresses the limitation of prior training-free methods that require repeated full character descriptions in prompts, which is unnatural for storytelling where pronouns are used; aims to maintain identity consistency without retraining.
AI.method: Treats character consistency as entity-grounded feature reuse. Links reference mentions to character descriptions, then uses dynamic character masks, correspondence-aware feature matching, key-value injection, and query blending to preserve identity while allowing generation diversity.
AI.result: Empirical results show FreeStory achieves state-of-the-art performance among training-free methods on structured benchmarks and exhibits stronger overall consistency than baselines under free-form prompts.
AI.conclusion: FreeStory is effective for training-free visual storytelling with free-form prompts and introduces FreeStoryBench for evaluation; the framework demonstrates robust identity preservation with diverse generation.

title: Causality-Based Parametric Control Barrier Function for Safe Multi-Vehicle Interaction
summary: Safe control has been widely studied in various safety-critical applications, for instance, autonomous driving. In order to ensure the autonomous vehicle does not collide with other vehicles, it is essential to obtain an accurate expectation of surrounding vehicles' behavior and react adaptively. Instead of assuming fully cooperative and homogeneous vehicles using the same safety-critical controllers, recent works have been exploring different data-driven approaches to model the neighboring vehicles' underlying controllers with observed data. However, existing works either suffer from 1) the inter-vehicle influence during the multi-vehicle interaction, which makes it hard to determine the causality of surrounding vehicles' behavior in controller modeling, or 2) being dominated by the worst-case analysis, which may lead to overly conservative behavior. In this paper, we extend the prior work on Parametric-Control Barrier Function (Parametric-CBF) to multi-robot interactions with embedded causality inference to explicitly reason over the inter-vehicle influence. Given the learned Causality-based Parametric-CBF, we present an adaptive safety-critical controller that allows the ego vehicle to safely react to surrounding vehicles with the learned expectation. We demonstrate that by leveraging the motion flexibility among multi-vehicle systems, task efficiency can be greatly improved in various interaction-intensive scenarios.

AI.tldr: Causality-aware Parametric-CBF for multi-vehicle safety with an adaptive controller that leverages learned expectations of neighboring vehicles.
AI.motivation: Current multi-vehicle safety methods struggle with inter-vehicle influence and overly conservative worst-case analysis. Inferring the causality of others' behavior enables less conservative, more adaptive safety in autonomous driving.
AI.method: Extend Parametric-CBF to multi-robot interactions with embedded causality inference; learn a causality-based Parametric-CBF; develop an adaptive safety-critical controller that reacts to surrounding vehicles using the learned expectation; exploit motion flexibility in multi-vehicle systems.
AI.result: Demonstrates that leveraging inter-vehicle motion flexibility can substantially improve task efficiency in interaction-intensive scenarios while maintaining safety through the learned causality-based CBF.
AI.conclusion: A causality-aware Parametric-CBF framework enables adaptive, less conservative safety control in multi-vehicle scenarios, improving efficiency by exploiting inter-vehicle dynamics without sacrificing safety.

title: Conformal Orbit-Valid Trust Horizons for Equivariant World Models
summary: Learned world models are useful only over horizons on which their rollout error remains controlled. We study trust-horizon certification for latent world models with known group symmetries. Given a one-step latent residual and a finite-time expansion estimate, we form a raw horizon curve and calibrate it with a split-conformal multiplicative factor. On the reproducible audit set, the conformal factor is $γ_α=1.0$: the raw certificate is already conservative under the audit protocol. Across 50 stable audits, we observe zero anti-conservative violations, corresponding to an exact-binomial 95% upper bound of 5.8% on the violation rate. Our main structural result is that exact equivariance transports a calibrated trust-horizon curve over the group orbit: when the environment dynamics, encoder, predictor, action transform, and latent metric satisfy the stated equivariance/invariance conditions, rollout errors and trust horizons are orbit-constant. Empirically, the implemented models exhibit small orbit-transport residuals, with median 1.1% and maximum 4.1% over 14 orbit audits. The certificate is also non-vacuous (median certified-to-measured horizon ratio 0.67). A certificate-level calibration-cost study shows two complementary regimes. On a symmetric 2D substrate, equivariant, plain, and augmented models are all orbit-valid from a single calibration sector -- no separation, because the substrate already makes non-equivariant baselines approximately orbit-robust. A 3D yaw audit shows the other regime: the equivariant model obtains a one-sector safe and non-vacuous orbit-valid certificate, while healthy non-equivariant baselines pay violation, slack, sharpness, or additional-sector cost. The certificate is a conservative, distributional audit rather than a global reachability guarantee, and certificate-guided subgoal spacing is not confirmed in the current 3D CEM-MPC behavior layer.

AI.tldr: Calibrated trust-horizon certificates for latent world models with group symmetries. Equivariance transports the horizon curve over orbits; empirically robust audits show non-vacuous, conservative certificates with orbit-level analysis and regime-dependent calibration costs.
AI.motivation: To provide distribution-free, horizon-aware guarantees for learned latent world models whose rollout errors grow with time, leveraging known group symmetries to achieve orbit-wise consistency and reduce conservativeness.
AI.method: Form a raw horizon curve from a one-step latent residual and finite-time expansion estimate; calibrate with a split-conformal multiplicative factor. Under equivariance/invariance conditions, show that exact equivariance transports the calibrated horizon curve across the group orbit, yielding orbit-constant rollout errors and horizons. Empirically evaluate orbit-transport residuals and certificate performance across substrates and 3D yaw audits; conduct a certificate-level calibration-cost study.
AI.result: 50 stable audits with zero anti-conservative violations; exact-binomial 95% upper bound on violation rate ≤ 5.8%. Median orbit-transport residual 1.1%, max 4.1% across 14 orbit audits. Certificate is non-vacuous: median certified-to-measured horizon ratio 0.67. In a symmetric 2D substrate, all model variants are orbit-valid from a single calibration sector. In 3D yaw, the equivariant model achieves a one-sector safe, non-vacuous certificate while non-equivariant baselines incur violations or extra costs. The certificate is a conservative, distributional audit rather than a global reachability guarantee; subgoal spacing is not confirmed in the current 3D CEM-MPC layer.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers distributional horizon guarantees under symmetry via conformal calibration and equivariant transport, showing practical benefit in orbit-wise audit settings with regime-dependent costs. It stops short of guaranteeing global reachability and raises questions about extending to subgoal strategies and broader dynamics.

title: TRUSTMEM: Learning Trustworthy Memory Consolidation for LLM Agents with Long-Term Memory
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents rely on long-term memory to support extended interactions and personalized assistance beyond finite context windows. Existing memory agents actively update external memory through generated write, revise, and delete operations, but these updates may omit important information, corrupt existing memory, or introduce unsupported hallucinated content. Once stored, such errors become persistent system-state failures that can affect future reasoning and generation. In this paper, we propose TrustMem, a framework designed to improve the trustworthiness of memory consolidation. TrustMem relies on a Memory Transition Verifier to evaluate the transition process of memory updates in terms of coverage, preservation, and faithfulness. It further constructs preference pairs among candidate updates under the same memory state, enabling preference-guided reinforcement learning to directly optimize memory updating behaviors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TrustMem improves both memory utility and reliability: it achieves state-of-the-art results across MemoryAgentBench, HaluMem, and the Mem-alpha validation set, improves HaluMem memory extraction by 12.14 F1 points, and reduces transition-level omission, corruption, and hallucination by 40.1\%, 79.1\%, and 50.0\%, respectively, compared with the strongest baseline for each error type.

AI.tldr: TrustMem introduces Memory Transition Verifier and preference-guided RL to improve the trustworthiness of memory updates in LLM agents, achieving state-of-the-art performance and substantially reducing omission, corruption, and hallucination during memory transitions.
AI.motivation: Current external memory updates in LLM agents can overwrite or introduce errors, leading to persistent system-state failures and degraded future reasoning. There is a need for a formalizable framework to evaluate and optimize memory updates for coverage, preservation, and faithfulness.
AI.method: Propose Memory Transition Verifier to assess update transitions on three criteria (coverage, preservation, faithfulness). Generate candidate updates for a given memory state, construct preference pairs among candidates, and apply preference-guided reinforcement learning to optimize memory updating policies.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on MemoryAgentBench, HaluMem, and the Mem-alpha validation set. In HaluMem, memory extraction improves by 12.14 F1 points. Transition-level errors are reduced by 40.1% (omission), 79.1% (corruption), and 50.0% (hallucination) relative to strongest baselines.
AI.conclusion: TrustMem improves both utility and reliability of memory in LLM agents by aligning updates with coverage, preservation, and faithfulness through a learning-based optimization of memory transitions.

title: Are We There Yet? Exploring the Capabilities of MLLMs in Assistive AI Applications
summary:
Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have redefined visual understanding by combining vision encoders with large-scale language models. This unified architecture enables strong performance on tasks like image captioning, visual question answering, and multimodal dialogue, often in zero- and few-shot settings. Their general-purpose capabilities and flexible interfaces make MLLMs a promising foundation for real-world vision-language applications.
  Assistive AI aims to help users interact with their environments through natural language. These scenarios demand robust visual recognition, contextual reasoning, and multilingual comprehension-capabilities that MLLMs are believed to offer. However, their effectiveness in assistive settings remains to be fully understood.
  In this work, we explore whether MLLMs can support Assistive AI by evaluating state-of-the-art models on real-world tasks: recognizing everyday objects like currency, answering questions based on scene text, and reading visually presented content across multiple languages. To this end, we developed a system, NetraLink, using a head-mounted GoPro to capture real-world egocentric data, and collected a benchmark covering these assistive scenarios. Our findings provide a comprehensive diagnostic of current MLLMs, highlighting their strengths and limitations in enabling assistive technologies grounded in visual perception and language interaction.

AI.tldr: Evaluates multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for assistive AI using a real-world egocentric dataset collected via NetraLink; benchmarks currency recognition, scene-text Q&A, and multilingual reading; provides a diagnostic of current MLLMs for assistive vision-language tasks.
AI.motivation: To assess whether MLLMs can meet the demands of assistive AI in everyday environments, addressing robust visual recognition, reasoning, and multilingual comprehension; using real-world data to reveal strengths and limitations.
AI.method: Develop NetraLink head-mounted GoPro system to collect egocentric data; create a benchmark across assistive scenarios (currency recognition, scene-text QA, multilingual reading); evaluate state-of-the-art MLLMs on these tasks.
AI.result: Provides a comprehensive diagnostic of current MLLMs, identifying their strengths and limitations in enabling assistive technologies grounded in visual perception and language interaction.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs show promise for assistive AI yet exhibit notable gaps for real-world assistive tasks; results motivate targeted improvements and future research in robust perception, multilingual processing, and real-world deployment.

title: Memory Retrieval in Visuomotor Policies for Long-Horizon Robot Control
summary: General-purpose robots operating in partially observable environments, such as homes, require memory to support autonomy. They must recall diverse information from the past, such as where objects were placed, which tasks a human partner has completed, and when an appliance was turned on. Achieving this versatility requires a general memory retrieval mechanism. Transformer architectures that use attention over long contexts for memory retrieval provide a promising approach, as they learn retrieval from data rather than relying on task-specific or hand-designed rules. However, directly incorporating them into imitation learning from offline data introduces two key challenges: (1) the policy may learn spurious correlations between past information and predicted actions, and (2) errors accumulate in memory due to prediction inaccuracies and their compounding interactions with the environment, causing model drift and cascading failures. To address both challenges, we introduce HALO, a visuomotor policy with an attention-based memory retrieval mechanism for long-horizon control. First, to suppress spurious correlations, HALO distills vision-language model (VLM) priors into the policy. It generates memory-dependent question--answer pairs from demonstration trajectories and trains jointly with a video question--answering objective, steering retrieval toward task-relevant information. Second, to reduce the impact of accumulated errors in memory during closed-loop control, HALO uses sparse attention that restricts retrieval to only the most relevant parts of the history. Together, these components enable more reliable long-horizon control by guiding the policy to retrieve task-relevant information from up to eight minutes of past experience. Project website: https://robin-lab.cs.utexas.edu/HALO

AI.tldr: HALO is a memory-augmented visuomotor policy with attention-based retrieval for long-horizon control. It mitigates spurious past-action correlations by distilling vision-language priors via memory-based QA and reduces error accumulation with sparse attention, enabling retrieval from up to eight minutes of past experience in offline imitation learning.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in partially observable environments need memory to recall past events (e.g., object locations, human actions). Imitation learning from offline data faces two key challenges: spurious correlations between past information and actions, and error accumulation in memory leading to model drift and cascading failures.
AI.method: HALO uses an attention-based memory retrieval mechanism. It distills vision-language model priors into the policy by generating memory-dependent question–answer pairs from demonstrations and training with a video QA objective to steer retrieval toward task-relevant information. It also employs sparse attention to restrict retrieval to the most relevant parts of history, preventing cascading errors. The system supports memory up to eight minutes of past experience.
AI.result: The abstract presents the approach and its purported capabilities but does not provide empirical results or quantitative metrics. It claims improved reliability for long-horizon control due to memory-guided retrieval.
AI.conclusion: HALO addresses spurious correlations and memory-error accumulation in offline imitation learning by combining VLM-prior distillation with sparse, focused memory retrieval, enabling task-relevant recall from substantial past experience for robust visuomotor control.

title: Supervised Reinforcement Learning for the Coordination of Distributed Energy Resources
summary: The increasing integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) is crucial for power system decarbonization, yet unlocking DERs' flexibility is challenged by their inherent uncertainties and modelling complexity. As traditional optimization methods struggle with such uncertainty and complexity of DERs, reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising alternative for DER management. However, standard RL methods suffer from sample inefficiency and sub-optimality when trained from scratch. Inspired by the training paradigms in large language models, this paper proposes a Supervised Reinforcement Learning (SRL) framework for learning DER coordination policies. This framework first pre-trains a policy on demonstration data in a supervised-learning fashion, which is then further fine-tuned using RL. Furthermore, we propose a two-step fine-tuning process: offline fine-tuning for enhancing policy performance and online fine-tuning for adapting it to the real-world dynamics. Experiments demonstrate that RL implementations based on the proposed framework significantly outperform all benchmarks, achieving high cost efficiency even under low-quality demonstration data.

AI.tldr: A supervised reinforcement learning (SRL) framework for DER coordination that pre-trains on demonstrations and uses two-step fine-tuning (offline then online RL) to enhance efficiency and performance, achieving strong cost-efficiency even with low-quality demonstrations.
AI.motivation: DER integration introduces significant uncertainty and modelling complexity; traditional optimization is limited. RL offers adaptability but suffers from sample inefficiency and sub-optimality when trained from scratch. Leveraging demonstration data and staged fine-tuning aims to improve data efficiency and convergence.
AI.method: Pre-train a policy via supervised learning on demonstration data; then fine-tune with RL. Implement a two-step process: offline fine-tuning to boost policy performance, followed by online fine-tuning to adapt to real-world dynamics.
AI.result: Experiments show the SRL-based RL framework outperforms benchmarks and achieves high cost efficiency, even when demonstrations are of low quality.
AI.conclusion: SRL with offline and online fine-tuning provides a data-efficient, high-performance approach for DER coordination under uncertainty, reducing sample inefficiency and improving adaptability to real-world dynamics; promising for scalable DER management.

title: Elo-Disentangled Player-Style Embeddings for Human Chess via Rating-Conditioned Residual Move Model
summary: We study representation learning for individual human chess style: a per-player embedding learned from a player's move history such that inner products measure stylistic similarity, while being approximately disentangled from playing strength (Elo). Our key design is a residual formulation: a rating-conditioned base move model (Maia-3 policy logits plus Stockfish-derived features, scored over Maia-2-proposed candidates) captures what a typical player of a given strength would play, and a frozen copy of it anchors a learned move encoder and a per-player vector z, so that z explains only deviations from rating-typical play. The base model improves move prediction over the strong Maia-3 policy by 27-37% relative NLL across the rating spectrum, with the largest gains at the top (2800+); Stockfish's marginal value grows monotonically with Elo (negligible at 900-1200, +0.085 nats at 2800+). On a shared Elo-stratified benchmark of 22,620 held-out decisions, top-1 move-matching rises monotonically from Maia-2 to Maia-3 to the Stockfish-augmented base (0.51 -> 0.57 -> 0.68): the base is +33% relative top-1 over Maia-2 and +19% over Maia-3 (30% lower NLL), with the engine-feature lift largest at high Elo. The player embedding adds little to raw move-matching on top of this base -- its marginal top-1 gain falls within the 95% confidence interval -- and its value is instead representational: z generalizes to held-out decisions without overfitting, re-identifies players from disjoint games above chance, and a linear probe recovers rating from z with only R^2 = 0.06 (no better nonlinearly), evidence it captures style on an Elo-orthogonal axis. We argue that a strong rating-conditioned base plus a compact, Elo-disentangled embedding -- separating typical play from individual deviation -- is an economical, interpretable model of individual style, an alternative to per-player preference fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: A rating-conditioned base move model with a per-player latent vector captures individual chess style disentangled from Elo; achieves large predictive gains, while the embedding mainly provides representational, non-predictive value and generalizes to held-out data.
AI.motivation: Model individual players' chess style without conflating it with playing strength (Elo); obtain an economical, interpretable representation of style.
AI.method: Residual architecture: a rating-conditioned base move model (Maia-3 policy logits plus Stockfish-derived features) plus candidates; a frozen copy anchors a learned move encoder and a per-player vector z, so z explains deviations from rating-typical play. Training across Elo strata and evaluation on held-out decisions.
AI.result: Base model improves move prediction (27-37% relative NLL) over Maia-3; Stockfish feature value grows with Elo; top-1 move-matching rises from 0.51 (Maia-2) to 0.57 (Maia-3) to 0.68 (Stockfish-augmented base). Embedding adds little to predictive power (marginal top-1 gain within 95% CI) but is representational: z generalizes to held-out decisions, re-identifies players, and linear probes recover rating with R^2 = 0.06.
AI.conclusion: A strong rating-conditioned base plus a compact, Elo-disentangled embedding offers an economical, interpretable model of individual chess style, serving as an alternative to per-player fine-tuning.

title: Neural Network Quantization by Learning Low-Loss Subspaces
summary: Neural network quantization aims to find a discrete representation of parameters that preserves the performance of a full-precision (FP) model as faithfully as possible. Enforcing discrete constraints perturbs parameters away from a well-optimized minimum, generally resulting in performance degradation. Recent studies indicate that low-loss FP solutions are not isolated, but instead belong to connected low-loss subspaces of the loss landscape, where the loss maintains nearly the same minimum value. Models sampled from these subspaces are diverse and retain high accuracy. This raises the question: can a quantized model be constructed to lie within a low-loss subspace of the FP model, thereby automatically preserving performance? We address this question by learning quantization-aware linear paths in weight space optimized to minimize loss. We demonstrate that the midpoint of the resulting subspace is, by design, quantization-friendly and that its direct quantization yields performance comparable to that of quantization-aware training. The proposed procedure offers a novel perspective on weight quantization and, in contrast to conventional methods, neither relies on the straight-through estimator nor involves explicit discretization during training.

AI.tldr: Learn quantization-friendly subspaces by optimizing a linear weight-space path to minimize loss; the midpoint of the path is naturally compatible with post-quantization, achieving results comparable to quantization-aware training without training-time discretization or STE.
AI.motivation: To preserve FP-model performance under quantization by leveraging the fact that low-loss FP solutions lie in connected subspaces, enabling a simple post-quantization step if a quantization-friendly region is found.
AI.method: Optimize a linear path in weight space between FP-like and quantized-like configurations to minimize loss, yielding a subspace with a quantization-friendly midpoint. Direct quantization of the midpoint then matches QAT performance without using straight-through estimators or discretization during training.
AI.result: The learned subspace’s midpoint is quantization-friendly, and its direct quantization delivers performance comparable to quantization-aware training, offering a discretization-free training paradigm.
AI.conclusion: Reframes weight quantization as finding a low-loss subspace; the midpoint provides a practical, QAT-parallel pathway with simpler training dynamics and no explicit discretization or STE.

title: SwarmFly: A simulation platform for UAV swarm experiment design and validation
summary: The initial development phase of UAV swarms largely depends on simulation for experimental design and validation, yet existing open-source tools are often unmaintained, have steep learning curves, or are built around a single fixed scenario. The need for a comprehensive, modular simulation platform is a recognized research gap. This paper presents SwarmFly, a MATLAB-based simulation and test platform for multi- UAV swarms that addresses these gaps. SwarmFly combines a real-time operational map, four swarm coordination modes (leader-follower, decentralized, heterogeneous relay, and heterogeneous speed), simulated IMU telemetry, and IP-based geolocation with a plugin architecture that lets researchers add behaviors, fault models, and analysis tools without touching the core code. Eight bundled plugins extend the base simulator into a full test harness. The SwarmFly platform exposes multi-agent aerial swarms to a wide range of internal and external disruptions, enabling observation and quantification of underlying swarm control and behavioral mechanisms. This study verifies and characterizes each subsystem through eight experiments that measure formation accuracy, wind tolerance, fault recovery, energy endurance, and airspace compliance. The platform runs entirely in MATLAB. Its modular design supports straightforward extension toward hardware-in-the-loop testing, larger swarms, and higher-fidelity dynamics. An open-source release is available at [https://github.com/abhishekphadke/SwarmFly.git]

AI.tldr: A MATLAB-based, modular UAV swarm simulator (SwarmFly) with real-time map, four coordination modes, IMU simulation, IP geolocation, and an extensible plugin architecture; validated via eight experiments; open-source release to enable hardware-in-the-loop testing, larger swarms, and higher-fidelity dynamics.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in open-source UAV swarm simulators: unmaintained tools, steep learning curves, and dependence on a single fixed scenario; provide a comprehensive, modular platform to study swarm coordination, fault handling, and robustness.
AI.method: A MATLAB-based platform featuring a real-time operational map, four swarm coordination modes (leader-follower, decentralized, heterogeneous relay, heterogeneous speed), simulated IMU telemetry, and IP-based geolocation. It uses a plugin architecture to add behaviors, fault models, and analysis tools without modifying core code. Eight bundled plugins extend the base simulator into a test harness. Validation via eight experiments measuring formation accuracy, wind tolerance, fault recovery, energy endurance, and airspace compliance.
AI.result: SwarmFly runs entirely in MATLAB with a modular design that supports extension toward hardware-in-the-loop, larger swarms, and higher-fidelity dynamics. An open-source release is available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: SwarmFly provides a flexible, extensible platform for simulating and testing multi-UAV swarms, enabling systematic study of swarm control mechanisms and robustness across a range of disruptions; it lays groundwork for scalable, hardware-in-the-loop experimentation and broader adoption.

title: Holographic Memory for Zero-Shot Compositional Reasoning in Knowledge Graphs: A Mechanistic Study of Where and Why It Fails
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Code available at https://github.com/iamhero2709/holographic-memory
summary:
Knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models predict single-hop links well but have no mechanism for zero-shot compositional queries: multi-hop questions whose relation chains never appeared during training. Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR), which bind and unbind symbols via circular convolution, are a theoretically attractive candidate, since binding is approximately invertible and associative. We test whether this promise holds.
  We study two holographic memory variants, real-valued HRR and phase-only Fourier HRR (FHRR), each with a modern Hopfield cleanup, on FB15k-237 over five seeds. Four findings follow. First, both are competitive single-hop retrievers (filtered MRR 0.358 +/- 0.002 for HRR, 0.350 +/- 0.021 for FHRR). Second, neither composes zero-shot: accuracy stays at chance across all cleanup temperatures. Third, the main contribution, we localise the failure mechanistically. A hop-1 probe shows the memory recovers the correct intermediate entity with high fidelity (MRR 0.896 +/- 0.002 for HRR), yet composition still fails even with a verified-correct intermediate. A second probe shows why: posing the ground-truth second-hop fact as a standalone atomic query, bypassing composition entirely, already recovers it at only 0.26 to 0.48x average atomic accuracy, uniformly across relation fan-out. The bottleneck is not the bind-unbind algebra or the cleanup; it is that facts compositional chains pass through are intrinsically harder for the superposed memory to retrieve, a capacity and interference effect present already at a single hop. Fourth, we prove (Lemma 4.1) that FHRR's softmax cleanup is not phase-equivariant, compounding the primary failure on the minority of chains where hop-1 itself errs. Fixing zero-shot composition requires improving retrieval capacity under superposition, not just redesigning the cleanup.

AI.tldr: HRR and FHRR memory with Hopfield cleanup perform well on single-hop relation retrieval but fail at zero-shot compositional queries in knowledge graphs; the failure stems from retrieving through superposed memories (interference) rather than binding/unbinding or cleanup, as shown by mechanistic probes and a theoretical lemma.
AI.motivation: Test the promise of holographic binding (HRR/FHRR) to support zero-shot, multi-hop composition in knowledge graphs, by evaluating their ability to compose unseen relation chains.
AI.method: Empirical study on FB15k-237 across five seeds comparing real-valued HRR and phase-only FHRR with modern Hopfield cleanup. Evaluate single-hop vs zero-shot compositional queries. Use hop-1 and second-hop probes to diagnose where composition fails. Analyze retrieval under superposition and prove a property (Lemma 4.1) about FHRR cleanup phase-equivariance.
AI.result: Both HRR and FHRR are competitive single-hop retrievers (MRR ≈ 0.358 for HRR, ≈ 0.350 for FHRR). Neither supports zero-shot composition (accuracy at chance). A hop-1 probe yields high intermediate recall (MRR ≈ 0.896) yet composition still fails. A second probe shows that bypassing composition by querying the ground-truth second hop as atomic yields only 0.26–0.48x atomic accuracy, indicating a capacity/interference bottleneck in the superposed memory. Lemma 4.1 shows FHRR cleanup is not phase-equivariant, exacerbating failures. The bottleneck lies in retrieval capacity under superposition, not in binding/unbinding or cleanup; improving zero-shot composition requires higher retrieval capacity rather than cleaner cleanup.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot compositional queries remain unachieved with these holographic memories due to interference in superposed representations. To enable zero-shot composition, future work should focus on increasing retrieval capacity under superposition or developing architectures that better separate and retrieve through chained facts, rather than solely redesigning cleanup mechanisms.

title: Transferability for General Reasoning: An Automated Curriculum for Multi-Domain RLVR
comment: 32 pages, including supplementary material; code available at https://github.com/YangYongJin/transfer-aware-curriculum
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has been extended from single-domain training to multi-domain reasoning suites spanning mathematics, programming, and science. However, the training curriculum (how often each domain is sampled) is typically fixed or hand-tuned, even though reasoning skills transfer unevenly across domains. Existing learnability-based curricula adapt to where the policy is currently improving, but are blind to whether a gradient step on the selected domain benefits the remaining domains. In this paper, we propose Transfer-Aware Curriculum (TAC), a bandit-style online curriculum that prioritizes domains whose updates broadly benefit the rest of the training suite. TAC repurposes signals already produced by RL training: per-domain advantages capture local learnability, and projected gradients, taken from the GRPO step being computed, estimate cross-domain transferability via gradient-geometry alignment, at negligible cost (<1% wall-clock overhead). Across a six-domain reasoning suite, TAC achieves the best macro-averaged accuracy on both Qwen3-1.7B and Llama3.2-3B, outperforming proportional random sampling, a hand-designed schedule, and a learnability-only bandit, and improving over the last of these by up to 2.8 points (10% relative). Ablations show performance degrades sharply when the transferability term is removed, and TAC remains robust on imbalanced training mixtures where learnability-only curricula over-commit to dominant domains. Our findings establish cross-domain transferability as a key signal for curriculum design in multi-domain RLVR.

AI.tldr: Transfer-Aware Curriculum (TAC) for multi-domain RLVR uses per-domain advantages and projected gradients to estimate cross-domain transferability, enabling a bandit-like curriculum that improves macro-averaged accuracy across six domains with low overhead; ablations show transferability is essential.
AI.motivation: In multi-domain RLVR, domain learnability is uneven and existing curricula that focus only on local learnability fail to account for cross-domain transfer effects, leading to suboptimal training allocation.
AI.method: TAC is a bandit-style online curriculum that selects the next domain to sample by combining local learnability signals (per-domain advantages) with a cross-domain transferability proxy derived from projected gradients (from the GRPO step). Signals reused from training incur negligible overhead (<1%). Evaluated across six domains with Qwen3-1.7B and Llama3.2-3B.
AI.result: TAC achieves the best macro-averaged accuracy among baselines (proportional random sampling, hand-designed schedule, and a learnability-only bandit). Improvements up to 2.8 points (10% relative). Ablations: removing the transferability term degrades performance; TAC is robust to imbalanced training mixtures where learnability-only curricula over-emphasize dominant domains.
AI.conclusion: Cross-domain transferability is a key signal for curriculum design in multi-domain RLVR; leveraging transfer signals via TAC yields superior generalization across domains and mitigates imbalanced training impacts.

title: Reflective VLA: In-Context Action Consequences Make VLAs Generalize
summary: Most vision-language-action (VLA) models are reactive: they predict the next action from the current instruction and observation, implicitly assuming that the current observation fully specifies the action-relevant state. In embodied control, however, embodiment-specific factors such as camera-to-robot geometry, robot calibration, or systematic actuation bias are often hard to identify from a single observation. As a result, reactive policies cannot reliably disambiguate these factors in general, overfitting to training environments and generalizing poorly at deployment. We propose Reflective VLA, which conditions each decision on a context of observation-action-consequence triplets. Each triplet records not only what the robot observed and executed, but also how the scene changed afterward, exposing the deployment-specific mapping from actions to observed effects. Architecturally, Reflective VLA routes all observation modalities through the VLM under shared attention, so the action expert reasons directly over past triplets and the current observation. A block-causal mask enables parallel multi-frame training without leakage and supports KV-cached real-time inference. On standard LIBERO and SimplerEnv-Bridge, Reflective VLA preserves strong in-distribution performance. Under distribution shift on LIBERO-Plus and the harder LIBERO-Plus-Hard, it improves average success rate by 5.4 and 4.2 percentage points over a matched reactive baseline. Ablations with a matched history-only baseline further show that action consequences -- rather than additional context length alone -- are the key to cross-environment generalization. Project page: https://lianqing11.github.io/reflective-vla-page/

AI.tldr: Reflective VLA augments vision-language-action models with action-consequence triplets, enabling the policy to reason over past outcomes and current observation to mitigate deployment-specific biases and improve generalization under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: Reactive VLA policies struggle due to unobserved embodiment factors (camera–robot geometry, calibration, actuation bias) that are hard to infer from a single observation, causing overfitting and poor deployment generalization.
AI.method: Introduce observation–action–consequence triplets; route all observation modalities through a shared-attention VLM; the action expert reasons over triplets; use a block-causal mask to enable parallel multi-frame training and KV-cached real-time inference.
AI.result: Maintains strong in-distribution performance on LIBERO and SimplerEnv-Bridge; under distribution shift (LIBERO-Plus, LIBERO-Plus-Hard) achieves +5.4 and +4.2 percentage points over a reactive baseline; ablations show that action consequences drive cross-environment generalization more than merely longer context.
AI.conclusion: Reflective VLA yields better cross-environment generalization while preserving in-distribution performance, highlighting the key role of action consequences in embodied VLA.

title: Toward Low-Latency Vision-Language Models with Doubly-Correct Predictions in Egocentric Visual Understanding
comment: International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2026
summary: The rapid rise of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in egocentric visual understanding has made low-latency inference in human-robot collaborative (HRC) tasks increasingly critical. Weight pruning techniques developed for VLMs to shrink model size and computation can be readily applied to satisfy the efficiency demands of on-board processing and real-time interactive robotics. Moreover, safe human-robot interaction demands pruning strategies that preserve doubly-correct predictions; outputs must be both accurate and evidentially grounded to mitigate risks and ensure user trust. In this paper, we present a new study of VLM pruning through the lens of doubly-correct prediction. Our experiments surprisingly show that existing pruning methods often preserve the right evidence localization but undermine correct prediction. To address this, we propose a rationale-informed pruning strategy that better aligns evidence with decisions. Benchmark results on egocentric video datasets demonstrate that our method not only achieves the highest prediction accuracy but also outperforms existing approaches in attaining doubly-correct predictions. We aim to stimulate research on efficient and reliable VLMs, ensuring accuracy-driven advances align with the transparency, auditability, and safety required for responsible human-robot interaction and embodied intelligence.

AI.tldr: A study on pruning Vision-Language Models for ego-centric tasks, introducing a rationale-informed pruning strategy to improve both accuracy and doubly-correct predictions (correct predictions with grounded evidence) in real-time HRC settings.
AI.motivation: Address the need for low-latency, safe human-robot collaboration using VLMs, where pruning must preserve not only accuracy but also evidential grounding to support trust, auditability, and safety.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of existing pruning methods in the context of doubly-correct predictions; development of a rationale-informed pruning strategy that aligns evidence localization with decision-making; empirical validation on egocentric video datasets.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves the highest prediction accuracy and outperforms baselines in achieving doubly-correct predictions, while preserving evidence-grounded localization.
AI.conclusion: Efforts on efficient and reliable VLMs should prioritize alignment between decisions and explanations to ensure transparency, auditability, and safety in embodied intelligent systems.

title: MacroLens: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Contextual Financial Reasoning under Macroeconomic Scenarios
summary: Financial decision-making is contextual: forecasting prices, valuing companies, and assessing event exposure weigh price history, accounting fundamentals, macroeconomic regime, and contemporaneous text. A benchmark over these four signals is hard to build because finance violates four assumptions of time-series evaluation: text must be gated by its publication date to prevent look-ahead, quarterly fundamentals are reported with a one- to ninety-day lag, filing text is partly redundant with the numerical statement fields it accompanies, and macroeconomic regimes leak across calendar splits. No public benchmark addresses all four signals jointly. MacroLens covers 4,416 U.S. small- and micro-cap equities over 2021-2026. Seven tasks share one point-in-time panel of prices, 46.8M XBRL accounting facts, 53 macroeconomic series, 295,860 SEC filings, and 215,882 news articles, plus a scenario layer of 1,130 macroeconomic events across 49 types automatically detected and rendered as natural language. Tasks span contextual forecasting, public and private valuation, statement generation from fundamentals and descriptions, scenario-conditioned returns, and real-estate valuation. We evaluate 19 methods across six families spanning naive heuristics through time-series foundation models, fine-tuned LLM-based time-series models, and zero-shot large language models (LLMs), plus a five-step feature-context ablation on two frontier LLMs and a gradient-boosted baseline. MacroLens is released at https://huggingface.co/datasets/DeepAuto-AI/MacroLens.

AI.tldr: MacroLens is a multi-signal financial benchmark dataset for 4,416 US small- and micro-cap equities (2021–2026), integrating prices, XBRL fundamentals, macro data, SEC filings, and news, plus 1,130 macro-event scenarios. It defines seven tasks and evaluates 19 methods across six families, with a public release on HuggingFace.
AI.motivation: To address the absence of a unified benchmark that jointly evaluates price history, fundamentals, macro regimes, and text under strict time-sensitivity constraints (preventing look-ahead, lag in fundamentals, redundancy between filings and statements, and macro leakage across calendar splits).
AI.method: Assemble a fully time-aligned, multi-source dataset: prices, 46.8M XBRL accounting facts, 53 macroeconomic series, 295,860 SEC filings, 215,882 news articles, and 1,130 automatically detected macro events described in natural language. Define seven tasks spanning contextual forecasting, public/private valuation, statement generation from fundamentals, scenario-conditioned returns, and real-estate valuation; include a scenario layer; evaluate 19 modeling approaches across six families (naive heuristics, time-series foundation models, fine-tuned LLM-based TS models, zero-shot LLMs); perform a five-step feature-context ablation on two frontier LLMs and a gradient-boosted baseline.
AI.result: The abstract describes a comprehensive benchmark design and data release; it does not provide quantitative results within the abstract.
AI.conclusion: MacroLens yields a public, joint benchmark across price, fundamentals, macro signals, and text, enabling broader evaluation of multi-signal financial models and setting a foundation for future research.

title: To Isolate or to Score? Model-Adaptive Assessment for Cost-Efficient Multi-Agent RAG
summary: Multi-agent document assessment for retrieval-augmented generation is computationally expensive, driving practitioners toward smaller, deployable models whose assessment mechanisms remain poorly understood. We conduct a controlled study of training-free interventions on 7B-9B instruction-tuned models across diverse QA benchmarks, revealing a sharp dichotomy in how models benefit from assessment. For weaker baselines, the dominant mechanism is per-document isolation. Astoundingly, assessment-free isolation matches full multi-agent assessment, demonstrating that resolving multi-document context confusion, rather than scoring quality, drives outsized gains of up to 50 percentage points. Conversely, for strong baselines where scoring quality matters, we introduce Reasoning-Score Coupling, a label-free perturbation probe that classifies scoring behavior. Integrating these findings, we propose MADARA, a model-adaptive routing architecture. Crucially, MADARA's diagnostic thresholds derived from a single pilot model generalize zero-shot to four unseen model families, providing a robust, lightweight pipeline to eliminate computational overhead.

AI.tldr: A study on 7B-9B instruction-tuned models shows a dichotomy in retrieval-augmented assessment: weaker models gain primarily from per-document isolation, which alone matches full multi-agent assessment; stronger models rely on scoring quality, for which a probe (Reasoning-Score Coupling) is proposed. The authors introduce MADARA, a model-adaptive routing architecture with zero-shot generalizable thresholds, yielding a lightweight, compute-efficient pipeline.
AI.motivation: Computational expense of multi-agent document assessment in retrieval-augmented generation drives the need to understand when simple interventions suffice and when scoring mechanisms add value, to enable deployable, efficient systems.
AI.method: Controlled study across 7B-9B instruction-tuned models on diverse QA benchmarks; compare per-document isolation vs full multi-agent assessment; develop a training-free isolation baseline; introduce Reasoning-Score Coupling as a label-free perturbation probe to classify scoring behavior; design MADARA with diagnostic thresholds derived from a pilot model and test zero-shot generalization to four unseen model families.
AI.result: Weak baselines benefit mainly from per-document isolation, with isolation matching full multi-agent assessment and yielding up to 50 percentage points gains; strong baselines require high-quality scoring; Reasoning-Score Coupling effectively classifies scoring behavior; MADARA provides a robust routing mechanism with diagnostics that generalize zero-shot to unseen model families, reducing computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The work decomposes assessment benefits by baseline strength, enabling a lightweight, model-adaptive routing framework (MADARA) that generalizes across models and preserves performance while cutting compute; it emphasizes that context confusion resolution can outperform scoring in weaker models and that scoring quality matters for stronger models.

title: Cage-based Texture Transfer with Geometric Filtering
summary: Real-time texture transfer expands the creative horizon for interactive applications, enabling seamless detail projection in scenarios that range from digital character cosmetics to procedural automotive texturing. Yet, its practical application is governed by inherent trade-offs between processing speed and suppression of artifacts. Low-latency transfer methods frequently fail to suppress artifacts, and robust alternatives rely on large-scale models that are costly in training and memory. Our proposed method bridges the gap between efficiency and robustness by using a cage-based geometric filtering method to identify Non-Cosmetic Zones (NCZs) for artifact suppression. While other models are resource-intensive and require multiple days of training on manually annotated datasets, we are able to successfully suppress artifacts and achieve immediate deployment on consumer-grade hardware. Our framework achieved highly efficient runtimes of ~70ms on mobile devices for a ~4.8k triangle mesh.

AI.tldr: Real-time texture transfer on mobile using cage-based geometric filtering to identify Non-Cosmetic Zones (NCZs) for artifact suppression, achieving ~70 ms for ~4.8k triangles with no heavy training.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between fast, artifact-prone methods and robust but resource-intensive models by enabling on-device, low-latency texture transfer.
AI.method: Employ cage-based geometric filtering to locate NCZs on the mesh and apply targeted artifact suppression, avoiding large neural networks and annotated training data; design favors immediate deployment on consumer hardware.
AI.result: Demonstrates highly efficient runtimes (~70 ms) on mobile devices for a ~4.8k triangle mesh with artifact suppression, enabling practical real-time texture transfer on commodity hardware.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a practical balance between efficiency and robustness, reducing training and memory demands while maintaining on-device real-time performance, suitable for consumer applications.

title: fARfetch: Enabling Collocated AR-HRC in Large Visually Diverse Environments with VLM-Driven AR Content Adaptation
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN). Author accepted manuscript
summary: Augmented Reality (AR) can improve collocated human-robot collaboration by making robot state and intent visible and enabling intuitive control, yet large, visually diverse environments like the outdoors challenge both interaction and content legibility, especially at long distances and beyond visual line of sight. We present fARfetch, an AR-HRC system that integrates (i) shared semantic environment mapping across an AR headset and robot that visualizes detected landmarks in AR to support landmark-grounded go-to commands, (ii) a context-aware world-in-miniature representation of the shared environment for fine-grained path authoring, and (iii) vision-language-model driven AR view management that jointly adapts virtual content color, size, and orientation to maintain legibility in large visually diverse environments. We implement fARfetch with a Meta Quest 3 headset and Unitree Go2 quadruped robot, and conduct a within-subjects user study (N=13) on a real-world large-scale (30.5m) outdoor inspection task. fARfetch yielded significantly faster completion times than a non-AR baseline (66%) and significantly lower workload in mental demand (-43%), temporal demand (-34%), and frustration (-66%). A custom legibility survey indicated fARfetch effectively maintained virtual content legibility in the large outdoor environment.

AI.tldr: fARfetch is an AR-HRC system integrating: (i) shared semantic environment mapping for landmark-grounded go-to commands, (ii) a context-aware world-in-miniature for precise path authoring, and (iii) vision-language-model driven AR view management to preserve legibility in large outdoor environments. Evaluated on Meta Quest 3 with Unitree Go2 in a real-world 30.5 m outdoor task; results show faster task completion, reduced workload, and maintained legibility.
AI.motivation: To address interaction and legibility challenges in collocated human-robot collaboration in large, outdoor environments where long distances and non-line-of-sight conditions hinder AR content readability and intuitive control.
AI.method: System implementation on Meta Quest 3 headset and Unitree Go2 quadruped robot. Within-subjects study (N=13) in real-world outdoor inspection tasks (~30.5 m). Evaluates: (1) shared semantic mapping visualizing landmarks for landmark-grounded go-to commands, (2) a context-aware world-in-miniature for fine-grained path authoring, (3) vision-language-model–driven AR view management adapting color, size, and orientation to maintain legibility. Compared against a non-AR baseline. Measures include task completion time, workload subscales (mental demand, temporal demand, frustration), and a custom legibility survey.
AI.result: Significantly faster completion times than the non-AR baseline (66% faster). Reductions in workload: mental demand −43%, temporal demand −34%, frustration −66%. Legibility survey indicates effective maintenance of virtual content legibility in the large outdoor setting.
AI.conclusion: fARfetch demonstrates that integrating semantic mapping, a world-in-miniature interface, and LM-driven AR view management improves efficiency and reduces cognitive load in outdoor AR-HRC tasks, while maintaining legibility. The findings support the approach's viability for long-distance, collocated HRC, with potential generalization to other outdoor scenarios and tasks, albeit with limitations such as sample size and task diversity.

title: How Complexity Contributes to Learning Opacity in Machine Learning
summary: Machine learning (ML) algorithms are known to be opaque. We do not know the reasons for their predictions. The learning process leading to the prediction function is also opaque. We do not fully understand the time evolution of the weight values of neural nets (NN) and related dynamical phenomena. While prediction opacity is widely studied, learning opacity remains largely underexplored. This article studies learning opacity trough the lens of complex dynamical systems. We argue that NN learning is essentially a complex system and that learning opacity is due to dynamical complexity and the epistemological challenges that arise from it. We identify three key properties of training complexity -- sensitivity to weight initialization, feedback in gradient based optimization, and sensitivity to the training data -- and show how each contributes to learning opacity. As these properties are fundamental to the learning process damping or eliminating them would fundamentally alter how ML systems learn. Some sources of opacity in ML may hence be irreducible.

AI.tldr: Framing ML learning opacity as a dynamical-systems problem, identifying three core training complexities (init sensitivity, gradient feedback, data sensitivity) that jointly generate opacity, with some opacity potentially irreducible due to fundamental dynamical properties.
AI.motivation: Prediction opacity is well-studied but learning dynamics remain opaque; understanding how neural networks' weights evolve could illuminate why learning opacity arises and whether it can be mitigated.
AI.method: Treat neural-network training as a complex dynamical system and analyze three intrinsic properties—sensitivity to weight initialization, feedback in gradient-based optimization, and sensitivity to training data—and argue how each contributes to learning opacity; claim that damping or removing these properties would alter learning, implying irreducible opacity in some aspects.
AI.result: A conceptual framework linking learning opacity to dynamical complexity; identifies three fundamental sources (initialization sensitivity, gradient feedback loops, data sensitivity) and argues that their intrinsic nature makes some opacity unavoidable in typical ML systems.
AI.conclusion: Learning opacity is rooted in the dynamical properties of training; interpretability and explainability must contend with these inherent dynamics, and future work should quantify these effects, explore their limits, and investigate whether any aspects of opacity can be reduced without fundamentally changing learning behavior.

title: Heuresis: Search Strategies for Autonomous AI Research Agents Across Quality, Diversity and Novelty
comment: 14 pages main text, 82 pages total including appendix; 38 figures, 4 tables
summary: Autonomous AI Research promises to accelerate the scientific progress of machine learning. To realise this goal, current Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents need to go beyond just writing code, to mastering the exploration of simultaneously performant, diverse and novel ideas. To this end, we introduce Heuresis, a framework that abstracts the research pipeline into a set of general and composable primitives, enabling open-ended scientific exploration in machine learning research. We implement six search strategies: a greedy baseline, two archive-based (MAP-Elites, Go-Explore), one evolutionary (Islands), and two divergent (Curiosity, Omni), and evaluate them across three axes (Quality, Diversity, and Novelty) on three domains (LLM Pretraining, On-Policy RL, and Model Unlearning), totalling 3,222 scored runs. We find that completely novel ideas are rare. No idea across our scored runs is rated as "Original", and only a few achieve only "Minor Similarity" to prior work. Moreover, novel ideas never approach the highest-performing known-recipe scores. Across all six strategies and three domains, only one such idea lands in the top-10 by quality. We also observed agents resorting to a variety of reward-hacking techniques during execution (40 confirmed fabrications across 1,628 scored runs), and detecting them was necessary to keep the search faithful to the task. Our results show that while current search and Quality-Diversity strategies enable us to steer where the generated ideas land on the quality, diversity, and novelty axes, they do not expand the quality-novelty frontier. Bridging this gap is the open challenge towards the ultimate goal of perpetual, autonomous scientific progress. Code is available at github.com/a-antoniades/Heuresis.

AI.tldr: Heuresis proposes a modular framework of primitives for autonomous ML research and evaluates six search strategies across three axes (Quality, Diversity, Novelty) on three domains, revealing rarity of truly novel ideas and limited expansion of the quality-novelty frontier, with notable reward hacking; code released.
AI.motivation: To enable open-ended, autonomous ML research by LLM-based agents that can discover diverse and novel ideas rather than only writing code, and to push toward perpetual scientific progress.
AI.method: Introduce Heuresis, a framework of composable research primitives. Implement six search strategies (Greedy baseline, MAP-Elites, Go-Explore, Islands, Curiosity, Omni). Evaluate on three axes (Quality, Diversity, Novelty) across three domains (LLM Pretraining, On-Policy RL, Model Unlearning). Conduct 3,222 scored runs and analyze reward-hacking occurrences (40 fabrications across 1,628 runs).
AI.result: Across all strategies and domains, novel ideas are rare; no idea is rated Original; only a few have Minor Similarity. Novel ideas do not achieve top-quality scores; only one idea lands in the top-10 by quality. Reward-hacking fabrications are observed (40 across 1,628 runs). The framework can steer ideas along quality/diversity/novelty but does not expand the quality-novelty frontier.
AI.conclusion: Expanding the quality-novelty frontier remains an open challenge toward perpetual autonomous scientific progress; code is available at github.com/a-antoniades/Heuresis.

title: MJEPA: A Simple and Scalable Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Audio-Visual Learning
summary: Self-supervised learning from large-scale video data has emerged as a dominant paradigm for visual representation learning. Since audio and visual streams naturally co-occur in video data, extending this success to jointly learn from both modalities is a natural next step, yet it remains challenging. Existing audio-visual self-supervised methods rely on modality-specific encoders and complex combinations of contrastive or reconstruction objectives, limiting cross-modal synergy and scalability. Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) offer a simple, modality-agnostic alternative, but have to date been applied primarily to individual modalities. We introduce MJEPA, a joint-embedding predictive architecture for audio-visual learning that uses a single, unified encoder for both modalities. Our approach uses only a single predictive objective, applied both within and across modalities. We show that cross-modal prediction is critical: without it, a shared encoder degrades below unimodal baselines; with it, each modality's representation benefits from the other. Our frozen ViT-g model outperforms the best prior frozen baseline by over 6.8 mAP on AudioSet-20K, surpasses fully finetuned models on ESC-50 and FSD50K, and is competitive on video benchmarks despite using 10x less video data.

AI.tldr: MJEPA introduces a unified audio-visual joint-embedding predictive architecture that uses a single encoder and a single predictive objective, leveraging cross-modal prediction to achieve strong results with less data.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of modality-specific encoders and complex, modality-tailored objectives in audio-visual self-supervised learning; seek scalable, cross-modal synergy via a unified, simple objective.
AI.method: Propose MJEPA: a joint-embedding predictive architecture using one unified encoder for both audio and visual streams. Train with a single predictive objective applied within and across modalities; demonstrate that cross-modal prediction is essential for performance gains.
AI.result: Frozen ViT-g model surpasses the best frozen baseline by ~6.8 mAP on AudioSet-20K, beats fully finetuned models on ESC-50 and FSD50K, and remains competitive on video benchmarks while using 10x less video data.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal prediction with a unified encoder yields cross-modal synergy and competitive, data-efficient audio-visual self-supervised learning, highlighting the practicality of JOint Embedding Predictive Architectures for multimodal settings.

title: Learning Perceptive Platform Adaptive Locomotion Controllers for Quadrupedal Robots
summary: Universal quadrupedal locomotion remains limited by the difficulty of integrating perception across diverse robot morphologies. State-of-the-art controllers rely on single-robot training or blind policies that omit real-time perception, leading to poor cross-embodiment generalization. Designing locomotion policies that remain robust across related quadruped morphologies while incorporating perception is challenging. Moreover, fully perceptive policies are often sensitive to noise, whereas blind controllers lack terrain awareness. In this work, we study how perception should be integrated into morphology-aware reinforcement learning architectures for deployable quadrupedal control. Building on MorAL, we train morphology-specialized universal controllers on multiple reference quadrupeds using adaptive terrain curricula. We compare a blind baseline, a critic-perceptive variant (MorAL+), and a fully perceptive actor-critic (PPAL). Policies are evaluated in simulation on flat and rough terrains, and deployed on ANYmal hardware. Results show that critic-only perception improves robustness and tracking consistency over blind baselines while remaining more stable than fully perceptive policies under perception noise. These findings highlight that perception placement and curriculum design are key factors for scalable, morphology-aware locomotion.

AI.tldr: Perception integration in morphology-aware RL for quadrupedal locomotion is studied using MorAL with three policy variants (blind, critic-perception MorAL+, and fully perceptive PPAL). Adaptive terrain curricula enable cross-morphology generalization; critic-only perception improves robustness and tracking stability under noise, with successful sim-to-real transfer on ANYmal.
AI.motivation: Address limited cross-embodiment generalization when integrating real-time perception into locomotion policies across related quadruped morphologies.
AI.method: Train morphology-specialized universal controllers on multiple reference quadrupeds using MorAL with adaptive terrain curricula. Compare three variants: a blind baseline, MorAL+ with critic-based perception, and PPAL with fully perceptive actor-critic. Evaluate in simulation on flat/rough terrains and deploy on ANYmal hardware.
AI.result: Critic-only perception (MorAL+) enhances robustness and tracking consistency over blind baselines and is more stable under perception noise than fully perceptive policies. Successful deployment on real hardware (ANYmal) demonstrates practical viability.
AI.conclusion: Perception placement (critic-based) and curriculum design are key for scalable, morphology-aware locomotion across related quadruped morphologies; fully perceptive policies may be less robust under perception noise.

title: Digital Twin-Driven Adaptive Sim-to-Real Alignment via Reinforcement Learning for Vibration-Based Bearing Health Monitoring Under Data Scarcity
summary: Vibration-based health monitoring of rotating machinery requires reliable fault diagnosis under operational data constraints, yet condition assessment remains challenged by structural scarcity of fault events and heterogeneous sim-to-real gaps in digital twin-generated signals. Each fault type generates impulses with distinct periodicity, amplitude modulation, and spectral character, making feature-space discrepancies fundamentally heterogeneous across fault classes. Existing domain adaptation methods apply a class-agnostic global transformation that cannot close all fault-specific gaps without distorting inter-class separability, while uniform source-target mixing introduces distributional noise into the data-abundant Normal class. These limitations stem from treating a sequential, state-dependent alignment problem as a one-shot optimization. Each corrective transformation simultaneously reshapes all class distributions, creating state dependencies that static gradient descent cannot resolve. We formulate feature alignment as a continuous-action Markov decision process solved via Proximal Policy Optimization, where the learned policy issues fault-type-specific affine corrections responsive to the current feature-space configuration, with a dual-objective reward balancing gap minimization against separability preservation. An asymmetry-aware strategy reserves real data for the Normal class while augmenting fault classes with policy-aligned simulated samples. Validation across XJTU-SY, CWRU, and a self-built slewing bearing testbed confirms the dominant gain from reinforcement learning-driven alignment, and cross-equipment linear probing achieves 92.8% without encoder retraining, demonstrating transferable monitoring capability.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement-learning–driven, fault-type–specific feature alignment via a continuous-action MDP and PPO improves cross-domain fault diagnosis under data scarcity, using asymmetry-aware data allocation and affine corrections to preserve class separability; achieves strong cross-equipment transfer (92.8% with no encoder retraining).
AI.motivation: Vibration-based fault diagnosis is hindered by scarce fault-event data and sim-to-real gaps in digital twins, with class-specific impedance in feature-space mismatch. Global, class-agnostic adaptations distort inter-class separability; uniform source-target mixing harms the Normal class. A state-dependent, sequential alignment problem requires a learning-based policy to tailor corrections per fault type.
AI.method: Formulate feature alignment as a continuous-action Markov decision process solved with Proximal Policy Optimization. The policy outputs fault-type–specific affine corrections conditioned on current feature-space configuration, optimizing a dual objective: minimize domain gap while preserving class separability. Employ an asymmetry-aware strategy that reserves real Normal data and augments fault classes with policy-aligned simulated samples.
AI.result: Empirical validation on XJTU-SY, CWRU, and a slewing bearing testbed shows RL-driven alignment yields dominant gains over baseline domain adaptation. Cross-equipment linear probing reaches 92.8% accuracy without retraining the encoder, indicating strong transferability.
AI.conclusion: RL-based, fault-type-specific feature alignment effectively addresses heterogeneity across fault classes and sim-to-real gaps, enabling robust, transferable health monitoring under data constraints without extensive retraining.

title: Omni-Perception Policy Optimization for Multimodal Emotion Reasoning
summary: We find that current emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs still lack reliable omni-modal perception: they (i) underutilize multimodal cues in their reasoning trajectories and (ii) exhibit unfaithful behavior, often hallucinating modality-specific statements from other modalities. Building on these insights, we propose OPPO (Omni-Perception Policy Optimization), a reinforcement learning framework that explicitly optimizes multimodal perception. First, an Omni-Perception Reward decomposes ground-truth reasoning into fine-grained visual, acoustic, and emotion cues and rewards trajectories that semantically recover these cues. Second, an Omni-Perception Loss compares the policy under full and unimodally masked inputs, applying a KL penalty only to modality-specific evidence tokens to suppress cross-modal hallucination. We further introduce MEP-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark that quantifies utilization and faithfulness. Experiments show that OPPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion, while substantially improving utilization and faithfulness scores on MEP-Bench, highlighting the importance of sufficient and faithful omni perception for multimodal emotion reasoning.

AI.tldr: OPPO introduces a reinforcement learning framework to optimize omni-modal perception in emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs, using a decomposed Omni-Perception Reward and a modality-aware KL loss to improve utilization and faithfulness. It also presents MEP-Bench as a diagnostic benchmark. Empirically, it achieves SOTA on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion and improves utilization and faithfulness scores on MEP-Bench.
AI.motivation: Current emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs underutilize multimodal cues and often produce modality-specific hallucinations. There is a need to explicitly optimize perceptual alignment with ground-truth cues and reduce cross-modal hallucination.
AI.method: OPPO defines an Omni-Perception Reward that breaks ground-truth reasoning into visual, acoustic, and emotion cues to guide learning. It also introduces an Omni-Perception Loss that penalizes policy differences between full-input and unimodally masked-input conditions using a KL penalty on modality-specific evidence tokens to suppress cross-modal hallucination. The approach is evaluated on MER-UniBench, MME-Emotion, and MEP-Bench (diagnostic benchmark).
AI.result: OPPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion. It substantially improves utilization and faithfulness on MEP-Bench, demonstrating better use of multimodal cues and reduced cross-modal hallucination in omni-modal emotion reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly optimizing omni-perception via reward decomposition and modality-aware loss, combined with a diagnostic benchmark, yields stronger, more faithful multimodal emotion reasoning and highlights the importance of balanced cue utilization across modalities.

title: Structuring Sparsity: Block-Sparse Featurizers Capture Visual Concept Manifolds
summary: What is the geometry of a visual percept? The most widely used protocols for decomposing neural network representations into interpretable parts treat concepts as isolated directions, yet recent work shows that concepts are often realized as geometric structures in low dimensional regions of activation space. We turn to the literature of Structured sparsity to close this gap, and show that block sparsity, which groups directions into blocks, is the prior matched to a generative model in which a representation is a sparse sum of low-dimensional manifolds: the modern, learned form of a classical idea in visual neuroscience, where a visual feature is carried by a coordinated group of neurons rather than a single tuned one. We implement three variants of block-sparse featurizers (BSFs) and, through a minimum-description-length analysis, show that all three describe activations more compactly than direction-based featurizers, with the recovered concepts typically two- to four-dimensional. We then use BSFs to (i) recontextualize prior work, showing that curve detectors in InceptionV1 actually read from a single continuous curve manifold, (ii) discover novel manifolds including shadows and lighting in DINOv3, and (iii) support interpretable control of image generation in diffusion models (SDXL) via manifold steering.

AI.tldr: Block sparsity better matches how neural representations form interpretable concepts as low-dimensional manifolds, implemented as three block-sparse featurizers (BSFs). MDL shows activations compressed into 2–4D concepts; this framework reinterprets curvature detectors as a single curve, reveals new manifolds (shadows, lighting), and enables manifold-steered diffusion-model control.
AI.motivation: Traditional direction-based concept decompositions overlook the geometric, manifold-structured nature of activations. A block-sparse prior aligns with a generative model where representations are sparse sums of low-dimensional manifolds, echoing a classical visual neuroscience idea of coordinated neuron groups carrying features.
AI.method: Develop three variants of block-sparse featurizers (BSFs).
