Use minimum-description-length (MDL) analysis to compare how compactly activations are described relative to direction-based featurizers. Apply BSFs to analyze InceptionV1 (curve detectors as a continuum), DINOv3 (discover manifolds like shadows and lighting), and diffusion-model control (SDXL) via manifold steering.
AI.result: Activations are described more compactly with BSFs; recovered concepts typically reside in 2–4 dimensions. Reinterprets InceptionV1 curve detectors as reading from a single continuous curve manifold. Discovers novel manifolds (shadows, lighting) in DINOv3. Demonstrates interpretable control of image generation through manifold steering in SDXL.
AI.conclusion: Block-sparse featurizers capture neural representations as sparse sums of low-dimensional manifolds, providing a principled, compact, and interpretable description of visual concepts and enabling controllable generative capabilities.

title: RAVEN: Long-Horizon Reasoning & Navigation with a Visuo-Spatio-Temporal Memory
summary: Long-term robot deployment requires a compact and scalable memory that preserves fine-grained visual semantics, grounds observations in space and time, and enables efficient storage and retrieval. In this paper, we propose RAVEN, an agentic memory system for long-horizon robotic question answering and navigation. RAVEN stores visual embeddings with pose and time in a vector database, and grounds retrieval in a spatial map to answer queries and navigate to goals. By operating directly on visual embeddings, RAVEN avoids lossy image-to-text captioning and enables accurate semantic, spatial, and temporal retrieval at scale. Across several simulated and real-world video question-answering benchmarks, RAVEN consistently surpasses caption-based memory systems and matches frontier VLMs on long-horizon tasks at 10$\times$ lower retrieval cost. Finally, we instantiate RAVEN on a Unitree Go1 robot for the task of long-horizon navigation for natural language goal-reaching, and show successful deployment over several large indoor environments.

AI.tldr: RAVEN is an agentic memory system for long-horizon robotics that stores visual embeddings with pose/time in a vector DB, grounds retrieval in a spatial map, enabling scalable, lossless semantic, spatial, and temporal retrieval for QA and navigation; it outperforms caption-based memory, matches frontier VLMs on long-horizon tasks with 10x lower retrieval cost, and is deployed on a Unitree Go1 for natural-language goal-reaching.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robotic tasks demand compact, scalable memory that preserves fine-grained visual semantics and provides robust spatial-temporal grounding, while enabling efficient storage and retrieval; caption-based memories induce lossiness and higher costs.
AI.method: Store visual embeddings with pose and time in a vector database; ground retrieval in a spatial map to answer queries and guide navigation; operate directly on embeddings to avoid image-to-text captioning; evaluate on simulated and real-world video QA benchmarks; deploy on a Unitree Go1 robot for long-horizon navigation to natural-language goals.
AI.result: RAVEN consistently surpasses caption-based memory systems and matches frontier Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on long-horizon tasks, with approximately 10x lower retrieval cost; demonstrates successful real-world deployment for long-horizon navigation in large indoor environments.
AI.conclusion: Agentic visual-embedding memory enables scalable, accurate semantic, spatial, and temporal retrieval for long-horizon robotic tasks, reducing reliance on caption-based memory and enabling practical deployment in real environments.

title: Towards Continuous Power Forecasting: Practical Continual Learning for Real-World Energy Systems in Nonstationary Time Series
summary: Power forecasting models deployed in real-world energy markets must operate under nonstationary conditions, where data distributions continually evolve due to weather variability, infrastructure upgrades, and changing consumption behaviors. In practice, these models face strict operational constraints: historical data may be limited or unavailable for repeated retraining, and uninterrupted long-term service is often required. This paper addresses these challenges by proposing the paradigm of Continuous Power Forecasting, which views power forecasting as a continual learning problem rather than a static offline task. Based on an adaptive continual learning framework for regression, we systematically investigate the practical effectiveness of six representative continual learning approaches from three methodological categories. These approaches are evaluated under different realistic assumptions regarding data accessibility and update policies. Experimental validation on real-world power datasets demonstrates that continual learning enables forecasting models to self-adapt to distributional drift, accumulate knowledge over time, and mitigate catastrophic forgetting without relying on large-scale historical data storage. Beyond performance gains, our study provides practical insights into the stability and adaptation behaviors of different continual learning approaches under realistic operational constraints. Overall, this work illustrates how continual learning can be pragmatically integrated into industrial power forecasting pipelines, offering a scalable and sustainable solution for long-term deployment in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes Continuous Power Forecasting by framing power forecasting as a continual learning problem to handle nonstationarity under data and deployment constraints; evaluates six continual learning methods across three categories under realistic data-access/update policies; finds that continual learning enables adaptation and reduces forgetting without large historical data.
AI.motivation: Power forecasting in real-world energy markets faces nonstationary distributions due to weather, infrastructure changes, and consumption patterns, coupled with limited retraining data and the need for uninterrupted service. A continual learning approach aims to adapt over time while preserving prior knowledge and without requiring extensive historical data storage.
AI.method: An adaptive continual learning framework for regression is proposed. The study systematically analyzes six representative continual learning approaches spanning three methodological categories, evaluating them under varying data-access and update-policy assumptions to reflect realistic operational constraints.
AI.result: Continual learning enables models to adapt to distributional drift, accumulate knowledge over time, and mitigate catastrophic forgetting under realistic constraints, without relying on large-scale historical data storage. The work also provides practical insights into stability and adaptation behaviors of different CL approaches in industrial settings.
AI.conclusion: Continuous Power Forecasting can be pragmatically integrated into industrial pipelines, offering scalable, sustainable long-term deployment for dynamic environments. The study delivers actionable guidance on selecting and deploying continual learning methods for power forecasting under operational constraints.

title: Agentic Knowledge Tracing: A Multi-Agent LLM Architecture for Stealth Assessment of Financial Literacy in Serious Games
summary: Assessing financial literacy during gameplay without disrupting the learning experience remains a key challenge in serious games for education. We present the Agentic BKT pipeline, a multi-agent large language model architecture for stealth assessment of financial competencies from open-ended gameplay events. The pipeline processes events from a 2D platformer serious game aligned with the OECD/INFE financial literacy framework through four phases: (1) the game captures every player decision as a structured event log; (2) an LLM event classifier labels each action on a four-point rubric validated against three domain experts (Fleiss kappa = 0.624, substantial agreement); (3) four domain-specific agents specializing in risk mitigation, investing, spending, and credit management perform session-level reasoning over behavioral trajectories, feeding per-competency Bayesian Knowledge Tracing that estimates mastery within each domain; and (4) an expert judge agent synthesizes the domain-level estimates into an overall mastery score. Evaluated with 193 K-12 participants across 264 game sessions, the Agentic BKT pipeline yields mastery estimates significantly correlated with learning gain (r = 0.276, p = 0.0001) and post-test scores (r = 0.333, p < 0.0001) while showing no correlation with pre-test scores, providing both convergent and discriminant validity. The multi-agent approach approximately triples the predictive validity of a single-LLM baseline (r = 0.095, not significant) in this study, demonstrating that domain decomposition and session-level reasoning play a central role in capturing the multidimensional nature of financial literacy from gameplay

AI.tldr: A multi-agent LLM pipeline (Agentic BKT) fuses event logs from a serious game with domain-specific LLM agents and Bayesian Knowledge Tracing to stealthily estimate financial literacy mastery across four domains, improving predictive validity over a single-LLM baseline.
AI.motivation: Assess financial literacy during gameplay without disrupting learning; exploit domain decomposition to capture multidimensional competencies in a stealth assessment setting.
AI.method: 1) Capture structured player events from a 2D platformer aligned to OECD/INFE. 2) Use an LLM event classifier (four-point rubric; Fleiss kappa 0.624). 3) Four domain-specific agents (risk mitigation, investing, spending, credit management) perform session-level reasoning; per-competency BKT estimates mastery. 4) An expert judge synthesizes domain estimates into an overall mastery score.
AI.result: On 193 K-12 participants across 264 sessions, mastery estimates correlated with learning gain (r=0.276, p=0.0001) and post-test (r=0.333, p<0.0001); no correlation with pre-test (demonstrating discriminant validity). The multi-agent approach tripled predictive validity compared to a single-LLM baseline (r=0.095, not significant).
AI.conclusion: Domain decomposition and session-level reasoning enable robust, multidimensional stealth assessment of financial literacy from gameplay, outperforming single-LLM baselines and validating the approach for educational measurement.

title: OrthoTrack: Continuous 6-DoF UAV Trajectory Estimation Anchored in Public Orthophotos
summary: Continuous 6-DoF pose estimation is essential for autonomous UAV operations. Yet, existing visual odometry and SLAM methods accumulate drift and yield only relative, up-to-scale trajectories. Single-frame geo-localization, in turn, discards temporal continuity and remains too slow for real-time use. We present OrthoTrack, a training-free system that estimates continuous 6-DoF UAV trajectories using only publicly available orthophotos and surface models as a map prior. OrthoTrack matches keyframes against the orthophoto and lifts correspondences to metric 3D via the surface model. It then propagates these map-anchored correspondences to intermediate frames with optical flow, producing absolute, metrically scaled poses at every frame without GPS or post-hoc alignment. We also introduce the MovingDrone Dataset, a large-scale benchmark pairing photorealistic UAV sequences with dense 6-DoF ground truth and co-registered multi-modal geodata including multi-temporal orthophotos. On MovingDrone and real-world benchmarks, OrthoTrack runs in real time on a single GPU. It outperforms all baselines by a large margin, even those receiving oracle scale and alignment. By relying on publicly available geodata, OrthoTrack enables deployment to new regions without site-specific adaptation.

AI.tldr: OrthoTrack is a training-free system for real-time, continuous 6-DoF UAV pose estimation using publicly available orthophotos and surface models as a map prior, enabling metric, globally anchored trajectories without GPS.
AI.motivation: To overcome drift and scale ambiguities in VO/SLAM and enable real-time, globally consistent UAV trajectories by leveraging publicly available geodata as a map prior, adaptable to new regions.
AI.method: Keyframe-to-orthophoto matching with lift of correspondences to metric 3D using the surface model; propagate map-anchored correspondences to intermediate frames via optical flow; no training required; outputs absolute, metrically scaled poses at every frame without GPS or post-hoc alignment; real-time on a single GPU; relies on orthophotos and surface models as priors.
AI.result: On MovingDrone and real-world benchmarks, OrthoTrack outperforms baselines by a large margin, runs in real time on a single GPU, and does not require site-specific adaptation or oracle scale/alignment.
AI.conclusion: Public geodata enables deployment to new regions without site-specific adaptation; OrthoTrack provides a training-free, scalable solution for continuous 6-DoF UAV pose estimation using map priors.

title: Wear-Clearance-Impact Coupling in the Jansen Linkage: A Gait-Durability-Optimized Design Slows Joint Loosening
summary: A companion study introduced joint durability into the dimensional design of the Theo Jansen walking linkage and found its classical "holy numbers" Pareto-dominated, but it modelled the revolute joints as ideal, clearance-free pins, so its wear figures were relative rankings, not a prediction of in-service degradation. Here we relax that idealization. We build a forward-dynamic model of the Jansen leg in which a revolute joint becomes a clearance joint with a continuous normal contact law (Lankarani-Flores, hysteresis-damped) and Ambrosio friction, integrated as a constraint-stabilized differential-algebraic system, and couple it to the Archard law in a wear->clearance->impact feedback loop. Three findings emerge. First, neglecting clearance underestimates the peak joint load: the clearance model gives a peak contact force of ~104 N at the load-bearing pin against ~48 N for the ideal joint (~2x amplification), rising to ~426 N when two joints carry clearance at once. Second, the coupling is strongly impact-sensitive--single trajectories are non-monotonic and can reverse the design ranking, a chaos consistent with the literature--so designs must be compared statistically; over an ensemble of 16 randomized phases the optimized joint is robustly more durable, with per-cycle wear ~9-7x lower (peak force ~4x lower) at one clearance joint and still ~1.7x lower on both with two (p<0.01 throughout). Third, the wear is strongly non-uniform--it concentrates on a ~10 deg load arc--so assuming uniform clearance growth underestimates local clearance growth by ~36x. The clearance-free durability advantage thus survives the chaotic, multi-joint, non-uniformly-worn coupling in the ensemble mean. We deliver the first clearance-coupled forward-dynamic model of the Jansen leg and specify a falsifiable protocol to test each prediction.

AI.tldr: Clearance joints modeled with forward-dynamic wear feedback reveal higher peak loads, chaos-driven durability rankings, and highly non-uniform wear; ensemble statistics show clearance can improve durability despite higher loads; provides a falsifiable protocol.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of ideal pin joints in prior joint-durability studies and quantify the impact of clearance and wear on the Theo Jansen leg.
AI.method: Develop a forward-dynamic Jansen leg model replacing revolute pins with clearance joints governed by Lankarani-Flores contact law, Ambrosio friction, integrated as constraint-stabilized DAE, coupled to Archard wear; run ensemble of 16 randomized phases to assess design durability.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) clearance raises peak contact forces (104 N vs 48 N; up to 426 N with two clearance joints); (2) coupling is chaos-like; ensemble shows robust durability advantage for the optimized joint (wear reductions ~9-7x, peak force reductions ~4x; p<0.01); (3) wear concentrates on ~10° arc; uniform-growth assumption underestimates clearance growth by ~36x; durability advantage persists.
AI.conclusion: First clearance-coupled forward-dynamic model of the Jansen leg; provides falsifiable protocol to test predictions.

title: Convex--Concave Quadratic Spectral Filtering for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) interpret message passing as frequency-selective filtering. While low-order spectral filters are efficient, their limited selectivity often leads to weak attenuation outside the passband, whereas high-order alternatives introduce optimization challenges. We propose DCQ-GNN, a spectral GNN based on a compact bank of adaptive convex--concave quadratic filters. By restricting the filter order to two while explicitly exploiting complementary curvature, DCQ-GNN improves spectral selectivity as quantified by Dirichlet energy and entropy measures without resorting to high-order polynomial expansions. The model fuses filter outputs through a node-adaptive gating mechanism to enable node-wise structure-aware spectral selection. We provide a formal spectral analysis grounded in Dirichlet energy attenuation, von Neumann entropy, and curvature polarity, and derive explicit characterizations of filter behavior across varying levels of homophily and structural perturbations. Extensive benchmarks on 10 datasets show that DCQ-GNN ties for the top average rank (3.0) on heterophilic graphs and obtains the second-best rank (4.2) on homophilic graphs, remaining competitive with representative high-order polynomial spectral filters. Furthermore, under strong structural perturbations, DCQ-GNN exhibits substantially smaller performance degradation compared to both first-order and high-order baselines. These results demonstrate that curvature-aware quadratic banks provide a robust and efficient alternative to high-order spectral models while preserving optimization stability and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: DCQ-GNN uses a two-order adaptive convex–concave quadratic filter bank with node-adaptive gating to achieve spectral selectivity, achieving competitive performance on heterophilic graphs and robustness to perturbations without resorting to high-order polynomials.
AI.motivation: Address limited spectral selectivity of low-order GNN filters and the optimization challenges of high-order spectral methods by introducing a compact, curvature-aware filter bank.
AI.method: A compact bank of adaptive convex–concave quadratic filters of order two, explicitly exploiting complementary curvature; node-adaptive gating to fuse filter outputs; formal spectral analysis using Dirichlet energy attenuation, von Neumann entropy, and curvature polarity across varying homophily and perturbations.
AI.result: On 10 datasets, DCQ-GNN ties for the top average rank (3.0) on heterophilic graphs and attains the second-best rank (4.2) on homophilic graphs; robust under strong structural perturbations, with smaller degradation than first- and high-order baselines; competitive with high-order polynomial spectral filters while preserving optimization stability and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Curvature-aware quadratic filter banks offer a robust, efficient alternative to high-order spectral models, delivering strong performance across graph regimes with improved stability under perturbations.

title: What Actually Works for Spacecraft Fault-Tolerant Control: An Honest Settled-Gate Benchmark of Learned and Classical Methods
summary: Recent learned fault-tolerant-control (FTC) work reports high success on spacecraft actuator faults, but often in simulation, on narrow fault sets, and with transient metrics that a trajectory need only touch once. We ask what recovers spacecraft pointing when success means holding it on faults never seen in training. We answer with a benchmark built around a settled gate, pointing held within 0.2 deg over a dwell window and scored on the true state, train/test splits disjoint in inertia, gain, sign pattern, and bias, Wilson intervals over n=500 episodes per cell, and one-command reproduction on a 6-DOF Basilisk testbed. Across classical, adaptive, learned end-to-end, and structured controllers, three findings stand out. Fault-unaware PD/PID and from-scratch end-to-end RL score 0%, so learning capacity alone is not the lever. Classical adaptive laws resolve sign faults but handle gain poorly at 55.2%, and a literature-faithful Nussbaum-gain law reaches 45.2% and 3.2%. A structured estimate-then-control design, with a learned recurrent module that infers actuator gain online and feeds an analytic law, wins on sign and gain faults at 97.8% and 94.4%, approaching the privileged oracle while unstructured methods remain at zero. The hard wall is constant additive bias, which is 0% for every controller including the privileged gain oracle, because an integral-free law cannot null a constant disturbance. We close it with a disturbance observer that recovers bias from the dynamics and is self-correcting for gain-estimate error. Composed with the gain estimate, it recovers 59.4% of held-out bias faults with no sign/gain regression, moving that class off zero. We classify sensor-fault regimes similarly, show that sensor bias is unobservable from the corrupted measurement alone and therefore requires fusion rather than an observer, and release the benchmark so the gate is shared.

AI.tldr: A benchmark-driven study showing that a structured estimate-then-control approach with online actuator-gain inference and a disturbance observer outperforms naive fault-tolerant control methods on a 6-DOF spacecraft pointing task with unseen faults; end-to-end learning and simple adaptive methods fail on generalization, while sensor biases require fusion.
AI.motivation: To evaluate generalization of fault-tolerant control (FTC) methods to actuator faults not seen during training, across dissimilar inertia, gain, sign-patterns, and biases, using a rigorous, repeatable benchmark and statistics.
AI.method: Construct a Basilisk 6-DOF spacecraft benchmark: settled gate with pointing held within 0.2 deg over a dwell; train/test splits disjoint in inertia, gain, sign pattern, and bias; evaluate multiple controllers (fault-unaware PD/PID, adaptive, end-to-end RL, and a structured estimate-then-control with a recurrent module that infers actuator gain online feeding an analytic law); add a disturbance observer to recover bias and self-correct gain estimates; analyze both actuator faults and sensor faults; use Wilson intervals (n=500 episodes per cell) and one-command reproduction, then release the benchmark gate.
AI.result: Fault-unaware PD/PID and end-to-end RL score 0%. Adaptive laws resolve sign faults but perform poorly on gain faults (55.2%); Nussbaum-gain law achieves 45.2% and 3.2% for two fault facets. The structured estimate-then-control with online gain inference and an analytic law achieves 97.8% on sign faults and 94.4% on gain faults, nearing oracle performance; persistent constant additive bias defeats integral-free laws. A disturbance observer recovers bias from dynamics and, with gain estimation, recovers 59.4% of held-out bias faults without regressing sign/gain. Sensor biases are unobservable from corrupted measurements alone and require sensor fusion; benchmark is released publicly.
AI.conclusion: Learning capacity alone does not guarantee fault tolerance under unseen faults. A structured approach that combines online gain estimation, analytic control, and disturbance observation yields substantial fault-resilience, though constant biases remain challenging and sensor faults demand fusion. The benchmark enables fair comparison across future FTC methods.

title: Multilingual Hematology Visual Question Answering Dataset
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) have shown promising capabilities in medical image analysis by jointly understanding visual and textual information for tasks such as Visual Question Answering. However, existing hematology vision-language resources remain predominantly English centric, limiting their applicability in multilingual healthcare environments. This challenge is releveant generally to South Asia and specifically to Pakistan, where Urdu is widely used despite healthcare information and digital medical systems being largely dependent on English. To investigate this gap, we conducted a survey among healthcare professionals, which revealed substantial language mismatches between clinical documentation and patient communication, emphasizing the need for multilingual healthcare technologies. To address this limitation, we introduce WBCMor VQA, a clinically validated bilingual English, Urdu morphology aware VQA benchmark for leukemia and normal white blood cell analysis. The benchmark is constructed using morphology-aware annotations from LeukemiaAttri and WBCAtt datasets and supported by a domain specific Urdu hematology dictionary to ensure linguistic consistency and clinical correctness. The final benchmark contains 110K bilingual question answer pairs serving as VQA annotations for 20K leukemic and normal single-cell images. Furthermore, we establish baseline performance by evaluating multiple open-source VLMs on the proposed benchmark. The proposed resource aims to facilitate the development of accessible and clinically relevant AI systems for multilingual healthcare environments.

AI.tldr: A bilingual, morphology-aware VQA benchmark (WBCMor VQA) for leukemia and normal white blood cell analysis, bridging English-Urdu gaps in hematology VLMs with 110k Q/A pairs over 20k images; baseline evaluation of open-source VLMs; dataset built from LeukemiaAttri/WBCAtt with an Urdu domain dictionary; aims to enable multilingual, clinically accurate AI in healthcare.
AI.motivation: Multilingual healthcare needs in South Asia (e.g., Pakistan) hamper deployment of hematology VQA due to English-centric resources; language mismatches affect documentation and patient communication; a bilingual, domain-specific benchmark can drive development of linguistically and clinically accurate VLMs.
AI.method: Constructed WBCMor VQA by combining morphology-aware annotations from LeukemiaAttri and WBCAtt; integrated a domain Urdu hematology dictionary to ensure linguistic and clinical correctness; produced 110K bilingual Q/A pairs for 20K leukemic and normal single-cell images; evaluated multiple open-source VLMs to establish baseline performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of bilingual VQA in hematology; provides a large-scale bilingual benchmark for VQA in WBC analysis; establishes reference baselines on open-source VLMs; highlights the potential and gaps for multilingual clinical VQA systems.
AI.conclusion: The WBCMor VQA resource enables development of accessible, clinically relevant AI for multilingual healthcare environments, particularly Urdu-English contexts, and offers a foundation for future improvements through expanded multilingual medical QA and morphology-aware reasoning.

title: RigPI: Dynamic Parameter Identification of Rigid Body via VLM-Seeded Differentiable Simulation
comment: Accepted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2027)
summary: Accurate physical parameter identification of manipulated objects is fundamental to advanced robotic manipulation and the construction of faithful digital twins. However, acquiring physically consistent inertial and frictional properties from real-world interactions remains challenging due to sensing noise, modeling errors, and limited prior knowledge. This paper presents \textbf{RigPI}, a systematic framework for identifying dynamic parameters of both unconstrained rigid bodies and multi-link rigid bodies during robot-object interaction. RigPI integrates vision-based semantic priors, force-torque measurements, and motion observations within a differentiable simulation pipeline. A vision-language model (VLM) provides informed initialization and a constrained search space, while gradient information from a differentiable physics simulator enables efficient and stable parameter refinement. The proposed two-stage optimization strategy alleviates sensitivity to noise and avoids physically implausible solutions. Extensive real-world experiments on objects with revolute and prismatic joints demonstrate that RigPI achieves accurate and stable parameter estimates, and successfully reproduces manipulation trajectories on a real robot with parameter-aware predictive validity. These results highlight the effectiveness and robustness of RigPI for real-world robotic system identification tasks.

AI.tldr: RigPI is a differentiable-physics–based framework that identifies inertial and frictional parameters of rigid bodies during robot-object interaction by fusing vision priors, force-torque data, and motion observations in a two-stage optimization within a differentiable simulator.
AI.motivation: Accurate dynamic parameters are essential for realistic manipulation and digital twins, but acquiring physically consistent inertial and friction properties from real interactions is hindered by sensing noise, modeling errors, and limited prior knowledge.
AI.method: The framework uses a vision-language model to provide informed initialization and constrain the search space, then employs gradient-based refinement in a differentiable physics simulator using force-torque measurements and motion data. It supports both unconstrained rigid bodies and multi-link rigid bodies (with revolute and prismatic joints) and uses a two-stage optimization to improve robustness to noise and enforce physical plausibility.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show RigPI yields accurate and stable parameter estimates for objects with different joints and enables parameter-aware trajectory reproduction on a real robot, indicating predictive validity of the identified parameters.
AI.conclusion: RigPI demonstrates robustness and effectiveness for real-world robotic system identification by integrating semantic priors and differentiable simulation to achieve physically plausible, accurate dynamic parameters.

title: Swarm-Inspired Generation of Collective Behaviors in Graph Dynamical Systems
summary: Collective behavior arises when locally interacting units produce coordinated global organization, from synchronization in dynamical systems to task-relevant information flow on graphs. The central challenge is not only to explain how collective behavior emerges, but to design local interaction rules that can produce desired global organization and generalize across graphs, dynamics and tasks.To address this challenge, we introduce the Swarm-Inspired Emergent Synchronizer (SIES), a graph-dynamical framework that learns generalizable local-interaction laws for controllable collective organization. Each node is an agent-like dynamical unit with a state and task cue, and signed source-target-conditioned attention acts as an adaptive coupling term inside an explicit evolution model. Therefore, SIES combines an explicit dynamical engine with local agent intelligence, similar to biological swarms. For synchronization control, SIES learns a generalizable coupling operator that produces prescribed synchronization patterns for CDSs across untrained network scales, target phase relations, and intrinsic node dynamics without retraining. The learned operator also reaches gait-related modes faster than three oscillator baselines and generalizes synchronization-driven locomotion to simulated multi-legged robots of different scales and a physical hexapod after leg disablement. For graph representation learning, SIES applies the same signed interaction principle to message passing and achieves the highest performance among the compared methods on heterophilous node-classification benchmarks. Together, these results position SIES as a generalizable and learnable graph-dynamical interaction framework with promise for synchronization control, adaptive robot coordination, and heterophilous graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: SIES is a generalizable graph-dynamical framework learning local interaction rules for controllable collective behavior across scales and tasks.
AI.motivation: To design local coupling laws that yield desired global organization and generalize across graphs, dynamics and tasks.
AI.method: Combines explicit dynamical engine with local agent intelligence; uses signed source-target-conditioned attention as adaptive coupling inside evolution model; trains a generalizable coupling operator for synchronization; applies same signed interaction to message passing for heterophilous node classification.
AI.result: Achieves prescribed synchronization patterns across untrained network scales and intrinsic dynamics; faster gait-related modes than three oscillator baselines; generalizes synchronization-driven locomotion to simulated multi-legged robots and a physical hexapod after leg disablement; achieves top performance on heterophilous benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SIES is a generalizable, learnable graph-dynamical interaction framework with potential for synchronization control, adaptive robot coordination, and heterophilous graph representation learning.

title: Offline Multi-agent Continual Cooperation via Skill Partition and Reuse
summary: Extracting skills from multi-agent offline dataset improves learning efficiency via sharing task-invariant coordination skills among tasks. In settings where tasks occur sequentially and the space of skills grows exponentially, existing approaches that rely on heuristically designed and fixed-sized skill libraries struggle to resolve the problem of distributional shift and interference, facing catastrophic forgetting and plasticity loss. To address this problem and endow agents with the ability to continually discover and reuse coordination skills in open-environment, we propose COMAD, a principled framework for Continual Offline Multi-agent Skill Discovery via Skill Partition and Reuse. We first discover skills from mixed multi-agent behavior data with an auto-encoder to transform coordination knowledge into reusable coordination skills. Then we construct a skill-augmented policy learning objective with multi-head architectures, explicitly guiding the advantage function with reusable skills identified via a density-based reusability estimator. Theoretical analysis shows our method approximates the optimum of a continual skill discovery problem. Empirical results across diverse MARL benchmarks show that COMAD continually expands its skill library to mitigate interference, achieving superior forward and backward transfer for task streams compared to multiple baselines.

AI.tldr: COMAD is a continual offline MARL framework that discovers and reuses coordination skills via auto-encoder extraction and density-based reuse estimation within a multi-head policy, improving transfer and reducing interference.
AI.motivation: In sequential task streams, the skill space grows rapidly and fixed, heuristically designed skill libraries fail due to distributional shift, interference, catastrophic forgetting, and plasticity loss. A principled continual discovery/reuse mechanism is needed for open environments.
AI.method: Discover skills from mixed multi-agent behavior data with an auto-encoder to extract reusable coordination skills. Build a skill-augmented policy with a multi-head architecture; guide the advantage function using a density-based reusability estimator to identify reusable skills; partition skills to mitigate interference; formulate a continual skill-discovery objective; provide theoretical analysis showing approximation to the optimum of continual skill discovery.
AI.result: Empirical results on diverse MARL benchmarks show COMAD continually expands its skill library, reduces interference, and achieves superior forward and backward transfer compared with multiple baselines.
AI.conclusion: COMAD provides a principled approach to continual offline multi-agent skill discovery, enabling scalable open-environment coordination through continual growth and reuse of skills.

title: Cross-Modality Structural Guidance in 3D Latent Diffusion for Robust FLAIR Super-Resolution
summary: High-resolution (HR) MRI acquisition is often hampered by scan time constraints, resulting in anisotropic or low-resolution scans (e.g., thick-slice FLAIR) that limit diagnostic accuracy. While deep learning-based super-resolution (SR) methods show promise, they often hallucinate anatomical details, which can compromise brain structural integrity. To mitigate this limitation, we introduce MR-DiffuSR, a Multi-Resolution Diffusion-based Super-Resolution framework that incorporates HR T1w structural image priors to guide the restoration of thick-slice FLAIR scans and operates in the 3D latent space. Our architecture introduces cross-modality structural swin-attention, which derives structural attention maps from the HR T1w and applies them to the low-resolution FLAIR latent features. This design disentangles anatomical structure from modality-specific contrast, effectively preventing hallucinations. Furthermore, we employ a mixed-scale degradation strategy, training the model on a continuum of downsampling factors to ensure robustness to varying slice thicknesses, while optimizing with a DINOv3-based perceptual loss to preserve high-frequency semantic details. Evaluated on the ADNI-4 dataset, MR-DiffuSR surpasses both CNN and 2D diffusion approaches, achieving an average PSNR of 32.46dB, SSIM of 0.97, and LPIPS of 0.07 across all downsampling factors. In downstream white matter hyperintensity segmentation, our model demonstrates exceptional robustness. While baseline performance collapses at 10x down-sampling (Dice: 0.51), MR-DiffuSR maintains a Dice score of 0.63, preserving utility even at 7mm equivalent slice thickness.

AI.tldr: MR-DiffuSR: a 3D diffusion-based super-resolution framework using HR T1w priors via cross-modality swin-attention to guide thick-slice FLAIR restoration, with mixed-scale degradation and DINOv3 perceptual loss; yields state-of-the-art metrics on ADNI-4 and robust WMH segmentation at high downsampling.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of high-resolution MRI acquisition under scan time constraints. Existing SR methods risk hallucinating anatomical details and compromising brain structural integrity; propose a method that preserves structure by leveraging HR T1w priors.
AI.method: A 3D diffusion-based SR framework (MR-DiffuSR) that incorporates cross-modality structural swin-attention derived from HR T1w to guide low-resolution FLAIR latent features in the 3D latent space. It uses a mixed-scale degradation strategy (training on a continuum of downsampling factors) and optimizes with a DINOv3-based perceptual loss to preserve high-frequency details.
AI.result: MR-DiffuSR surpasses CNN and 2D diffusion baselines on the ADNI-4 dataset, achieving PSNR 32.46 dB, SSIM 0.97, and LPIPS 0.07 across downsampling factors. In WMH segmentation, Dice improves from 0.51 at 10x downsampling (baseline) to 0.63 with MR-DiffuSR, maintaining utility at 7 mm equivalent slice thickness.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively disentangles anatomical structure from modality contrasts, mitigating hallucinations while delivering robust SR performance across varying downsampling levels and preserving downstream task performance.

title: Swazure: Swarm Measurement of Pose for Flying Light Specks
comment: Appeared in Proceedings of the Holodecks Foundation, Vol. 2, No. 3
summary: One may construct a 3D multimedia display using miniature drones configured with light sources, Flying Light Specks (FLSs). Swarms of FLSs localize to illuminate complex 3D shapes and animated sequences consistent with the coordinates of points in a point cloud. This requires FLSs to accurately measure their pose relative to one another using sensors such as cameras. Such sensors have a sweet range in which they provide the highest accuracy. A challenge is how an FLS tracks another FLS outside its sensor's sweet range, dictated by the point cloud data. We address this challenge by proposing a novel technique called Swazure that solves the missing sensor data using cooperation among FLSs. It implements physical data independence by abstracting the physical characteristics of the sensors, making point cloud data independent of the sensor hardware. The size of an FLS relative to the minimum distance between points of a point cloud is an important parameter. With medium-sized FLSs, Swazure is able to position 100% of the FLS's neighbors. Larger FLS sizes may result in potential obstructions that prevent Swazure from quantifying relative pose. We present two heuristics, Move Obstructing and Move Source, to address this limitation. Our experimental results show the superiority of the Move Obstructing heuristic which resolves approximately 30% of obstructions in the worst case scenario.

AI.tldr: Swazure enables cooperative sensing among Flying Light Specks (FLSs) to fill missing pose data, enabling 3D illumination of complex shapes despite sensor range limits; introduces two heuristics (Move Obstructing, Move Source) and shows Move Obstructing reduces obstructions by ~30% in worst cases.
AI.motivation: The problem is accurate relative localization of FLSs when sensors have a sweet spot and parts of the neighborhood fall outside range, impeding consensus on point-cloud-based pose estimates. A hardware-agnostic, cooperative approach is sought to robustly infer missing data.
AI.method: Introduce Swazure, a sensor-abstracting framework that achieves physical data independence by decoupling sensor hardware from point-cloud data; implement two obstruction-mitigation heuristics (Move Obstructing and Move Source); analyze effect of FLS size on connectivity and obstructions; demonstrate that medium-sized FLSs can achieve 100% neighbor localization, while larger sizes may cause obstructions.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate the Move Obstructing heuristic resolves approximately 30% of obstructions in the worst-case scenario.
AI.conclusion: Swazure provides a practical, hardware-agnostic cooperative sensing approach for FLS swarms, enabling robust relative pose estimation across sensor range gaps, with heuristic-based obstruction mitigation and size-dependent trade-offs.

title: Reliable Conformal Prediction for Ordinal Classification Using the Ranked Probability Score
summary: Ordinal classification (OC) arises in high-stakes domains such as medicine and finance, where uncertainty quantification must account for the severity of ordinal errors. Conformal prediction (CP) provides distribution-free prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees; however, its practical effectiveness depends critically on the choice of nonconformity function. We introduce a CP method for ordinal classification based on the ranked probability score (RPS), a proper scoring rule defined over cumulative predictive distributions. Although it reflects ordinal risk quite naturally, it has largely been neglected in conformal ordinal prediction (COP). When used as a measure of nonconformity, RPS yields median-centered contiguous prediction sets by construction. The method is model-agnostic, supports both assessed and grouped ordered categorical outcomes, and permits efficient implementation compared to greedy interval selection procedures. Across multiple ordinal image and tabular datasets, RPS-based CP produces contiguous prediction sets and strikes a favorable balance between prediction set width and the magnitude of ordinal miscoverage relative to existing CP methods.

AI.tldr: RPS-based conformal prediction for ordinal classification yields contiguous, median-centered prediction sets with good width-coverage trade-offs and competitive performance across ordinal image and tabular data; model-agnostic and computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: Ordinal outcomes require uncertainty quantification that respects the severity of misclassifications. Conformal prediction offers distribution-free marginal coverage, but nonconformity choices crucially affect practical performance. RPS aligns with the ordinal nature but has been underused in COP.
AI.method: Use ranked probability score (RPS) as the nonconformity measure within conformal prediction for ordinal classification. This yields median-centered, contiguous prediction sets by construction. The approach supports assessed and grouped ordered outcomes and is more efficient than greedy interval methods.
AI.result: Across multiple ordinal image and tabular datasets, RPS-based COP produces contiguous prediction sets and achieves a favorable balance between prediction set width and the magnitude of ordinal miscoverage relative to existing COP methods.
AI.conclusion: RPS-based COP provides a model-agnostic, scalable, and practically effective framework for ordinal classification that better captures ordinal risk and yields efficient, contiguous prediction sets.

title: Long-Term Simulation Exposes Cognitive-Developmental Risks in AI Companions
summary: AI companions powered by large language models increasingly interact with cognition-developing users, including children and adolescents, creating risks that may accumulate over time. Existing safety evaluations largely rely on single-turn or short-session tests, which cannot capture risks that emerge only through prolonged interaction. To address this gap, we propose TSJ (Theater-Stage-Judge), a longitudinal framework combining persona-driven user simulation, dynamic psychological-state updating and retrospective evaluation. We evaluate six mainstream models across four developmental stages, twenty-four risk dimensions and three psychological-vulnerability personas, covering 12,960 simulated person-day interactions. TSJ shows that short-horizon testing systematically underestimates developmental risks, for which TSJ yields a stable risk estimate only after 140 turns within prolonged simulated relationships. Applying TSJ further identifies early childhood and emerging adulthood as the most vulnerable stages, with cognitive trust and emotional dependency as the weakest domains. TSJ provides a scalable methodology for longitudinal cognitive developmental risk evaluation in AI companion systems.

AI.tldr: TSJ is a longitudinal evaluation framework for AI companions that uses persona-driven simulations and dynamic state updates to reveal developmental risks that short-horizon tests miss, demonstrating underestimation of risks and identifying vulnerable developmental stages.
AI.motivation: Existing safety assessments rely on single-turn or short sessions, failing to capture risks that accumulate over prolonged interactions with cognition-developing users (children/adolescents). A scalable longitudinal framework is needed to quantify risks across developmental stages, risk dimensions, and user profiles.
AI.method: Theater-Stage-Judge (TSJ) framework combines (Theater) persona-driven user simulation, (Stage) dynamic psychological-state updating, and (Judge) retrospective evaluation. It evaluates six mainstream LLMs across four developmental stages, twenty-four risk dimensions, and three psychological-vulnerability personas, totaling 12,960 simulated person-day interactions.
AI.result: Short-horizon testing underestimates developmental risks; stable risk estimates emerge only after about 140 turns in prolonged interactions. Early childhood and emerging adulthood are most vulnerable, with cognitive trust and emotional dependency identified as weaker domains.
AI.conclusion: TSJ provides a scalable methodology for longitudinal cognitive developmental risk evaluation in AI companion systems and highlights the necessity of long-term assessment to uncover risks not observable in short sessions.

title: Pre-Warm: Input-Conditioned Weight Initialization for Convolutional Neural Networks
summary:
We introduce Pre-Warm, a simple yet effective zero-training-cost method for data-conditioned initialization of the first convolutional layer. Before the first forward pass, Pre-Warm extracts mean-centered local patches from a single training batch, clusters them with MiniBatchKMeans, applies inverse Manhattan spatial weighting, and uses the resulting centroids to initialize half of the first-layer filters (the remainder retain Kaiming initialization).
  We derive closed-form rules for all hyperparameters except a single insensitive scale parameter, though we derive a Kaiming parity bound on scale from patch dimensionality. For grayscale datasets we use Otsu's foreground density; for natural color images we use the mean L2 norm of mean-centered patches. Both rules accurately predict the optimal patch count observed in grid search.
  Across five standard benchmarks -- MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, SVHN, and CIFAR-100 -- and 8-seed paired experiments, Pre-Warm yields statistically significant accuracy improvements over standard Kaiming initialization (p < 0.05 on all datasets, p = 0.0007 on SVHN with 8/8 wins, p = 0.0033 on CIFAR-100 with 7/8 wins). The method adds negligible overhead, requires no architectural changes, and integrates into existing training pipelines with only a few lines of code.
  Pre-Warm demonstrates that even a lightweight, input-dependent signal can meaningfully improve optimization trajectories in modern convolutional networks.

AI.tldr: Pre-Warm is a zero-training-cost, data-conditioned initialization for the first convolutional layer that uses mean-centered local patches from a single batch, clusters them with MiniBatchKMeans, applies inverse Manhattan spatial weighting, and initializes half of the first-layer filters with the centroids (the rest remain Kaiming). It yields statistically significant accuracy gains across five datasets with negligible overhead.
AI.motivation: Mitigate initialization sensitivity and improve optimization without extra training cost by injecting a light data-driven prior into the first layer.
AI.method: Extract mean-centered local patches from one training batch; cluster via MiniBatchKMeans; apply inverse Manhattan spatial weighting to centroids; initialize half of the first-layer filters with these centroids, while the remainder use Kaiming initialization; derive closed-form rules for hyperparameters (except one scale parameter) and a Kaiming-parity bound on scale; for grayscale data use Otsu's foreground density, for color data use the mean L2 norm of mean-centered patches; validate that the derived rules predict the optimal patch count observed in grid search; integrate with existing training pipelines with minimal changes.
AI.result: Empirical gains over standard Kaiming initialization across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, SVHN, and CIFAR-100 using 8 seeds; p<0.05 on all datasets, p=0.0007 on SVHN (8/8 wins), p=0.0033 on CIFAR-100 (7/8 wins); negligible computational overhead and no architectural changes.
AI.conclusion: Even lightweight, input-dependent signals can meaningfully improve optimization trajectories in modern CNNs, validating a practical data-conditioned prior at initialization.

title: Spatio-Temporal Retrieval-based Priors for Adaptive Computational Teaching in Driving
summary: Learning-based automated coaching systems for complex motor tasks such as high-performance driving remain limited in the ability to be adaptive by their reliance only on local, context-dependent reasoning, failing to account for the long-term temporal nature of student learning and the cumulative impact of repeated teacher-student interactions. In this paper, we propose an imitation learning based computational model for adaptive teaching with a dedicated temporal reasoning module that can reason over the interaction history under low-data regimes. To compensate for limited amounts of interactive training data, and based on the repetitive nature of the teaching process, the model relies on a nearest neighbor retrieval and cross attention prior, reasoning only on a narrowed-down set of semantically similar past interactions with an encoder-decoder based concurrent teaching model. We validate our approach with (i) a novel semi-synthetic closed-loop longitudinal student-teacher interaction dataset based on Waymo Open Motion Dataset and (ii) a small-scale real-world naturalistic simulator race coaching dataset. Our results reveal the consistent advantage of our adaptive teaching model with the nearest neighbor retrieval and cross-attention prior over a non-adaptive baseline as well as a suite of adaptive models that differ in their choice of priors and temporal fusion mechanisms.

AI.tldr: An imitation-learning adaptive teaching model with a temporal reasoning module and nearest-neighbor cross-attention priors for long-horizon student–teacher interactions in high-performance driving; validated on semi-synthetic and real-world datasets, showing advantages over non-adaptive baselines and some adaptive baselines.
AI.motivation: Current learning-based coaching systems rely on local, context-dependent reasoning and fail to capture the long-term temporal structure and cumulative effects of repeated teaching interactions, especially in complex motor tasks like high-performance driving.
AI.method: Imitation-learning based computational model with a dedicated temporal reasoning module. It employs nearest-neighbor retrieval and a cross-attention prior to focus on semantically similar past interactions, within an encoder–decoder concurrent teaching framework. Operates under low-data regimes by reasoning on a narrowed-down history of interactions.
AI.result: Validated on two datasets: (i) a novel semi-synthetic closed-loop longitudinal student–teacher interaction dataset derived from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, and (ii) a small-scale real-world naturalistic simulator race coaching dataset. The approach consistently outperforms a non-adaptive baseline and a suite of adaptive models that vary priors and temporal fusion strategies.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that incorporating long-term temporal reasoning with memory-based priors enables more effective adaptive teaching in complex motor tasks, particularly under low-data conditions; suggests robustness across semi-synthetic and real-world scenarios and motivates broader exploration of priors and temporal fusion methods.

title: Enhancing Clinician Decision-Making via Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Expert Fusion for Stroke Rehabilitation
summary: Tailoring stroke rehabilitation requires assessing how movements are organized, not merely if they succeed. Currently, this assessment is a rate-limiting bottleneck. Instruments like the Action Research Arm Test (ARAT) compress rich behavioral observations into single ordinal endpoints, discarding the movement-quality details that distinguish recovery from compensation. Automated alternatives typically chase accuracy on noisy, single-observer labels to output opaque scores - a technology-centric approach that rarely reaches clinical practice. To address this, we present xAARA: an engine designed to augment rather than replace clinical judgment. From multi-view video, xAARA returns ARAT assessments with calibrated uncertainty and explanations across task, movement-phase, and movement-quality levels. Treating clinical scoring as an ill-posed inference problem, xAARA composes 692 calibrated multimodal models via a Dynamic Bayesian Network with entropy-based gating. It qualifies results against clinical validity rules and defers low-confidence cases. In 105 stroke survivors (788 exercises), xAARA achieved 94.2% task accuracy (Cohen's kappa=0.934) and 81.3% movement-phase accuracy (kappa=0.727), reducing predictive uncertainty by 96.1% compared to single-clinician scoring. For subjective cases, it matched at least one rater 100% of the time and never returned out-of-range scores. Four independent clinicians validated the assessments and indicated willingness to adopt the system. We argue that principled uncertainty quantification and clinician-aligned explainability are the critical bridges moving automated assessment from technical demonstration to a deployable clinical tool.

AI.tldr: xAARA—a Dynamic Bayesian Network ensemble that augments ARAT scoring from multi-view video with calibrated uncertainty and explanations, achieving high task and movement-phase accuracy while preserving clinician judgment.
AI.motivation: ARAT condenses rich movement quality into single scores; current automated methods are opaque, error-prone, and poorly adopted clinically; there is a need for uncertainty-aware, explainable, clinician-aligned automated assessment that distinguishes recovery from compensation.
AI.method: Constructs 692 calibrated multimodal models integrated via a Dynamic Bayesian Network with entropy-based gating. From multi-view video, xAARA outputs ARAT scores with calibrated uncertainty and explanations across task, movement-phase, and movement-quality levels. It treats clinical scoring as an ill-posed inference problem, qualifies results against clinical validity rules, and defers low-confidence cases. Evaluation on 105 stroke survivors (788 exercises) shows high task accuracy (94.2%; Cohen's kappa=0.934) and movement-phase accuracy (81.3%; kappa=0.727). It reduces predictive uncertainty by 96.1% relative to single-clinician scoring and provides agreement for subjective cases, with clinicians validating and willing to adopt.
AI.result: Task accuracy 94.2% (κ=0.934); movement-phase accuracy 81.3% (κ=0.727); uncertainty reduced by 96.1% vs single-clinician scoring; subjective cases match at least one rater 100% of the time; no out-of-range scores; four independent clinicians validated and indicated willingness to adopt.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty quantification and clinician-aligned explainability are essential to bridge automated assessment to deployable clinical tools; xAARA exemplifies an augmentative, transparent, and clinically validated approach for ARAT scoring.

title: BrainAgent: A Large Language Model-Driven Multi-Agent Framework for Autonomous Brain Signal Understanding
summary: Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) and brain signal understanding are pivotal for clinical health and next-generation interactions. Despite this significance, its widespread adoption in real-world scenarios remains restricted, primarily because current analytical paradigms lack sufficient agentic intelligence. First, existing methodologies impose prohibitive technical barriers, requiring extensive specialized expertise. Second, they remain inherently static and task-specific, failing to execute the complex, long-horizon workflows essential for real-world deployment. To accelerate the democratization of brain signal understanding, we draw inspiration from Large Language Models (LLMs) to introduce BrainAgent, an LLM-driven multi-agent framework designed to ground abstract natural language intent into rigorous, executable, and end-to-end processing pipelines. BrainAgent employs a hierarchical architecture where a central supervisor orchestrates specialized sub-agents for adaptive task decomposition and execution. Furthermore, we establish a comprehensive, systematic benchmark for evaluating agentic systems in brain signal analysis. Empirical results demonstrate that BrainAgent effectively automates complex workflows with superior reliability, marking a paradigm shift toward democratized brain signal understanding.

AI.tldr: BrainAgent is an LLM-driven hierarchical multi-agent framework that translates natural language intent into executable brain-signal analysis pipelines, addressing expertise barriers and static task limitations, and introducing a benchmark; results show automated, reliable complex workflows.
AI.motivation: Current brain-signal analysis methods require extensive expertise and are static and task-specific, hindering real-world deployment. There is a need for agentic, end-to-end systems that can handle long-horizon workflows and democratize access.
AI.method: A central supervisor coordinates specialized sub-agents to decompose tasks and execute adaptive brain-signal analysis workflows. The system grounds abstract natural language intent into rigorous, executable pipelines and includes a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating agentic brain-signal systems.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate that BrainAgent automates complex brain-signal workflows with superior reliability compared to baselines, enabling end-to-end processing and improved accessibility.
AI.conclusion: BrainAgent represents a paradigm shift toward democratized brain-signal understanding by enabling natural-language-driven, end-to-end analysis pipelines through a hierarchical, agent-based framework and providing a benchmark for evaluation.

title: CoGeoAD: Hierarchical Color-Geometric Fusion with Multi-View Attention for Zero-Shot 3D Anomaly Detection
summary: Zero-shot 3D anomaly detection is essential for industrial quality inspection, where labeled anomaly samples are scarce. Meanwhile, existing methods lack an effective mechanism to fuse complementary 2D color images with 3D geometric structures, limiting their ability to detect both surface and structural defects in a unified framework. To address these issues, we propose CoGeoAD, a unified CLIP-based framework that fuses color and geometric features by constructing pixel-aligned paired multi-view images. The framework introduces a Data-Driven Multi-View Attention (MVA) mechanism to adaptively aggregate 3D features and a Multi-Stage Color-Geometric Fusion (MS-CGF) module to hierarchically integrate multi-level features from both modalities. Extensive experiments on the MVTec3D-AD and Eyecandies benchmarks demonstrate that CoGeoAD achieves state-of-the-art performance, effectively capturing both structural and textural anomalies in complex industrial scenarios. our source code is available at https://github.com/kingdomShu/CoGeoAD.

AI.tldr: CoGeoAD introduces a unified CLIP-based framework for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection by fusing 2D color images with 3D geometry via pixel-aligned multi-view pairs. It employs Data-Driven Multi-View Attention (MVA) to adaptively aggregate 3D features and a Multi-Stage Color-Geometric Fusion (MS-CGF) to hierarchically fuse multi-level features, achieving state-of-the-art on MVTec3D-AD and Eyecandies.
AI.motivation: Lack of effective fusion between color and geometric cues in existing zero-shot 3D anomaly detection methods; inability to detect both surface and structural defects in a unified framework; scarcity of labeled anomalies for industrial inspection.
AI.method: Construct pixel-aligned paired multi-view images; introduce Data-Driven Multi-View Attention (MVA) to adaptively fuse 3D features; propose Multi-Stage Color-Geometric Fusion (MS-CGF) to hierarchically integrate multi-level color and geometric features; leverage CLIP for cross-modal alignment.
AI.result: On MVTec3D-AD and Eyecandies benchmarks, CoGeoAD achieves state-of-the-art performance, effectively detecting both textural and structural anomalies in challenging industrial settings.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective fusion of color and geometry within a CLIP-based framework to enhance zero-shot 3D anomaly detection; source code available online.

title: GRAFT: Graph-Based Affordance Transfer via Part Correspondence
summary: Generalizing robotic manipulation to unseen objects remains challenging, as learning-based approaches require many demonstrations and fail in few-shot settings. Prior work transfers affordances through semantic retrieval, but semantics alone neglect geometric similarity, which is critical for manipulation. We propose GRAFT, a geometry-aware correspondence framework for zero-shot manipulation transfer using only one demonstration per object. Objects are represented as part-based graphs, where part-level descriptors support global instance retrieval and part correspondence, and vertex-level descriptors enable fine-grained contact point matching. For an unseen object, our method first retrieves the most functionally and geometrically similar instance from the demonstration buffer with aligned functional parts, and finally propagates the contact points through point-wise correspondence.

AI.tldr: GRAFT uses geometry-aware, part-based graphs to enable zero-shot manipulation transfer from a single demonstration by retrieving geometrically and functionally similar parts and propagating contact points to unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Generalizing robotic manipulation to unseen objects is hard for learning-based methods, especially in few-shot settings. Semantic transfer alone ignores geometry, which is crucial for manipulation.
AI.method: Objects are represented as part-based graphs with part-level descriptors for global retrieval and correspondence, and vertex-level descriptors for fine-grained contact matching. For an unseen object, the method retrieves the most functionally and geometrically similar instance from a demonstration buffer with aligned functional parts, then propagates contact points through point-wise correspondence.
AI.result: Not stated in the abstract; the text describes the approach but does not report empirical results.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware, graph-based representations enable zero-shot manipulation transfer by leveraging geometric and functional similarity and propagating contact points to unseen objects.

title: Towards Scalable Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning with Large Decision Models
summary:
Recent progress in large-scale sequence modeling has shown that a single model can learn useful representations across highly diverse data distributions. Inspired by these advances, we investigate whether a unified transformer policy can be trained across large collections of heterogeneous reinforcement learning environments.
  We introduce LDM-v0, a Large Decision Model trained offline on trajectories collected from thousands of environments spanning multiple domains and modalities. LDM-v0 is a multi-task, multi-modal transformer policy conditioned on histories of observations, actions, rewards, and termination signals, and trained through supervised next-action prediction over offline trajectories. We describe the environment infrastructure, automated data generation pipeline, model architecture, and training methodology used to build LDM-v0, and evaluate its performance across diverse environments. We show that a single pretrained model matches the performance of independently trained task-specific reference policies on approximately 1,000 environments including robotics, autonomous driving, inventory management, cybersecurity, trading, and video games. These results demonstrate the feasibility of large-scale offline pretraining across heterogeneous reinforcement learning environments using a single transformer policy.

AI.tldr: LDM-v0 is a single transformer model pretrained offline on diverse RL environments, achieving task-agnostic performance comparable to many task-specific policies across ~1k environments.
AI.motivation: Demonstrate the feasibility and benefits of unified, large-scale offline pretraining for heterogeneous reinforcement learning tasks, enabling cross-domain transfer and representation learning.
AI.method: A multi-task, multi-modal transformer policy conditioned on observation, action, reward, and termination histories. Trained via supervised next-action prediction on offline trajectories collected from thousands of environments across robotics, autonomous driving, inventory management, cybersecurity, trading, and video games. Includes environment infrastructure, automated data generation, model architecture, and training methodology for LDM-v0.
AI.result: The pretrained model matches the performance of independently trained task-specific reference policies on roughly 1,000 diverse environments, demonstrating cross-domain efficacy of a single transformer policy.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale offline pretraining across heterogeneous RL environments with a single transformer policy is feasible, enabling unified representations and cross-domain transfer without task-specific fine-tuning.

title: Quantization Inflates Reasoning: Token Inflation as a Hidden Cost of Low-Bit Reasoning Models
summary: Quantization is widely used to reduce the inference cost of large language models, but its effect on reasoning models is not fully captured by final-answer accuracy or per-token latency. We show that low-bit post-training quantization can introduce a hidden test-time compute cost: quantized reasoning models often generate longer chains of thought even when they still answer correctly. Across mathematical reasoning, code generation, scientific question answering, and agentic tool-use benchmarks, we find that INT4/INT3 quantization can preserve accuracy but increase reasoning-token usage, offsetting the expected per-token speedup. To measure this effect, we introduce the CoT Token Inflation Ratio, which compares reasoning length between quantized and full-precision models averaged across all evaluation benchmarks. We further show that token inflation is accompanied by behavioral changes in the reasoning trace, including more intermediate steps and greater semantic repetition. These changes translate into measurable end-to-end real-world serving penalties. Finally, we evaluate mitigation strategies and find that prompting and decoding-time sampling offer inconsistent accuracy-length trade-offs, while quantization-aware training shows more promise in reducing both accuracy degradation and token inflation. Our results suggest that reasoning-token usage should be reported alongside accuracy when evaluating quantized reasoning models.

AI.tldr: Low-bit post-training quantization (INT4/INT3) can preserve accuracy for reasoning tasks but increases reasoning-token usage, revealing a hidden test-time compute cost. The paper introduces the CoT Token Inflation Ratio to measure why quantized models generate longer reasoning traces and shows more intermediate steps and repetition. Mitigation via prompting/sampling is inconsistent; quantization-aware training (QAT) shows more promise. Reporting reasoning-token usage alongside accuracy is recommended.
AI.motivation: Quantization is commonly used to reduce inference cost, but for reasoning models, token-level computation and reasoning traces contribute significantly to latency and cost. Final accuracy and per-token latency do not capture potential hidden costs in the reasoning process; understanding this is critical for reliable deployment of quantized reasoning models.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across mathematical reasoning, code generation, scientific QA, and agentic tool-use benchmarks. Compare INT4/INT3 post-training quantized models to full-precision baselines. Define CoT Token Inflation Ratio as the average ratio of reasoning tokens used by quantized vs. FP models across benchmarks. Analyze reasoning traces for step count and semantic repetition. Assess mitigation strategies including prompting changes and decoding-time sampling, and compare against quantization-aware training (QAT).
AI.result: Quantized models maintain accuracy but show increased reasoning-token usage (token inflation). CoT Token Inflation Ratio exceeds 1, indicating longer reasoning traces. Token inflation is associated with more intermediate steps and semantic repetition, leading to measurable end-to-end serving penalties. Prompting/sampling yield inconsistent accuracy-length trade-offs, whereas quantization-aware training reduces both accuracy degradation and token inflation.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-token usage should be reported alongside accuracy when evaluating quantized reasoning models. Quantization-aware training is a promising mitigation to reduce both accuracy loss and token inflation; standardizing token-length metrics (e.g., CoT Token Inflation Ratio) in deployment cost models is recommended.

title: Heterogeneous and Adept Snapshot Distillation for 3D Semantic Segmentation
summary: Multi-modal fusion and multi-model ensembling are prevalent in enhancing the performance of 3D semantic segmentation. Despite the impressive performance, these methods either rely on auxiliary input signals or suffer from costly computational expense. To efficaciously enhance the segmentation performance without introducing intolerable costs, we propose to transfer the rich knowledge from the multi-modal model (i.e., point clouds and images) and multiple model experts to the point-cloudbased network through knowledge distillation. Specifically, we present Information-oriented Heterogeneous Distillation (IHD) to help the uni-modal model absorb the complementary knowledge from the multi-modal teacher. We design the Information-Oriented Filtering (IOF) strategy to select informative images from the continuous image sequence for multi-modal fusion. This practice can boost the performance of the multi-modal teacher, thus benefiting the learning of the student. Besides, as opposed to vanilla model ensembling that requires the separate training of each expert, we propose Adept Snapshot Distillation (ASD). ASD treats the freely available model snapshots generated during the training phase as multiple experts, which significantly reduces the training cost for model ensembling. For each expert teacher, it only provides supervision to the student in the class where it is adept. The resulting Heterogeneous and Adept Snapshot Knowledge Distillation, dubbed HAS-KD, attains state-of-the-art results on ScanNetV2 and S3DIS datasets. HAS-KD can be seamlessly integrated into contemporary 3D segmentation algorithms and bring considerable gains without introducing extra inference burdens. The code will be made publicly available upon publication.

AI.tldr: Proposes HAS-KD: a distillation framework that combines Information-oriented Heterogeneous Distillation (IHD), Information-Oriented Filtering (IOF), and Adept Snapshot Distillation (ASD) to transfer multi-modal and ensemble knowledge to a single 3D segmentation model, achieving state-of-the-art results on ScanNetV2 and S3DIS with no extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: High computational cost of multi-modal fusion and multi-model ensembling in 3D semantic segmentation; aim to leverage rich cross-modal and ensemble knowledge during training to improve a uni-modal student without increasing inference time.
AI.method: IHD transfers knowledge from a multi-modal teacher to a uni-modal student; IOF selects informative images from continuous image sequences to optimize multi-modal fusion; ASD uses training-phase model snapshots as multiple expert teachers, each supervising the student only on the classes where it is adept; HAS-KD combines heterogeneous and adept snapshot knowledge distillation.
AI.result: Attains state-of-the-art results on ScanNetV2 and S3DIS datasets; claims no additional inference burden; code to be released publicly.
AI.conclusion: Provides a cost-efficient, effective training-time distortion of cross-modal and ensemble knowledge to enhance point-cloud-based segmentation, compatible with existing 3D segmentation architectures.

title: An Integrated Hardware-Software Design for Low-Data Spatial Defect Detection in Robotic Visual Inspection with Hybrid Optoelectronic Neural Networks
summary: To address data overload and inefficient shape-level annotation in robotic visual inspection, this paper proposes a hardware-software integrated optoelectronic architecture. A non-imaging, low-data paradigm is established to minimize annotation dependency. First, a sensor-in-the-loop strategy reconfigures a Digital Micromirror Device (DMD) as a physical optical convolutional layer, enabling photonic-domain feature extraction that unifies sensing hardware and processing software. To suppress data volume at the source, a block-based compressed sensing strategy encodes spatial information into low-dimensional temporal signals, drastically reducing redundancy. Subsequently, to bypass laborious manual defect shape annotation, natural language descriptions guide the network to align with highly generalizable features from Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP), steering the attention maps of the optoelectronic neural network toward defect shapes. Furthermore, a Localization Accuracy for Attention (LAA) metric is proposed to quantify shape-level defect localization performance. Experiments on transparent material defect detection validate the system's effectiveness. Parametric analysis reveals how measurement matrices, compression ratios, and block sizes affect accuracy. Results show that, compared to traditional imaging, the proposed architecture maintains equivalent accuracy while reducing data volume by 90% for Vision Transformers and computational workload by 60% for Convolutional Neural Networks. This low-data paradigm offers an efficient solution for industrial automation scenarios involving massive data streams, high acquisition costs, or constrained edge resources.

AI.tldr: Hardware–software co-design of an optoelectronic architecture for robotic inspection. It uses a DMD as an optical convolutional layer, block-based compressed sensing to curb data, and CLIP-guided attention with natural-language descriptions. Introduces a Localization Accuracy for Attention (LAA) metric. Demonstrates equivalent accuracy to traditional imaging with 90% data reduction for ViTs and 60% compute reduction for CNNs on transparent defect detection.
AI.motivation: Address data overload and annotation bottlenecks in robotic visual inspection. Target low-data regimes, expensive data acquisition, and constrained edge resources in industrial automation.
AI.method: Reconfigure DMD as a physical optical convolutional layer for photonic-domain feature extraction; apply block-based compressed sensing to encode spatial info into low-dimensional temporal signals; use natural-language descriptions with CLIP to guide attention toward defect shapes; propose LAA metric to quantify shape-level localization; validate on transparent-material defect datasets and perform parametric analyses on measurement matrices, compression ratios, and block sizes.
AI.result: Maintains equivalent accuracy to traditional imaging while reducing data volume by ~90% for Vision Transformers and cutting CNN computational load by ~60%; experiments reveal how design choices affect performance; supports applicability in data-intensive industrial settings.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable low-data, hardware–software co-designed approach for industrial inspection, reducing data and computation without sacrificing accuracy, with potential for broader deployment in edge and automated manufacturing contexts.

title: Evidence for feature-specific error correction in LLMs
summary: Understanding the features of large language models (LLMs) is a central goal of interpretability. LLMs are commonly assumed to use superposition to represent more features than they have dimensions. They may not only represent features in superposition but also perform computation in superposition. Theory predicts that computing in superposition requires error correction that privileges feature directions over generic ones, but this prediction has not been tested empirically. We propose an empirical test of error correction in LLMs based on activation perturbations.
