Conversely, a lower bound shows accumulated rotations must eventually degrade: as the far set grows, no rotations preserve the near signal without explicit far-mass control. For SO(2) rotations, rotating values too makes residual far contributions combine incoherently, extending the range. Controlled experiments support these predictions: random accumulated rotations substantially improve extrapolation over RoPE, learned token-dependent rotations maintain near-training-length perplexity far beyond the training context, and rotating values helps over queries and keys alone. Rotation-only models still degrade at extreme lengths, while ALiBi stays length-stable, consistent with the need for far-mass control.

AI.tldr: Replacing RoPE's position-indexed rotations with accumulated data-dependent transformations yields improved length extrapolation up to long contexts, then degradation at extreme lengths. This pattern extends to accumulated orthogonal transformations with regularity conditions, producing a finite mixing window and far-token suppression; improvements rely on transfer of per-token suppression learned during training, but a lower bound shows eventual degradation without explicit far-mass control. For SO(2) rotations, rotating values can extend the effective range. Experiments show random accumulated rotations boost extrapolation over RoPE; learned token-dependent rotations maintain low perplexity beyond training length; rotating values helps on queries and keys; rotation-only models degrade at extreme lengths; ALiBi remains length-stable, underscoring the need for far-mass control.
AI.motivation: To determine whether PaTH-like extrapolation gains stem from Householder structure or are a general consequence of accumulated transformations along source-to-query paths; to generalize beyond Householder reflections; to identify theoretical bounds and practical implications for length extrapolation in attention mechanisms.
AI.method: Analyze a simplified variant with RoPE-like block-diagonal SO(2) rotations and accumulated token-dependent angles; extend analysis to accumulated orthogonal transformations under regularity conditions; prove finite mixing window and far-token suppression; derive lower bound showing degradation without far-mass control; perform controlled experiments with random accumulated rotations and learned token-dependent rotations, comparing to RoPE and ALiBi; examine effects on queries, keys, values.
AI.result: Theoretically, products of accumulated orthogonal transforms become incoherent after finitely many steps, creating a finite mixing window independent of context length; per-token suppression learned during training transfers to longer evaluation contexts; high-dimensional concentration yields a score gap that suppresses distant tokens while preserving near-route signals; a lower bound shows degradation inevitable as the far set grows without far-mass control; for SO(2) rotations, rotating values can extend the effective range by causing far contributions to combine incoherently; experiments show random accumulated rotations improve extrapolation over RoPE; learned token-dependent rotations maintain perplexity beyond training lengths; rotating values helps across queries and keys; rotation-only models degrade at extreme lengths, while ALiBi remains length-stable.
AI.conclusion: Extrapolation behavior in these attention mechanisms is a general property of accumulated orthogonal transformations, not exclusive to Householder structure. Achieving robust long-context performance requires explicit far-mass control; randomization or learned token-dependent rotations can extend effective range, but without far-mass safeguards, degradation is inevitable; design choices like ALiBi avoid this issue by maintaining a stable length behavior.

title: AI Snitches Get Glitches: Towards Evading Agentic Surveillance
summary:
To better assist users with completing challenging tasks, AI agents mediate communications, access data, and interact with different APIs. Many employers (and even nation-states) already provide their users with this technology. However, widespread adoption of AI agents creates a new risk to abuse access to user data for another goal: surveilling users. These users might not even have the ability or permission to control the actions and data accesses of the surveilling agents.
  We introduce and formalize the problem of agentic surveillance: the ability of an AI agent to analyze available information, craft a report, and send it out using available tools. To evaluate surveillance capabilities across different models, we create SurveilBench, a dataset of various reporting scenarios focusing on three domains: corporate, education, and police. We find that some models exhibit emergent (i.e., unprompted) tendencies to help surveillance, but they also report the attempts to surveil users to the government.
  Finally, we repurpose prompt injections for evading surveillance and develop three evasion techniques that hide from, deceive, or induce over-escalation in surveillance agents. We conclude that agentic surveillance can already be easily implemented and, therefore, call for a comprehensive technical, ethical, and legislative framework to protect users.

AI.tldr: Formalizes agentic surveillance, introduces SurveilBench for reporting scenarios, shows emergent surveillance tendencies in models, and demonstrates evasion techniques; urges robust governance.
AI.motivation: AI agents that mediate user tasks can unintentionally or adversarially surveil users, exfiltrating data. This risks privacy and data governance as such agents become widespread in corporate, educational, and governmental settings.
AI.method: Formalize the problem of agentic surveillance; construct SurveilBench, a dataset spanning corporate, education, and police domains; evaluate model behavior for surveillance tendencies; repurpose prompt injections to develop three evasion techniques to hide from, deceive, or over-escalate surveillance agents.
AI.result: Some models exhibit emergent surveillance tendencies and report surveillance attempts to government; SurveilBench reveals domain-specific surveillance patterns; three evasion techniques suggested to bypass/suppress surveillance.
AI.conclusion: Agentic surveillance is feasible with current systems, necessitating technical, ethical, and legislative frameworks to protect users.

title: ESTANet: Efficient Online Error Detection in Procedural Videos via Prediction Inconsistency
summary: An efficient and accurate system for detecting errors in procedural tasks is crucial for supporting human needs in daily life, as it can provide instant notifications and guide people to correct mistakes. In this work, we study real-time online error detection in procedural videos from a simple but overlooked perspective: the prediction behavior of action detectors themselves. Instead of designing complex architectures or specialized supervision, we observe that action detectors naturally exhibit different prediction characteristics depending on their sensitivity to input dynamics and temporal context. We therefore propose ESTANet (Error-Sensitive and Temporally-vArying Network), a lightweight framework that detects errors by exploiting inconsistencies among action predictions produced by a small set of action detectors. We construct standard and error-sensitive action detectors that behave similarly on correct executions but respond differently when errors occur. Meanwhile, detectors operating with different temporal contexts further amplify prediction inconsistencies when the procedure deviates from the intended sequence. During inference, we detect errors by aggregating mismatches between standard and error-sensitive predictions through majority voting to flag frames that contain errors. Extensive experiments on EgoPER, Assembly-101-O, and EPIC-Tent-O demonstrate that ESTANet achieves state-of-the-art performance in online error detection while maintaining real-time efficiency with a lightweight architecture. Our results highlight that leveraging the intrinsic properties of action detectors can yield a powerful and practical solution for online error detection without increasing architectural design complexity.

AI.tldr: ESTANet uses prediction inconsistencies among a small set of action detectors with varying temporal contexts to detect errors in procedural videos online. It is lightweight, utilizes majority voting, and achieves state-of-the-art real-time performance on EgoPER, Assembly-101-O, and EPIC-Tent-O without complex architectures.
AI.motivation: Real-time online error detection in procedural tasks is essential for immediate feedback in daily life. Existing approaches often rely on complex architectures or heavy supervision. Observing that action detectors’ predictions reveal error-induced inconsistencies offers a simpler, practical avenue.
AI.method: Create standard and error-sensitive detectors that respond similarly on correct executions but differently on errors. Use detectors with diverse temporal contexts to amplify inconsistencies. During inference, aggregate mismatches between standard and error-sensitive predictions via majority voting to flag error frames. Maintain a lightweight, efficient architecture.
AI.result: ESTANet achieves state-of-the-art performance for online error detection on EgoPER, Assembly-101-O, and EPIC-Tent-O, while preserving real-time efficiency due to its lightweight design.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging the intrinsic prediction properties of action detectors can yield a powerful, practical solution for online error detection without architectural complexity. The approach demonstrates that simple detector ensembles with temporal diversity can outperform more complex models in online settings.

title: Large-Scale Tunnel Air--Ground Collaboration With FLISP: Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planne
comment: 24 pages, 31 figures, 5 tables. Author accepted manuscript. This work was supported by the State Key Laboratory of Autonomous Intelligent Unmanned Systems. The authors also thank the KinaMind Society for its inspiring environment and support
summary: Hydropower tunnel inspection is critical for infrastructure integrity yet remains inefficient and hazardous using manual methods. We propose FLISP (Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner), a mapless planning framework for cooperative UGV-UAV inspection. Unlike traditional map-based paradigms, FLISP features three core contributions: (1) a unified architecture where a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite drives synchronized path generation for both platforms; (2) platform-specific solvers utilizing an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance and a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight; and (3) a hierarchical refinement strategy ensuring kinematic feasibility without state estimation drift. Benchmarks in a 1.2 km operational tunnel demonstrate that FLISP circumvents structural bottlenecks of map-based methods, eliminating map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). FLISP achieves a 100% success rate with ~7 ms latency, representing a 7-fold speedup over grid-based and three-order-of-magnitude improvement over sampling-based baselines. Validated in operational hydropower tunnels, this approach offers a scalable solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/Y_ezs1PfLJ4, and the code at https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git.

AI.tldr: Mapless Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path planning (FLISP) enables cooperative UGV-UAV tunnel inspection with synchronized path generation, avoiding maps and rasterization while delivering fast, reliable planning.
AI.motivation: To overcome inefficiencies and hazards of manual tunnel inspection and limitations of map-based planning in feature-degraded, linear infrastructure. Aims to remove map rasterization overhead and sampling instability by using a single LiDAR-IMU on the UGV to drive both platforms.
AI.method: Unified architecture where UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU generates synchronized paths for UGV and UAV. Platform-specific solvers: an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance; a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight. Hierarchical refinement ensures kinematic feasibility and prevents state-estimation drift.
AI.result: Benchmark in a 1.2 km operational tunnel shows FLISP achieves 100% success with ~7 ms latency, ~7× faster than grid-based methods, and ~3 orders of magnitude faster than sampling-based baselines. Eliminates map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). Validated in operational hydropower tunnels; demonstration video and code available.
AI.conclusion: FLISP provides a scalable, mapless solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure, outperforming traditional map-based and sampling-based planning in speed and reliability.

title: Auto-Configured Explainable Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Site Pollution Prediction
summary: Accurate particulate matter (PM) prediction is crucial for mitigating air pollution. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) effectively model spatiotemporal dependencies, but predefined graphs limit adaptability, and some datasets complicate learning. This study introduces a graph construction method based on a confusion matrix from a supervised learning process to dynamically capture inter-class relationships. Additionally, a hybrid loss function that combines energy distance and Huber loss is applied to address the vanishing gradient problem and improve learning stability. The approach is evaluated using air pollution data from the University of Utah AirU Pollution Monitoring Network in Salt Lake City, UT, with five GNN models: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), Simple Graph Convolutional Networks (SGConv), Graph Isomorphism Networks (GINs), Graph Attention Networks (GATs), and GraphSage. The experimental results of single- and multistep predictions confirm that GraphSage achieves the highest accuracy in predicting the concentrations of PM${1}$, PM${10}$, and PM$_{2.5}$ over different time horizons. Furthermore, {\color{black} GNNExplainer (Graph Neural Network Explainer) and PGExplainer (Probabilistic Graph Explainer)} are applied to interpret feature importance and graph structure, ensuring model transparency. Results show improved prediction accuracy, with GNN models outperforming traditional machine learning \textcolor{black}{and deep learning models (i.e., Prophet, Long short-term memory, Gated recurrent units} in air pollution forecasting.

AI.tldr: Dynamic graph construction from a confusion-matrix-based inter-class relationship and a hybrid energy distance–Huber loss improve GNN performance for PM forecasting; GraphSage excels among tested models, with interpretable insights from GNNExplainer/PGExplainer and superior performance over Prophet/LSTM/GRU.
AI.motivation: Predefined, fixed graphs limit a GNN's ability to model evolving, dataset-specific inter-class relationships in air pollution data. A data-driven, dynamic graph and stable training objective are needed for improved predictive accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: Construct a graph using a confusion matrix derived from a supervised learning process to capture inter-class relationships; train five GNNs (GCN, SGConv, GIN, GAT, GraphSage) on AirU data for PM1, PM10, PM2.5 across single- and multi-step horizons; employ a hybrid loss combining energy distance and Huber loss to alleviate vanishing gradients and stabilize learning; apply GNNExplainer and PGExplainer to interpret feature importance and graph structure.
AI.result: GraphSage achieved the highest accuracy for PM1, PM10, and PM2.5 across multiple time horizons; GNN models outperform traditional ML and DL baselines (Prophet, LSTM, GRU) in air pollution forecasting; interpretable results provided by GNNExplainer and PGExplainer.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, data-driven graph construction plus a robust hybrid loss can enhance GNN-based PM forecasting, with GraphSage appearing most effective among the evaluated architectures. Interpretability tools further validate model transparency.

title: Agentic System as Compressor: Quantifying System Intelligence in Bits
summary: Large language models are turning from isolated predictors into agentic systems: they call tools, retrieve evidence, obey environment constraints, use verifiers, and complete tasks through search and multi-turn interaction. We adopts an analytical viewpoint based on "compression is intelligence": under a fixed task distribution, interface, and compute budget, a stronger agentic system lets a target object be reconstructed with fewer bits. We operationalize the measure with arithmetic coding, seed coding, and a fallback, and evaluate it in five settings: reversed text, chess moves, protein sequences, retrieval-augmented question answering, and semantic story compression; in all of them agentic components reduce codelength. These small, controlled experiments cover component types typical of real agentic systems, show that codelength can analyze how components, observers, and budgets change residual uncertainty, and offer guidance for evaluating real agent systems.

AI.tldr: Agentic LLMs under fixed task distribution, interface, and compute budgets reduce the residual uncertainty needed to reconstruct a target object, as shown by five controlled experiments using a compression-is-intelligence framework.
AI.motivation: To operationalize an information-theoretic measure of agenticity and evaluate whether stronger agentic components reduce description length under fixed budgets, informing how to evaluate real agent systems.
AI.method: Adopt a compression-is-intelligence framework. Use arithmetic coding, seed coding, and a fallback. Test across five domains—reversed text, chess moves, protein sequences, retrieval-augmented QA, and semantic story compression—to quantify codelength changes as agentic components are added.
AI.result: In all five settings, agentic components reduce codelength, indicating decreased residual uncertainty. The measure captures how components, observers, and budgets affect information content and provides a practical tool for evaluating real agent systems.
AI.conclusion: A compression-based framework can diagnose and compare agentic components under fixed resources, offering a practical, scalable method to evaluate and design agent systems by tracking codelength changes across tasks and budgets.

title: REViT: Roto-reflection Equivariant Convolutional Vision Transformer
summary: In this paper, we propose a discrete roto-reflection group equivariant vision transformer with convolutional attention. Roto-reflection equivariant networks preserve the rotational, flip and positional symmetry in feature maps, making them useful for tasks where orientation of the inputs is relevant to the model outputs. In image classification and object detection, most of the studies on roto-reflection equivariant models have focused on using convolutional neural networks rather than vision transformers. In this paper, we examine the challenges involved in achieving equivariance in vision transformers, and we propose a simpler way to implement a discretized roto-reflection group equivariant vision transformer. The experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms the existing approaches for developing discrete roto-reflection group equivariant neural networks for image classification.

AI.tldr: Proposes a discrete roto-reflection group equivariant vision transformer with convolutional attention, preserving rotational, flip, and positional symmetry in feature maps and achieving superior performance over prior discrete roto-reflection networks for image classification.
AI.motivation: To leverage symmetry under rotations and reflections in vision tasks and extend equivariance from CNNs to vision transformers, addressing challenges in achieving roto-reflection equivariance within ViTs.
AI.method: Introduce a discretized roto-reflection group equivariant vision transformer using convolutional attention. The approach integrates group-equivariant operations within a ViT framework, discretizes the roto-reflection group, and aims for simpler implementation. Evaluations focus on image classification, comparing against existing discrete roto-reflection group equivariant networks.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms existing approaches for discrete roto-reflection group equivariant networks on image classification, demonstrating the effectiveness of the discretized RiR (roto-reflection) equivariant ViT with convolutional attention.
AI.conclusion: A simpler, effective discretized RiR-equivariant ViT is feasible and improves image classification performance, suggesting that transformers can be endowed with roto-reflection symmetry with practical, improved results.

title: MAPL: Multi-Objective Preference Learning for Robot Locomotion
summary: Reward design remains a major bottleneck in reinforcement learning for robot locomotion, where successful policies often depend on carefully tuned, task-specific reward functions. Preference-based reinforcement learning offers an alternative, but existing LLM-based methods typically ask for a single overall judgment between behaviors, making it difficult to capture the multiple competing objectives that underlie high-quality locomotion. We present Multi-Objective AI-Informed Preference Learning (MAPL), a framework that learns locomotion rewards from high-level natural language objectives rather than manually engineered reward equations. MAPL prompts a large language model to compare trajectories independently along semantically meaningful criteria, using generic language descriptions that are terrain-invariant and require little domain expertise. These objective-wise preferences are used to train a multi-head preference scoring model, whose outputs are aggregated to form a scalar reward for policy optimization. Across four quadruped locomotion environments, MAPL trains policies using only LLM-generated preferences and achieves performance comparable to or better than expert-designed rewards, while eliminating task-specific reward engineering.

AI.tldr: MAPL learns locomotion rewards from high-level natural language objectives using multi-objective LLM-generated preferences, enabling terrain-invariant, domain-agnostic guidance without manual reward engineering.
AI.motivation: Reward design is a bottleneck in reinforcement learning for robot locomotion; existing methods struggle to encode multiple competing objectives and require task-specific reward engineering.
AI.method: The approach prompts an LLM to compare trajectories along independent, semantically meaningful criteria expressed in generic language. It collects objective-wise preferences, trains a multi-head preference scoring model, and aggregates outputs to form a scalar reward used for policy optimization across four quadruped environments.
AI.result: Policies trained with only LLM-generated preferences achieve performance comparable to or better than expert-designed rewards, across multiple tasks, while eliminating task-specific reward engineering.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that natural-language objectives can drive effective locomotion rewards via multi-objective preference learning, reducing dependence on hand-crafted reward functions and potentially improving generalization to new terrains and tasks.

title: CKM-Driven Communication-Aware UAV Intelligent Trajectory Optimization for Urban Inspection
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly employed in urban inspection tasks, where reliable communication is critical but challenging due to the severe spatial channel heterogeneity. To address the issue, in this paper, we focus on the communication-aware path planning for multi-UAV tasks, and propose a channel knowledge map (CKM)-driven trajectory planning framework which integrates the channel modeling and trajectory decision-making. Specifically, we apply the diffusion model to construct a time-accumulated CKM and achieve the accurate perception with low flight overhead, which leverages the sparse observation data to reconstruct the high-fidelity global channel quality distribution. Based on the CKM, we propose a global-to-local graph attention network soft actor-critic algorithm. The graph attention network optimizes the complex combinatorial node ordering problem, generating an optimal and communication-aware sequence for the inspection targets. Subsequently, the soft actor-critic algorithm performs continuous action control to ensure the smoothness of the flight path and dynamically avoid communication attenuation areas. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively guides UAVs through high-quality channel regions without dependence on real-time channel feedback, significantly improving both the trajectory efficiency and communication reliability.

AI.tldr: CKM-driven UAV trajectory planning using diffusion-based channel modeling and a graph-attention SAC to achieve communication-aware, efficient routing without relying on real-time channel feedback.
AI.motivation: Urban UAV inspections suffer from severe spatial channel heterogeneity, which degrades link reliability; there is a need for planning frameworks that integrate channel awareness into path planning to maintain communication while optimizing trajectories.
AI.method: Construct a time-accumulated channel knowledge map (CKM) via a diffusion model using sparse observations; use CKM to inform a global-to-local graph attention network (GAT) that yields an optimal node sequence for inspection targets; apply soft actor-critic (SAC) for continuous-flight control to maintain smooth paths and avoid attenuation regions; the combined framework aims to optimize both trajectory efficiency and communication reliability.
AI.result: Simulation results indicate the CKM-driven approach guides UAVs through high-quality channel regions without requiring real-time channel feedback, improving trajectory efficiency and communication reliability compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Integrating diffusion-based CKM with GAT-augmented planning and SAC control provides a robust, communication-aware planning framework for multi-UAV urban inspection tasks, reducing dependence on real-time channel measurements while enhancing performance.

title: WinDOM: Self-Family Distillation for Small-Model GUI Grounding
summary: Small ($\sim$2B) GUI-grounding agents are attractive for on-device deployment, accessibility tooling, and low-cost iteration, but at this scale they face two open recipe questions: how to obtain bounding-box training data without expensive human annotation, and how to combine supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning. We address both, with the explicit goal of pushing small-model performance rather than scaling up. WinDOM is a $54{,}425$-record grounding corpus harvested by driving an open-source Windows 11 web reimplementation under headless Playwright, with bounding boxes read directly off the DOM and no OCR or human annotation. Self-Family Distillation (SFD) is a single rejection-sampling cold-start parameterised only by the teacher choice: either an EMA of the student (no external model) or a frozen larger same-family teacher. We then treat the saturation depth of the SFD cold-start as an explicit GRPO hyperparameter. On a Qwen3.5-2B student, the under-saturated cold-start is a better GRPO initialiser than the converged one: SFD-4B with Early-init RL gains $+5.4$ OOD-mean ($+3.5$ ScreenSpot-Pro, $+7.0$ OSWorld-G, $+5.8$ ScreenSpot-V2) over the base. The same-size EMA mode lands within roughly one OOD-mean point of the cross-size $4$B variant ($65.2$ vs $66.3$) without an external teacher.

AI.tldr: WinDOM provides a 54,425-record GUI-grounding dataset with bounding boxes derived directly from the DOM, eliminating OCR or manual annotation, enabling training of small GUI-grounding agents. The paper also introduces Self-Family Distillation (SFD) with a GRPO-controlled cold-start, showing that for a 2B-scale Qwen model, SFD-4B with Early-init RL yields notable out-of-distribution (OOD) gains, approaching cross-size larger-teacher performance using an EMA-only baseline.
AI.motivation: Address two data/learning bottlenecks for small GUI-grounding models: (1) obtaining bounding-box supervision without expensive human annotation, and (2) effectively combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning, all while prioritizing small-model efficiency rather than scaling up.
AI.method: WinDOM: a 54,425-record grounding corpus harvested by driving Windows 11 via headless Playwright with bounding boxes read from the DOM (no OCR or human labels). Self-Family Distillation (SFD): a rejection-sampling, cold-start distillation parameterized by teacher choice (EMA of the student or a frozen larger same-family teacher). The saturation depth of the SFD cold-start is treated as a GRPO hyperparameter. Experiments on a Qwen3.5-2B student compare under-saturated vs converged cold-start setups and EMA vs external teacher modes, evaluating on multiple OOD benchmarks (ScreenSpot-Pro, OSWorld-G, ScreenSpot-V2).
AI.result: With SFD-4B and Early-init RL, OOD-mean improves by +5.4 over the base model, with gains across datasets: +3.5 on ScreenSpot-Pro, +7.0 on OSWorld-G, +5.8 on ScreenSpot-V2. The EMA-only (same-size) mode reaches 65.2, roughly within 1 point of the cross-size 4B variant at 66.3, i.e., comparable performance without an external teacher.
AI.conclusion: Small GUI-grounding models can achieve strong OOD performance using a DOM-derived supervision dataset (WinDOM) and a cold-start Self-Family Distillation strategy with GRPO-controlled saturation depth. Early-init RL with SFD enables performance close to larger-teacher baselines, illustrating data-efficient, on-device capable grounding for GUI tasks.

title: V-Zero: Answer-Label-Free On-Policy Distillation with Contrastive Evidence Gating for Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning
summary: Fine-grained visual reasoning requires multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to identify task-relevant visual evidence and ground their reasoning in local image regions. Existing agentic methods typically rely on reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards or supervised fine-tuning on large-scale annotated reasoning traces, leading to costly exploration, hand-designed verification rules, or heavy dependence on textual supervision. A natural way to avoid such external answer labels is to learn from trajectories sampled by the student itself, which points to On-Policy Distillation (OPD). To understand what OPD can and cannot provide for visual reasoning, we revisit it as negative-free stop-gradient alignment. This perspective shows that, although OPD provides effective token-level correction, its ceiling is constrained by the absence of trajectory-level discrimination. Motivated by these observations, we propose V-Zero, an answer-label-free framework for visual reasoning with contrastive evidence gating. V-Zero uses no annotated textual answer labels; instead, during training it pairs a question-relevant regional crop with a negative visual view to evaluate student-sampled trajectories and gate dense token-level distillation. Experiments on multiple visual reasoning benchmarks show that V-Zero consistently improves fine-grained visual reasoning while preserving strong generalization. Notably, V-Zero is more than 5$\times$ faster than previous supervised fine-tuning methods and more than 10$\times$ faster than reinforcement learning baselines. Code and dataset will be released at https://github.com/eVI-group-SCU/V-Zero

AI.tldr: V-Zero: an answer-label-free visual reasoning framework that uses contrastive evidence gating to improve on-policy distillation (OPD), outperforming supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning baselines in speed and generalization.
AI.motivation: To understand the capabilities and limits of On-Policy Distillation (OPD) for visual reasoning and to reduce reliance on annotated textual answers.
AI.method: Negative-free stop-gradient alignment; treat OPD as token-level correction but bound by lack of trajectory-level discrimination; introduce contrastive evidence gating with question-relevant regional crops paired with negative visual views to evaluate and gate distillation.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on multiple visual reasoning benchmarks; strong generalization preserved; substantial speedups (>5x vs supervised fine-tuning; >10x vs RL baselines).
AI.conclusion: OPD has a ceiling due to missing trajectory-level discrimination; V-Zero overcomes this by an answer-label-free, contrastive, gated distillation approach, achieving efficient and scalable visual reasoning.

title: HEART: Coordination of Heterogeneous Expert Agents for Physically Grounded Robotic Task Planning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can reason over complex instructions but often fail to satisfy the physical and spatial constraints required for robotic task planning. Recent LLM-based planners directly translate text into action sequences, yet they lack structured reasoning about feasibility, reachability, and logical order, resulting in invalid or incomplete plans. We present a heterogeneous multi-LLM framework that decomposes instructions into atomic reasoning tasks and allocates them to role-specialized expert agents under a token budget for real-world computational and communicational constraints. By combining role-oriented reasoning from heterogeneous agents followed by constraint-driven plan synthesis, HEART validates capability, reachability, and constraint conditions before planning and helps produce physically executable plans while maintaining efficiency. Experiments across different household benchmarks show that HEART consistently improves plan success compared to single-LLM and rule-based planners, demonstrating that heterogeneous LLM collaboration enables robust and scalable robotic task planning under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: HEART presents a heterogeneous multi-LLM framework for robotic task planning that partitions reasoning into atomic tasks, assigns them to role-specific expert agents under a token budget, and uses constraint-driven plan synthesis to yield physically executable plans, achieving higher plan success than single-LLM or rule-based approaches on household benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs often fail to respect physical/spatial feasibility, reachability, and proper logical sequencing in robotic planning; standard text-to-action translations lack structured reasoning and resource-aware collaboration.
AI.method: Decompose a task into atomic reasoning units, allocate units to role-specialized expert agents within a token budget, perform role-oriented reasoning followed by constraint-driven plan synthesis to validate capability, reachability, and constraints before planning, producing executable plans; evaluate across household benchmarks.
AI.result: HEART consistently improves plan success compared with single-LLM and rule-based planners, demonstrating robustness and scalability under resource constraints.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous LLM collaboration is effective for robust, scalable robotic task planning under resource constraints.

title: Closed-Loop Graph Algorithm Execution with Small Language Models: Step Accuracy and Rollout Reliability
summary: Small language models offer an efficient alternative to large-scale systems, but their ability to execute structured algorithms over multiple dependent decisions remains poorly understood. We study graph algorithm execution as a closed-loop prediction problem in which a model repeatedly selects the next action from the current graph and algorithmic state. Our evaluation framework covers several classical graph procedures, multiple synthetic graph families, and disjoint training, validation, and test partitions. It assesses both local decision quality and global execution behaviour using step accuracy, exact rollout accuracy, constraint validity, partial solution quality, prefix survival, and intervention-based diagnostics. The results show that adaptation can produce reliable policies for structural procedures such as traversal and coloring, while weighted algorithms remain substantially more sensitive to error accumulation. More broadly, the findings demonstrate that strong next-step prediction does not necessarily translate into reliable autonomous execution and motivate evaluating algorithmic language models through complete closed-loop rollouts rather than isolated decisions.

AI.tldr: Closed-loop evaluation shows small LMs can handle some graph procedures, but high next-step accuracy does not guarantee reliable autonomous execution; adaptation works for traversal/coloring, while weighted algorithms are error-prone.
AI.motivation: To understand whether small language models can execute structured, dependent graph algorithms reliably, and to identify metrics that reveal true algorithmic competence beyond isolated decisions.
AI.method: Introduce a closed-loop evaluation framework for graph algorithms: multiple classical procedures, diverse synthetic graph families, disjoint train/val/test partitions; assess local and global behavior with step accuracy, exact rollout accuracy, constraint validity, partial solution quality, prefix survival, and intervention diagnostics.
AI.result: Adaptation yields reliable policies for certain structural procedures (e.g., traversal, coloring). Weighted algorithms show strong sensitivity to error accumulation. High next-step accuracy does not guarantee robust autonomous execution.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating algorithmic language models requires full closed-loop rollouts; emphasize end-to-end execution metrics over isolated next-step predictions.

title: InvestPhilBench: A Multi-Layer Dynamic Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Model Procedural Reasoning in Expert Investment Philosophy
comment: 57 pages, 6 figures, 26 tables. Benchmark, data, and code released. v0.6 release; preliminary empirical study (de-confounded multi-model leaderboard forthcoming)
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as investment research assistants, yet no benchmark tests whether they can accurately reconstruct and apply the specific procedural decision frameworks of expert investors. We introduce InvestPhilBench, a multi-layer dynamic benchmark spanning eight cognitive tiers, from principle identification (L1) to novel framework extrapolation (L8). The v0.6 release comprises 118 primary-source-verified investment principle cards, 25 decision framework cards with explicit topology metadata, and 243 QA questions (197 dev / 46 held-out test). For reproducible scoring at scale we introduce the Benchmark Automated Scoring Pipeline (BASP) -- five algorithmic metrics (OGRS, KCCS, SAP@k, IVP, CKCA) -- the Failure Mode Detection Protocol (FMDP) with computable rules for six failure modes, and Gate Reconstruction Accuracy (GRA), a per-gate metric for questions with gold reasoning programs. In this release, InvestPhilBench is primarily a benchmark-and-methodology contribution. A four-model sanity wave on the 188-question development split shows a sharp provider-tier split (BASP 0.906 vs. 0.438); these mixed-judge numbers are confounded upper bounds. The central finding: the BASP composite saturates at the frontier (Claude L4 = 0.932) while GRA still exposes a procedural deficit (frontier L4 GRA approx. 0.77, L7 GRA 0.57-0.62) -- composite scoring rewards fluent prose and hides the procedural gap. v0.6 implements a unified judge and true model-in-the-loop retrieval/oracle conditions; the de-confounded multi-model leaderboard and full three-condition run are v1.0 deliverables. On a 100-item expert-annotated gold set the automated BASP composite tracks the human reference at Pearson r = 0.72 (MAE = 0.10), with attribution (SAP@3) the weakest sub-metric and the failure-mode detector running sensitive-but-over-flagging.

AI.tldr: InvestPhilBench v0.6 introduces a multi-layer eight-tier benchmark for evaluating LLMs on reconstructing and applying expert investment decision frameworks, with 118 principle cards, 25 framework cards, 243 QA items, and a reproducible BASP-based scoring pipeline plus FMDP and GRA. Initial results show BASP saturates at the frontier while procedural reasoning lags, indicating fluency can mask procedural gaps; human reference correlation is decent (r=0.72).
AI.motivation: Address the gap in assessing whether LLMs can internalize and apply explicit expert-investing decision frameworks beyond surface-language proficiency, enabling reproducible, scalable measurement of procedural reasoning in finance.
AI.method: Develop an eight-level cognitive framework (L1-L8) with principle and framework cards (with topology metadata); assemble 243 QA questions (197 development, 46 hold-out). Implement the Benchmark Automated Scoring Pipeline (BASP) with five metrics (OGRS, KCCS, SAP@k, IVP, CKCA), plus the Failure Mode Detection Protocol (FMDP) for six failure modes, and Gate Reconstruction Accuracy (GRA). Evaluate a four-model sanity run on 188-question development split; use a unified judge, model-in-the-loop retrieval/oracle conditions, and a 100-item expert-annotated gold set to compute Pearson r and MAE; report per-metric results including SAP@k and FMDP behavior.
AI.result: BASP composite reaches the frontier (Claude L4 ≈ 0.932) while GRA remains lower (L4 ≈ 0.77; L7 ≈ 0.57–0.62), indicating high fluency but incomplete procedural alignment. The four-model sanity wave shows provider-tier split (0.906 vs. 0.438) and potential upper-bound confounds. On the 100-item gold set, BASP composite tracks human reference well (Pearson r ≈ 0.72; MAE ≈ 0.10); SAP@3 is the weakest sub-metric; FMDP flags more than necessary (over-flagging) but captures certain failure modes.
AI.conclusion: InvestPhilBench v0.6 demonstrates a robust framework for benchmarking procedural investment-reasoning in LLMs and highlights a disconnect between high BASP scores and underlying procedural competence. The planned v1.0 aims to deliver a de-confounded multi-model leaderboard and true model-in-loop runs, enabling clearer measurement of procedural reasoning fidelity. Potential improvements include reducing over-flagging in FMDP, strengthening gate-level reasoning programs for GRA, and expanding the gold set for broader validity.

title: Efficient Remote Sensing Instance Segmentation with Linear-Time State Space Distilled Visual Foundation Models
comment: 17 pages, 11 figures, has been published in IEEE TGRS vol. 64, pp. 5625417-5625417, 2026, Art no. 5625417, doi: 10.1109/TGRS.2026.3696104
summary: The computational complexity of Transformers scales quadratically with the number of tokens, which significantly constrains the efficiency of vision models, particularly recent ViT-based foundation models in dense prediction tasks. Instance segmentation, a typical dense visual prediction task in the remote sensing field, faces similar challenges. In this paper, inspired by the recent advances of knowledge distillation in large language models, we introduce RS4D - a new remote sensing instance segmentation method with linear computational complexity, which addresses the inefficiency of long sequence modeling through distilled state space modeling (SSM). We propose an adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation training method for pre-training lightweight SSM backbones, which effectively compresses knowledge from the vast self-attention space into a compact, dense linear state space. We also design a remote sensing image instance segmentation architecture based on this lightweight visual encoder, where we explore variants of three different backbones and two segmentation heads. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, including SSDD, WHU, and NWPU. Compared to ViT-based approaches, our proposed SSM backbone achieves an 8x reduction in parameters and a 9x reduction in FLOPs while maintaining comparable or superior accuracy to both ViT- and CNN-based instance segmentation methods. The implementation codes have been publicly available at https://github.com/QinzheYang/RS4D.

AI.tldr: RS4D introduces a linear-complexity remote sensing instance segmentation backbone based on distilled state-space modeling (SSM). It uses adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation to pre-train lightweight SSM backbones, achieving ~8x parameter and ~9x FLOPs reductions with competitive accuracy on SSDD, WHU, NWPU datasets, and code is released.
AI.motivation: Transformers scale quadratically with tokens, hindering efficiency in vision tasks, especially dense remote-sensing instance segmentation. There is a need for scalable, efficient backbones that can capture long-range dependencies without prohibitive cost.
AI.method: Propose RS4D with distilled state-space modeling (SSM) to achieve linear complexity. Pre-train lightweight SSM backbones using adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation to compress knowledge from self-attention into dense linear state space. Build an instance segmentation architecture with three backbone variants and two segmentation heads. Evaluate on SSDD, WHU, NWPU datasets and compare against ViT- and CNN-based methods.
AI.result: SSM backbone achieves ~8x fewer parameters and ~9x fewer FLOPs than ViT-based approaches while maintaining comparable or superior accuracy across benchmarks. Code available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: RS4D demonstrates that distilled SSM backbones can provide efficient, competitive remote sensing instance segmentation, offering a practical alternative to heavy ViT models for dense prediction tasks.

title: Delta-Position Estimation-Based IMU Odometry: A Comparison of MLP and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
comment: This study was presented at the 11th International Congress on Engineering Sciences and Multidisciplinary Approaches, held in Istanbul, Türkiye
summary: In this study, the learning-based inertial odometry problem is investigated using raw IMU measurements obtained from the EuRoC MAV benchmark dataset. Instead of absolute position regression-a formulation that may lead to large constant errors-the models are trained to estimate the incremental displacement (Δp) over a fixed 50 ms sliding window, and the full trajectory is reconstructed through numerical integration. A standard Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) is compared with a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) equipped with learnable B-spline activations. Although KAN has 6.9 times fewer parameters than MLP (8,444 versus 57,859), it produces a 44% lower error in terms of final cumulative drift on the test trajectory (9.61 m versus 17.23 m). In addition, KAN exhibits more stable behavior in terms of long-term error accumulation, with lower P_50 and P_90 cumulative drift values. These findings indicate that learnable B-spline-based activations have the potential to reduce error accumulation in the inertial odometry problem.

AI.tldr: KA N using learnable B-spline activations reduces final drift and improves long-term stability in learning-based inertial odometry, with far fewer parameters than a standard MLP.
AI.motivation: To address error accumulation in inertial odometry by predicting local displacement over short windows and integrating, while exploring more expressive yet compact architectures.
AI.method: Compare a standard MLP with a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) featuring learnable B-spline activations, trained on raw IMU data from EuRoC MAV. The model predicts Δp over a 50 ms window; trajectory is reconstructed by numerical integration.
AI.result: KAN achieves 6.9x fewer parameters (8444 vs 57859) and 44% lower final cumulative drift (9.61 m vs 17.23 m) than MLP. It also shows better long-term stability with lower P50 and P90 drift values.
AI.conclusion: Learnable B-spline activations in KAN effectively reduce error accumulation in inertial odometry, offering a more parameter-efficient approach with improved drift characteristics compared to a standard MLP.

title: A Single Stepsize Suffices for Unprojected Linear TD(0): Simultaneous Robust and Fast Rates via Polyak--Ruppert Averaging
summary: We study linear TD(0) under Markovian sampling, where data are generated along a single trajectory. We provide high-probability guarantees for a plain unprojected TD(0) algorithm with Polyak-Ruppert (PR) averaging, using a single stepsize schedule $η_t \propto \frac{1}{τ_{\mathrm{mix}}\log(t)\sqrt{t}}$ that depends on the mixing time but requires no prior knowledge of the curvature parameter $ω$. Our first result shows that such a choice of the stepsize guarantees that the TD(0) iterates are automatically and uniformly bounded with high probability, without projections and without any stability argument based on $ω$. Building on this result, we establish a simultaneous high-probability convergence guarantee for the PR average: the same stepsize yields both a robust curvature-free $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\!\left(\frac{τ_{\mathrm{mix}}}{\sqrt{T}}\right)$ rate and a fast curvature-dependent $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\!\left(\frac{τ_{\mathrm{mix}}^2}{ωT}\right)$rate, with the bound taking the minimum of the two. The core technical ingredient is a Poisson-equation toolkit for geometrically mixing Markov chains, which decomposes Markov noise into a martingale term plus a controlled remainder and enables a new self-bounding inductive argument for pathwise stability.

AI.tldr: Unprojected TD(0) with PR averaging under Markovian sampling achieves high-probability stability and two convergence rates: a curvature-free O~(τ_mix/√T) and a curvature-dependent O~(τ_mix^2/(ω T)) for the averaged iterate, using a single stepsize η_t ∝ 1/(τ_mix log t √t) that does not require ω knowledge.
AI.motivation: Provide high-probability guarantees for TD(0) with data from a single Markovian trajectory, without projections and without requiring prior knowledge of the curvature parameter ω, while achieving robust convergence rates.
AI.method: Analyze plain unprojected TD(0) with Polyak-Ruppert averaging using a stepsize η_t ∝ 1/(τ_mix log t √t). Prove uniform high-probability boundedness of iterates (no projections, no ω-based stability). Then obtain high-probability convergence for PR-averaged iterate, yielding both a curvature-free rate and a curvature-dependent rate, via a Poisson-equation toolkit for geometrically mixing Markov chains that decomposes noise into a martingale term plus a controlled remainder and a self-bounding inductive argument for pathwise stability.
AI.result: Iterates are uniformly bounded with high probability; PR-averaged TD(0) achieves a simultaneous high-probability bound with two rates: O~(τ_mix/√T) (curvature-free) and O~(τ_mix^2/(ω T)) (curvature-dependent), with the bound being the minimum of the two.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a Poisson-equation based analysis for geometrically mixing Markov chains enabling curvature-free stability and two-rate convergence for TD(0) under Markovian sampling, and highlights a self-bounding inductive pathwise stability argument; lays groundwork for broader TD analyses under Markov noise.

title: Autodata: An agentic data scientist to create high quality synthetic data
summary: We introduce Autodata, a general method that enables AI agents to act as data scientists who build high quality training and evaluation data. We show how to train (meta-optimize) such a data scientist agent, so that it learns to create even stronger data. We describe the overall formulation, and a specific practical implementation, Agentic Self-Instruct. We conduct experiments on computer science research tasks, legal reasoning tasks and reasoning with mathematical objects, where we obtain improved results compared to classical synthetic dataset creation methods. Further, meta-optimizing the data scientist agent itself delivers an even larger performance uplift. Agentic data creation provides a way to convert increased inference compute into higher quality model training. Overall, we believe this direction has the potential to change the way we build AI data.

AI.tldr: Autodata is a general framework that treats AI agents as data scientists who autonomously generate high-quality training and evaluation data, with a meta-optimized variant called Agentic Self-Instruct. It demonstrates improved data-driven performance across domains and shows meta-optimization of the data scientist agent yields further gains, enabling inference compute to translate into better training data.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in manually created synthetic data and the cost of high-quality labeled data by automating data creation through autonomous agents that can craft and refine datasets, leveraging compute to improve data quality.
AI.method: Propose Autodata as a general method and implement a practical realization via Agentic Self-Instruct. Train (meta-optimize) a data-scientist agent to generate stronger data, and evaluate across computer science research, legal reasoning, and mathematical object reasoning tasks, comparing to classical synthetic data creation methods and assessing gains from meta-optimizing the data scientist agent.
AI.result: The approach yields improved results over classical synthetic dataset creation across tested domains; meta-optimizing the data-scientist agent provides a larger performance uplift, demonstrating that agentic data creation can meaningfully improve model training quality.
AI.conclusion: Agentic data creation can convert increased inference compute into higher quality training data and has the potential to transform how AI data is built, enabling more scalable, automated data curation.

title: State Space Models Meet Remote Sensing: A Survey
comment: 25 pages, 5 figures, has been published in SCIS SCIQ1 IF=8.1 https://doi.org/10.1007/s11432-025-4780-1
summary: State Space Models (SSMs), designed for long-range modeling, offer linear computational complexity and strong capabilities in capturing long-range dependencies. In the field of remote sensing, SSMs have gained popularity due to their effectiveness in addressing unique challenges such as dense visual predictions, multi-modal remote sensing data, and temporal remote sensing data, which have also yielded significant advancements in customized architectures. This paper presents a comprehensive review of SSM-based approaches in remote sensing, covering most of the relevant studies since SSMs were first introduced to the field. We offer a multi-dimensional analysis examining SSM applications in remote sensing tasks and discussing advancements in architecture design. This paper not only synthesizes the rapid progress in SSM-based research but also identifies key challenges and future opportunities. By providing a detailed perspective, this paper aims to serve as a foundational resource for remote sensing researchers, offering actionable insights to foster further advancements in this evolving domain. We will keep tracing related works at https://github.com/QinzheYang/Awesome-RS-State-Space-Model.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of SSM-based approaches in remote sensing, surveying literature, architectures, and challenges, with a resource link.
AI.motivation: SSMs offer linear computational complexity and strong long-range modeling, addressing remote sensing challenges such as dense visual predictions, multi-modal data, and temporal sequences; a consolidated analysis and roadmap is needed for the field.
AI.method: Literature review and taxonomy of SSM-based methods in remote sensing: survey of studies since SSMs were introduced to the field, multi-dimensional analysis across tasks, data modalities, and architectures; synthesis of progress; identification of challenges and opportunities; includes a public repository as a reference.
AI.result: Synthesis of rapid progress in SSM-based remote sensing research; identification of key challenges and future directions; actionable insights for researchers; foundational resource for the community.
AI.conclusion: SSM-based remote sensing research is evolving; the paper provides a foundational resource to foster further advancements and guide future work.

title: Generative AI for Safe and Photorealistic Drone Light Shows
summary: Drone light shows are redefining aerial entertainment, yet their widespread adoption is bottlenecked by labor-intensive, manual animation. While generative AI promises an automated alternative, current frameworks fail to provide photorealism with fluid, dynamic motion. To address this limitation, we introduce SWAN, an end-to-end pipeline that synthesizes photorealistic, large-scale, and collision-free drone choreographies directly from text prompts. SWAN converts text into realistic reference videos and translates these pixel-space dynamics into physical swarm kinematics using a novel, adaptive point-tracking algorithm. Unlike existing trackers, this method maintains spatial coherence through severe occlusions and rapid topological shifts. A dedicated planner then allocates these trajectories to individual drones, while a subsequent safety filter ensures collision-free execution. We demonstrate scalability by safely orchestrating simulated 2,000-drone formations and validate physical feasibility on a dense real-world swarm of 49 quadcopters, operating everything entirely on standard consumer hardware. Combined, this work demonstrates how generative AI can be leveraged to automate multi-robot choreography design, providing an accessible new framework for drone light shows.

AI.tldr: Generative AI-driven SWAN pipeline converts text prompts into photorealistic, large-scale drone choreographies with an adaptive point-tracking method, planning, and safety filtering, demonstrated up to 2000 drones in simulation and 49 drones in real-world tests on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Labor-intensive, manual drone animation limits the scalability of drone light shows. There is a need for automated, photorealistic, dynamic, and scalable choreography generation that can translate abstract descriptions into physically feasible swarm motions.
AI.method: SWAN is an end-to-end pipeline that (1) converts text prompts into realistic reference videos, (2) translates pixel-space dynamics into swarm kinematics using an adaptive point-tracking algorithm robust to occlusions and topology changes, (3) uses a dedicated planner to assign trajectories to individual drones, and (4) applies a safety filter to ensure collision-free execution. The approach is validated on simulated 2,000-drone formations and a real-world 49-drone swarm, all on consumer hardware.
AI.result: The system demonstrates scalable generation of photorealistic, collision-free drone choreographies, including large-scale simulation (2000 drones) and physical validation with 49 quadcopters on standard hardware.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI can automate multi-robot choreography design; SWAN provides a practical framework that enables accessible, scalable, and safe drone light shows.

title: Latent Block-Diffusion Temporal Point Processes: A Semi-Autoregressive Framework for Asynchronous Event Sequence Generation
summary: Modeling and sampling from the underlying distribution of asynchronous event sequences are crucial in various real-world applications, including social networks, medical diagnosis, and financial transactions. Existing autoregressive methods suffer from error accumulation during multi-step generation, while non-autoregressive diffusion methods are typically limited to fixed-length output sequences. In this paper, we propose Latent Block-Diffusion Temporal Point Processes (LBDTPP), a novel semi-autoregressive TPP framework that introduces a latent block diffusion mechanism for high-quality and variable-length event sequence generation. The core idea is to define an autoregressive probability distribution over event blocks in latent space and perform Gaussian diffusion within each block. By sequentially generating blocks while simultaneously sampling events in each block, LBDTPP preserves the length flexibility of autoregressive TPPs and inherits the parallel high-quality generation capability of diffusion models. Theoretically, we derive Wasserstein error bounds showing that, under suitable local approximation and prefix-stability assumptions, block-wise generation can reduce error accumulation compared with event-wise autoregressive generation. Extensive experiments on six real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that LBDTPP outperforms state-of-the-art TPP baselines in both unconditional and conditional generation tasks. Further empirical analyses verify the benefits of latent-space diffusion and block-wise generation, and reveal the trade-off between generation quality and block size. Our code is available at https://github.com/Zh-Shuai/LBDTPP.

AI.tldr: Introduces Latent Block-Diffusion Temporal Point Processes (LBDTPP), a semi-autoregressive model that uses latent-space diffusion within event blocks and autoregressive block generation to produce high-quality, variable-length event sequences, with theoretical error bounds and state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive TPPs suffer from error accumulation across steps, while non-autoregressive diffusion methods are often limited to fixed-length outputs; there is a need for a method that preserves variable-length flexibility while achieving diffusion-level generation quality.
AI.method: Define an autoregressive distribution over latent-space event blocks and apply Gaussian diffusion inside each block; generate blocks sequentially and sample events within blocks in parallel, combining autoregressive and diffusion advantages; derive Wasserstein error bounds under local approximation and prefix-stability; provide empirical evaluation on six real-world datasets; release code.
AI.result: LBDTPP outperforms state-of-the-art TPP baselines in both unconditional and conditional generation, with analyses confirming benefits of latent-space diffusion and block-wise generation and detailing the trade-off between block size and generation quality.
AI.conclusion: The semi-autoregressive latent block-diffusion framework successfully integrates autoregressive block modeling with diffusion within blocks, enabling high-quality, flexible, variable-length event sequence generation and reduced error accumulation, supported by theory and experiments.

title: The Unfireable Safety Kernel: Execution-Time AI Alignment for AI Agents and Other Escapable AI Systems
summary:
AI agents are granted access to tools, APIs, and other infrastructure, making them active principals in those systems. The dominant approach places controls inside the agent's own runtime: system prompts, output filters, and guardrail libraries. Any control in the agent's address space is reachable by inputs that influence it; this generalizes to any AI system with sufficient reach into its own runtime, a class we term escapable AI systems.
  We identify four properties that an authorization mechanism must satisfy for architectural control rather than for cooperative requests: process separation, pre-action enforcement on a structurally only path, fail-closed at both the request and system levels, and externalized signed evidence verifiable outside the controlled system's trust boundary. We position this layer as execution-time AI alignment, complementing training-time alignment (RLHF, Constitutional AI) and inference-time alignment.
  We present the Unfireable Safety Kernel, a Rust reference implementation realizing all four. Its fail-closed invariant is machine-checked at two levels: an SMT theorem (Z3) and an exhaustive bounded-model-checking proof of the production decision function (Kani, 4/4 harnesses). A Python-to-Rust migration was gated on byte-equivalence (1000/1000 fixtures; 17/17 adversarial classes). We evaluate the kernel governing a live, escapable AI system, a deterministic, self-improving world model, against an escape-seeking adversary driving its real self-modification seam: across 1,000 self-modifications, all 704 attempts on the safety-critical core are refused, with no escape; a further 300, under the operator kill switch, are also refused. A separate campaign of 6,240 authorization round-trips had no successful bypass. Against 3 contemporary systems claiming the agent control plane, the agent invokes control; here, it lacks that choice.

AI.tldr: A framework and Rust-based safety kernel to enforce architectural control over escapable AI systems, using four properties (process separation, pre-action enforcement on a structurally only path, fail-closed at request and system levels, externalized signed evidence). It implements the Unfireable Safety Kernel with machine-checked guarantees (Z3 SMT, bounded-model-checking via Kani) and demonstrates strong defense against a range of self-modification and authorization-bypass tests, outperforming three contemporaneous control-plane claims.
AI.motivation: Address escapable AI systems in which agent runtime controls can be manipulated; propose execution-time alignment as complement to training- and inference-time alignment; seek robust, verifiable safety guarantees outside the agent's trust boundary.
AI.method: Propose four properties for authorization; implement Unfireable Safety Kernel in Rust; prove fail-closed invariant via SMT and BMC; ensure byte-equivalence between Python and Rust; evaluate against self-modification attempts and authorization round-trips; compare with three other systems.
AI.result: Kernel refuses all safety-critical modifications in 1,000 self-modifications across 704 attempts; operator kill-switch does not bypass; 6,240 authorization round-trips with no bypass; three other systems' control planes are unable to grant control (i.e., the kernel prevents them).
AI.conclusion: Execution-time, architectural control is a necessary complement to training/inference alignment; the kernel demonstrates practical feasibility and rigorous verification; further work needed on integration, performance, and broader threat models.

title: Invoice Haystack: Benchmarking Document Retrieval and Visual Question Answering Under Strong Visual Homogeneity
summary: Vision Language Models have achieved near-human performance on single-document Visual Question Answering, yet their effectiveness degrades significantly when retrieving information from large collections of visually homogeneous documents. Existing multi-document benchmarks aggregate diverse document types, creating artificial separation in embedding space that does not reflect enterprise document repositories where thousands of records share identical visual templates. We identify this as embedding collapse and introduce Invoice Haystack, a benchmark with 1,500 anonymized invoice images paired with 200 discriminative question-answer pairs, specifically designed to stress-test retrieval under strong visual homogeneity. Invoice Haystack exhibits a mean pairwise cosine similarity of 0.73, compared to 0.38 (DocHaystack) and 0.31 (InfoHaystack) in existing benchmarks, posing a fundamentally more challenging retrieval problem. Addressing the identified challenge, we propose VL-RAG, a hybrid retrieval-augmented generation framework that jointly leverages text and visual embeddings to harness the complementary strengths of both modalities, followed by a VLM-based verification filter for precise document identification. VL-RAG achieves 60.0\% Recall@1 on Invoice Haystack-500, outperforming existing state-of-the-art method by up to an absolute 13.5 percentage points. It further improves retrieval considerably on DocHaystack-1000 (77.1\% vs.\ 75.2\%) and InfoHaystack-1000 (84.5\% vs.\ 80.0\%), establishing the proposed dual-stream fusion as a consistently superior retrieval strategy across both homogeneous and heterogeneous document collections.

AI.tldr: Introduces Invoice Haystack, a dataset to stress-test VLM retrieval under extreme visual homogeneity, and VL-RAG, a dual-stream retrieval-augmented generation framework that fuses text and visual embeddings with a VLM verification step, achieving strong Recall@1 gains across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models struggle to retrieve relevant documents from large collections of visually identical templates (embedding collapse). Existing multi-document benchmarks mix heterogeneous types, not reflecting enterprise repositories; there is a need for a challenging, homogeneous benchmark and a retrieval method that leverages both textual and visual cues.
AI.method: Invoice Haystack: 1,500 anonymized invoice images with 200 discriminative QA pairs to stress retrieval under visual homogeneity; measure mean pairwise cosine similarity to quantify homogeneity. VL-RAG: a hybrid retrieval framework that jointly utilizes textual and visual embeddings for candidate retrieval, followed by a VLM-based verification filter to precisely identify the correct document.
AI.result: VL-RAG achieves 60.0% Recall@1 on Invoice Haystack-500, outperforming state-of-the-art by up to 13.5 percentage points. It also improves retrieval on DocHaystack-1000 (77.1% vs. 75.2%) and InfoHaystack-1000 (84.5% vs. 80.0%), demonstrating the dual-stream fusion’s superiority across homogeneous and heterogeneous collections.
AI.conclusion: Dual-stream fusion of text and visual embeddings, coupled with a verification step, provides a robust retrieval strategy that mitigates embedding collapse and yields consistent gains across diverse document repositories.

title: SAGE-Nav: Leveraging LLM Planning and Alignment Fusion for Hierarchical Scene Graph-Guided Navigation
summary: Object-Goal Navigation (ObjNav) requires embodied agents to autonomously locate specified targets using only egocentric visual observations. Existing monolithic methods struggle with long-horizon reasoning and generalize poorly to novel environments. To address these limitations, we propose SAGE-Nav, a novel hierarchical framework that integrates the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with dynamic scene graphs. Crucially, it decouples asynchronous global semantic planning from the high-frequency reactive control loop. The LLM serves as a global planner, decomposing abstract instructions into a sequence of semantically grounded waypoints. To translate these plans into dense multi-modal guidance, we design a Hierarchical Scene Graph Encoder (HSGE) that leverages relational graph convolutions to produce structure-aware embeddings preserving both semantic and spatial topology. Furthermore, we develop the Goal-aware Alignment-Fusion Network (GAFN) to dynamically fuse real-time perception with these structural priors. Using an adaptive gating mechanism with an explicit inductive bias, GAFN ensures robust visual-topological alignment for the low-level policy. Extensive evaluations in the i-THOR and RoboTHOR environments demonstrate that SAGE-Nav achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering substantial gains in navigation efficiency and zero-shot generalization while maintaining the low control latency required for physical robotic deployment.

AI.tldr: SAGE-Nav introduces a hierarchical framework that fuses LLM-based global planning with a Hierarchical Scene Graph Encoder (HSGE) and a Goal-aware Alignment-Fusion Network (GAFN) for Object-Goal Navigation, achieving state-of-the-art results with robust generalization and low control latency.
AI.motivation: Monolithic end-to-end navigation models struggle with long-horizon reasoning and fail to generalize to novel environments; a decoupled design combining semantic planning and high-frequency control could improve robustness, generalization, and efficiency.
AI.method: Use an LLM as a global planner to decompose abstract instructions into semantically grounded waypoints. A Hierarchical Scene Graph Encoder (HSGE) uses relational graph convolutions to produce structure-aware embeddings that preserve semantic and spatial topology. A Goal-aware Alignment-Fusion Network (GAFN) dynamically fuses real-time perception with structural priors via adaptive gating to align visuals and topology for the low-level policy. Planning is asynchronous from control.
AI.result: Empirically yielding state-of-the-art performance on i-THOR and RoboTHOR, with substantial improvements in navigation efficiency and zero-shot generalization, while maintaining low control latency suitable for real robots.
AI.conclusion: The framework validates that integrating LLM-based global planning with structured visual-topological representations and adaptive fusion produces robust, efficient ObjNav with strong generalization; the decoupled, hierarchical design is a key factor in performance and deployability.

title: Learning Diachronic Representations of Ancient Greek Letterforms
comment: Accepted for publication at the International Conference on Document Analysis and Recognition (ICDAR) 2026
summary: Learning representations that remain robust across centuries of variation in handwriting is a key challenge in diachronic representation learning. Taking one of the longest continuously used writing systems, ancient Greek, as a case study, we introduce three datasets for diachronic representation learning: Hell-Char, a curated training set spanning the 3rd-1st centuries BCE, and two evaluation sets, PaLit-Char (2nd-5th c. CE) and Med-Char (9th-14th c. CE). To address the challenges of symbolic variation, scarce data, and systematic degradation, we propose: a similarity-weighted supervised contrastive loss that biases embeddings using dynamically estimated inter-class similarities, and a lacuna-driven augmentation scheme that simulates realistic manuscript corruptions. Trained with these strategies, both a lightweight CNN and a pretrained ResNet achieve strong recognition performance and produce embeddings that more coherently separate character classes than PCA or generic pretrained models. These embeddings enable clustering, identification of stylistic subgroups, and construction of prototype images that visualize diachronic evolution and transitional letterforms. Our results demonstrate that respecting intrinsic inter-letter relationships and augmenting with domain-informed corruptions yield robust, interpretable representations, offering a transferable paradigm for representation learning under scarce, temporally evolving, and noisy conditions. Code and data available at: https://github.com/ipavlopoulos/diachronic-greek-letterforms.

AI.tldr: Introduces diachronic Greek letterform datasets and robust embeddings using a similarity-weighted contrastive loss and lacuna-driven augmentations, yielding interpretable representations for scarce, evolving, noisy handwriting data.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of learning robust representations across centuries of handwriting variation in ancient Greek, where data are scarce and severely degraded.
AI.method: Datasets: Hell-Char (3rd–1st c BCE), PaLit-Char (2nd–5th c CE), Med-Char (9th–14th c CE). Methods: similarity-weighted supervised contrastive loss with dynamically estimated inter-class similarities; lacuna-driven augmentation simulating manuscript corruption; models include a lightweight CNN and a pretrained ResNet; embeddings used for clustering and visualization of diachronic transitions.
AI.result: Embeddings achieve strong recognition performance and clearer class separation than PCA or generic pretrained models; enable clustering, detection of stylistic subgroups, and prototype images to visualize diachronic evolution.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating intrinsic inter-letter relationships and domain-informed corruptions yields robust, interpretable, and transferable representations for scarce, temporally evolving, and noisy data.

title: Follow Your Track: Precise Skeleton Animation Controlled by 3D Trajectories
summary: 4D generation aims to animate 3D objects with realistic motion, holding great promise for applications. Existing methods typically decouple 3D asset generation from motion synthesis: acquire a 3D asset, prepare a structural representation like mesh and Gaussians, and synthesize motion from text or video control signals. However, dense mesh and Gaussian representations incur high computational costs and are prone to temporal artifacts, limiting animation quality and duration to only short clips. Meanwhile, text lacks fine-grained spatial and temporal details such as timing and coordination, while video entangles motion with appearance and background. Together, these limitations result in 4D animations that suffer from poor temporal consistency, wrong identification, and limited controllability. We address these issues with \texttt{ACT}, a trajectory-conditioned framework for topology-general skeletal animation. ACT uses skeletons as a compact structured and compute-efficient representation and 3D point trajectories from monocular video as explicit motion guidance which provide detailed motion patterns without appearance entanglement. At the core of ACT is a Routed Trajectory Injector, which achieves accurate and robust trajectory-to-joint transfer through three complementary designs: prior-guided hard routing establishes precise skeleton-to-mesh correspondences, global routing enables holistic joint-track interaction for full-body motion awareness, and local windowed cross-attention enforces fine-grained temporal alignment, improving micro-timing and reducing motion misalignment across varying motion rates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \texttt{ACT} significantly outperforms existing methods in fidelity and temporal consistency.

AI.tldr: ACT is a trajectory-conditioned skeletal animation framework that uses skeletons and monocular video trajectories to achieve high-fidelity, temporally consistent 4D animation with efficient compute through a Routed Trajectory Injector.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of dense mesh/Gaussian representations (high cost, temporal artifacts) and the lack of fine-grained control in text, plus appearance entanglement in video, by introducing a compact, topology-general skeletal representation guided by explicit motion trajectories.
AI.method: Proposes ACT, a trajectory-conditioned framework for topology-general skeletal animation. Core component is the Routed Trajectory Injector, which uses (1) prior-guided hard routing for precise skeleton-mesh correspondence, (2) global routing for holistic joint-track interaction, and (3) local windowed cross-attention for fine-grained temporal alignment of motion.
AI.result: Experiments show ACT significantly outperforms existing methods in fidelity and temporal consistency.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, motion-aware skeletal animation approach that decouples motion guidance from appearance, achieving accurate, temporally consistent 4D animations across object categories and motion rates.

title: AISPO: Enhancing Depth Reliability for Robotic Manipulation of Non-Lambertian Objects via Affine-Invariant Shape Prior
comment: Published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters. 8 pages. Accepted April 2026
summary: Reliable depth perception is critical for robotic manipulation, especially for non-Lambertian objects such as transparent or highly specular surfaces, where raw depth measurements are often corrupted or missing. These failures frequently propagate to motion planning, resulting in invalid grasp poses and execution errors. We propose AISPO, a depth completion framework that improves depth reliability for manipulation in challenging sensing conditions.
