AISPO combines multi-scale RGB-D feature fusion with an affine-invariant shape prior to enforce geometric consistency and mitigate catastrophic depth failures. Unlike methods that focus primarily on average depth accuracy, our approach emphasizes physical plausibility and structural integrity of the predicted depth maps. Extensive benchmark evaluations demonstrate competitive performance and strong generalization to unseen objects and novel scenes. Real-world grasping experiments further show that enhanced depth reliability significantly improves manipulation success rates, particularly for transparent objects where many existing methods fail to produce physically usable depth estimates.

AI.tldr: AISPO merges multi-scale RGB-D fusion with an affine-invariant shape prior to produce depth maps that are geometrically consistent and physically plausible, improving depth reliability for manipulation on challenging surfaces (e.g., transparent/specular) beyond average-depth accuracy.
AI.motivation: Depth sensing for robotic manipulation is unreliable for non-Lambertian objects, causing planning and grasp failures. A depth completion method should prioritize physical plausibility and structural integrity over mere average depth error.
AI.method: Multi-scale RGB-D feature fusion paired with an affine-invariant shape prior to enforce geometric consistency and mitigate catastrophic depth failures, focusing on the structural integrity of depth maps.
AI.result: Benchmark evaluations show competitive depth completion performance with strong generalization to unseen objects and scenes. Real-world grasping experiments demonstrate that improved depth reliability boosts manipulation success, notably for transparent objects where many methods fail to yield usable depth.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multi-scale feature fusion with a geometric (affine-invariant) prior enhances depth reliability and physical plausibility, enabling more robust manipulation under challenging sensing conditions.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Personalization with Foundation Models for Wearable Stress Detection
summary: Personalization in wearable-based stress detection remains challenging due to substantial inter-individual variability in physiological and behavioral responses. While traditional approaches rely on user-specific fine-tuning or costly self-supervised pre-training on large datasets, we propose a lightweight alternative based on retrieval-augmented personalization. Our method leverages frozen, out-of-domain foundation models to retrieve similar patterns from a target user's history and encode them into a compact personalized embedding that modulates representations extracted by a lightweight transformer network. We evaluate our approach on the WESAD stress detection dataset with N=15 users, comprising wrist-worn physiological (EDA, BVP, temperature) and activity (accelerometer) signals, and report gains of +3.92\% in accuracy and +4.76\% in macro F1-score over a non-personalized transformer baseline, approaching supervised fine-tuning performance without requiring any labeled user data. We further show that temporal retrieval, where only prior user samples are available, achieves performance close to full intra-user retrieval, demonstrating robustness to limited user history. Finally, we explore personalization in a cross-dataset retrieval setting, leveraging embeddings from the K-Emocon dataset to personalize representations for stress detection on the WESAD dataset.

AI.tldr: Retrieval-augmented personalization for wearable stress detection using frozen foundation models to fetch intra-user historical patterns, creating a personalized embedding that modulates a lightweight transformer. Shows accuracy and macro F1 gains on WESAD (N=15) without labeled user data; robust to limited history; demonstrates cross-dataset retrieval (K-Emocon) for WESAD.
AI.motivation: Address substantial inter-individual variability in wearable stress signals and reduce dependence on user-specific fine-tuning or large self-supervised pre-training.
AI.method: Leverage frozen, out-of-domain foundation models to retrieve similar patterns from a target user's history; encode them into a compact personalized embedding that modulates representations from a lightweight transformer network; evaluate with temporal retrieval and cross-dataset retrieval (K-Emocon to WESAD).
AI.result: On WESAD (N=15), gains of +3.92% accuracy and +4.76% macro F1 over a non-personalized transformer baseline; performances approach supervised fine-tuning without labeled user data; temporal retrieval robust even with only prior samples; cross-dataset retrieval demonstrates generalization.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented personalization provides effective, label-free personalization for wearable stress detection, improving generalization and robustness, with potential data-efficiency benefits; limitations include small sample size and cross-dataset transfer considerations.

title: Hypergraph Normal World Models for Logical Visual Anomaly Detection
summary: Visual anomaly detection is often deployed with only normal training images. Most one-class detectors map test patches or features to a normal reference distribution. This works well for local structural defects. Logical anomalies are different. Each visible part may look normal, while the whole image violates a normal count, co-occurrence, or spatial relation. This paper studies whether a model can learn such a category-specific normal world from nominal images alone. We propose the Hypergraph Normal World Model, a normal-only detector that distills frozen DINOv2 patch tokens into patch, relation, and hypergraph statistics. It builds spatial hyperedges over token groups. It then scores each test image with an information quotient that separates local, relational, hyperedge, and hyperedge-relation evidence. On the available MVTec LOCO breakfast-box validation data, the full hypergraph model improves logical anomaly AUROC from 0.8434 for DINOv2 patch-kNN to 0.9279. It also improves over the non-hypergraph variant, from 0.9013 to 0.9279. Few-shot experiments show that the model remains effective with very limited normal images. We also test whether the score reflects normal-world knowledge rather than a shallow mapping. t-SNE separates logical anomalies in the learned energy space. Relation counterfactuals increase the information quotient by 83.13 on average. Random hypergraphs reduce logical AUROC, and hyperedge attribution is much larger on logical anomalies. Qualitative examples show that high scores are driven by relation-bearing terms. These results suggest that logical visual anomaly detection should model normal relations, not only normal local patches.

AI.tldr: Proposes Hypergraph Normal World Model for one-class visual anomaly detection that models normal relations via hypergraphs, distilling DINOv2 patch tokens into patch, relation, and hyperedge statistics; achieves strong improvements on MVTec LOCO breakfast-box data, including few-shot regimes; evidence suggests relation-aware modeling is beneficial for logical anomalies.
AI.motivation: Traditional one-class detectors focus on local patch statistics, which misses logical anomalies where the whole image violates normal relational constraints (counts, co-occurrence, spatial relations). A category-specific normal world must be learned from nominal data.
AI.method: Freeze DINOv2 patch tokens and distill them into a set of statistics: patch-level, relation-level, and hypergraph-based (high-order) statistics. Construct spatial hyperedges over groups of tokens to capture higher-order relations. Compute an information-theoretic score (information quotient) that aggregates local evidence, relational evidence, hyperedge evidence, and hyperedge–relation evidence to detect anomalies.
AI.result: On MVTec LOCO breakfast-box validation data, the full hypergraph model improves logical anomaly AUROC from 0.8434 (DINOv2 patch-kNN) to 0.9279, and from 0.9013 (non-hypergraph variant) to 0.9279. Few-shot experiments show robustness with limited normal data. t-SNE indicates energy-space separation of logical anomalies; relation counterfactuals boost information quotient by 83.13 on average. Random hypergraphs reduce AUROC, and hyperedge attribution is stronger for logical anomalies.
AI.conclusion: Modeling normal relations—via hypergraphs and relation-aware statistics—improves logical visual anomaly detection beyond patch-only approaches, suggesting normal-world knowledge is essential for detecting category-specific logical violations.

title: GROVE: Grounded Pedestrian Simulation via Natural Language for Interactive Social Robot Navigation
summary: Pedestrian simulation is a critical component for training and deploying social robot navigation approaches, yet it remains a largely rigid system that repeatedly requires manual data generation to define even simple scenarios. We propose GROVE, a text-to-scenario pedestrian simulation framework that combines state-of-the-art approaches to produce realistic, socially challenging scenarios for social robot navigation. Our framework allows users to customize one of several common presets (emergency, queuing, normal) or even enter a fully independent prompt to generate a highly customizable pedestrian simulation. Multiple modules separately ensure the realism and soundness of long-horizon human behavior, medium-horizon pedestrian navigation, and short-horizon robot/social interactions. Each module is tuned by the prompt in a way that reflects the user intent across all aspects of pedestrian simulation. By dynamically selecting one of several state-of-the-art (SotA) approaches in our modules based on the scenario, we capture many situational nuances of pedestrian behavior in order to narrow the simulation-to-real (sim2real) gap. The human simulation is directly integrated into Isaac Sim, Gazebo, and RViz simulators for robot deployment in highly social environments. We validate our approach through qualitative comparison against existing pedestrian simulation baselines across scenarios of varying complexity in residential, hospital, and office environments. The result is a high-fidelity pedestrian simulation that challenges social robot navigation with complex, diverse, realistic human behaviors.

AI.tldr: GROVE is a text-to-scenario pedestrian simulation framework that generates realistic, socially challenging pedestrian scenarios for social robot navigation using modular long-/mid-/short-horizon components, multiple presets or prompts, dynamic SotA-method selection, and integration with Isaac Sim, Gazebo, and RViz, validated qualitatively across residential, hospital, and office settings.
AI.motivation: Pedestrian simulation is essential for social robot navigation but existing systems are rigid, manual, and limited in diversity. A flexible, high-fidelity, prompt-driven framework is needed to cover varied scenarios and reduce sim2real gaps, with modular behavior across time horizons and simulator integration.
AI.method: GROVE comprises three modules for long-horizon human behavior, medium-horizon pedestrian navigation, and short-horizon robot/social interactions. It dynamically selects among state-of-the-art approaches per scenario, supports presets (emergency, queuing, normal) or full prompts, and integrates into Isaac Sim, Gazebo, and RViz for deployment. Validation is performed via qualitative comparisons against baselines across residential, hospital, and office environments.
AI.result: Qualitative validation shows GROVE produces high-fidelity, diverse, and realistic pedestrian behaviors that challenge social robot navigation more effectively than existing baselines, across increasingly complex environments.
AI.conclusion: GROVE offers a flexible, high-fidelity, prompt-driven pedestrian simulation framework that narrows the sim2real gap and integrates with major simulators, enabling robust evaluation and deployment of social robot navigation in varied real-world-inspired settings.

title: When Multi-Sensor Fusion Fails to Generalize: Cattle Posture Classification Under Animal-Level and Temporal Distribution Shift
summary: Automated cattle posture-classification systems frequently report near-perfect accuracy, yet their robustness under realistic deployment conditions remains largely unknown. In particular, it is unclear whether multimodal sensor fusion improves generalisation or leads models to rely on context-specific signals that fail under distribution shift. Here, we evaluate the robustness of automated posture classification (lying versus standing) using collar accelerometers, rumen-bolus sensors, and environmental measurements collected from a pasture-based beef cattle herd across two consecutive years (2024-2025). XGBoost served as the primary model, with Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and Long Short-Term Memory networks evaluated as comparative baselines. Model robustness was assessed under progressively more stringent evaluation protocols, ranging from conventional random train-test splits to leave-one-animal-out validation and cross-year evaluation on an independent cohort of previously unseen animals recorded one year later. While multimodal models achieved strong within-year performance (macro-F1 0.94), the performance declined substantially under cross-year evaluation (macro-F1 0.49). Explainability analysis revealed persistent reliance on rumen-bolus activity and environmental variables even when predictive performance deteriorated. Distribution-shift diagnostics further confirmed substantial differences in feature distributions between recording years. Our findings demonstrate that commonly used evaluation protocols can substantially overestimate real-world performance and that multimodal sensor fusion may reduce, rather than improve, robustness under temporal distribution shift. More broadly, the results highlight that benchmark accuracy alone is insufficient to assess deployment readiness and underscore the need for robustness-centred evaluation in livestock-monitoring research.

AI.tldr: Multimodal sensor fusion for cattle posture classification yields high within-year accuracy but poor cross-year robustness; suggests evaluation protocols inflate real-world performance and that fusion may rely on non-generalizable context signals.
AI.motivation: Assess real-world robustness of automated cattle posture classification under distribution shifts and determine whether multimodal fusion improves generalization or causes reliance on context-specific signals.
AI.method: Longitudinal, multimodal sensor dataset from pasture-based beef cattle (collar accelerometers, rumen-bolus sensors, environmental measurements) across two years (2024-2025). Models: XGBoost (primary), with Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and LSTM baselines. Evaluation via random train-test splits, leave-one-animal-out, and cross-year validation on unseen animals. Explainability analysis and distribution-shift diagnostics conducted.
AI.result: Within-year macro-F1 for multimodal models = 0.94; cross-year macro-F1 drops to 0.49. Explainability shows continued reliance on rumen-bolus and environmental features even as accuracy declines. Distribution-shift diagnostics reveal substantial feature-distribution differences between years. Conclusion: robust evaluation reveals that multimodal fusion may undermine robustness under temporal distribution shift; benchmark accuracy alone overstates deployment readiness.
AI.conclusion: Robustness-centered evaluation is essential for livestock monitoring. Benchmark accuracy should be complemented with cross-year and distribution-shift analyses; multimodal fusion may not universally improve generalization and can introduce reliance on non-generalizable cues. Recommend adopting temporal robustness tests and explainability-focused analyses in deployment-ready assessment.

title: Beyond Visual Forensics: Auditing Multimodal Robustness for Synthetic Medical Image Detection
summary: With the rapid adoption of generative AI, synthetic medical images pose growing risks, including diagnostic deception and insurance fraud. Although prior work has explored vision-language model (VLM)-based synthetic image detection, these evaluations typically consider images in isolation. In clinical practice, however, images are interpreted alongside structured records and metadata, and VLMs are increasingly deployed under joint image-record inputs. We uncover a previously underexamined multimodal vulnerability: when given both modalities, VLMs may overweight record context in authenticity judgments, such that the same image receives different predictions solely due to changes in its accompanying text. This raises concerns about robustness in real-world deployment. To systematically characterize this effect, we reformulate synthetic medical image detection as an audit of multimodal robustness at the image-record interface and introduce a paired benchmark that holds the image fixed while swapping controlled metadata variants. Across multiple imaging modalities, we evaluate diverse open-weight and frontier API VLMs and quantify how metadata alone shifts authenticity predictions. Our benchmark provides a standardized tool for assessing and improving multimodal robustness beyond image-only settings. The code is available at https://github.com/chiuhaohao/Beyond-Visual-Forensics.

AI.tldr: Multimodal VLMs used to detect synthetic medical images can be biased by accompanying records; a paired image-record benchmark reveals robustness issues at the image-record interface, with metadata alone shifting authenticity judgments; code is released for standardized evaluation.
AI.motivation: Clinical practice often interprets images alongside structured records and metadata. Prior work largely evaluated image-only detection and may overlook how records influence authenticity judgments when VLMs are used in real-world, multimodal settings.
AI.method: Introduce a paired benchmark that fixes the image while swapping controlled metadata variants (image-record interface audit). Evaluate multiple imaging modalities with diverse open-weight and frontier API VLMs, quantifying how metadata alone shifts authenticity predictions.
AI.result: Metadata variants can meaningfully shift VLM authenticity predictions, revealing a previously underexamined multimodal vulnerability. The benchmark provides a standardized tool to assess and improve multimodal robustness beyond image-only evaluations; code is available at the referenced repository.
AI.conclusion: Robust synthetic medical image detection requires auditing at the image-record interface. The proposed benchmark enables systematic evaluation across modalities and models, guiding methods to mitigate metadata-driven biases in multimodal detection systems.

title: ASSCG: Just-Right Gating over Chattering for Fast-Slow LLM Planning in Autonomous Driving
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can improve autonomous driving planning but are costly to query online, and existing fast-slow planners often rely on hand-designed triggering rules that either over-call the slow system or call it at the wrong times. We formulate slow-system invocation as a resource-aware sequential decision problem and propose the Adaptive Slow-System Control Gate (ASSCG), which makes frame-level Query/Cache/Drop decisions to refresh, reuse, or suppress slow guidance. ASSCG uses an RWKV backbone for efficient long-horizon gating and is trained with supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO-style compute-aware reinforcement fine-tuning. We apply ASSCG to two different fast-slow architectures: (i) AsyncDriver on nuPlan Hard20 closed-loop evaluation, where ASSCG improves score to 67.28 (+2.28) while reducing average end-to-end inference latency by 60%; and (ii) a RecogDrive-based dual system that we build by replacing its original VLM-2B module with a lightweight ViT-based fast planner and adding an LLM slow planner, evaluated on NAVSIM, where ASSCG achieves 91.4 PDMS (+0.6) and increases average speed by 25%. The project page, including video visualizations and additional results, is available at https://williamxuanyu.github.io/asscg/.

AI.tldr: ASSCG is a resource-aware gating module that reduces slow-system queries in LLM-enhanced autonomous driving by making frame-level Query/Cache/Drop decisions; it improves planning performance and lowers latency across two architectures.
AI.motivation: LLMs offer planning improvements but incur high online query cost; traditional fast-slow planners use hand-designed triggers that misallocate slow guidance. A principled, compute-aware controller is needed to manage when to query, reuse, or drop slow guidance.
AI.method: Introduce Adaptive Slow-System Control Gate (ASSCG) with frame-level Query/Cache/Drop decisions using an RWKV backbone for long-horizon gating. Train via supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO-style compute-aware reinforcement fine-tuning. Evaluate on two architectures: (i) AsyncDriver on nuPlan Hard20; (ii) RecogDrive-based dual system with a lightweight ViT fast planner and an LLM slow planner on NAVSIM.
AI.result: On AsyncDriver nuPlan Hard20, ASSCG raises score to 67.28 (+2.28) and cuts average end-to-end latency by 60%. On NAVSIM with RecogDrive, ASSCG yields 91.4 PDMS (+0.6) and 25% faster average speed.
AI.conclusion: ASSCG provides effective resource-aware slow-system invocation that improves planning performance while reducing compute. It generalizes across different fast-slow architectures and demonstrates practical gains in both score and latency, with a public project page for visualization and additional results.

title: Low-Cost High-Order Singular Value Decomposition for Tensor-Based Reconstruction from Sparse Sensor Measurements: Urban Flow and Air-Quality Applications
summary: Urban flow and air-quality simulations generate high-dimensional datasets describing velocity and pollutant transport across multiple spatial, temporal, and physical-variable dimensions. Reconstructing these fields from sparse sensor measurements is a fundamental challenge in environmental monitoring, digital twins, forecasting, and data assimilation. Existing low-cost reconstruction approaches are commonly based on matrix decompositions, which require multidimensional datasets to be flattened into two-dimensional snapshot matrices, thereby discarding important structural information. This work introduces the low-cost High-Order Singular Value Decomposition (lcHOSVD), a novel tensor-based sparse-sensing reconstruction framework for high-dimensional environmental fields. To the authors' knowledge, this is the first methodology that combines sparse sensing and HOSVD for field reconstruction. Unlike matrix-based approaches, lcHOSVD preserves the natural tensor structure of the data, enabling the exploitation of correlations across spatial, temporal, and physical-variable dimensions while substantially reducing the computational requirements of conventional HOSVD. The methodology is applied to urban flow and air-quality datasets, where three-dimensional velocity and pollutant concentration fields are reconstructed using only 1-4% of the available spatial locations. While lcSVD provides larger computational speed-ups, lcHOSVD consistently achieves lower reconstruction errors in configurations characterized by strong multidimensional coupling and heterogeneous dynamics across dimensions. Additional sensor-anisotropy analyses demonstrate that the tensor formulation is significantly more robust to uneven sensor distributions, a common situation in practical environmental monitoring networks.

AI.tldr: A tensor-based, low-cost reconstruction framework (lcHOSVD) that uses sparse sensing and high-order SVD to recover high-dimensional environmental fields from sparse measurements, outperforming matrix-based approaches and lcSVD in strongly coupled multidimensional settings.
AI.motivation: Environmental monitoring needs accurate reconstruction of velocity and pollutant fields from sparse sensors without destroying natural multi-way structure; flattening data into matrices loses inter-dimensional correlations and increases computational burden.
AI.method: Develop lcHOSVD, a tensor-based sparse-sensing reconstruction that preserves the data's tensor structure and leverages cross-dimensional correlations via a low-cost HOSVD, reducing computation relative to full HOSVD.
AI.result: Applied to urban flow and air-quality data, reconstructing 3D velocity and pollutant fields from only 1–4% of spatial locations. lcHOSVD yields lower reconstruction errors than matrix-based or SVD approaches in regimes with strong multidimensional coupling; lcSVD offers faster computation but higher error. Tensor formulation also shows robustness to uneven sensor distributions (sensor anisotropy).
AI.conclusion: lcHOSVD provides a robust, efficient approach for high-dimensional environmental-field reconstruction from sparse data, with particular advantages when cross-dimensional dynamics are strongly coupled and sensor layouts are heterogeneous.

title: Transferable Attack against Face Swapping in an Extended Space
summary: Although deep Face Swapping (FS) models may benefit the entertainment industry, they pose severe threats to privacy and security. Existing protections, including deepfake detection and adversarial perturbation, are either passive responses or ineffective to unseen subject-agnostic FS models. In this paper, we propose a transferable attack against subject-agnostic FS models named Additive Identity attack based on a Relighting function (AIR). AIR leverages reillumination and additive perturbations to mislead the identity extraction modules in subject-agnostic FS models. By using these two types of perturbations simultaneously, the attack space is extended such that stronger but more visually natural adversarial examples can be identified. To further enhance the visual quality while preserving the effectiveness of the attack, an adaptive translation-invariant operation and an illumination control scheme are designed for AIR. Unlike other methods, AIR does not require a surrogate FS model to achieve high transferability. In addition, a mathematical proof is given for the extension of the attack space. Extensive experiments using 1000 image pairs across various state-of-the-art subject-agnostic FS models, including GAN and diffusion-based FS models, show that AIR surpasses all existing attacks in terms of both attack success rate and image quality.

AI.tldr: AIR is a transferable, subject-agnostic deepfake attack that combines relighting-based reillumination with additive perturbations to mislead identity extraction. It uses adaptive translation-invariant processing and illumination control, does not require a surrogate FS model, and is mathematically grounded. Empirically, AIR achieves state-of-the-art attack success and image quality across GAN and diffusion-based FS models on 1000 image pairs.
AI.motivation: Privacy and security risks from deep face swapping; existing defenses (detectors/adversarial perturbations) are passive or fail against unseen subject-agnostic FS models. A transferable, high-quality attack is needed to evaluate and stress-test defenses and to understand vulnerabilities in FS pipelines.
AI.method: Dual perturbation strategy: (1) relighting-based reillumination and (2) additive perturbations to perturb identity features. Adds adaptive translation-invariant operations and an illumination control scheme to improve visual quality while maintaining effectiveness. No surrogate FS model is required for transferability; includes a mathematical proof extending the attack space.
AI.result: AIR achieves higher attack success rates and better image quality than existing attacks across state-of-the-art subject-agnostic FS models, including GAN- and diffusion-based approaches, validated on 1000 image pairs.
AI.conclusion: AIR provides a robust, transferable adversarial mechanism against subject-agnostic face-swapping models, expanding the attack space with illumination-aware perturbations, and highlighting potential weaknesses in current defenses while transferring without a surrogate model.

title: One Body, Two Minds: Variable Autonomy Approach for a Co-embodied Robotic Hand
summary:
Assistive robotic systems face a fundamental trade-off: fully autonomous systems lack user agency, while fully user-controlled systems demand continuous cognitive effort. Existing shared autonomy approaches blend human and robot commands but are mostly deployed in separate physical bodies. We introduce co-embodiment with variable autonomy, where human and robot share a single physical body and operate at different autonomy levels across task phases, from mutual autonomy during object search and grasping to human-dominant control during actuation.
  We present a co-embodied, wearable robotic hand that has its own ``mind'' and operates with variable autonomy levels. A learning-from-demonstration visuomotor diffusion policy enables autonomous grasping when the user positions the hand near known objects. Once grasped, the system signals completion and the human can actuate the grasped tool (drill, spray bottle, infrared thermometer, lighter, and ice-cream scoop) via hands-free head gestures. The human retains veto authority at all times through a release gesture that returns the system to the initial phase. Unlike blended autonomy, where control is continuously negotiated, our co-embodied approach consists of variable autonomy from full human control to full independent actions while maintaining physical coupling, realizing a one body, two minds paradigm.
  In a user study with 44 participants performing five bimanual tasks, users rapidly adapted to this ``two minds'' paradigm: completion times improved by 23.3% across trials ($p < 0.001$, Cohen's $d = 0.94$), the best-performing policy variant reached a 93.6% task success rate, and acceptance ratings were high (5.70/7 overall impression, 5.52/7 daily use willingness). This work establishes co-embodiment with variable autonomy as a viable approach for assistive robotics, enabling human-robot collaboration through co-embodiment.

AI.tldr: Co-embodiment with variable autonomy enables a wearable robotic hand that shares a single body with humans, balancing autonomy and user control across task phases; autonomous grasping with a learning-from-demonstration diffusion policy, human veto via gestures, and a user study showing improved performance and high acceptance, supporting viability of the paradigm.
AI.motivation: To overcome the fundamental trade-off in assistive robotics between full autonomy (low user agency) and full user control (high cognitive load), and to extend shared autonomy into a single-embodiment setup where humans and robots share a body.
AI.method: Develop a co-embodied wearable robotic hand with an autonomous 'mind' and variable autonomy levels. Use a learning-from-demonstration visuomotor diffusion policy for autonomous grasping when the hand is near known objects. Upon grasp, the system signals completion and the human actuates the tool via hands-free head gestures. The human retains veto authority with a release gesture that reverts to the initial phase. The system maintains physical coupling to realize a one body, two minds paradigm. A user study with 44 participants tested five bimanual tasks.
AI.result: Participants rapidly adapted to the two-minds paradigm. Completion times improved by 23.3% across trials (p < 0.001, d = 0.94). The best policy variant achieved 93.6% task success. Acceptance ratings were high (5.70/7 overall; 5.52/7 daily use willingness).
AI.conclusion: Co-embodiment with variable autonomy is a viable approach for assistive robotics, enabling effective human–robot collaboration within a single physical envelope and establishing a one body, two minds paradigm.

title: Uncertainty-aware reinforcement learning for chemical language models
summary:
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a powerful paradigm for de novo molecular design, enabling Chemical Language Models (CLMs) to navigate and explore the chemical space while optimizing specific desired properties. However, the existing RL frameworks treat all scoring functions as deterministic oracles, neglecting the inherent uncertainty attached to the predictions of the different molecular properties. This can lead to the exploration of highly-uncertain regions of the chemical space, focusing on the generation of highly scored molecules which are poorly supported by the training data. This can destabilize the optimization process, yielding predictions that are far from their true values.
  We propose and compare two complementary ways of incorporating predictive uncertainty into RL. In the first one, uncertainty is treated as an additional optimization objective and incorporated along with the rest of the scoring functions, allowing the policy to trade off exploitation against reliability. Secondly, uncertainty is used to modulate policy updates, reducing the influence of molecules whose properties lie far outside the scoring function confidence domain.
  Both approaches were evaluated across three different settings: (i) a controlled model system, in which the prediction error is modeled as a Gaussian distribution, with a variance proportional to the distance to the training data; and two real-world tasks, making use of (ii) ChemProp models and (iii) a Conformal Prediction wrapper applied to a Random forest classifier.
  We show that uncertainty-aware RL enables CLMs to explore chemical space more robustly by favoring lower-uncertainty regions. This leads to more reliable hit discovery without compromising molecular score, increasing the true hit rate by 0.25 (from 0.5 to 0.75), and nearly doubling the total number of true hits.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware reinforcement learning (RL) for chemical language models improves robustness and hit discovery by incorporating predictive uncertainty into the objective and into policy updates; two strategies are proposed and validated across controlled and real-world tasks, yielding higher true hit rates without sacrificing scores.
AI.motivation: Molecular property predictions carry uncertainty; ignoring it in RL for de novo design can drive exploration into uncertain regions, destabilizing optimization and reducing reliability of discovered molecules.
AI.method: Two complementary approaches: (1) treat predictive uncertainty as an additional optimization objective to balance exploitation and reliability; (2) modulate policy updates by down-weighting molecules with properties far outside the model's confidence domain. Evaluations include (i) a Gaussian error model with variance proportional to distance from training data, and real-world setups using (ii) ChemProp models and (iii) a conformal prediction wrapper applied to a random forest classifier.
AI.result: Uncertainty-aware RL steers exploration toward lower-uncertainty regions, improving robustness and reliability. The approach increases true hit rate from 0.5 to 0.75 and nearly doubles the total number of true hits, without compromising molecular scores.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for predictive uncertainty in RL for CLMs enhances robust exploration and hit discovery, yielding more reliable candidates while maintaining scoring performance.

title: Anatomically-conditioned Latent Diffusion Model for Data-Efficient Few-Shot Cross-Domain 3D Glioma MRI Synthesis
summary: Accurate classification of diffuse gliomas is often hindered by domain shifts across centers and a lack of large, annotated datasets. We propose the Anatomically-conditioned Latent Diffusion Model (ALDM), a novel framework for data-efficient, few-shot 3D volumetric MRI synthesis. ALDM utilizes a two-stage approach: a 3D variational autoencoder learns anatomical priors from a data-rich source domain, while a conditional latent diffusion model, guided by tumor masks via a ControlNet, generates structurally coherent volumes for a data-scarce target domain. Evaluated in an extreme few-shot setting with only 16 target images, ALDM outperformed GAN and hybrid baselines, achieving a superior Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of 85.40 and a downstream classification AUC of 0.987. Qualitative results confirm that the model preserves sharp pathology boundaries and cross-modal consistency, with visual fidelity improving progressively during training. By capturing essential diagnostic features, ALDM provides a robust tool for clinical data augmentation in low-resource settings. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/Analytics-Everywhere-Lab/anatomically-conditioned-LDM.

AI.tldr: Two-stage Anatomically-conditioned Latent Diffusion Model (ALDM) synthesizes 3D MRI by learning anatomical priors with a 3D VAE from a data-rich source and then a ControlNet-guided latent diffusion model to generate target-domain volumes from few-shot data.
AI.motivation: Domain shifts across centers and scarcity of large, annotated 3D MRI datasets hinder accurate glioma classification; a data-efficient, anatomically-consistent generative augmentation method is needed for low-resource settings.
AI.method: Stage 1: train a 3D variational autoencoder on a data-rich source domain to learn anatomical priors. Stage 2: train a conditional latent diffusion model guided by tumor masks via a ControlNet to generate structurally coherent volumes for a data-scarce target domain, operating in the VAE latent space for efficiency and enabling cross-modal consistency.
AI.result: In an extreme 16-shot setting, ALDM outperformed GAN and hybrid baselines, achieving a Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of 85.40 and a downstream classification AUC of 0.987; qualitative results show preserved sharp pathology boundaries and improved cross-modal fidelity during training.
AI.conclusion: ALDM provides a robust data augmentation tool for clinical 3D MRI in low-resource settings and is available publicly (GitHub).

title: WOLF-VLA: Whole-Body Humanoid Optimal Locomotion Framework for Vision-Language-Action Learning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently demonstrated strong generalization in robotic manipulation, yet their applicability to whole-body, contact-rich humanoid locomotion remains severely underexplored due to data scarcity, the absence of dynamically consistent demonstrations, and the difficulty of encoding optimality and safety in learning-based pipelines. This work introduces a unified framework WOLF-VLA that integrates whole-body optimal-control (OC) motion synthesis with large-scale multi-modal dataset to train VLAs capable of generating humanoid locomotion policies directly from natural-language instructions. We construct a comprehensive dataset of dynamically feasible humanoid trajectories across six locomotion-related task families, each parameterized by environmental variations, object colors, placements, and visual distractors. We train a VLA model using the collected joint trajectories, ego-centric visual observations and natural language instruction, yielding a policy that exhibits strong reasoning and robustness to initial-condition variability, and competitive performance across several tasks and environment settings. A systematic ablation study demonstrates the impact of each modality on the model performance. The full dataset, model checkpoints, and benchmarking simulation suite will be openly released, establishing a reproducible dynamically consistent benchmark for whole-body humanoid locomotion rich VLA control and enabling future research in scalable transfer of instruction-driven locomotion policies.

AI.tldr: WOLF-VLA unifies whole-body optimal-control motion synthesis with large multi-modal data to train vision-language-action models for instruction-driven humanoid locomotion, including a dataset of dynamically feasible trajectories and a benchmarking suite to enable reproducible evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity, lack of dynamically consistent demonstrations, and the challenge of encoding optimality and safety in learning-based whole-body locomotion from natural-language instructions.
AI.method: Integrates whole-body optimal-control motion synthesis with a large multi-modal dataset to train a VLA model. Constructs a dataset of dynamically feasible humanoid trajectories across six locomotion task families, with environmental variations and visual distractors, and trains the VLA on joint trajectories, ego-centric visuals, and natural-language instructions. Includes a systematic ablation study.
AI.result: The trained VLA policy demonstrates strong reasoning, robustness to initial-condition variability, and competitive performance across tasks and environments. An ablation study quantifies the contribution of each modality.
AI.conclusion: Open release of the full dataset, model checkpoints, and benchmarking simulation suite to provide a reproducible, dynamically consistent benchmark for whole-body humanoid locomotion with VLA control, enabling future research on scalable instruction-driven locomotion.

title: The Geometry of Sequential Learning: Lie-Bracket Prediction of Transfer Order
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026. 20 pages, including appendices
summary: Sequential learning is order-dependent: from Pile-style next-token domain adaptation to instruction-SFT and DPO, N candidate sources induce N! possible curricula. We show that the local order effect is governed by a computable geometric quantity, the Lie-bracket commutator of gradient update fields, yielding a pairwise score for whether A->B or B->A is better for a target domain. The pairwise bracket primitive also defines a Lie-Bracket Tournament: with a shared theta_0 target-gradient reference, Hessian symmetry gives Borda/row-sum scores from one Hessian-vector product per source, O(N) dot products, and an O(N log N) sort, without materializing the O(N^2) edge matrix. Empirically, the planner reaches 98.1%/98.9% pairwise accuracy at k=1 for instruction-SFT/DPO, remains at 73.1%/72.2% at k=20, and preserves the original pretraining-domain evidence with 82.4-92.0% accuracy across four LLMs and 91.1% on diffusion. At curriculum scale, it recovers the best of all 3! schedules in 87.5% of trials, ranks 85 Stack programming-language source domains for a Python target in the 99th sampled percentile, and reaches the 99.0-99.6th sampled percentile on 56 MMLU subjects, sharply above the reported descending gradient-norm baseline. These results reframe sequential learning as a geometric tournament problem: commutators provide both local pairwise order information and a scalable primitive for many-domain schedules.

AI.tldr: A geometric, Lie-bracket-based framework for sequencing multiple curricula in sequential learning that achieves scalable, near-optimal scheduling via pairwise comparisons and a tournament-style aggregation.
AI.motivation: To overcome the combinatorial explosion of possible curricula orders in sequential learning (N sources -> N! orders) by deriving a computable, local ordering criterion from gradient-field Lie brackets, enabling scalable, near-optimal scheduling across many sources and domains.
AI.method: Define gradient-update fields for each source and compute the Lie-bracket commutator to obtain a local pairwise score between curricula A and B (A->B vs B->A). Introduce Lie-Bracket Tournament using a shared theta_0 gradient reference and Hessian symmetry to convert pairwise brackets into Borda-like scores with only one Hessian-vector product per source, yielding O(N log N) sorting without forming an O(N^2) edge matrix. Empirically validate across instruction-SFT, DPO, LLMs, diffusion, and programming-language/source-domain tasks.
AI.result: The planner achieves high pairwise accuracy (98.1%/98.9% at k=1 for instruction-SFT/DPO; ~73% at k=20), preserves pretraining-domain evidence across four LLMs and 91.1% on diffusion, and, at scale, recovers the best of all 3! schedules in 87.5% of trials. It ranks 85 Stack domains for Python targets in the 99th percentile and attains the 99.0–99.6th percentile on 56 MMLU subjects, outperforming the descending gradient-norm baseline.
AI.conclusion: Sequential learning can be reframed as a geometric tournament problem where Lie-bracket commutators provide both local pairwise order information and a scalable primitive for assembling effective curricula across numerous sources and domains.

title: Teach-to-Reason: Competition-Guided Reasoning with a Self-Improving Teacher
summary: Chest X-ray visual question answering (CXR VQA) requires models not only to predict correct answers, but also to produce reliable medical reasoning. However, existing reinforcement-learning-based training typically relies on answer-level rewards, which are often too coarse to improve chain-of-thought (CoT) quality and can become ineffective when group-level advantages collapse to zero. We propose \textbf{Teach-to-Reason (T2R)}, a framework that introduces comparison-based supervision into CoT optimization through a self-improving \emph{Teacher} and a competition-guided \emph{Reasoner}. As the Teacher is iteratively strengthened via self-competition, the Reasoner is optimized against progressively stronger Teacher-generated references. We further introduce a case-wise reward design that preserves the original reward-induced positive/negative partition when it is informative, and restores supervision from competition scores when the original reward signal degenerates. Experiments on multiple CXR open-ended VQA benchmarks show that T2R consistently outperforms strong baselines, indicating that comparison-based supervision, when integrated in a controlled and principled manner, provides a more effective training signal for reasoning optimization.

AI.tldr: Proposes Teach-to-Reason (T2R) for Chest X-ray VQA, using comparison-based supervision between a self-improving Teacher and a competition-driven Reasoner to enhance chain-of-thought reasoning and achieve stronger performance than baselines.
AI.motivation: CoT optimization in medical VQA is hampered by coarse answer-level rewards and potential collapse of group-level advantages; a more fine-grained, comparison-based supervision signal is needed to guide reasoning quality.
AI.method: Introduce a Teacher and a Reasoner. The Teacher is strengthened via self-competition; the Reasoner learns by optimizing against progressively stronger Teacher-generated references. A case-wise reward design preserves informative reward structure and, when rewards degenerate, supplements supervision with competition scores.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple open-ended CXR VQA benchmarks shows that T2R consistently outperforms strong baselines, indicating that comparison-based supervision can effectively train reasoning in medical VQA.
AI.conclusion: Carefully integrated comparison-based supervision can provide a more effective training signal for reasoning optimization in medical VQA, particularly through a self-improving Teacher and a competition-guided Reasoner.

title: 1000 Rallies: An Event-Camera Dataset and Real-Time Learned Ball-State Estimation for Robotic Table Tennis
summary: Robotic table tennis has emerged as a compelling benchmark for real-time robotic perception due to its fast ball dynamics and stringent timing requirements. Accurate, high-frequency, and low-latency ball state estimation is critical for reliable trajectory prediction and timely control. Traditional frame-based cameras face an inherent trade-off: low frame rates leave temporal blind spots that miss fast-moving objects and high frame rates raise data and computational cost. Event cameras instead offer microsecond temporal resolution and, under sufficient illumination, remain largely free of motion blur even at high ball speeds. However, the community lacks large-scale datasets to develop and benchmark event-based perception in realistic sports scenarios. We address this gap by introducing the first large-scale event-camera dataset for table tennis, comprising over 1000 rallies from a diverse group of players ranging from amateurs to elite-level athletes. Each recording captures the event stream alongside 14 synchronized high-speed frame-based cameras at 200 FPS, which we use to produce 1 kHz pseudo ground-truth labels for ball position, velocity, and spin. Building on this dataset, we train a convolutional neural network robust to background player motion that jointly estimates the ball's position and velocity in the image-plane from events. Treating the predicted velocity as an additional measurement in the Kalman filter reduces bounce-point prediction error by 36% relative to a position-only baseline. Finally, we close the perception-action loop by integrating the event-based system with a Stäubli robotic arm, enabling the first real-time human-robot table tennis rallies driven by event-based perception.

AI.tldr: First large-scale event-camera dataset for table tennis; 1000+ rallies; 1 kHz pseudo ground-truth for ball state; CNN estimates ball position and velocity from events; Kalman filter using velocity improves bounce-point prediction; real-time human-robot rallies with an arm.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between frame-rate and latency in traditional cameras for fast ball dynamics; leverage event cameras' microsecond temporal resolution and low motion blur; fill the lack of large, realistic sport datasets for event-based perception.
AI.method: Collect a large-scale asynchronous-event dataset of table-tennis rallies with 14 synchronized 200 FPS frame cameras to produce 1 kHz pseudo ground-truth labels; train a CNN robust to background motion to estimate ball position and velocity from events; integrate velocity as a measurement in a Kalman filter to improve trajectory prediction; demonstrate real-time closed-loop perception-action by controlling a Stäubli robotic arm for rallies.
AI.result: Bounce-point prediction error reduced by 36% when incorporating velocity measurements in the Kalman filter versus position-only baseline; first demonstration of real-time human-robot table tennis rallies driven by event-based perception; dataset enables benchmarking of event-based perception in fast sports.
AI.conclusion: Established the first large-scale event-camera dataset for table tennis and demonstrated practical benefits of event-based perception for high-speed sports, including improved trajectory estimation and a complete perception-action loop with robotics.

title: ExTra: Exploratory Trajectory Optimization for Language Model Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for language-model reasoning can fail at both extremes of task difficulty: easy prompts often produce all-correct, low-diversity rollout groups with little gradient signal, while hard prompts can produce all-incorrect groups with no positive reward. We introduce ExTra (Exploratory Trajectory Optimization), a GRPO-compatible framework that extracts exploration signals from the model's own rollouts. ExTra combines two mechanisms: (i) a novelty reward that adds embedding-based diversity bonuses after GRPO normalization, rewarding diverse correct solutions; and (ii) entropy-guided prefix regeneration, which scores partial trajectories using entropy signals and continues exploration from promising intermediate steps. Across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ExTra improves Qwen3-1.7B over GRPO by about +5 points on pass@1 and +7 points on pass@16, showing that trajectory-level exploration signals can improve both single-sample accuracy and inference-time coverage.

AI.tldr: ExTra adds novelty and entropy-guided exploration to GRPO-based RLVR for LM reasoning, improving pass@k on six math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: RLVR-GRPO struggles at both easy and hard prompts due to weak gradient signals and lack of diverse successful trajectories; trajectory-level exploration signals from the model's own rollouts can improve coverage and accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes ExTra, a GRPO-compatible framework with two components: (i) a novelty reward—embedding-based diversity bonuses after GRPO normalization to reward diverse correct solutions; (ii) entropy-guided prefix regeneration—scoring partial trajectories with entropy signals to continue exploration from promising intermediate steps.
AI.result: On six mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ExTra yields approximately +5 points on pass@1 and +7 points on pass@16 over GRPO for Qwen3-1.7B, indicating improved single-sample accuracy and inference-time coverage.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-level exploration signals derived from model rollouts can meaningfully improve RLVR-GRPO performance in language-model reasoning tasks.

title: Gastroendoscopy View Synthesis: A New Real Dataset and Evaluation
summary: Novel view synthesis (NVS) is an active research topic in computer vision, owing to the success of neural radiance field (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) methods. While NVS opens the door to potential applications in gastroendoscopy, such as extending the field of view of endoscopic images and enabling digital twins for 3D archiving and endoscopist manipulation training, the dataset is insufficient to evaluate NVS for gastroendoscopy. In this paper, we present the first real gastroscopy dataset for NVS, namely the GastroNVS dataset, which contains a set of gastroscopic images, camera poses, and a point cloud for real gastroendoscopy inspection. To assess the suitability of the GastroNVS dataset, we evaluate several 3DGS methods and discuss the challenges for future development. The dataset is available on request from our project page.

AI.tldr: Presents GastroNVS, the first real gastroscopy NVS dataset with images, poses, and a point cloud; benchmarks 3DGS methods and outlines future challenges.
AI.motivation: NVS in gastroendoscopy is promising for FOV extension and digital twins, but there is a lack of real datasets to evaluate methods on medical endoscopy data.
AI.method: Collect real gastroscopy data including images, camera poses, and a point cloud; assess several 3DGS methods on GastroNVS; discuss challenges and future directions.
AI.result: Introduces GastroNVS and provides initial evaluations of multiple 3DGS methods; dataset is available on request and serves as a benchmarking resource for future NVS work in gastroendoscopy.
AI.conclusion: GastroNVS enables evaluation and development of NVS approaches in gastroendoscopy; highlights key challenges (e.g., dynamic tissue, lighting, viewpoint changes) and the need for standardized benchmarks.

title: Event-Adaptive Motion Planning with Distilled Vision-Language Model in Safety-Critical Situations
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures, 4 tables. Accepted by IROS 2026
summary: Robot navigation in safety-critical scenarios faces significant challenges from unforeseen semantic events, where collisions arise primarily from the unpredictable behaviors of dynamic agents rather than unseen objects. While large vision-language models (VLMs) offer remarkable capabilities in commonsense reasoning, frequently invoking them within the continuous control loop introduces severe computational latency, fundamentally destabilizing physical execution. To address these challenges, we propose event-adaptive motion planning (EAMP), an efficient framework for VLM-based robot navigation. Specifically, a prompt-configurable semantic event trigger (PC-SET) selectively activates semantic intervention by continuously monitoring short temporal clips for behavioral anomalies. Upon triggering, an event-triggered distilled SemNav-VLM, fine-tuned via physically verified semantic distillation, maps detected anomalies into discrete strategy-level decisions. Subsequently, a semantic model predictive control (SMPC) module translates these strategies into dynamic reconfigurations of optimization objectives and geometric references. Extensive experiments in safety-critical logistics scenarios demonstrate that EAMP effectively aligns high-level reasoning with low-level control, significantly improving dynamic safety margins over existing baselines while preserving real-time efficiency.

AI.tldr: An event-adaptive framework (EAMP) integrates vision-language model (VLM) reasoning into robot navigation by triggering semantic intervention only on anomalous events detected in short video clips, using a distilled VLM (SemNav-VLM) and semantic distillation, followed by semantic model predictive control (SMPC) to reconfigure objectives and references, yielding safer, more real-time dynamic navigation in safety-critical settings.
AI.motivation: Dynamic agents cause collisions in safety-critical navigation; conventional VLM integration incurs prohibitive latency in real-time control. A selective, event-driven approach aims to retain benefits of VLM reasoning while preserving control stability and efficiency.
AI.method: 1) PC-SET monitors short temporal clips to detect behavioral anomalies. 2) On trigger, an event-triggered distilled SemNav-VLM maps anomalies to discrete strategy-level decisions, via physically verified semantic distillation. 3) SMPC translates strategies into reconfigurations of optimization objectives and geometric references for the planner/controller.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in safety-critical logistics scenarios show EAMP improves dynamic safety margins and maintains real-time efficiency compared to baselines, by aligning high-level semantic reasoning with low-level control.
AI.conclusion: EAMP effectively bridges high-level semantic reasoning with low-level control in robot navigation under dynamic uncertainties, offering improved safety margins without sacrificing real-time performance.

title: Are Tabular Foundation Models Robust to Realistic Query Distribution Shifts in Microbiome Data?
summary: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) achieve strong performance on microbiome abundance data, yet their robustness under realistic distribution shift remains poorly characterized. We introduce a benchmark that evaluates the robustness of TFMs to biologically inspired perturbations across six gut microbiome datasets spanning four disease contexts. In this in-context learning setting, models receive unperturbed support sets as context and are evaluated on perturbed query samples. To isolate robustness beyond "shortcut" features, we preserve the most discriminative taxa and apply three controlled perturbation strategies: (i) removal of high-abundance (uninformative) taxa, (ii) sparsification via increased zero-inflation, and (iii) zero-imputation via spurious non-zero injections. Our results show that protecting discriminative features is insufficient to guarantee stability under support-query shift: across datasets, all perturbations degrade model performance, with zero-imputation consistently the most harmful, indicating that corrupting global feature structure can break generalization even when key taxa are retained. Sparsification disproportionately affects TFMs relative to a classical random forest baseline, suggesting greater sensitivity to zero-inflation-type shifts. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/UMMISCO/metagenomics-fm/.

AI.tldr: Benchmark shows Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) for microbiome data degrade under biologically inspired perturbations; zero-imputation is the most harmful and TFMs are more sensitive to zero-inflation than random forests.
AI.motivation: Evaluate robustness of TFMs to distribution shifts in microbiome data beyond shortcut features, using biologically plausible perturbations across multiple gut-disease datasets.
AI.method: In-context learning setup with unperturbed support as context and perturbed query samples; apply three controlled perturbations while preserving discriminative taxa: (i) removal of high-abundance uninformative taxa, (ii) increased zero-inflation (sparsification), (iii) zero-imputation via spurious non-zero injections.
AI.result: All perturbations degrade performance across datasets; zero-imputation is consistently most harmful; protecting discriminative features does not guarantee stability; TFMs are more sensitive to zero-inflation perturbations than a baseline random forest; code available at the stated GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: TFMs exhibit fragile generalization under support-query shifts in microbiome abundance data; robust evaluation with biologically inspired perturbations is essential; findings highlight vulnerability to perturbation-type shifts despite preserving key taxa.

title: PRISM: Feed-Forward Single-Image 3D Reconstruction via Geometric Warp-Residual Modeling
summary: Reconstructing 3D scenes from a single image is a fundamental challenge in computer vision, with broad applications in virtual reality, robotics, and content creation. Recent methods achieve outstanding performance by leveraging camera-controlled video diffusion models, but rely on iterative diffusion sampling, which greatly limits their practical deployment. We observe that geometric forward warping alone can cover the majority of a target view directly from the input image, with only a compact residual left for the encoder to correct. Motivated by this observation, we propose PRISM, a feed-forward framework that decomposes multi-view latent prediction into a parameter-free geometric prior and a learned residual correction, with no diffusion sampling required at inference. To enable generalization from purely synthetic training data, we devise a two-stage training strategy combining latents supervised distillation for geometric generalization and perceptual fine-tuning for appearance quality optimization. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that PRISM achieves competitive reconstruction quality compared with diffusion-based methods, while reducing inference time dramatically to only 36 seconds per scene.

AI.tldr: A feed-forward 3D reconstruction from a single image, PRISM, uses a geometric forward-warp prior plus a learned residual; avoids diffusion sampling at inference; trained with two-stage strategy (latent distillation for geometry, perceptual fine-tuning for appearance); achieves competitive results with significantly faster inference (36s per scene).
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational bottleneck of diffusion-based single-view 3D reconstruction and exploit a strong geometric prior to enable fast, generalizable reconstructions.
AI.method: Decompose multi-view latent prediction into a parameter-free geometric prior (forward warping) and a learned residual correction. Replace diffusion sampling with a feed-forward network. Two-stage training: latent supervised distillation to encourage geometric generalization from synthetic data, followed by perceptual fine-tuning for appearance quality.
AI.result: Competitive reconstruction quality on three benchmarks compared to diffusion-based methods, while dramatically reducing inference time to about 36 seconds per scene.
AI.conclusion: PRISM demonstrates that a simple geometric prior augmented with a learned residual can match diffusion-based approaches in single-image 3D reconstruction while enabling practical, fast deployment and better synthetic-to-real generalization.

title: Calousel: Extrinsic Calibration of Non-overlapping Multi-camera Systems from Pure Rotation
summary: Extrinsic calibration of multi-camera systems with non-overlapping FOVs has been a challenging problem in the robotics literature. Conventional target-based methods impose substantial target setup overhead, either deploying large calibration targets or requiring pre-measured multi-target poses. Motion-based approaches instead suffer from drift error, scale ambiguity, and motion degeneracy. Securing both accuracy and usability, we propose a novel calibration method that leverages pure rotational motion, requiring only a single static calibration board. The key idea is to make all cameras sequentially observe the same target under a shared geometric reference, even without overlapping views. To integrate these time-separated observations, we formulate the problem using a latent turntable frame and a 3D error on SE(3) within a global optimization framework. We validate the proposed method on both a controlled camera rig and a full-scale vehicle platform with heterogeneous cameras, and analyze robustness under non-ideal turntable motion. Extensive experiments show that our approach maintains competitive accuracy without specialized precision hardware, proving its strong suitability for realistic on-site deployments. Our code is publicly available here.

AI.tldr: Pure-rotation extrinsic calibration for multi-camera rigs with non-overlapping FOVs using a single static calibration board, via a latent turntable frame and SE(3) optimization over time-separated observations; validated on rig and vehicle, robust to non-ideal turntable motion, code released.
AI.motivation: Calibrating multi-camera systems with non-overlapping Fields of View is challenging: traditional target-based methods require heavy setup; motion-based methods suffer from drift and ambiguity. A method that is accurate, usable on-site, and hardware-light is needed.
AI.method: Cameras observe the same target sequentially under a shared geometric reference during pure rotational motion. Introduces a latent turntable frame and a 3D error on SE(3) within a global optimization framework to align time-separated observations from different cameras.
AI.result: Experiments on a controlled camera rig and a full-scale vehicle platform with heterogeneous cameras show competitive accuracy without specialized precision hardware. The method is robust to non-ideal turntable motion and code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: A practical calibration approach achieves accurate extrinsic calibration for non-overlapping FOV multi-camera systems using pure-rotation motion and a latent reference frame, suitable for on-site deployment without expensive hardware.

title: From Forecasting Leaderboards to Deployment Decisions: A Fail-Closed Certification Protocol
summary: Forecasting leaderboards rank models by predictive quality, but their winners are often read as deployment-ready top-1 advice. That reading can fail when forecasts are passed through a fixed decision interface, such as an alert threshold, a top-k budget, or a switching-cost policy. We study when a forecast-side winner can be certified as deployment-actionable for a specified interface and deployed utility. We introduce a fail-closed certification protocol whose gates are sufficient evidential conditions for a strong claim: a friction-caused, non-tie, statistically supported, and recurrent deployment-side reversal. Traffic-Hourly provides a certified anchor: winners agree at zero friction, but positive switching friction makes the forecast winner deployed-suboptimal. A locked native audit tests overclaiming: across 22 verified candidates and 362 full-grid cells, 155 apparent forecast/deployment winner inversions are blocked before certification. The contribution is not a new forecaster, metric, or universal utility, but a conservative protocol for deciding when forecasting leaderboard winners should be read as deployment-actionable top-1 advice.

AI.tldr: A conservative fail-closed certification protocol decides when forecast leaderboard winners are deployment-actionable for a fixed interface, showing friction can invalidate deployment-winning forecasts; empirical audit blocks inversions; frames deployment-certification as a separate add-on rather than new forecasting methods.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between forecast leaderboard success and deployment utility by providing evidential gates that certify deployment-actionable winners under a specified interface and switching costs.
AI.method: Propose fail-closed certification gates based on friction, non-tie, statistical support, and recurrence of deployment-side reversals. Use Traffic-Hourly as a certified anchor where zero friction aligns winners; perform a locked native audit across 22 verified candidates and 362 full-grid cells to detect and block forecast/deployment inversions before certification.
AI.result: At zero switching friction, winners agree with deployment; with positive switching friction, the forecast winner can be deployed-suboptimal. The audit blocked 155 apparent forecast/deployment inversions across 22 candidates and 362 cells, supporting the protocol’s conservatism and reliability.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a conservative protocol to decide when leaderboard winners should be treated as deployment-actionable top-1 advice for a given interface, rather than proposing new forecasters, metrics, or universal utilities.

title: LinStereo: Linear-Complexity Global Attention for Multi-Scale Iterative Stereo Matching
summary: Existing Vision Foundation Model (VFM)-based iterative stereo pipelines under-exploit three information pathways: multi-scale backbone features are collapsed into single-level correlations, geometric priors remain untapped at initialization, and context propagates only locally. These gaps widen under degraded photometric cues, making underwater scenes a stringent generalization test. To address this, we propose LinStereo, built upon Depth Anything V3, whose core is a Position-Aware Linear Attention (PALA) module that replaces local recurrence with global aggregation at linear cost, propagating reliable estimates from well-matched regions into degraded areas while preserving disparity structure. PALA is made effective by two enabling components: Hierarchical Semantic Cost Volumes (HSCV), which supply scale-aligned correlations from the VFM feature hierarchy, and a Depth Prior Initialization (DPI) that converts monocular depth into a metrically calibrated warm start. LinStereo achieves state-of-the-art-level accuracy on standard benchmarks and strong cross-domain generalization, particularly on underwater scene where severe photometric degradation makes stereo matching particularly challenging, attaining the best overall accuracy with consistent gains 28% lower AbsRel on TartanAir-UW, 26% on SQUID, a real-world underwater dataset).

AI.tldr: LinStereo integrates a Position-Aware Linear Attention (PALA) with Hierarchical Semantic Cost Volumes (HSCV) and Depth Prior Initialization (DPI) on Depth Anything V3, achieving state-of-the-art stereo accuracy and strong cross-domain generalization, notably in underwater scenes, with ~28% AbsRel improvement on TartanAir-UW and ~26% on SQUID.
AI.motivation: VFM-based iterative stereo pipelines under-utilize three information pathways: (1) multi-scale backbone features collapsed into single-level correlations, (2) geometric priors are untapped at initialization, and (3) contextual information propagates only locally. These gaps become critical under degraded photometric cues, making underwater scenes a stringent generalization test.
AI.method: LinStereo adopts a Position-Aware Linear Attention (PALA) module, replacing local recurrence with global aggregation at linear cost to propagate reliable estimates from well-matched regions into degraded areas while preserving disparity structure. It is augmented by Hierarchical Semantic Cost Volumes (HSCV), supplying scale-aligned correlations from the VFM feature hierarchy, and Depth Prior Initialization (DPI), converting monocular depth into a metrically calibrated warm start. The framework is built on Depth Anything V3.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art-level accuracy on standard stereo benchmarks and demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization, particularly in underwater scenes. It attains the best overall accuracy with consistent gains, including 28% lower AbsRel on TartanAir-UW and 26% on SQUID (and a real-world underwater dataset).
AI.conclusion: LinStereo advances vision foundation-model-based stereo by leveraging global linear attention, multi-scale correlations, and depth priors to improve robustness and generalization, especially under challenging underwater photometric degradation.

title: Learning to Adapt: Reptile-D-Learning for Robust and Efficient Control Under Parametric Uncertainty
summary: Learning-based Lyapunov Control (LLC) provides formal stability guarantees for nonlinear systems, but its validity relies heavily on accurate system models. Parameter variations and uncertainties may invalidate stability constraints, leading to costly retraining. Although D-learning can estimate Lyapunov derivatives without relying on explicit dynamics models, it remains limited by single-task dynamics and degrades under large parameter shifts. We propose Reptile-D-learning, a framework that leverages the Reptile meta-learning algorithm to capture shared dynamical structures across systems with different parameters, thereby learning a generalizable Lyapunov network initialization and a high-performance controller. Experiments on multiple nonlinear control systems demonstrate that Reptile-D-learning significantly improves both generalization and rapid adaptation to unseen parameter configurations.

AI.tldr: Meta-learning-based Lyapunov control (Reptile-D-learning) generalizes Lyapunov networks across systems with varying parameters, enabling rapid adaptation and maintaining stability guarantees with fewer retraining efforts.
AI.motivation: Lyapunov-based learning control (LLC) offers stability guarantees but is fragile to parameter variations and model mismatch; D-learning can estimate Lyapunov derivatives without full dynamics but struggles with multi-task generalization and large parameter shifts; there is a need for cross-system generalization and fast adaptation under uncertainty.
AI.method: Integrate Reptile meta-learning with D-learning for Lyapunov derivatives to learn a shared initialization of the Lyapunov network and high-performance controller across systems with different parameters; meta-train across diverse system instances so that fine-tuning on a new parameter requires few gradient steps.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple nonlinear control systems shows significant improvements in generalization to unseen parameter configurations and in rapid adaptation after deployment, with better performance than single-task D-learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: Reptile-based meta-learning yields a transferable Lyapunov network and controller that leverage shared dynamical structure across parameter variations, enabling robust generalization and faster adaptation in LLC under uncertainty.

title: What's in an Earth Embedding? An Explainability Analysis of Location Encoders
summary: Geographic implicit neural representations (INRs) learn to map any coordinate on Earth to a location embedding, implicitly encoding geospatial data into the weights of a neural network. Location embeddings are widely used off the shelf as general-purpose geospatial representations, yet users lack principled tools to audit what geographic or semantic information these embeddings capture. In this work, we analyze the information content of geographic INRs through their location embeddings. We decompose these embeddings into human-interpretable features$\unicode{x2014}$namely, (i) sparse latent concepts, (ii) natural language concepts, and (iii) visual features. The latent concept embeddings are learned using sparse autoencoders. To recover natural language concepts, we apply sparse linear concept embeddings (SpLiCE) over a predefined geospatial dictionary. Finally, visual features are extracted using saliency maps derived from CLIP Surgery. We show that location embeddings can be decomposed into human-interpretable representations while retaining high reconstruction capability, revealing interpretable geographic structures such as forests, deserts, and urban features. Across methods, sparse decompositions expose systematic differences in encoded information, ranging from urban structures to broader biome and climate signals, and pretraining-space saliency maps further highlight complementary features such as roads and landmarks. We hope this work provides a first step toward interpretable geospatial representations.

AI.tldr: Geographic INRs can be decomposed into interpretable location embeddings (sparse latent concepts, natural language concepts via SpLiCE, and visual features via saliency maps) while preserving reconstruction, exposing geography- and climate-related structure and enabling auditability of geospatial representations.
AI.motivation: Users lack principled tools to audit what geographic location embeddings encode. Interpreting the information content of INRs is crucial for trust, fairness, and downstream usability in geospatial tasks.
AI.method: 1) Sparse autoencoders extract sparse latent concept embeddings from location embeddings. 2) SpLiCE (sparse linear concept embeddings) maps location embeddings to natural language concepts using a predefined geospatial dictionary. 3) CLIP Surgery-derived saliency maps extract visual features corresponding to location embeddings. 4) Evaluation shows reconstructions with interpretable components and analysis of encoded information across domains (urban vs biome/climate) and pretraining spaces.
AI.result: Location embeddings decompose into human-interpretable representations with high reconstruction fidelity. Identified geographic structures include forests, deserts, urban features. Sparse decompositions reveal systematic differences (urban, biome, climate signals). Saliency maps highlight roads and landmarks, indicating complementary visual cues. This constitutes an initial step toward interpretable geospatial representations.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that geographic INRs encode meaningful, interpretable geographic and semantic information in their location embeddings without sacrificing reconstruction performance, offering a framework for auditing and interpreting geospatial neural representations and guiding future design of interpretable INRs.

title: C3-Bench: A Context-Aware Change Captioning Benchmark
summary: While Change Captioning systems have garnered substantial attention to respond to our evolving world, their true performance on diverse real-world change contexts remains largely unexplored due to the lack of comprehensive evaluation frameworks. To fill this gap, we propose C3-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Context-aware Change Captioning. C3-Bench features: (1) 4,996 human-labeled image pairs of 51 real-world change contexts across four domains (e.g., natural scenes, remote sensing imagery, image editing, and anomalies), each with diverse, carefully curated scenarios derived from multiple change-centric communities; and (2) the first LLM-as-Judge evaluation framework in the change captioning task that measure fine-grained dimensions (e.g., correctness, specificity, fluency, and relevance), along with a novel reversibility metric exploring whether models understand changes with symmetric consistency. Based on C3-Bench, we benchmark 32 models -- including conventional change captioning models, proprietary Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), and 2B-90B open-source LMMs. We reveal a fundamental blind spot in the prevailing change captioning paradigm: Once the change context departs from training-style regimes, conventional models collapse, and even state-of-the-art LMMs such as GPT-5.2 exhibit systematic domain- and position-dependent errors that distort reliable change understanding. By making these hidden failure modes explicit and measurable, we delineate the next frontier for building generalizable and trustworthy change captioning systems. All codes and datasets are publicly available on the project page.
