On a large-scale dataset (10M users, 1.5% fraud rate) with 85M parameter models, we demonstrate that (1) sequence models significantly outperform gradient-boosted trees operating on aggregated features (0.74 vs. 0.99 AUROC), (2) per-stream encoding is essential -- a single-stream Transformer baseline with matched parameter budget reaches only 0.82 AUROC, an 18-point gap that confirms the multi-stream inductive bias is necessary, (3) time-aware positional encoding achieves the highest discrimination (0.9961 AUROC), (4) gated fusion yields the best precision (0.989) suitable for production deployment, and (5) the risk event stream provides the strongest individual signal contribution. We further validate on proprietary production data from a digital banking platform, showing over 22% relative AUROC improvement over the XGBoost baseline.

AI.tldr: Multi-stream fraud transformer (MSFT) encodes each event stream with its own Transformer and fuses representations via several strategies; time-aware positional encoding performs best; multi-stream encoding outperforms single-stream and GBMs; production data shows meaningful AUROC gains.
AI.motivation: Fraud detection in digital banking requires jointly reasoning over heterogeneous streams (transactions, logins, risk signals). Single-stream or feature-aggregated models miss cross-stream interactions; a systematic ablation across fusion methods clarifies the most effective fusion strategy and encoding schemes at scale.
AI.method: MSFT uses independent Transformer encoders per stream; fusion strategies include concatenation, gated fusion, time-aware positional encoding, cross-stream attention, and a full combination. Evaluated on a large dataset (10M users, 1.5% fraud) with 85M-parameter models; compared against gradient-boosted trees; production validation against XGBoost.
AI.result: GBMs on aggregated features achieve 0.74 AUROC; MSFT achieves 0.99 AUROC (substantial improvement). A single-stream Transformer baseline with same parameter budget reaches 0.82 AUROC (−18 points). Time-aware positional encoding yields 0.9961 AUROC. Gated fusion gives best precision (0.989). Risk event stream is the strongest individual signal. Production data shows ~22% relative AUROC improvement over XGBoost.
AI.conclusion: A multi-stream inductive bias is essential for fraud detection across heterogeneous streams. Per-stream encoding is crucial; time-aware positional encoding provides strongest discrimination; gated fusion balances precision for deployment; risk stream is highly informative; results extend to production, outperforming strong baselines.

title: SAC$^2$-Net: Semantic Anchoring and Complementary-Consensus Fusion for Multimodal Micro-Expression Recognition
summary: Micro-expression recognition (MER) is challenging due to subtle facial movements, limited data, and the ambiguous relationship between Action Units (AUs) and emotion categories. Optical flow and motion magnification have been widely used to describe subtle facial dynamics from different perspectives: the former captures local motion displacement, while the latter amplifies weak appearance changes. In this work, we observe that these two modalities often exhibit asymmetric failure patterns: one modality may become noisy, distorted, or uninformative, while the other still preserves discriminative AU-related evidence. This phenomenon reveals their complementarity, but also raises two key challenges for fusion: cross-modal heterogeneity and spatially varying modality reliability. Motivated by this observation, we propose SAC$^2$-Net, a Semantic Anchoring and Complementary-Consensus Network for multimodal MER, which first aligns visual modalities with semantic anchors and then performs reliability-aware fusion. To reduce cross-modal heterogeneity before fusion, we introduce Semantic Anchoring Soft Alignment (SASA), which converts activated AUs into textual prompts and uses them as stable semantic anchors to align motion-magnified and optical-flow representations. Unlike hard contrastive learning, SASA constructs hierarchical AU-aware soft labels to preserve semantic proximity among samples with overlapping or anatomically related AU patterns. Based on the aligned representations, Complementary-Consensus Fusion (CCF) first repairs unreliable local evidence through complementary exchange and then enforces a shared spatial focus through consensus refinement. Extensive experiments on five MER benchmarks show that SAC$^2$-Net achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across coarse-grained, fine-grained, large-scale, and cross-dataset evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: A multimodal MER framework SAC^2-Net with Semantic Anchoring Soft Alignment (SASA) and Complementary-Consensus Fusion (CCF) that aligns optical flow and motion magnification via AU-driven semantic anchors and performs reliability-aware fusion, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive results on five MER benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Optical flow and motion magnification offer complementary cues for micro-expressions but suffer asymmetric failure modes, creating cross-modal heterogeneity and reliability concerns during fusion. A method that aligns modalities semantically and fuses them reliably is needed.
AI.method: SASA converts activated Action Units (AUs) into textual prompts to create semantic anchors and uses hierarchical AU-aware soft labels to softly align the two modalities, reducing cross-modal heterogeneity. CCF first exchanges complementary evidence to repair unreliable local cues and then enforces a shared spatial focus through consensus refinement.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five micro-expression benchmarks show SAC^2-Net achieving state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across coarse-grained, fine-grained, large-scale, and cross-dataset evaluation settings.
AI.conclusion: SAC^2-Net effectively leverages semantic anchors to align modalities and a reliability-aware fusion mechanism to exploit their complementarity, yielding robust MER performance and strong generalization.

title: DeformGen: Dynamics-Based Topology Augmentation for Deformable Manipulation Policy Learning
summary: Demonstration augmentation is proposed for cost-efficient data acquisition, but existing methods are fundamentally limited in deformable manipulation due to two challenges: (1) the state space is high-dimensional with physics-induced constraints, making valid configurations impossible to reach via low-dimensional pose perturbations; and (2) trajectory transfer is non-equivariant, as material points no longer move rigidly together under deformation. We present DeformGen, a dynamics-based augmentation framework that achieves topological diversity for deformable objects. For the state challenge, DeformGen expands the valid state distribution by applying localized physical disturbances and forward-simulating the dynamics to obtain topology-coherent, physically plausible deformable states. For the trajectory challenge, DeformGen transfers source manipulation trajectories via deformation-field warping, which lifts per-particle displacements into a continuous spatial function to adapt the end-effector trajectory consistently with the deformed geometry. In this way, our method jointly augments the state distribution and its associated manipulation behavior. Experiments on high-fidelity deformable manipulation benchmarks show that DeformGen generally improves policy learning compared with training on the original demonstrations alone and with rigid-style augmentation baselines.

AI.tldr: Dynamics-based augmentation for deformables that expands valid states with localized disturbances and warps trajectories via deformation fields for topology-coherent, physically plausible manipulation.
AI.motivation: Deformable manipulation faces a high-dimensional, constrained state space and non-equivariant trajectory transfer, limiting effective data augmentation for learning policies.
AI.method: Augment state by applying localized physical disturbances and forward-simulating to obtain topology-coherent, plausible deformable configurations; transfer trajectories by deformation-field warping, lifting per-particle displacements into a continuous spatial function to adapt end-effector paths to the deformed geometry.
AI.result: Policy learning improves when trained with DeformGen-demonstration augmentation compared to using original demonstrations alone or rigid augmentation baselines, across high-fidelity deformable manipulation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DeformGen jointly augments the state distribution and the associated manipulation behavior, enabling more data-efficient learning for deformable manipulation.

title: Neural Scaling Universality: If Exponents Are Fixed, Time to Understand Coefficients
summary: Neural scaling laws describe how pre-training loss decays as power laws with training time, model size, and compute. This position paper argues that the exponents of these power laws are fixed by generic mechanisms: a one-third time scaling due to the strong nonlinearity of Softmax, an inverse width scaling due to representational superposition, and an inverse depth scaling due to ensemble averaging of Transformer layers. These mechanisms are robust to a wide range of data structures and architectural details, placing current large language models in a universality class with fixed exponents. The coefficients, however, are expected to be sensitive to data and architecture details, and directly determine practical quantities such as the optimal model shape and the compute-optimal frontier. We therefore argue that understanding the coefficients is the key to near-term performance improvements, and that a closer examination of the current universality class may reveal pathways to better universality classes.

AI.tldr: Neural scaling laws have fixed exponents arising from generic mechanisms (time, width, depth) with data/architecture-dependent coefficients; understanding these coefficients is key to practical improvements and can guide searches for better universality classes.
AI.motivation: Explain why scaling exponents appear universal and identify practical levers (coefficients) for model design and compute efficiency.
AI.method: The paper presents a theoretical position arguing for three generic mechanisms: (1) time scaling ~1/3 due to Softmax nonlinearity, (2) inverse width scaling due to representational superposition, and (3) inverse depth scaling due to ensemble averaging across Transformer layers; claims robustness across data structures and architectures, placing models in a universality class with fixed exponents.
AI.result: Exponents are argued to be fixed by the proposed mechanisms; coefficients are sensitive to data and architecture and determine practical quantities such as optimal model shape and compute-optimal frontier; understanding these coefficients is the key to near-term gains and may reveal pathways to new universality classes.
AI.conclusion: Focus on measuring and adjusting the scaling coefficients to improve performance; the current universality class may be extended or revised by future work that uncovers how coefficients vary with data/architecture and whether alternative mechanisms yield different exponent values.

title: TensorLDM: A Component-Wise Latent Diffusion Model for Volumetric DTI Reconstruction from Sparse DWIs
summary: Reconstructing diffusion tensors from sparse DWIs is critical for accelerating Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) in clinical settings, yet current deep learning approaches frequently yield anatomically inconsistent or physically implausible tensors. We introduce TensorLDM, a component-wise latent diffusion model that processes the six tensor components through two group-specific encoders (for diagonal and off-diagonal elements) while maintaining anatomical consistency via shared DWI conditioning. TensorLDM uses an Anatomy-Conditioned Autoencoder that encourages the latent to focus on tensor properties rather than re-encoding structural information. A shared Cross-Component Attention (CCA) mechanism, applied in both autoencoder refinement and diffusion fine-tuning, models inter-component dependencies, while a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) DWI conditioner provides component-adaptive conditioning. On the Human Connectome Project (HCP) dataset under a single-shell, four-volume sparse acquisition, TensorLDM produces the most accurate downstream tractography and tensors with near-ground-truth physical validity (SPD-violation rate 1.54% vs. 1.40%), with the best or comparable voxel-wise reconstruction accuracy. Geodesic tensor error measured by the Log-Euclidean Metric (LEM) corroborates these gains.

AI.tldr: TensorLDM is a component-wise latent diffusion model for reconstructing diffusion tensors from sparse DWIs, ensuring anatomical and physical plausibility via shared conditioning and cross-component attention; it improves tractography on HCP data with near-ground-truth tensor geometry.
AI.motivation: Address anatomical inconsistency and physical implausibility in diffusion-tensor reconstructions from sparse DWI acquisitions to enable accurate tractography in clinical settings.
AI.method: Two encoders for diagonal and off-diagonal tensor components; Anatomy-Conditioned Autoencoder; shared DWI conditioning; Cross-Component Attention (CCA); Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) DWI conditioner; diffusion fine-tuning; evaluation on HCP single-shell, four-volume acquisition.
AI.result: TensorLDM achieves the most accurate downstream tractography and tensor estimates with near-ground-truth physical validity; SPD-violation rate reported around 1.54% (TensorLDM) vs. 1.40% in comparison); voxel-wise reconstruction accuracy is best or comparable; geodesic tensor error (Log-Euclidean Metric) supports improved tensor geometry.
AI.conclusion: A component-wise latent diffusion framework with inter-component attention and anatomy-conditioned supervision yields anatomically consistent, physically plausible tensor reconstructions from sparse DWIs, enabling improved tractography; broader validation across datasets and acquisition protocols is warranted.

title: DSP-SLAM++: A Unified Framework for Multi-Class, High-Fidelity Object SLAM in the Wild
summary: Existing object-aware SLAM systems force a trade-off between real-time performance, multi-class support, and the generation of high-fidelity, semantically coherent object models. To address this trade-off, we present DSP-SLAM++, which extends the DSP-SLAM framework with an asynchronous mapping pipeline for real-time performance and dedicated sensor fusion adaptations for a monocular fisheye-LiDAR suite. Experiments demonstrate that our system generates fine-grained, geometrically-complete shapes for multiple object classes while eliminating severe mapping thread bottlenecks by reducing maximum object processing latency by up to 70\% compared to the state-of-the-art baseline, enabling robust, real-time performance on a challenging 25 Hz multi-class datasets. This work makes high-fidelity, multi-class object SLAM more practical for real-world applications like autonomous driving and robotic manipulation by enabling its use on platforms with common fisheye-LiDAR sensor setups. The open-source code is available at: [github.com/AUBVRL/DSP-SLAMpp].

AI.tldr: DSP-SLAM++ extends DSP-SLAM with an asynchronous mapping pipeline and monocular fisheye-LiDAR fusion to enable real-time, multi-class object SLAM with fine-grained, high-fidelity object models while reducing processing latency.
AI.motivation: Existing object-aware SLAM systems trade off real-time performance, multi-class support, and high-fidelity object modeling. A method achieving all three is needed for practical autonomous systems.
AI.method: Introduce DSP-SLAM++: an asynchronous mapping pipeline and sensor fusion adaptations tailored to a monocular fisheye-LiDAR setup, built on top of the DSP-SLAM framework.
AI.result: Experiments show fine-grained, geometrically complete object shapes across multiple classes, with up to 70% reduction in the maximum object processing latency compared to a state-of-the-art baseline, enabling robust real-time performance on a 25 Hz multi-class dataset.
AI.conclusion: DSP-SLAM++ makes high-fidelity, multi-class object SLAM more practical for real-world applications (e.g., autonomous driving, robotic manipulation) on platforms with fisheye-LiDAR setups; code is open-source at github.com/AUBVRL/DSP-SLAMpp.

title: Emergent Capabilities Arise Randomly from Learning Sparse Attention Patterns
summary: Neural scaling laws for transformer language models predict smooth improvements in pretraining loss with increasing parameters, but downstream capabilities such as in-context learning are known to emerge abruptly past a certain model scale. In this paper, we show that emergent capabilities arise stochastically throughout training, with larger models acquiring them earlier on average. We demonstrate that the emergence of capabilities such as pattern completion and indirect object identification corresponds to the abrupt learning of task-relevant attention patterns. To isolate this phenomenon, we train transformer models on synthetic linear map and cellular automata datasets, and we show that the difficulty of learning attention patterns depends on context length and pattern sparsity. Moreover, scaling the number of attention heads improves learning efficiency on our synthetic tasks, while increasing the head dimension yields diminishing returns past a minimum capacity. We additionally investigate architectures with alternative attention mechanisms, showing that MLP-Mixer outperforms a transformer on linear map tasks with complex attention patterns. Our findings provide a mechanistic insight into emergence, showing that downstream capabilities arise abruptly due to the intrinsic difficulty of learning sparse attention patterns in transformer models.

AI.tldr: Emergent capabilities in transformer language models arise stochastically during training due to the difficulty of learning sparse attention patterns; scaling the number of attention heads improves learning efficiency, while increasing head dimension yields diminishing returns; alternative architectures (MLP-Mixer) can outperform transformers on tasks with complex attention patterns.
AI.motivation: Provide a mechanistic understanding of how and when downstream capabilities emerge in large language models, and how architectural choices shape emergent behavior.
AI.method: Train transformer models on synthetic linear map and cellular automata datasets; analyze the learning of task-relevant attention patterns; vary context length and sparsity; scale the number of attention heads and head dimension; compare transformer and alternative attention mechanisms (MLP-Mixer).
AI.result: Emergent capabilities appear abruptly and stochastically during training; larger models acquire them earlier on average; emergence aligns with abrupt learning of task-relevant attention patterns; more attention heads improve learning efficiency; head dimension shows diminishing returns past a minimum capacity; MLP-Mixer can outperform a transformer on linear map tasks with complex attention patterns.
AI.conclusion: Emergence in transformers is driven by the intrinsic difficulty of learning sparse attention patterns; downstream capabilities arise non-smoothly and are influenced by architectural choices and scaling strategies that affect attention-pattern learning.

title: Disease-Centric Vision-Language Pretraining with Hybrid Visual Encoding for 3D Computed Tomography
summary: Vision-language pre-training (VLP) holds great promise for general-purpose medical AI by leveraging radiology reports as rich textual supervision, yet existing methods struggle with 3D CT imaging due to inefficient visual backbones and coarse semantic alignment. To address these issues, we propose a tailored VLP framework featuring three key components: (1) a CNN-ViT hybrid encoder that replaces ViT's patch embedding with a 3D CNN backbone to efficiently capture local anatomical details while preserving global attention and compatibility with pre-trained cross-modal priors; (2) a disease-level contrastive learning mechanism using learnable query tokens to dynamically extract disease-specific semantics from full reports and align them with corresponding visual features, thereby disentangling distinct diseases within the same anatomical region; and (3) a diagnosis-aware prompt strategy that employs real clinical phrases and aggregated disease prototypes to bridge the pre-training-inference gap and enhance zero-shot diagnostic reliability. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on CT-RATE (84.4% AUC, +5.1%) and Rad-ChestCT (75.4% AUC, +5.4%), with even larger gains (+9.8% AUC) on a challenging 60-disease benchmark, and demonstrates strong transferability to radiology report generation, underscoring the generality and clinical utility of our approach.

AI.tldr: A CNN-ViT hybrid framework for 3D CT VLP with disease-level contrastive learning and diagnosis-aware prompts, achieving state-of-the-art AUC on CT-RATE and Rad-ChestCT and broad transfer to report generation.
AI.motivation: Existing VLP for 3D CT is limited by inefficient visual backbones and coarse semantic alignment between text and images. There is a need for efficient, fine-grained anatomical representation and robust cross-modal alignment to support zero-shot and few-shot diagnosis.
AI.method: 1) CNN-ViT hybrid encoder using a 3D CNN backbone for patch embedding to capture local anatomy while preserving global attention and compatibility with pre-trained cross-modal priors. 2) Disease-level contrastive learning with learnable query tokens to extract disease-specific semantics from full reports and align with visual features, enabling disentanglement of co-located diseases. 3) Diagnosis-aware prompt strategy using real clinical phrases and aggregated disease prototypes to close the pre-training–inference gap and improve zero-shot reliability.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on CT-RATE (84.4% AUC, +5.1%); Rad-ChestCT (75.4% AUC, +5.4%); and a 60-disease benchmark with larger gains (+9.8% AUC). Shows transferability to radiology report generation, indicating strong generalization and clinical utility.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates high generality and clinical value for medical VLP on 3D CT, delivering efficient local-detail capture, principled disease-centric alignment, and practical zero-shot diagnostic capability.

title: From Rubble Simulation to Active Magnetic Mapping: Quantum Sensing for Disaster Response
summary:
Locating survivors of building collapses within the first 72 hours is a critical challenge in disaster response, and existing sensing modalities provide only partial information about the structure beneath the rubble.
  This paper proposes drone-based quantum magnetometry as a complementary modality and develops a simulation pipeline spanning rubble physics, sensor-array deployment, and active spatial reconstruction. We use Unreal Engine to generate a steel-reinforced concrete parking-garage collapse and compute the induced magnetic field via a per-triangle dipole approximation, establishing that meaningful magnetic structure is recoverable in the sub-pT to sub-nT range from roughly 1 m above the roofline. Then, we feed sparse multi-sensor samples into a Gaussian Process Regression back-end driven by Bayesian active sampling and validate the pipeline across multiple independent collapse realizations; a three-sensor array optimizes the trade-off between gradient resolution and UAV payload constraints, and active sampling reaches peak structural correlation in roughly $100$ samples. Together, these results indicate that quantum-grade sensing could become a useful tool for drone-based structural analysis and potentially void detection in collapsed buildings.

AI.tldr: Drone-based quantum magnetometry for post-collapse structural sensing; simulated pipeline shows detectable magnetic signatures; three-sensor array with GP regression and active sampling achieves meaningful correlation with ~100 samples.
AI.motivation: Locating survivors within the first 72 hours is critical in disaster response, and existing sensing modalities provide only partial information about the structure beneath rubble; quantum-grade magnetic sensing could complement other modalities by revealing magnetic structure from steel-reinforced rubble.
AI.method: Develop a simulation pipeline spanning rubble physics, sensor-array deployment, and active spatial reconstruction. Use Unreal Engine to model a steel-reinforced concrete parking-garage collapse and compute the induced magnetic field via a per-triangle dipole approximation. Place sensors roughly 1 m above the roofline. Feed sparse multi-sensor samples into a Gaussian Process Regression back-end driven by Bayesian active sampling. Validate across multiple collapse realizations. Evaluate a three-sensor array for trade-off between gradient resolution and UAV payload. Assess active sampling performance.
AI.result: Magnetic structure recoverable in sub-pT to sub-nT range from ~1 m above roofline. Three-sensor array balances gradient resolution and UAV payload constraints. Active sampling yields peak structural correlation in ~100 samples. Pipeline validated across multiple collapse realizations.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-grade sensing could become a useful tool for drone-based structural analysis and potentially void detection in collapsed buildings.

title: Bias-Controlled Primal-Dual Natural Actor-Critic: Optimal Rates for Constrained Multi-Objective Average-Reward RL
summary: Many reinforcement learning (RL) problems in the infinite-horizon average-reward setting require optimizing multiple conflicting objectives while satisfying multiple safety constraints. A common approach is concave scalarization, where the agent maximizes a utility $ f(J^π_{r_1}, \ldots, J^π_{r_M}) $ subject to a scalarized constraint $ g(J^π_{c_1}, \ldots, J^π_{c_N}) \ge 0 $, where $J^π_{r_m}$ and $J^π_{c_n}$ denote the average-reward and cost under policy $π$. However, the nonlinearity of $f$ and $g$ introduces bias in policy-gradient and actor-critic methods, since gradients must be evaluated using noisy estimates of $J^π,$ and $ \mathbb{E}[\partial f(J^π)] \neq \partial f(\mathbb{E}[J^π]),$ and this bias propagates through both primal and dual updates. We propose an MLMC-based primal-dual Natural Actor-Critic algorithm for average-reward MDPs that controls bias in scalarized objectives, constraint evaluation, and actor-critic estimation without requiring mixing-time knowledge. We show that the algorithm achieves optimal global convergence and constraint-violation rates of $ \tilde{O}(1/\sqrt{T}) $. To our knowledge, this is the first result establishing optimal convergence for concave scalarized multi-objective RL in the average-reward setting, both with and without constraints, and the first to do so without mixing-time information even in the absence of scalarization.

AI.tldr: Introduces an MLMC-based primal-dual Natural Actor-Critic for average-reward MDPs to address bias from concave scalarization in multi-objective RL; achieves optimal convergence O~(1/√T) in both constrained and unconstrained settings without requiring mixing-time knowledge.
AI.motivation: Confronts bias in gradients due to nonlinear scalarization and noisy estimates in policy-gradient/actor-critic methods; seeks provable convergence for concave scalarized multi-objective RL in the average-reward setting with multiple safety constraints, without relying on mixing-time information.
AI.method: MLMC-based primal-dual Natural Actor-Critic algorithm for average-reward MDPs that controls bias in scalarized objectives, constraint evaluation, and actor-critic estimation; does not require mixing-time information.
AI.result: Global convergence with constraint-violation rates of ~O(1/√T) (tilde notation). First result establishing optimal convergence for concave scalarized multi-objective RL in the average-reward setting, both with and without constraints, and without mixing-time information even without scalarization.
AI.conclusion: Advances theoretical guarantees for multi-objective RL in the average-reward regime by providing optimal convergence and bias-control without mixing-time knowledge, enabling reliable handling of multiple objectives and safety constraints.

title: Do Thinking Tokens Help with Safety?
summary: Today's reasoning models use thinking tokens to attain stronger performance on benchmarks than their instruction-tuned counterparts. It is also generally believed that this more "deliberative" mode should improve alignment and safety, by providing the model a safe space to consider whether its planned answer to a request violates its safety principles. We present evidence that this intuition is not always correct. Across frontier open-weight reasoning models spanning GPT-OSS, Qwen, Olmo, and Phi families, we find that the eventual refusal/compliance outcome is already strongly predictable via a trained head on the first token's hidden representation ($0.84$-$0.95$ AUROC and $\sim88\%$ balanced accuracy for predicting refusal/compliance) before any visible thinking. The thinking process turns out to be more akin to prefix completion than to deliberative revision, with the final outcome rarely changing after the first $\sim20\%$ of thinking, despite giving the appearance of deliberation at the text level ($\sim74\%$ of text-level deliberations occur when the response distribution is already locked to one refusal/compliance side). We also find that existing inference-time and training-based safety interventions, despite being motivated by the goal of inducing deliberation, largely shift model behavior toward over-refusal while suppressing already-scarce deliberation signals. Our results suggest that safety behavior in current reasoning models is much less deliberative than commonly assumed, and highlight the need for methods that induce real safety deliberation.

AI.tldr: Reasoning tokens in frontier models are not as deliberative as assumed; early hidden state signals predict refusal/compliance; most apparent deliberation does not change outcomes; safety interventions tend to over-refuse and suppress deliberation; need methods for genuine safety deliberation.
AI.motivation: Test the assumption that deliberative thinking improves alignment and safety in instruction-following models by analyzing thinking tokens across frontier models and distinguishing apparent text-level deliberation from actual decision-making.
AI.method: Train a classifier head on the first token's hidden representation to predict refusal vs compliance; evaluate across GPT-OSS, Qwen, Olmo, and Phi families; analyze timing of decisions, and categorize thinking as prefix completion vs deliberation; assess impact of inference-time and training-based safety interventions on behavior.
AI.result: The outcome (refusal/compliance) is strongly predictable before any visible thinking (AUROC 0.84–0.95, ~88% balanced accuracy). Thinking resembles prefix completion; final outcome rarely changes after ~20% of thinking. ~74% of text-level deliberations occur when the response distribution is already locked to one side. Safety interventions tend to increase refusal and suppress deliberation signals.
AI.conclusion: Current safety behavior in reasoning models is far less deliberative than assumed; to improve safety, develop methods that elicit genuine safety deliberation rather than superficial, early-refusal signals.

title: Efficient Cross-Scale Invertible Hiding Network with Spatial-Frequency Collaboration and Non-Invertible Mechanism
comment: IEEE TNNLS submitted by Junxue Yang, Xin Liao (https://msf-hnu.github.io/)
summary: Image hiding aims to conceal image-level messages within cover images at the same resolution. Invertible neural networks (INN)-based image hiding has emerged as an important branch. It treats concealing and revealing as a pair of inverse problems on image domain transformation and uses INN's forward and backward processes to address them. Due to architectural constraints, existing INN-based methods suffer from single-scale and single-domain feature extraction and limited nonlinear representation capability, resulting in inferior image quality. To mitigate these limitations, we propose an efficient cross-scale invertible hiding network with the spatial-frequency collaboration and the non-invertible mechanism, termed CrosInv. CrosInv exploits cross-scale and spatial-frequency collaborative features while enhancing nonlinear representation. Specifically, we introduce a cross-scale invertible module that bijectively maps inputs to cross-scale representations. To effectively integrate spatial and frequency information, the cross-scale invertible module employs pixel shuffle, Haar wavelet transformation, and their inverse operations for scale transformation. Furthermore, a non-invertible cross dense module is integrated to enhance the nonlinearity. Comprehensive experiments verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed CrosInv.

AI.tldr: CrosInv is an efficient cross-scale invertible hiding network for INN-based image hiding that uses a cross-scale invertible module with pixel shuffle and Haar wavelet transforms and a non-invertible cross dense module to enhance nonlinear representation, achieving superior image quality.
AI.motivation: INN-based image hiding suffers from single-scale/single-domain features and limited nonlinear representation, leading to inferior image quality. Integrating cross-scale and spatial-frequency collaboration with non-invertible components aims to boost reconstruction quality and hiding capacity.
AI.method: Introduce a cross-scale invertible module that bijectively maps inputs to cross-scale representations using pixel shuffle and Haar wavelet transformation and their inverse for scale changes. Integrate a non-invertible cross dense module to enhance nonlinearity within the INN framework for hiding and revealing.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of CrosInv over baselines, indicating improved image quality and potentially better hiding capacity across tested scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CrosInv effectively leverages cross-scale and spatial-frequency collaboration, along with a non-invertible mechanism, to enhance INN-based image hiding, delivering higher-quality reconstructions and more robust hiding performance.

title: Mixture-of-Experts RL for Fault-Tolerant Legged Locomotion
summary: Legged robots deployed in planetary exploration and other remote environments must maintain reliable locomotion despite actuator failures and challenging terrain conditions. Although reinforcement learning has achieved strong results in legged locomotion, monolithic policies can struggle to efficiently represent the diverse control strategies required to compensate for different fault conditions. In this work, we propose a fault-aware modular control architecture that explicitly leverages fault-diagnosis information to activate specialized control experts associated with distinct actuator failure modes. Experimental results show that explicit fault-conditioned modular policies consistently outperform monolithic policies of comparable size, achieving higher locomotion performance across failure scenarios. Moreover, the proposed modular architecture retains competitive performance even under significantly reduced network capacity, highlighting its suitability for compute-constrained robotic platforms, such as those typically employed in space applications. The code associated with this work is available at: https://github.com/iit-DLSLab/fault-locomotion-isaaclab.

AI.tldr: Fault-aware modular control architecture using fault-diagnosis conditioned experts improves legged locomotion under actuator failures, outperforming monolithic policies; maintains performance with reduced network capacity and is suitable for compute-constrained space robotics.
AI.motivation: Tackle the brittleness of monolithic policies under diverse actuator fault conditions in legged robots and meet compute constraints on space-grade platforms.
AI.method: Introduce modular control with separate experts for distinct actuator failure modes; use fault-diagnosis to activate appropriate expert; train under varying fault conditions; evaluate across failure scenarios and under reduced network capacity; code released.
AI.result: Fault-conditioned modular policies consistently outperform monolithic baselines of equal size across failure scenarios; robust performance under significant capacity reduction; demonstrates compute efficiency suitable for space applications.
AI.conclusion: Explicit fault conditioning and modularization yield superior fault-tolerant locomotion with favorable compute- efficiency, supporting deployment in resource-constrained planetary rovers; potential for scaling to more fault scenarios and hardware.

title: Concept Removal for Frontier Image Generative Models
summary: Image generative models are trained on massive, largely uncurated internet-scale datasets that contain undesirable visual concepts. Efficiently removing such concepts from the model generations without degrading the quality of output images remains challenging. We introduce a novel concept removal method for frontier diffusion and image autoregressive models, such as SD3.5, Flux, and Infinity. Our intervention replaces the internal bottleneck layer present in all these modern models with a transcoder that is trained to replicate the original layer while structuring it into distinct activation features. This in-place substitution creates an integrated filter through which concept-specific signals can be selectively disabled while preserving the rest of the model's behavior. Since the intervention modifies the model backbone rather than attaching an external component, it remains persistent under white-box access. Empirically, the approach achieves state-of-the-art concept removal performance across modern diffusion and autoregressive models, maintains visual generation quality, provides robustness against adversarial prompts, and supports sequential removal of diverse concepts. This positions our method as a practical approach for concept removal in frontier image generative models.

AI.tldr: A backbone-level concept removal method for frontier diffusion and autoregressive image models: replace the internal bottleneck with a trained transcoder that partitions activations, enabling selective disabling of concept signals while preserving overall image quality and robustness.
AI.motivation: Addresses the presence of undesirable concepts learned from massive internet-scale, largely uncurated datasets. Seeks a persistent, integrative solution that works across diffusion and autoregressive architectures without relying on external adapters.
AI.method: Substitute the model's internal bottleneck layer with a transcoder trained to replicate it and to structure activations into distinct features. This creates an integrated filter that can selectively disable concept-specific signals. The substitution is in-place and maintained under white-box access, ensuring persistence.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art concept removal across modern diffusion and autoregressive models (e.g., SD3.5, Flux, Infinity), while preserving image generation quality, demonstrating robustness to adversarial prompts, and enabling sequential removal of multiple concepts.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical, backbone-integrated approach to concept removal in frontier image generative models, with persistent and broad applicability and minimal impact on ordinary generative performance.

title: A Sensorised Lattice Footplate for a Semi-Active Prosthetic Foot
summary: This paper investigates whether magnetic plantar sensing can be embedded directly inside the load-bearing compliant element of a low-cost semi-active prosthetic foot. We present a prototype integrating a sensorised 3D-printed lattice footplate, a servo-adjustable hydraulic damper, and a reduced-order ankle model. The damper is experimentally characterised to relate adjustment angle to damping coefficient. Controlled compression tests show tunable lattice stiffness, while cyclic normal loading shows that the embedded sensor tracks the testing-machine reference force, supporting plantar-force estimation without an external insole layer. Static-posture trials under approximately body-weight loading show that forefoot and rearfoot loading distributions are separable across four prescribed stance configurations, providing a preliminary check of the sensing pipeline. A feedforward damping schedule approximates the dorsiflexion trend of a reference ankle trajectory through early-to-mid stance, while exposing the expected limitation that a purely dissipative mechanism cannot generate active push-off. Together, these results demonstrate that sensing can be embedded inside the load-bearing compliant element of a prosthetic foot and used to drive semi-active damping.

AI.tldr: Embedded magnetic plantar sensing in a 3D-printed lattice, load-bearing prosthetic foot enables plantar-force estimation and semi-active damping without external insoles; feasible but passive damping cannot supply push-off.
AI.motivation: To enable direct sensing inside the load-bearing element of a prosthetic foot to estimate plantar force and enable semi-active control, reducing the need for external sensing layers.
AI.method: Prototype integration of a sensorised 3D-printed lattice footplate, a servo-adjustable hydraulic damper, and a reduced-order ankle model; damper characterization vs adjustment angle; controlled compression tests for stiffness; cyclic normal loading to validate force-tracking; static-posture trials for forefoot/rearfoot loading across four stance configurations; development of a feedforward damping schedule to approximate dorsiflexion trajectory.
AI.result: Embedded sensor tracks reference force under cyclic loading; forefoot and rearfoot loads separable in stance configurations; damping schedule approximates dorsiflexion trend; identified limitation that a purely dissipative mechanism cannot generate active push-off.
AI.conclusion: Feasibility demonstrated for embedding sensing inside the load-bearing prosthetic foot element to drive semi-active damping; sensing pipeline validated from load to ankle dynamics, with limitations tied to active propulsion.

title: LLM-ACES: Closed-Loop Discovery of Dynamical Systems with LLM-Guided Adaptive Search
summary: Recovering governing Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) from data is a central challenge in modeling dynamical systems across scientific domains. Existing approaches cast discovery as a static inference problem over fixed datasets, assuming that the observed trajectories are sufficiently informative. However, dynamical systems evolve over large state spaces, and limited data can make multiple equations observationally indistinguishable, leading to identifiability gaps and the recovery of incorrect governing equations. To address this, we introduce LLM-ACES, or LLM-guided Active Closed-loop Equation Search, a closed-loop framework that jointly optimizes symbolic hypothesis construction and adaptive data acquisition. In LLM-ACES, a large language model (LLM) proposes operator priors that partition the large search space into distinct regions, within which candidate equations are fit to the observed data. The disagreement among these candidates guides the acquisition of informative trajectories, creating a feedback loop that iteratively refines both the hypothesis space and the discovered dynamics. On 122 ODE systems spanning ODEBench and ODEBase, LLM-ACES achieves the lowest median NMSE, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by several orders of magnitude while achieving a high symbolic accuracy of 46.2% and 52.4%, respectively. Our analysis further shows that LLM-ACES is sample-efficient, achieving better performance with one-tenth the data. Furthermore, LLM-ACES's feedback-driven data acquisition makes it robust to noise and recovers the correct symbolic structure, while baselines introduce spurious terms that fit the data locally but obscure the true governing relationships.

AI.tldr: A closed-loop framework (LLM-ACES) for discovering governing ODEs by combining LLM-driven hypothesis priors, region-based search, and active data acquisition; it yields substantial improvements in accuracy and data efficiency, with robustness to noise and reduced spurious terms.
AI.motivation: Identifiability gaps in static, data-limited equation discovery; large state spaces cause multiple equations to fit data; need integrate hypothesis construction with adaptive data collection.
AI.method: LLM proposes operator priors to partition search space; fit candidate equations within regions; use disagreement to guide acquisition of informative trajectories; iterative refinement of hypotheses and dynamics; evaluated on 122 ODE systems from ODEBench/ODEBase.
AI.result: Lower median NMSE by orders of magnitude vs baselines; symbolic accuracy 46.2% and 52.4%; sample-efficient (one-tenth data); robust to noise; avoids spurious terms.
AI.conclusion: LLM-ACES demonstrates effective, robust, data-efficient discovery of governing equations via closed-loop hypothesis search and active data acquisition; highlights benefits of LLM-guided priors and feedback-driven data collection.

title: H-Adapter: Pose-Robust Hairstyle Transfer via Attention-Derived, Source-Aligned Hair Masks
summary: Hairstyle transfer has practical applications such as virtual try-on, yet remains challenging when the source and reference exhibit large head-pose discrepancies. We propose H-Adapter, which improves pose robustness by training with a region-specific loss that disentangles hair and non-hair objectives and thereby induces spatially disentangled cross-attention, from which a source-aligned hair edit mask is derived to guide diffusion-based inpainting. Experiments on pose-agnostic and pose-different subsets demonstrate strong quantitative results, including the best FID, $\mathrm{FID}_{\mathrm{CLIP}}$, and CLIP-I under pose differences, while maintaining competitive non-hair preservation and improving qualitative fidelity to fine-grained reference hairstyle details. Beyond source-conditioned transfer, H-Adapter supports practical extensions including text-to-image generation, auxiliary prompt-based hair color control, and compatibility with an identity-preserving IP-Adapter variant. We also introduce a VLM-as-a-judge protocol and observe consistent gains in hairstyle faithfulness, non-hair preservation, and artifact quality.

AI.tldr: H-Adapter introduces a region-specific loss to disentangle hair and non-hair objectives, enabling spatially disentangled cross-attention and a source-aligned hair edit mask to guide diffusion-based inpainting for pose-robust hairstyle transfer; it achieves strong metrics under pose differences and supports text-to-image generation, hair-color control, and IP-Adapter compatibility, with VLM-based evaluation showing consistent gains.
AI.motivation: Tackle the challenge of large head-pose discrepancies between source and reference in hairstyle transfer, improving pose robustness, fidelity, and practicality for applications like virtual try-on.
AI.method: Train with a region-specific loss that disentangles hair vs non-hair objectives to induce spatially disentangled cross-attention. Derive a source-aligned hair edit mask from the cross-attention to guide diffusion-based inpainting. Extend to text-to-image generation and auxiliary hair-color prompts; ensure compatibility with an identity-preserving IP-Adapter variant.
AI.result: On pose-agnostic and pose-different subsets, H-Adapter achieves leading quantitative results (best FID, FID_CLIP, and CLIP-I under pose differences); maintains competitive non-hair preservation and improves fidelity to fine-grained reference hairstyles. A VLM-as-a-judge protocol shows consistent gains in hairstyle faithfulness, non-hair preservation, and artifact quality.
AI.conclusion: H-Adapter enhances pose robustness in hairstyle transfer, enabling practical extensions (text-to-image, hair color control) and compatibility with IP-Adapter; a VLM-based evaluation confirms improved hairstyle fidelity and artifact quality.

title: Action ControlNet: A Lightweight Delay-Aware Adapter for Smooth Asynchronous Control in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robot manipulation, but their inference latency remains a major obstacle to stable high-frequency control. Asynchronous execution mitigates this bottleneck by overlapping policy inference with action execution, yet the next action chunk is still predicted from stale observations while the robot continues to move. Direct chunk stitching therefore introduces handoff discontinuities, action jitter, and failures in contact-rich manipulation. Existing remedies typically require either full-policy retraining or architecture-specific runtime logic. This work proposes Action ControlNet (ACNet), a lightweight delay-aware adapter that uses the executed motion suffix as a residual condition for a mostly frozen action head. ACNet leaves the pretrained backbone unchanged, introduces few trainable parameters, and remains compatible with generative action heads such as diffusion and flow matching. On Kinetix, Meta-World MT50, and a real-world SO-ARM101 platform, ACNet improves robustness under inference delay and yields smoother asynchronous trajectories than direct chunk stitching, while remaining more lightweight than full delay-conditioned retraining.

AI.tldr: ACNet is a lightweight delay-aware adapter that conditions a mostly frozen action head on the executed motion suffix to compensate for inference delays in VLA-based robot control, improving robustness and smoothness with minimal retraining.
AI.motivation: Inference latency blocks high-frequency control in vision-language-action models; asynchronous execution leads to stale observations and handoff discontinuities; existing fixes require full retraining or architecture-specific runtime logic.
AI.method: Introduce Action ControlNet (ACNet): a small adapter that uses the executed motion suffix as a residual condition for the action head; backbone frozen; few trainable parameters; compatible with diffusion and flow matching heads; works with asynchronous action chunks without full policy retraining.
AI.result: Empirical results on Kinetix, Meta-World MT50, and real-world SO-ARM101 show ACNet improves robustness under inference delay and yields smoother asynchronous trajectories compared to direct chunk stitching; lighter than full retraining.
AI.conclusion: ACNet provides a practical, low-cost solution to latency-induced instability in VLA manipulations, enabling stable high-frequency control with existing pretrained backbones.

title: Adapt Only When It Pays: Budgeted Decision-Loss Priority for Delayed Online Time-Series Adaptation
summary: Online time-series forecasters receive labels only after horizon-dependent delays, while every adaptation step spends limited compute. We study when an online learner should update, not how to adapt at every opportunity, and introduce ADOWIP: a residual-adapter framework with sealed delay queues, exact budget accounting, and auditable update telemetry. Its main scheduler is an observed decision-loss priority gate that updates only after feedback is revealed, when downstream loss, optionally penalized by prediction MSE, exceeds a calibrated empirical quantile and budget remains. We prove hard-budget feasibility, projected-OGD regret for a convex linear accepted-update subproblem, and stability plus conditional finite-sample gate-selection statements. On public ETT capacity-planning tasks, a frozen calibration/evaluation split selects a gate that lowers held-out decision loss against always, fixed-period, and drift-triggered exact-update baselines under matched compute. Secondary threshold/load-index ETT suites are mixed: 33 of 41 selected contrasts clear the stricter cross-artifact Holm family, and the 8 nonpassing rows are explicitly excluded from primary claims. The same protocol improves an external UCI Bike capacity proxy with 20/0 held-out wins, and a fixed gate passes three full-year Capital Bikeshare station-rebalancing contrasts. Probe-based and finance experiments remain negative, delimiting the current scope of decision-prioritized adaptation.

AI.tldr: Introduces ADOWIP, a decision-prioritized online adaptation framework for time-series with delayed labels and tight compute budgets, featuring sealed delay queues and a gate that updates after feedback when loss exceeds a calibrated empirical quantile; provides theoretical guarantees and empirical gains on ETT and bike-sharing tasks, with some negative experiments.
AI.motivation: To address online time-series forecasting under horizon-dependent label delays and limited per-update compute by deciding when to update rather than updating at every opportunity.
AI.method: Residual-adapter framework (ADOWIP) with sealed delay queues, exact budget accounting, and auditable telemetry. The main scheduler is an observed decision-loss priority gate that triggers updates only after feedback is revealed and when downstream loss (potentially penalized by MSE) exceeds a calibrated empirical quantile and budget remains. Theoretical results include hard-budget feasibility, projected-OGD regret for a convex linear accepted-update subproblem, and stability plus conditional finite-sample gate-selection statements.
AI.result: Empirical gains on public ETT capacity-planning tasks: a frozen calibration/evaluation split selects a gate that lowers held-out decision loss against baselines (always update, fixed-period update, drift-triggered exact-update) under matched compute. Secondary threshold/load-index ETT suites: 33 of 41 selected contrasts pass the stricter Holm family; 8 nonpassing rows excluded from primary claims. The protocol also improves an external UCI Bike capacity proxy with 20/0 held-out wins, and a fixed gate succeeds on three full-year Capital Bikeshare station-rebalancing contrasts. Probe-based and finance experiments are negative, delimiting the current scope of decision-prioritized adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Decision-prioritized adaptation with theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements under delayed feedback and budget constraints; the approach is effective in select domains with delayed labels and limited compute, but not universally beneficial across all tasks, as evidenced by negative results in probe-based and finance settings.

title: FeVOS: Foresight Expression Video Object Segmentation
summary: Existing Referring Video Object Segmentation tasks focus on referring expressions describing events, actions or appearances of relevant objects within the observed frames, lacking evaluation in scenarios that require pre-decisive spatio-temporal reasoning, thereby limiting their applicability. To address this, we propose Foresight Expression Video Object Segmentation, a task that queries future events in upcoming video segments and requires masks of the objects in the observed frames as visual answers. For example, in ego-centric scenes, the question "What tool will be used?" demands reasoning over spatio-temporal cues to predict the masks of the next tool to be used, which helps with the understanding of future actions and decisions. To support this task, we introduce FeVOS, a dataset with 968 video clips, 14,525 foresight expressions, and 2,904 chain-of-thought annotations to provide explicit and interpretable reasoning steps. We further develop FeVOS-R1, an MLLM-based model trained on our dataset via a two-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. FeVOS-R1 not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on FeVOS, but also demonstrates strong generalization to existing RVOS benchmarks. We hope this work can inspire more research on predictive reasoning in video perception.

AI.tldr: We propose a foresight video object segmentation task (predicting masks for observed objects in future events) with the FeVOS dataset and FeVOS-R1 model; supports future-event reasoning and shows SOTA performance and transferability to RVOS.
AI.motivation: Current RVOS lacks pre-decisive spatio-temporal reasoning; enabling predictive understanding of future actions in video (e.g., tool use) is beneficial for egocentric perception and decision making.
AI.method: Introduce Foresight Expression Video Object Segmentation; create FeVOS dataset (968 clips, 14,525 foresight expressions, 2,904 chain-of-thought annotations); develop FeVOS-R1, an MLLM-based model trained with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning in a two-stage pipeline.
AI.result: FeVOS provides a new benchmark with substantial data; FeVOS-R1 achieves state-of-the-art on FeVOS and generalizes to existing RVOS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This work enables predictive, interpretable reasoning in video understanding and encourages further research into foresight-based perception and reasoning.

title: FORCE: Efficient VLA Reinforcement Fine-Tuning via Value-Calibrated Warm-up and Self-Distillation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are often constrained by the imitation ceiling imposed by sub-optimal data. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning can surpass this limit, it is notoriously sample inefficient. This challenge arises from two core issues: (1) catastrophic initial unlearning due to an unstable Q-function and (2) inefficient policy updates caused by low-quality exploration data, often forcing a reliance on costly human interventions. We introduce FORCE, a 3-stage framework that stabilizes fine-tuning by tackling both issues. FORCE first incorporates a Value-Calibrated Warm-Up phase, utilizing on-policy rollouts to mitigate the distributional shift of the Q-function. Subsequently, during the online stage, this calibrated Q-function acts as a filter for both the policy's own action proposals and expert data, ensuring only high-value actions are used for the policy update. We evaluate FORCE on various simulation and real-world tasks, and the result shows that FORCE achieves a 79% absolute improvement in success rates and outperform prior RL methods by 10%, while accelerating training by 32.5%. Critically, it mitigates the common success rate drop and achieves this robust performance without human intervention, marking a significant step towards deploying capable and autonomous robotic agents.

AI.tldr: FORCE is a three-stage RL fine-tuning framework for Vision-Language-Action models that stabilizes learning via a Value-Calibrated Warm-Up and uses a calibrated Q-function to filter action proposals and expert data, achieving higher success rates, faster training, and autonomous deployment without human input.
AI.motivation: Imitation-based VLA systems suffer from an imitation ceiling due to sub-optimal data. While RL fine-tuning can surpass this limit, it is highly sample-inefficient because of (i) catastrophic unlearning from an unstable Q-function and (ii) inefficient policy updates driven by poor exploration data requiring costly human intervention.
AI.method: FORCE comprises three stages: (i) Value-Calibrated Warm-Up using on-policy rollouts to reduce distributional shift in the Q-function; (ii) online fine-tuning where the calibrated Q-function filters both the policy’s proposed actions and expert data, so only high-value actions are used for policy updates; (iii) comprehensive evaluation across simulation and real-world tasks showing stability and robustness, including zero human intervention.
AI.result: Empirical results show a 79% absolute improvement in success rates, a 10% lead over prior RL methods, and a 32.5% reduction in training time. FORCE also mitigates common success-rate drops and demonstrates robust autonomous performance without human input.
AI.conclusion: FORCE delivers a stable, data-efficient RL fine-tuning paradigm for VLA models, enabling autonomous deployment by mitigating Q-function instability and poor exploration without requiring human interventions.

title: GCT-MARL: Graph-Based Contrastive Transfer for Sample-Efficient Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at The Continual RL Workshop, RLC 2026
summary: In cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), from a deployment perspective, it is challenging and expensive to train agents from scratch for each new environment or task. In this work, we propose GCT-MARL, a transfer learning framework that builds on the multi-view graph contrastive backbone of MAIL and augments it with a per-view, adaptively weighted alignment loss and a two-phase training protocol specifically designed for transfer across populations of varying sizes and compositions. We empirically demonstrate that the proposed framework markedly accelerates convergence on the target task relative to from-scratch training, in both homogeneous (within-faction, varying N) and heterogeneous (cross-faction and mixed unit-type) transfer scenarios. Furthermore, we show that the framework naturally supports continual learning by sequentially chaining the two-phase transfer protocol across a series of related tasks. Overall, this work provides a unified approach to mitigating key limitations in current MARL transfer methods with new insights at both methodological and empirical levels.

AI.tldr: GCT-MARL extends MAIL with per-view adaptive alignment loss and a two-phase transfer protocol to enable efficient cross-population MARL transfer and continual learning, showing faster convergence in homogeneous and heterogeneous transfers.
AI.motivation: Reduce the cost and fragility of transferring MARL policies across environments and populations of varying sizes/types; enable continual learning via chained transfers.
AI.method: Instrumental framework GCT-MARL uses a multi-view graph contrastive backbone (MAIL) augmented with a per-view adaptively weighted alignment loss and a two-phase training protocol designed for population-size/composition variation; supports sequential transfer for continual learning.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates markedly accelerated convergence on target tasks relative to from-scratch training in both homogeneous (varying N within faction) and heterogeneous (cross-faction and mixed unit-type) transfers; enables continual learning by chaining transfers across related tasks.
AI.conclusion: GCT-MARL provides a unified, scalable approach to MARL transfer, addressing key limitations of existing methods with methodological and empirical insights, and enabling efficient, continual transfer across diverse populations.

title: VPA-Guard: Defending and Benchmarking Image-to-Video Generation Against Visual Prompt Attacks
summary: Recent advancements in Image-to-Video (I2V) generation have transformed input images from simple appearance references into interactive control interfaces where visual cues such as arrows, sketches, and emojis orchestrate complex video dynamics with unprecedented controllability. However, these seemingly innocuous static cues can be interpreted by models as executable temporal instructions, unfolding into harmful actions in the generated videos. Despite the severity of this threat, existing safety benchmarks remain predominantly focused on text-based and content-only image-based jailbreaks, leaving implicit visual prompt attacks insufficiently explored. To bridge this gap, we present VVA-Bench, the first systematic benchmark for evaluating video generation safety under categorized vision-centric prompt attacks. Extensive experiments on VVA-Bench demonstrate that state-of-the-art models are highly susceptible to such attacks, with Attack Success Rates (ASR) reaching 100.0\% on Wan 2.7 and 74.8\% on Veo 3.1. To mitigate these risks, we propose VPA-Guard, a retrieval-augmented and self-evolving defense framework. By leveraging few-shot reasoning to identify latent malicious intents, our method reduces the attack ASR by 44.2\% and the harmfulness score by 73.4\% on average, while maintaining the model's utility for legitimate user edits. Our work provides both a rigorous benchmark and an effective defense strategy to advance safe and socially responsible multimodal generation.

AI.tldr: Introduces VVA-Bench, a benchmark for assessing safety of video generation under vision-centric prompt attacks, and VPA-Guard, a retrieval-augmented defense using few-shot reasoning; results show high vulnerability of current models and substantial mitigation with modest impact on legitimate edits.
AI.motivation: Image-to-Video generation allows visual cues to steer videos, but cues can be misinterpreted as temporal instructions leading to harmful outputs. Existing safety benchmarks focus on text/image prompts; there's a gap for implicit visual prompt attacks.
AI.method: Proposes VVA-Bench to systematically categorize and evaluate vision-centric prompt attacks in video generation. Conducts extensive experiments on Wan 2.7 and Veo 3.1. Introduces VPA-Guard, a retrieval-augmented, self-evolving defense framework that uses few-shot reasoning to identify latent malicious intents and reduce attack success and harmfulness while preserving legitimate use.
AI.result: Attack Success Rates reach 100.0% on Wan 2.7 and 74.8% on Veo 3.1, indicating strong vulnerability to vision-centric prompts. VPA-Guard reduces ASR by 44.2% and harmfulness score by 73.4% on average, without sacrificing utility for legitimate edits.
AI.conclusion: Provides the first systematic benchmark and effective defense for video generation safety under vision-centric prompts, advancing responsible multimodal generation and offering directions for robust, safer I2V systems.

title: Emcar: Embodied Controller for Animating Robots
comment: Published in Lecture Notes in Computer Science, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-032-15501-6_11
summary: This chapter describes EMCAR, a novel software tool for programming robot motion that leverages the unique affordances of artistic practices such as puppetry and drawing to conceive, design, and program novel interactions and realize new use cases for HRI. The advantage of this no-code platform is that it expands creative applications for collaborative robots - putting robots directly in the hands of artists - and provides an inclusive environment that enables individuals with little or no technical backgrounds to engage meaningfully in collaborations and robotics research.

AI.tldr: EMCAR is a no-code tool enabling artists to program robot motion for HRI using puppetry and drawing, expanding inclusive collaboration with collaborative robots.
AI.motivation: Lower barriers to robot programming and expand HRI use cases by integrating artistic practices that attract non-technical participants.
AI.method: Development of EMCAR as a no-code platform that leverages artistic affordances (puppetry, drawing) to conceive, design, and program novel robot interactions for HRI.
AI.result: The abstract presents EMCAR as an enabling tool that broadens access to robotics research and collaborative robot applications, enabling new interaction paradigms and use cases.
AI.conclusion: EMCAR democratizes robotics research and expands creative applications for collaborative robots by empowering artists and novices to participate meaningfully.

title: Training for the Model You Return: Improving Optimization for Iterate-Averaged Language Models
summary: Many modern Language Model (LM) pipelines return an averaged model, such as an exponential moving average of the training iterates, rather than the final iterate itself. This raises a fundamental question: given that we will return an iterate average, how should we change training to improve the performance of this average? We study this question by formulating optimizer design for the iterate-average estimator as an optimal-control problem. In a continuous-time stochastic quadratic model, we solve for the control strategy that minimizes the error of the returned average subject to a penalty on the size of the intervention. A practical approximation to this controller yields PACE, a lightweight wrapper around AdamW that pulls the live weights toward their exponential moving average with a clipped, per-coordinate control strength. We prove that a stylized version of PACE converges at the standard stochastic convex optimization rate, up to a factor depending on the averaging rule, while in the quadratic setting it can strictly improve the limiting squared error of the iterate-average estimator and can do so by an arbitrarily large factor on some instances. Empirically, our results suggest that PACE improves over AdamW and EMA-evaluated AdamW in supervised fine-tuning of 1-2B parameter LMs and in GPT-2 pretraining on FineWeb for a wide range of learning rates, decay schedules, and other hyperparameters.

AI.tldr: The paper designs an optimizer for iterate-average estimators via optimal control, introduces PACE (a per-coordinate clipped EMA pull toward the EMA integrated with AdamW), shows theoretical convergence and potential error improvement, and provides empirical gains on large LM fine-tuning and GPT-2 pretraining.
AI.motivation: Many language-model pipelines return an averaged iterate (e.g., EMA of iterates). The key question is how to train to maximize the usefulness of this average. The paper casts this as an optimal-control problem to improve the error of the returned average while controlling intervention size.
AI.method: Model the training with a continuous-time stochastic quadratic model and solve for an optimal control that minimizes the average error subject to a penalty on intervention. Derive a practical approximation, LPACE/PACE (a lightweight wrapper around AdamW) that nudges live weights toward their EMA with per-coordinate clipping. Prove convergence in a stylized convex setting and show potential error improvement in the quadratic case; validate empirically on 1–2B parameter LM fine-tuning and GPT-2 pretraining on FineWeb across multiple hyperparameters.
AI.result: Theoretically, a stylized version of PACE achieves standard stochastic convex optimization convergence rates and can reduce the limiting squared error of the iterate-average estimator in the quadratic setting, sometimes by an arbitrarily large factor. Empirically, PACE outperforms AdamW and EMA-based AdamW in large LM fine-tuning and GPT-2 pretraining across a broad range of learning rates and schedules.
AI.conclusion: PACE is a light-weight, practical wrapper that aligns optimization with the desiderata of iterate-average estimators, offering both theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over conventional EMA/adaptive methods in large-scale language-model training.

title: Expresso-AI: Explainable Video-Based Deep Learning Models for Depression Diagnosis
comment: 8 pages. Accepted at the 2023 11th International Conference on Affective Computing and Intelligent Interaction (ACII). Code: https://github.com/felmoreno1726/Expresso-AI
summary: Given the widespread prevalence of depression and its consequential impact on individuals and society, it is crucial to obtain objective measures for early diagnosis and intervention. As a multidisciplinary topic, these objective measures should be interpretable and accessible to health care professionals, ensuring effective collaboration and treatment planning in the realm of mental health care. Even though current automated depression diagnosis approaches improved over the last decade, a critical gap exists as they often lack affect-specificity and interpretability, limiting their practical application and potential impact on mental health care. In particular, interpretability from temporal activities from videos when deep models are used is not fully explored. In this study, we present a novel framework for analyzing Deep Neural Networks' decisions when trained on facial videos, specifically focusing on automatic depression severity diagnosis. By fine-tuning Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN) pre-trained on Action Recognition datasets on depression severity facial videos from AVEC depression dataset, our framework is able to interpret the model's saliency maps by examining face regions and temporal expression semantics. Our approach generates both visual and quantitative explanations for the model's decisions, providing greater insight into its reasoning. In addition to this interpretability, our video-based modeling has improved upon previous single-face benchmarks for visual depression diagnosis, resulting in enhanced predictive performance. Overall, our work demonstrates the successful development of a framework capable of generating hypotheses from a facial model's decisions while simultaneously improving depression's predictive capabilities.

AI.tldr: A DCNN-based framework analyzes facial video data to diagnose depression severity, offering interpretable saliency-based explanations (face regions and temporal expressions) and showing improved predictive performance on the AVEC dataset versus single-face baselines.
AI.motivation: Depression requires objective, interpretable biomarkers for early diagnosis and intervention. Existing automated approaches often lack affect-specificity and temporal interpretability, hindering clinical adoption of video-based models.
AI.method: Fine-tune DCNNs pretrained on action recognition datasets on AVEC depression facial videos. Examine model saliency to identify face regions and temporal expressions driving decisions, providing both visual and quantitative explanations. Compare against prior single-face benchmarks.
AI.result: The approach yielded improved depression severity prediction over single-face benchmarks and produced interpretable explanations (saliency-based visualizations and quantitative metrics) of the model's decisions, including temporal expression semantics.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that a facial-video model can both enhance predictive performance and generate hypotheses about its reasoning, contributing to interpretable, video-based depression assessment suitable for clinical contexts.

title: FAR-LIO: Enabling High-Speed Autonomy through Fast, Accurate, and Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry
comment: 8 pages, accepted for publication at IROS2026 (IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems 2026)
summary: Robust and accurate odometry estimation is essential in modern robotics. In environments characterized by highly dynamic motion and sensor noise, odometry estimation becomes increasingly challenging. Autonomous racing combines both factors in an unstructured setting, where minimizing odometry latency is essential for stable closed-loop control. This paper introduces FAR-LIO, a highly optimized CUDA-accelerated LiDAR-inertial odometry framework developed for Fast, Accurate, and Robust performance. Our system leverages a novel CUDA-based voxel hashmap to enable parallelized nearest-neighbor search and efficient map updates. We employ a sparsity-aware Generalized Iterative Closest Point algorithm with adaptive thresholding on top of the CUDA-based voxel hashmap with adaptive density to achieve low-latency without compromising accuracy. An Extended Kalman Filter serves as a robust backend. It utilizes an upsampling and delay compensation strategy to fuse the LiDAR odometry with high-frequency IMU data, thereby ensuring a robust and smooth odometry output. We evaluate FAR-LIO across four different sensor setups, using both public datasets and data from two autonomous racecars driving at speeds of up to 250 km/h. FAR-LIO achieves an average 6.9% reduction in the positional error and 38.4% lower runtime compared to state-of-the-art baselines on target hardware using a single parameter set. This demonstrates its computational efficiency and broad applicability. To build upon our work, our code is available open-source on https://github.com/TUMFTM/FAR-LIO.

AI.tldr: CUDA-accelerated LiDAR-inertial odometry framework FAR-LIO with a voxel hashmap, sparsity-aware generalized ICP with adaptive thresholding, and EKF fusion; achieves lower error and latency; open-source.
AI.motivation: Odometry under dynamic motion and sensor noise is challenging in autonomous racing and unstructured environments; need low-latency, accurate, robust estimation; existing methods struggle to balance accuracy and real-time performance.
AI.method: CUDA-accelerated voxel hashmap enabling parallel nearest-neighbor search and map updates; sparsity-aware generalized ICP with adaptive thresholding and density adaptation; EKF backend with upsampling and delay compensation for LiDAR-IMU fusion; evaluation across four sensor setups, including data from two racecars at up to 250 km/h; single parameter set; open-source.
AI.result: Average 6.9% reduction in positional error; 38.4% lower runtime compared with state-of-the-art baselines on target hardware; robust across sensor setups and high-speed data; code available.
AI.conclusion: FAR-LIO provides a fast, accurate, and robust LiDAR-inertial odometry solution with GPU acceleration and adaptive mechanisms for low latency and reliability; suitable for autonomous racing and other dynamic environments; broadly applicable and open-source.

title: How Modular Is a Frontier Mixture-of-Experts? A Pre-registered Causal Test in Which Apparent Expert Modularity Mostly Dissolves
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models route each token to a few of many experts, inviting the hypothesis that experts form functional modules tied to capabilities or languages. We test this causally on Command A+, a frontier open-weights MoE (218B total / 25B active; 128 experts, 8 active, +1 shared). We build a routing-mass atlas, pre-register six family-to-axis hypotheses before any intervention, and ablate each family at inference time against a size-matched random-expert null, measuring whether it selectively breaks its own axis (worst off-target effect at most one third of on-target). Crucially, we test the same families under four metrics and a held-out, independent-corpus run with bootstrap confidence intervals. Our finding is cautionary: robust functional modularity is rare and measurement-dependent. Of six pre-registered families, only one, the Arabic-language family, is a clean selective module that survives an independent corpus and a conservative statistical bar (1/6; a more permissive pre-registered point rule admits 3/6, but that count is threshold-sensitive). Every other family has a real causal effect yet fails selectivity, and its apparent modularity flips with the measurement: with the corpus, the metric, and the statistical bar. A positive control on Qwen3-30B-A3B recovers its published disjoint structure, confirming the method detects modularity when present. The verdict reproduces on the un-quantized BF16 model, ruling out a 4-bit quantization artifact. We conclude that ablation-based modularity verdicts are not safe unless the corpus, metric, and statistical bar are controlled. We release the atlas and ablation data.

AI.tldr: Sparse MoE modularity claims are not robust: only the Arabic-language family shows clean selective modularity across independent data; most families show non-selective causal effects that flip with measurement choices; ablation-based verdicts require careful control of corpus, metric, and statistics.
