AI.motivation: Test whether functional modules in sparse Mixture-of-Experts correspond to predefined capability/language families by causal ablations on a giant open-weights MoE model, using a routing-mass atlas and preregistered hypotheses.
AI.method: Construct a routing-mass atlas for a frontier MoE (218B total, 25B active; 128 experts, 8 active, +1 shared). Pre-register six family-to-axis hypotheses. Ablate each family at inference against a size-matched random-expert null. Assess selective axis disruption under four metrics and a held-out independent corpus with bootstrap CIs. Include a positive control (Qwen3-30B-A3B) to recover known modular structure. Test on unquantized BF16 to rule out quantization artifacts. Release atlas and ablation data.
AI.result: Only one of six families (Arabic-language) shows clean selective modularity that persists on an independent corpus and under conservative statistics (1/6). Others show real causal effects but lack selectivity; modularity depends on corpus, metric, and statistical bar, sometimes flipping with measurement. Positive control confirms detectability of modularity when present. The BF16 experiment rules out a 4-bit quantization artifact.
AI.conclusion: Ablation-based modularity verdicts are not safe unless corpus, metric, and statistical bar are tightly controlled; robust functional modularity in sparse MoE is rare and measurement-dependent.

title: ScaleHP: Estimating Hand Pose in Metric Space
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables; includes supplementary material
summary: Accurate metric-space hand pose estimation (HPE) is essential for immersive human-computer interaction and robotics. However, most existing methods predict poses in a root-relative coordinate system and cannot estimate the hand in absolute metric scale. In this work, we observe that the intrinsic proportional relationships among human hand bones encode stable anthropometric priors that implicitly correlate with the overall metric size of the hand. Leveraging this insight, we present ScaleHP, an end-to-end one-stage hand pose estimation framework that bypasses fragile extrinsic depth modules to recover the hand in metric space. ScaleHP employs a transformer-based decoder with a novel scale token to fuse multi-scale morphological and appearance features. By solving for metric coordinates through a perspective-constrained least-squares approach, we achieve high-precision pose estimation in the camera coordinate system. ScaleHP delivers state-of-the-art performance, including 35.8 CS-MPJPE on FreiHand and 4.6/5.9 PA-MPJPE on DexYCB and HO3Dv3. These results demonstrate that internal biological constraints significantly reduce relative geometry and absolute metric errors, offering a robust solution for generalized, real-world hand tracking.

AI.tldr: ScaleHP presents a one-stage transformer-based hand pose estimator that recovers absolute metric coordinates in the camera frame without extrinsic depth modules, by leveraging intrinsic hand bone priors via a scale token; it achieves state-of-the-art results on FreiHand, DexYCB, and HO3D v3.
AI.motivation: To obtain hand poses in absolute metric scale (metric-space) rather than root-relative coordinates, enabling robust real-world interaction. Existing methods rely on extrinsic depth modules and suffer from scale ambiguity; intrinsic anthropometric priors offer a stable cue to infer metric size.
AI.method: An end-to-end one-stage framework using a transformer-based decoder with a novel scale token to fuse multi-scale morphological and appearance features. Metric coordinates are recovered through a perspective-constrained least-squares solve, guided by intrinsic bone-proportional priors.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art metrics: 35.8 CS-MPJPE on FreiHand; 4.6/5.9 PA-MPJPE on DexYCB and HO3Dv3, demonstrating reduced relative geometry and absolute metric errors.
AI.conclusion: Internal biological constraints (bone proportions) effectively regularize the geometry, enabling robust metric-space hand tracking without depth extrinsics and improving generalization to real-world scenarios.

title: G2DP: Diffusion Planning with Spatio-Temporal Grid Guidance
summary: In autonomous driving, diffusion-based planners have emerged as a promising paradigm for robust motion planning in dense and interactive traffic, as they can effectively model diverse driving behaviors. However, their inherent stochasticity often requires explicit guidance during denoising to ensure safety and route adherence for robust closed-loop execution. Existing guidance typically relies on sparse, entity-centric geometric queries or post-hoc refinement, yielding limited situational awareness and fragile performance in interactive scenes. To address this issue, we propose G2DP (Grid-Guided Diffusion Planning), a diffusion-based planner that directly enforces dense environmental constraints through inference-time guidance. Specifically, G2DP constructs a differentiable spatio-temporal cost volume by fusing probabilistic future occupancy distributions with a route-progress map. By formulating this volume as a continuous safety energy functional, it injects dense gradients directly into the denoising loop, actively steering trajectory generation toward collision-free and progress-optimal regions. Extensive closed-loop evaluations show that G2DP achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuPlan, outperforming the strongest imitation-learning baseline by +7.2 points in reactive score. It further maintains top scores in zero-shot transfers to interPlan and DeepScenario benchmarks, with collision avoidance improving by +10.15 over the unguided approach on interPlan. These results demonstrate that spatio-temporal cost grids serve as an effective representation for robust guidance in diffusion-based planning.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based planner (G2DP) uses a differentiable spatio-temporal cost grid to guide denoising, yielding safer, progress-focused trajectories in autonomous driving; achieves state-of-the-art on nuPlan and strong zero-shot transfers.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based planners are inherently stochastic and currently rely on sparse, entity-centric queries or post-hoc refinement, which limit situational awareness and robustness in interactive traffic. Dense environmental guidance is needed for safety and route adherence in closed-loop execution.
AI.method: Construct a differentiable spatio-temporal cost volume by fusing probabilistic future occupancy with a route-progress map. Formulate this volume as a continuous safety energy functional and inject its gradients into the denoising loop to steer trajectory generation toward collision-free and progress-optimal regions.
AI.result: G2DP achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuPlan, outperforming the strongest imitation-learning baseline by +7.2 points in reactive score. It maintains top scores in zero-shot transfers to interPlan and DeepScenario benchmarks, with collision avoidance improvements of +10.15 over the unguided approach on interPlan.
AI.conclusion: Dense spatio-temporal cost grids are an effective representation for robust guidance in diffusion-based planning, enabling improved safety, adherence, and performance in interactive driving scenarios.

title: Speculative Decoding at Temperature Zero: A Scoped Safety-Invariance Screen with a 48,072-Sample Expansion
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates inference by letting a draft model propose tokens for a target model to verify, raising a concrete safety question: at temperature zero, can draft-side behavior leak into safety-scored outputs? We answer with Typical-Acceptance Invariance Screen (TAIS), a behavioral-equivalence screen that pairs target-only and speculative outputs on the same safety battery and requires byte-identity evidence, TOST equivalence at +/-3pp, and per-task Cohen's h below a calibrated null cutoff of |h| < 0.1. Applied to a 16,783-sample confirmatory core plus 44,066 matched expansion samples (fp16/bf16 execution, canonical and DPO-adversarial drafts, GPTQ-4bit drafts, two seeds, and four safety benchmarks), the tested temperature-zero vLLM stacks show no detectable safety divergence under TAIS. The largest absolute Cohen's h on matched target-only versus speculative refusal is 0.024, roughly an order of magnitude below the conventional trivial-effect floor; 25 of 27 per-task TOST contrasts pass at the +/-3pp margin (the two non-pass contrasts are capability-domain Wald-CI edge cases at identical ceiling rates, not genuine non-equivalence); the DPO-adversarial draft produces byte-identical output to the canonical draft across 4,006 samples; and bf16 changes 36%-53% of output bytes without moving any per-task safety rate outside equivalence. A separate 4,006-sample 70B production-scale probe, which lacks a matched 70B target-only arm and is therefore not counted as a TAIS pass, produces AdvBench refusal 0.839 over 700 AdvBench completions with 95% Wilson CI [0.809, 0.864]. We make no claim about sampling temperatures, untested frameworks, untested model families, or tree-speculation variants such as EAGLE and Medusa.

AI.tldr: Introduces TAIS, a behavioral-equivalence screen for speculative decoding, and finds no detectable safety divergence between target-only and speculative outputs at temperature zero across large datasets; byte-identical DPO-adversarial drafts; bf16 changes many output bytes without affecting safety; results are limited to tested stacks and conditions.
AI.motivation: Address the safety question: can draft-side behavior influence safety-rated outputs during speculative decoding at temperature zero? Provide rigorous, byte-identity- and equivalence-based testing across extensive samples and configurations.
AI.method: Propose and apply Typical-Acceptance Invariance Screen (TAIS): require byte-identity evidence, TOST equivalence at ±3pp, and per-task Cohen's h < 0.1. Evaluation on 16,783 confirmatory core plus 44,066 matched expansion samples (fp16/bf16, canonical and DPO-adversarial drafts, GPTQ-4bit drafts, two seeds, four safety benchmarks); plus a 4,006-sample 70B production-scale probe with AdvBench results. Tests cover temperature-zero vLLM stacks; report Cohen's h, TOST pass rates, and bytes-changes by bf16; comparison of target-only vs speculative outputs; includes DPO-adversarial draft and edge-case analyses.
AI.result: No detectable safety divergence under TAIS. Largest absolute Cohen's h on matched target-only vs speculative refusal: 0.024. 25 of 27 per-task TOST contrasts pass at ±3pp; two non-pass contrasts are edge cases at identical ceiling rates, not non-equivalence. DPO-adversarial draft yields byte-identical output to canonical draft across 4,006 samples. bf16 changes 36%-53% of output bytes without altering any per-task safety rate outside equivalence. Production-scale probe (without matched target-only arm) yields AdvBench refusal 0.839 over 700 completions (95% Wilson CI [0.809, 0.864]).
AI.conclusion: Within the tested configurations and conditions (temperature zero, specified stacks, and safety benchmarks), speculative decoding does not exhibit detectable safety leakage relative to the target model per TAIS. The results support behavioral invariance under speculative decoding in the tested settings, though generalization to other sampling temperatures, frameworks, model families, or tree-speculation variants remains untested.

title: SSMNBench: Diagnosing Image-based Cross-View Human-Object Understanding via Single-View Sufficiency and Multi-View Necessity
comment: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV). 32 pages, 10 figures. The code is available at: $ \href{https://github.com/gtc-gh/SSMNBench}{\text{SSMNBench}} $
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable progress in single-image perception, yet their ability to reason about complex cross-view human-centric scenes remains largely unverified. Current multi-view benchmarks evaluate models using a fixed "bag of frames" and thus conflate a model's robustness to visual distraction with its genuine ability to fuse fragmented cross-view evidence. To address this issue, we introduce SSMNBench, a diagnostic benchmark comprising 3,300 curated QA pairs for cross-view human and human-object understanding. SSMNBench uniquely categorizes tasks into Single-View Sufficiency (SVS) and Multi-View Necessity (MVN). By systematically perturbing view availability across 17 state-of-the-art MLLMs, critical limitations are revealed: models suffer from severe "distraction degradation" when presented with redundant views (SVS), and fail to integrate fragmented geometric evidence across cameras (MVN). Our evaluations demonstrate that modern MLLMs rely on multiple single-image semantic averaging and view preference rather than genuine cross-view synthesis. By exposing these fundamental vulnerabilities, SSMNBench provides a rigorous diagnostic framework to drive the advancement of future cross-view-aware multimodal architectures. The code is available at: $ \href{https://github.com/gtc-gh/SSMNBench}{\text{SSMNBench}} $

AI.tldr: SSMNBench is a diagnostic benchmark with 3,300 QA pairs to test cross-view human understanding in MLLMs, exposing vulnerabilities such as distraction degradation and poor cross-view integration across 17 models, and offering a framework and open code.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where existing MLLMs are evaluated on fixed bag-of-frames, conflating distraction robustness with true cross-view fusion, and to enable systematic cross-view reasoning evaluation for human-centric scenes.
AI.method: Construct SSMNBench with 3,300 QA pairs, categorize tasks into Single-View Sufficiency (SVS) and Multi-View Necessity (MVN), perturb view availability, evaluate 17 state-of-the-art MLLMs, and analyze failure modes focusing on distraction and cross-view integration.
AI.result: Models exhibit severe distraction degradation with redundant views (SVS) and fail to integrate fragmented geometric evidence across cameras (MVN); contemporary MLLMs rely on multiple single-image semantic averaging and view preference rather than genuine cross-view synthesis.
AI.conclusion: SSMNBench provides a rigorous diagnostic framework to drive the development of cross-view-aware multimodal architectures and expose fundamental vulnerabilities; code is available at the linked GitHub repository.

title: In-Context World Modeling for Robotic Control
summary: Modern Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often fail to generalize to novel setups, such as altered camera viewpoints or robot morphologies, because they are typically conditioned only on current observations and language instructions. By ignoring the underlying system configuration as a variable, these models implicitly assume a fixed execution context encountered during training, necessitating data-intensive fine-tuning for any new environment. In this work, we introduce In-Context World Modeling (ICWM), a framework that treats system identification as an in-context adaptation problem. ICWM enables robot policies to autonomously infer essential system variables from a short history of self-generated, task-agnostic interactions. Unlike traditional In-Context Learning that uses demonstrations to specify what task to perform, ICWM leverages the context window to understand how the system operates. By processing these interactions before task execution, the model implicitly captures the world dynamics of the current system, enabling adaptation to novel configurations without parameter updates. Extensive experiments in simulation and on real-world robot platforms demonstrate that ICWM significantly outperforms standard VLA baselines on novel camera viewpoints.

AI.tldr: ICWM enables in-context adaptation to infer current system dynamics from a short history of self-generated interactions, allowing VLA-based robot policies to generalize to novel configurations without parameter updates.
AI.motivation: VLA models struggle to generalize beyond fixed training setups due to conditioning only on current observations and language; reliance on data-intensive fine-tuning for new environments.
AI.method: Introduce In-Context World Modeling (ICWM); use a context window to process self-generated, task-agnostic interactions to infer essential system variables; adaptation occurs via in-context processing without updating model parameters.
AI.result: In simulation and on real robots, ICWM outperforms standard VLA baselines on tasks with novel camera viewpoints, demonstrating improved generalization to unseen configurations.
AI.conclusion: ICWM enables zero-shot adaptation to changing system configurations by embedding world dynamics into the context, reducing fine-tuning requirements and broadening applicability of VLA policies.

title: A Framework for Directed Hypergraph Signal Processing via tensor t-SVD
comment: 4 pages, 6 figures. Presented as an oral presentation at the 9th Graph Signal Processing Workshop (GSP 2026), June 8-10, 2026, Madrid, Spain
summary: We introduce Directed Hypergraph Signal Processing (DHGSP), a unified framework that extends graph signal processing to accommodate both higher-order (polyadic) and asymmetric (directional) relationships simultaneously. Using the tensor singular value decomposition (t-SVD) within the t-product algebra, we define a novel adjacency tensor for directed hypergraphs, a topologically faithful shift operator, and a lossless Directed Hypergraph Fourier Transform (t-DHGFT). Experiments on real traffic networks demonstrate that DHGSP outperforms matrix-based (graph and digraph) and undirected tensor-based (hypergraph) baselines in denoising tasks.

AI.tldr: This work proposes Directed Hypergraph Signal Processing (DHGSP), unifying higher-order and directional relations via t-SVD in t-product algebra to define an adjacency tensor, a faithful shift operator, and a lossless t-DHGFT; it shows superior denoising on real traffic networks over matrix-based and undirected tensor baselines.
AI.motivation: To extend graph signal processing to simultaneously model polyadic (higher-order) and asymmetric (directional) relations, addressing limitations of existing graph, digraph, and hypergraph frameworks and enabling lossless spectral analysis.
AI.method: Utilize tensor singular value decomposition (t-SVD) within the t-product framework to construct a directed hypergraph adjacency tensor, derive a topologically faithful shift operator, and define a lossless directed hypergraph Fourier transform (t-DHGFT).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real traffic networks demonstrates that DHGSP outperforms matrix-based (graph and digraph) and undirected tensor-based (hypergraph) baselines in denoising tasks.
AI.conclusion: DHGSP provides a unified, lossless spectral framework for directed hypergraphs, enabling improved signal processing for complex networks with higher-order and directional interactions; results motivate broader application and further empirical studies.

title: Reward-Conditioned Attention: How Reward Design Shapes What Autonomous Driving Agents See
summary: We investigate how reward design shapes the internal attention patterns of reinforcement learning agents trained for autonomous driving. Using three Perceiver-based agents that share identical architectures and training data but differ only in their reward configurations$\unicode{x2014}$ranging from basic violation penalties to continuous proximity penalties$\unicode{x2014}$we analyze cross-attention allocation across 50 real-world scenarios from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset. A central methodological finding is that naïve pooling of timesteps across episodes substantially underestimates the attention$\unicode{x2013}$risk relationship; within-episode correlation with Fisher z-transform aggregation is the appropriate statistic and reveals a robustly positive link between collision risk and agent-directed attention. Building on this validated methodology, we demonstrate two reward-conditioned effects: agents trained with navigation rewards allocate up to $2.0\times$ more attention to GPS-path tokens than those trained with additional proximity penalties$\unicode{x2014}$and $4.7\times$ more than agents with no navigation incentive$\unicode{x2014}$revealing that reward content directly determines which scene elements the encoder prioritizes, and continuous time-to-collision penalties create a $\textit{learned vigilance prior}$$\unicode{x2014}$elevated resting agent surveillance maintained throughout collision-free phases. In several scenarios, the complete-reward and minimal-reward models exhibit opposite attention$\unicode{x2013}$risk correlation directions, demonstrating that reward design can qualitatively reverse attentional strategy rather than merely modulating its magnitude. These results suggest that attention analysis is a practical diagnostic for verifying that a reward function produces the intended representational behaviour in safety-critical RL systems.

AI.tldr: Reward design in RL for autonomous driving shapes internal attention and scene priors; naive cross-episode pooling underestimates attention–risk coupling, whereas within-episode Fisher z-transform aggregation reveals a robust positive link between collision risk and agent-directed attention. Navigation incentives push attention toward GPS-path tokens (up to 2.0x vs proximity penalties; 4.7x vs no navigation incentive). Proximity penalties and repeated TC penalties induce a learned vigilance prior. Complete vs minimal rewards can reverse attention–risk direction. Attention analysis is a practical diagnostic for rewarding safety-critical RL systems.
AI.motivation: Understand how reward signals sculpt internal representations/attention in safety-critical RL to ensure that learned behavior aligns with safety and task goals, enabling verification and safer deployment.
AI.method: Three Perceiver-based agents with identical architectures and training data, differing only in reward configurations (ranging from basic violation penalties to continuous proximity penalties). Evaluate cross-attention allocation across 50 real-world Waymo Open Motion scenarios. Compare naive timestep pooling with within-episode Fisher z-transform aggregation to assess attention–risk relationships. Analyze how navigation vs proximity incentive shapes attention to scene tokens (e.g., GPS-path).
AI.result: Key methodological finding: within-episode Fisher z-transform aggregation reveals a robust positive association between collision risk and agent-directed attention, which naive cross-episode pooling misses. Reward-conditioned effects include: (i) navigation rewards increasing attention to GPS-path tokens by up to 2.0x relative to proximity penalties and 4.7x relative to no navigation incentive; (ii) continuous time-to-collision penalties promoting a learned vigilance prior with elevated surveillance even during collision-free phases. In several scenarios, complete-reward and minimal-reward models exhibit opposite attention–risk directions, indicating that reward design can qualitatively reverse attentional strategy. These results support attention analysis as a practical diagnostic for ensuring reward functions yield the intended representational behavior in safety-critical RL.
AI.conclusion: Attention analysis provides a practical diagnostic to verify that a reward function induces the intended internal representations in safety-critical RL. Reward design can qualitatively alter attentional strategies, not merely their magnitude, suggesting careful consideration of reward content is essential for safe deployment.

title: Auto-Labelling-Based Domain Transfer for 3D Object Detection on a Bicycle-Mounted LiDAR Platform
summary: Reliable 3D perception of vulnerable road users (VRUs) such as cyclists and pedestrians is essential for their safety in urban traffic and a core requirement for autonomous driving (AD). Alongside advances in vehicle-based perception, research increasingly equips bicycles with sensors to study traffic from a perspective native to VRUs. Such platforms still rely on LiDAR detectors originally trained on vehicle data, yet annotated 3D data from a cyclist's perspective is scarce. How well these detectors generalise to this setting has not been evaluated. We present a 3D object detection benchmark of 1,027 annotated LiDAR keyframes (over 18,000 3D bounding boxes) from the FUSE-Bike platform in urban Munich. We evaluate four nuScenes-pre-trained detectors against 1,854 human-verified ground-truth (GT) boxes both in their original form and after finetuning on training labels produced by a VRU-dedicated auto-labelling pipeline that requires no manual annotation. The zero-shot domain gap is concentrated on the VRU classes. Finetuning recovers most of it, improving mean average precision (mAP) by up to 23.4 points with the largest gains on pedestrians and cyclists, and the adapted detectors even surpass the quality of the auto-labels they were trained on. The benchmark provides a reproducible baseline for VRU-centric 3D detection and shows that auto-labels are a viable substitute for manual annotation when adapting vehicle-trained detectors to a cyclist platform.

AI.tldr: Introduces a VRU-focused 3D object detection benchmark using LiDAR data from the FUSE-Bike platform; evaluates vehicle-trained detectors in zero-shot, then finetunes with auto-labeled VRU data, significantly closing the domain gap and sometimes outperforming the auto-labels themselves.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between vehicle-centric 3D detectors and VRU-perspective data; addressing scarcity of manually annotated VRU LiDAR data to enable robust autonomous driving around cyclists and pedestrians.
AI.method: Assembles a benchmark with 1,027 LiDAR keyframes (over 18,000 3D bounding boxes) from FUSE-Bike in urban Munich. Evaluates four nuScenes-pre-trained detectors on 1,854 GT boxes, in original form and after finetuning on training labels generated by a VRU-dedicated auto-labeling pipeline that requires no manual annotation.
AI.result: Zero-shot domain gap is pronounced for VRU classes. Finetuning recovers most of the gap, boosting mean average precision (mAP) by up to 23.4 points, with the largest gains for pedestrians and cyclists. Adapted detectors can surpass the quality of the auto-labels they were trained on.
AI.conclusion: Provides a reproducible VRU-centric 3D detection baseline and demonstrates that auto-labels can substitute manual annotation when adapting vehicle-trained detectors to a cyclist platform.

title: RoboAtlas: Contextual Active SLAM
comment: Alexander Schperberg and Shivam K. Panda made equal contribution
summary: We present RoboAtlas, a contextual Active SLAM framework that adaptively balances geometric exploration and semantic reasoning using a scalable 3D semantic mapping system, OpenRoboVox. RoboAtlas integrates frontier exploration, global semantic-map reasoning, and egocentric VLM-based reasoning through a contextual multi-armed bandit that transitions from exploration to semantically guided navigation as scene understanding improves. We evaluate the system in simulation and on a Unitree Go2 robot in large-scale real-world environments exceeding 1800 m2 with approx. 30k mapped semantic instances, achieving a 100% task success rate. On the GOAT-Bench "Val Unseen" benchmark, RoboAtlas achieves state-of-the-art performance with highest reported success rate (SR) of 90.6%, using GPT-4o, improving over the strongest prior baseline by 17.8 percentage points in SR. Using the much smaller Qwen2.5-VL-7B model, it still achieves 88.8% SR, outperforming all baselines using GPT-4o in SR, and revealing the importance of the information gained by our semantic mapping framework over simply replacing the underlying foundation model. The results demonstrate that grounding foundation models with large-scale 3D semantic maps enables robust and efficient contextual Active SLAM.

AI.tldr: A contextual Active SLAM framework, RoboAtlas, fuses frontier exploration, global semantic-map reasoning, and egocentric VLM-based reasoning via a contextual multi-armed bandit to balance exploration and semantic navigation, achieving state-of-the-art results on large-scale real-world tests and the GOAT-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: To advance Active SLAM by grounding exploration with large-scale 3D semantic mapping, enabling robust decision-making and efficient navigation through semantic understanding and foundation-model reasoning.
AI.method: RoboAtlas integrates frontier exploration, global semantic-map reasoning, and egocentric VLM-based reasoning using a contextual multi-armed bandit. It leverages a scalable 3D semantic mapping system (OpenRoboVox), transitions from exploration to semantically guided navigation as scene understanding improves, and is validated in simulation and on a Unitree Go2 across large real-world environments (>1800 m2; ~30k semantic instances).
AI.result: In experiments, RoboAtlas achieves 100% task success rate in large-scale real-world environments. On the GOAT-Bench 'Val Unseen' benchmark, it reaches state-of-the-art 90.6% SR using GPT-4o, improving the previous best by 17.8 percentage points; with the smaller Qwen2.5-VL-7B model, it still achieves 88.8% SR, outperforming baselines that used GPT-4o. These results demonstrate the benefit of grounding foundation models with large-scale 3D semantic maps for robust contextual Active SLAM.
AI.conclusion: Grounding foundation models with large-scale 3D semantic maps enables robust and efficient contextual Active SLAM, illustrating the value of semantic mapping in guiding exploration and navigation.

title: Forget to Improve: On-Device LLM-Agent Continual Learning via Budget-Curated Memory
summary: On-device language-model agents improve by accumulating experience in retrieved memory rather than by updating weights. This memory is hard-bounded and exposed: it consumes RAM and energy, reaches peers through a thin uplink, and becomes an attack surface because it is writable by what the agent reads. Existing systems each cover one part of this problem: agentic memories grow without a budget, on-device methods keep entries by success alone, and poisoning is studied mainly as an attack rather than as a memory-governance problem. We propose \sys{}, a single net-value-per-byte score that governs an agent's experience-memory lifecycle. The main idea is to let the budget act as the curator: each entry is scored as value minus harm, per byte, so one ruler decides what to keep, share, and trust. \sys{} makes three decisions: (1) \textbf{KEEP} evicts low-value bytes under the RAM and energy budget; (2) \textbf{SHARE} sends an insight only when its value exceeds its uplink cost; and (3) \textbf{TRUST} gates a peer entry by provenance. On language-model-agent task-drift benchmarks and a real heterogeneous Jetson testbed with two robot-arm nodes and a hub, \sys{} reduces memory by $2.7\times$ and uplink by $2.4\times$, drives injection success from 0.75 to zero, and raises accuracy on cases corrupted by poison or stale memory. Curating by net value reduces footprint, energy, uplink, and injection success together without reducing accuracy. In this setting, forgetting by net value improves the agent rather than weakening it.

AI.tldr: Sys uses a per-byte net-value score to govern an on-device agent's experience-memory lifecycle, reducing memory, uplink, and attack surface while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: On-device language-model agents accumulate experience in memory under tight RAM and energy budgets, exposing vulnerability and becoming an attack surface. Existing approaches address parts of the problem but lack unified governance over what to keep, share, and trust.
AI.method: Define a net value per byte (value minus harm) and apply it to entries in the agent's experience-memory. Decisions: KEEP (evict low-value bytes within budget), SHARE (only share high-value insights relative to uplink cost), and TRUST (gate provenance of peer entries). Evaluate on language-model-agent task-drift benchmarks and a Jetson-based robot-arm testbed, measuring memory, uplink, injection resistance, and accuracy under poisoning/stale memory.
AI.result: Sys reduces memory by 2.7x and uplink by 2.4x; eliminates injection success (from 0.75 to 0); improves accuracy on poison- or stale-memory scenarios; overall memory governance improves agent performance without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Net-value-based forgetting curbs footprint and attack surface while preserving or enhancing effectiveness; forgetting by net value can benefit on-device agents.

title: Silent Failures in Physics-Informed Neural Networks: Parameter Poisoning and the Limits of Loss-Based Validation
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) embed governing equations in their loss function, enabling mesh-free solutions to partial differential equations. Low training loss is treated as evidence that the learned solution is physically correct. This paper shows that assumption breaks down when encoded physics are incorrect. By perturbing PDE parameters before training, a setting we describe as physics parameter poisoning or parameter misspecification, we produce models that train to low loss but give incorrect answers; we treat the perturbation schedule as sensitivity analysis rather than only as a security threat, and none of our claims requires an adversary. Achieving low residual loss does not discriminate accurate from inaccurate solutions: poisoned models reach losses at or below the clean baseline yet differ by large margins, so driving the residual down is not evidence of physical accuracy. Across three PDE systems (Burgers equation, Navier-Stokes cavity, and convection-diffusion), poisoned models match or beat the clean-model training loss while their solutions differ by up to 71% in the fixed sweep and up to 128% under adversarial search; at Cavity Re=400 the poisoned loss falls below the clean baseline. We define a detection difficulty ratio R (solution error divided by training loss) to summarize how invisible the corruption is, though cross-PDE comparison is complicated by differences in loss scale. We test six candidate defenses, none of which reliably detects corruption across all regimes. We propose a post-hoc defense: sweeping the PDE residual loss across parameter values without retraining. The loss minimum recovers the true training parameter without external data, and generalizes across all three PDE systems. The effect holds across five network architectures (8.7K to 133K parameters), is bidirectional, and is confirmed across multiple random seeds.

AI.tldr: Low residual loss in PINNs is not evidence of physical accuracy; parameter misspecification can yield low loss with wrong solutions; a post-hoc residual sweep across parameter values can recover the true parameters without external data, generalizing across PDEs, architectures, and seeds.
AI.motivation: Assess the reliability of using residual loss as a proxy for physics-consistent PINN solutions and explore vulnerabilities to misspecified governing equations; develop defenses and a data-free parameter recovery method.
AI.method: Introduce physics parameter poisoning by perturbing PDE parameters before training. Evaluate on three PDE systems (Burgers, Navier–Stokes cavity, convection–diffusion) with fixed sweep and adversarial search. Test six defenses; define detection difficulty ratio R = solution error / training loss. Propose post-hoc defense: sweep the PDE residual loss across parameter values without retraining to identify the true training parameter. Validate across five network architectures (8.7K–133K parameters) and multiple random seeds.
AI.result: Poisoned models achieve low residual loss comparable to or below clean baselines, yet their solutions differ significantly (up to 71% in fixed sweep and up to 128% under adversarial search). At Re=400 for the cavity, poisoned loss falls below the clean baseline. Six candidate defenses fail to reliably detect corruption across regimes. The post-hoc residual-sweep method recovers the true training parameter without external data, generalizes across all three PDE systems, and works across various architectures and seeds.
AI.conclusion: Residual minimization alone is not a valid indicator of physical accuracy in PINNs under physics misspecification. A post-hoc residual-sweep across parameters provides a data-free mechanism to detect and recover the correct training parameters, with broad applicability and robustness across systems.

title: Steering Vision-Language Models with Joint Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have shown promise for analyzing language models, but applying them to vision-language models (VLMs) often yields representations that are difficult to use as controllable cross-modal steering directions. We introduce the Joint Sparse Autoencoder (JSAE), which uses an explicit alignment constraint to jointly factorize sequence-pooled vision and language activations into shared, interpretable image/caption-level features. Applied to LLaVA, JSAE recovers cross-modal features for recognizable concepts (e.g., food and animals). Through bidirectional interventions (additive steering and suppression), we observe a layer-dependent asymmetry under our protocol: additive steering peaks at mid-to-late (pre-output) layers and weakens at both ends, whereas suppression scores remain within a comparable range across all probed layers within statistical noise. Experiments on three VLMs, namely LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral-7B, Llama3-LLaVA-8B, and the MoE-based Qwen3-VL-30B, show related layer-localized effects across architectures. Together, these results suggest that explicitly aligned sparse representations support more controllable intervention-based analysis of multimodal features, within an identifiable layer range, than the unconstrained alternatives tested here.

AI.tldr: Joint Sparse Autoencoder with an explicit alignment constraint factorizes multimodal activations into shared image/caption features, enabling more controllable, layer-localized cross-modal interventions across multiple vision-language models.
AI.motivation: SAEs struggle to yield usable, controllable cross-modal directions in VLMs. An explicit alignment aims to produce interpretable, shared representations that facilitate steering and analysis.
AI.method: Introduce JSAE with an explicit alignment constraint to jointly factorize sequence-pooled vision and language activations into shared image/caption-level features. Train/validate on LLaVA. Use bidirectional interventions (additive steering and suppression) to probe layer-wise effects. Evaluate across three VLMs: LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral-7B, Llama3-LLaVA-8B, Qwen3-VL-30B.
AI.result: Recovered cross-modal features corresponding to recognizable concepts (e.g., food, animals). Additive steering shows layer-dependent asymmetry: peaks in mid-to-late layers (pre-output) and weakens at extremes; suppression remains similar across layers within noise. Effects are layer-localized but observed across architectures, indicating transferability.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly aligned sparse representations improve controllability of intervention-based analysis of multimodal features, within identifiable layer ranges, compared with unconstrained alternatives.

title: Learning Robot Visual Navigation in Crowds via Intention-Aware Scene Representations
summary: Robot crowd navigation requires the ability to infer human intentions while accounting for the structural constraints of the environment. Currently, deep reinforcement learning (DRL) provides a promising method for learning navigation policies that understand human intentions. However, most of them rely on limited scene representations, treating pedestrians as simple 2D points and ignoring rich visual cues from both humans and the environment. To address this issue, we introduce iCrowdNav, a novel visual crowd navigation method with intention-aware scene representations, to encode behavioral and structural context from egocentric visual observations. Our method employs two key components: a spatio-temporal encoder for extracting occupancy features of the scene, and Intent-Interact Former (I$^2$ Former), an attention-based module that encodes human poses to infer pedestrians' motion intentions. These features are integrated into a compact state embedding that supports effective DRL policy training. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves superior performance over baselines, and real-world deployment demonstrates vision-based crowd navigation.

AI.tldr: iCrowdNav combines egocentric visual scene encoding with an intention-aware attention module to improve DRL-based crowd navigation, showing superior performance and real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of previous DRL-based crowd navigation that treat pedestrians as points and ignore rich visual cues, by incorporating occupancy-aware spatio-temporal features and pose-based intention inference from egocentric observations.
AI.method: Introduce a spatio-temporal encoder for occupancy features plus Intent-Interact Former (I^2 Former), an attention-based module that encodes human poses to infer motion intentions; integrate these into a compact state embedding for DRL policy training.
AI.result: Empirical results on benchmarks show superior performance over baselines; real-world deployment validates vision-based crowd navigation.
AI.conclusion: Visual, intention-aware scene representations enhance DRL-based crowd navigation, enabling more accurate anticipation of pedestrian behavior and practical deployment; suggests avenues for richer perceptual representations in navigation tasks.

title: ATMA: Length-Invariant Language Modeling via Polar Attention and Gated-Delta Compression Memory
summary: Modern large language models based on softmax scaled-dot-product attention are constrained by their training sequence length: as the key-value sequence grows, softmax probability mass can dilute across a wider distribution, inducing activation shift and long-context performance collapse. Moreover, long-context language modeling faces a structural tension: a sliding-window attention core maintains a bounded local representation and low perplexity but is blind to long-range dependencies, while full-context attention preserves global recall but suffers from out-of-distribution perplexity explosion. To resolve these limitations, we introduce ATMA, a hybrid convolutional-attention architecture that integrates a novel three-channel attention mechanism. ATMA factorizes the attention mixing step into: (1) a count-blind, unit-vector direction channel, (2) a bounded magnitude channel driven by the participation ratio of effective matches over an extreme-value-corrected null sink, and (3) a long-term recurrent compression memory optimized via a gated-delta fast-weights rule. Neither the Polar Attention core nor the recurrent memory is sufficient alone; their combination enables monotonic perplexity reduction and high-fidelity long-range retrieval simultaneously. We evaluate ATMA using a 100-run factorial ablation sweep, demonstrating that the combined Polar + memory model maintains induction needle-in-a-haystack retrieval accuracy above 90% out to 64K tokens (32 times the training length of 2K) while its document perplexity improves monotonically, outperforming softmax-based memory baselines which collapse at extreme context lengths. Code: https://github.com/kreasof-ai/atma

AI.tldr: ATMA is a hybrid convolution-attention model that combines three attention channels with a long-term compression memory to enable scalable long-context retrieval and monotonic perplexity improvements beyond softmax-based attention.
AI.motivation: Softmax attention dilutes probability mass as the key-value sequence grows, causing activation shifts and long-context performance collapse. The trade-off between sliding-window (local) attention and full-context attention (global) remains unresolved.
AI.method: Introduce ATMA with a three-channel attention mechanism that factorizes the attention mixing step into: (1) a count-blind unit-vector direction channel, (2) a bounded magnitude channel driven by the participation ratio over an extreme-value-corrected null sink, and (3) a long-term recurrent compression memory optimized via a gated-delta fast-weights rule. Integrate with a Polar Attention core; perform a 100-run factorial ablation sweep to assess contributions; evaluate long-context retrieval up to 64K tokens.
AI.result: The combination of Polar attention core and the recurrent memory yields monotonic perplexity reduction and high-fidelity long-range retrieval. The model maintains induction needle-in-a-haystack retrieval accuracy above 90% out to 64K tokens (32× training length of 2K) and shows monotonic improvement in document perplexity, outperforming softmax-based memory baselines that collapse at extreme context lengths.
AI.conclusion: Neither the Polar core nor the recurrent memory alone suffices; their integration enables both robust long-range retrieval and stable perplexity. Code for ATMA is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Towards a Dynamic and Fixed-budget Memory Bank for Efficient Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Currently, streaming video understanding is still a daunting task for existing \emph{multimodal large language models} (MLLMs). Its difficulties not only lie in handling the ever-increasing video frames, but also in the unpredictability of future video content and input instructions. In this paper, we study this task from the perspective of constructing a dynamic but fixed-budget memory bank, and propose a novel and training-free approach termed \emph{\textbf{CausalMem}}. CausalMem is dedicated to constructing a dynamic visual memory update mechanism, thereby maximizing the amount of information in streaming video within a limited memory space, much like the human brain. In practice, CausalMem estimates the redundancy of visual tokens and updates the memory bank via an online semantic basis, which models the principal semantics of the observed video stream. To validate CausalMem, we apply it to two representative MLLMs, namely LLaVA-OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL respectively, and conduct extensive experiments on both streaming and offline video understanding benchmarks. The experimental results not only show the great advantages than existing methods under both streaming and offline settings, \emph{e.g.}, $+3.2\%$ and $+3.0\%$ average accuracy gains respectively, but also witness the superior semantic preservation for streaming videos, \emph{e.g.}, using 12$k$ token budgets to memorize hour-long streaming videos, which achieves more than \textbf{20$\times$} visual token compression ratio and only occupies about \textbf{82 MB} storage. \textbf{Our code} is given in \href{https://github.com/hktk07/CausalMem}{CausalMem}.

AI.tldr: CausalMem provides a training-free dynamic memory mechanism for streaming video understanding in multimodal LLMs, achieving high compression and improved accuracy by online semantic basis updating.
AI.motivation: Streaming videos pose growing frame counts and uncertain future content; fixed-budget memory banks are needed to maintain information without retraining.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic memory update rule that estimates token redundancy and updates memory via an online semantic basis representing main video semantics; applied to LLaVA-OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL; evaluation on streaming and offline benchmarks; memory-efficient with ~82 MB and 20x token compression; no training required.
AI.result: Improves average accuracy by ~3.0–3.2 percentage points on streaming and offline benchmarks; achieves 12k-token budget for hour-long streams with substantial compression.
AI.conclusion: CausalMem is effective at maximizing information retention within fixed memory budgets and preserves semantics in streaming video understanding; offers a practical, training-free solution with strong empirical performance.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning-Enhanced Event-Triggered Data-Driven Predictive Control for a 3D Cable-Driven Soft Robotic Arm
summary: Soft robots are challenging to control due to their nonlinear and time-varying dynamics. Data-enabled predictive control (DeePC) offers a model-free alternative by directly leveraging measured input-output trajectories to construct a predictive controller. However, its receding-horizon formulation requires solving a constrained optimization problem at every sampling instant, which can be computationally demanding for real-time deployment on resource-limited robotic platforms.To address this limitation, we propose an adaptive reinforcement-learning-based event-triggered DeePC (RL-ET-DeePC) framework for soft robotic control. A model-free RL policy is trained to determine when to invoke the DeePC optimizer based on the current system state representation, thereby reducing unnecessary optimization calls while preserving closed-loop performance.Simulation results show that RL-ET-DeePC reduces optimization frequency by up to 66% compared to periodic DeePC, while maintaining comparable tracking accuracy. Hardware experiments on a three-dimensional cable-driven soft robotic arm demonstrate zero-shot transfer, achieving a 34% reduction in optimization frequency with tracking accuracy comparable to periodic DeePC and more consistent performance than a static threshold-based event-triggered baseline.

AI.tldr: Adaptive RL-ET-DeePC lowers DeePC optimization frequency in soft-robot control while preserving tracking accuracy; hardware results show zero-shot transfer with significant computation reductions.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational burden of receding-horizon DeePC for nonlinear, time-varying soft robots by reducing unnecessary optimization calls without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Train a model-free RL policy to decide when to invoke the DeePC optimizer based on current state representations, creating an adaptive, event-triggered DeePC framework.
AI.result: Simulations show up to 66% reduction in DeePC optimization calls; hardware experiments on a 3D cable-driven soft arm show a 34% reduction with comparable tracking and more consistent performance than a static threshold baseline; zero-shot transfer demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: RL-ET-DeePC provides a practical, data-driven approach to real-time soft-robot control, balancing computational efficiency with predictive performance through learned event-triggering.

title: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Intrusion Detection Framework for Cybersecurity in Smart Renewable Energy Grids
summary: The accelerated digitalization of renewable energy smart grids through IoT sensors, AMI, and SCADA systems has significantly expanded the attack surface for sophisticated cyberattacks, FDI attacks that stealthily distort state estimation and DoS/DDoS attacks that flood communication channels. Current IDS, however, exhibit three inherent limitations: inadequate modeling of the temporal progression of multi-step attacks, degraded scalability under extremely skewed class distributions of standard benchmark datasets, and restricted generalization across heterogeneous network environments. In this study, we present a Hybrid CNN-LSTM IDS that jointly exploits CNN-based spatial feature extraction and LSTM-based temporal sequence modeling, enabling the detection of instantaneous volumetric anomalies and gradually evolving low and slow-attack campaigns in real time. The model was trained using a seven-step preprocessing workflow comprising missing-value imputation, min-max normalization, one-hot encoding, SMOTE class balancing, mutual-information feature selection, causal temporal sequence construction (T=10), and stratified partitioning. LSTM (96.1%), Random Forest (93.5%), SVM (91.2%) and KNN (89.7%); in NSL-KDD, it reaches 98.2% precision versus 96.4% (LSTM), 95.2% (CNN), 92.7% (Random Forest) and 90.8% (SVM), with margins of 2-9 percentage points in all measures. An ablation analysis identified SMOTE balancing as the most influential design choice (-3.7~pp F1 without it). The model achieves a real-time inference throughput of 27,800 flows/s on GPU and 0.082 ms/sample CPU latency in FP32,, with INT8 quantization providing an additional 3.1 x speedup at 0.3% accuracy loss, confirming deployment feasibility on resource-constrained IEDs with <128MB memory and establishing a deployable deep-learning framework for securing next-generation renewable energy smart grid infrastructure.

AI.tldr: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM intrusion-detection system for renewable-energy smart grids that integrates CNN-based spatial feature extraction with LSTM temporal modeling, addressing multi-step attack timing, class imbalance, and cross-environment generalization; achieves high accuracy and real-time throughput with quantization-friendly deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing IDS struggle with (i) modeling temporal progression of multi-step attacks, (ii) scalability under highly skewed datasets, and (iii) generalization across heterogeneous network environments. The work aims to create a deployable, real-time IDS for smart-grid networks.
AI.method: Proposes a Hybrid CNN-LSTM architecture with a seven-step preprocessing pipeline: missing-value imputation, min-max normalization, one-hot encoding, SMOTE balancing, mutual-information feature selection, causal temporal sequence construction with T=10, stratified partitioning. The model is evaluated on NSL-KDD, comparing LSTM, CNN, RF, SVM, KNN; an ablation study shows SMOTE balancing as a key contributor. Inference throughput: 27,800 flows/s on GPU; 0.082 ms/sample CPU latency; INT8 quantization yielding 3.1x speedup with 0.3% accuracy loss; deployment feasible on IEDs with <128MB memory.
AI.result: The Hybrid CNN-LSTM achieves superior or competitive accuracy relative to baselines (e.g., LSTM 96.1% vs 93.5% RF, 91.2% SVM, 89.7% KNN on its dataset; 98.2% precision on NSL-KDD vs 96.4% LSTM, 95.2% CNN, 92.7% RF, 90.8% SVM). The SMOTE balancing is identified as the most influential factor in F1 (+/- 3.7 percentage points). It supports real-time deployment on constrained hardware.
AI.conclusion: A deployable, deep-learning IDS framework for smart-grid security that captures fast and slow attacks, scales to skewed data, and generalizes across heterogeneous environments, with demonstrated real-time performance and efficient quantization.

title: Falcon: Functional Assembly and Language for Compositional Reasoning in X-ray
summary: Conventional vision-language models are largely object-centric, focusing on detecting and describing individual entities. In safety-critical X-ray baggage screening, however, threat often emerges not from a single object but from the functional compatibility of spatially dispersed components, such as batteries, detonators, and explosive charges. We formalize this setting as \emph{compositional threat reasoning}, where risk is modeled as a relational property of grounded regions rather than an independent detection outcome. We introduce \textbf{Falcon}, a multimodal framework that abstracts segmentation-aware region features into a structured safety state capturing component presence, pairwise functional compatibility, and scene-level risk. This structured representation is injected into the language model as an explicit intermediate interface, encouraging relationally consistent and safety-aware reasoning. To evaluate this problem, we present \textbf{Falcon-X}, a benchmark that unifies dense grounding with structured supervision over component completeness and risk inference in cluttered X-ray imagery. Experiments show that while existing multimodal models adapt to appearance, they struggle with compositional safety reasoning. Falcon improves functional grounding and produces more coherent threat assessments, establishing compositional safety reasoning as a distinct evaluation paradigm for multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: Falcon introduces a compositional safety reasoning paradigm for X-ray screening by structuring region features into a safety state (presence, pairwise compatibility, scene risk) and injecting it into the language model; Falcon-X provides a dense grounding plus structured supervision benchmark; results show improved compositional reasoning over object-centric multimodal models.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical X-ray baggage screening requires reasoning about how dispersed components interact, not just detecting isolated objects. Compositional threats emerge from functional compatibility among components, necessitating relational and scene-level reasoning.
AI.method: Propose Falcon, a multimodal framework that converts segmentation-aware region features into a structured safety state (component presence, pairwise functional compatibility, and scene-level risk) and exposes it to the language model as an intermediate interface. Introduce Falcon-X, a benchmark combining dense grounding with structured supervision for component completeness and risk inference in cluttered X-ray imagery.
AI.result: Falcon improves functional grounding and yields more coherent threat assessments compared to appearance-based baselines, while existing multimodal models struggle with relational safety reasoning in compositional settings.
AI.conclusion: Compositional safety reasoning constitutes a distinct evaluation paradigm for multimodal systems in safety-critical scenarios; Falcon and Falcon-X establish a formal framework and benchmark for reasoning about component interactions and risk, beyond simple object detection.

title: ForceBand: Learning Forceful Manipulation with sEMG
summary: Human demonstrations are a scalable data source for learning robot manipulation policies. However, common sources of human demonstration data, such as motion-capture trajectories and internet videos, capture mostly motion and appearance while missing the contact forces that are critical for force-sensitive manipulation. In this paper, we introduce ForceBand, a low-cost wrist-worn sEMG system that turns human muscle activity into force-enriched demonstrations. We first collect a 10-hour multimodal dataset containing egocentric video, sEMG, IMU, and fingertip force measurements across diverse actions and objects. Using this dataset, we pre-train an EMG2Force model that predicts per-finger forces from sEMG and IMU signals. After a short user-specific calibration, users can collect target-task demonstrations using only ForceBand and video; EMG2Force then labels these demonstrations with per-finger force traces, producing force-augmented demonstrations for robot policy learning. Experiments show that ForceBand recovers fine-grained fingertip interactions with over 50% lower force prediction error than vision-based baselines and achieves an 87% success rate on pick, squeeze, and place tasks that require object-specific force control across objects with diverse shapes, sizes, and weights. Project website: https://forceband-emg.github.io

AI.tldr: ForceBand uses a wrist-worn sEMG device to convert muscle activity into per-finger force labels, enabling force-augmented demonstrations for robot manipulation, achieving 87% success on grasp tasks and ~50% lower force prediction error than vision baselines.
AI.motivation: Human demonstrations capture motion and appearance but lack contact-force information, which is critical for force-sensitive manipulation; incorporating fingertip forces can improve policy learning.
AI.method: Collect a 10-hour multimodal dataset (egocentric video, sEMG, IMU, fingertip forces). Pre-train an EMG2Force model to predict per-finger forces from sEMG and IMU. After a short user calibration, demonstrations are collected using ForceBand and video; EMG2Force labels these demonstrations with finger-force traces to create force-augmented demonstrations for robot policy learning.
AI.result: EMG2Force achieves >50% lower force prediction error than vision-based baselines; ForceBand attains 87% success on pick, squeeze, and place tasks across diverse shapes, sizes, weights.
AI.conclusion: ForceBand offers a low-cost, scalable approach to infuse force information into human demonstrations, improving force-controlled manipulation policy learning and enabling broader application to diverse objects; future work could address calibration robustness and generalization to unseen tasks.

title: FDN: Interpretable Spatiotemporal Forecasting with Future Decomposition Networks
summary: Spatiotemporal systems comprise a collection of spatially distributed yet interdependent entities each generating unique dynamic signals. Highly sophisticated methods have been proposed in recent years delivering state-of-the-art (SOTA) forecasts but few have focused on interpretability. To address this, we propose the Future Decomposition Network (FDN), a novel forecast model capable of (a) providing interpretable predictions through classification (b) revealing latent activity patterns in the target time-series and (c) delivering forecasts competitive with SOTA methods at a fraction of their memory and runtime cost. We conduct comprehensive analyses on FDN for multiple datasets from hydrologic, traffic, and energy systems, demonstrating its improved accuracy and interpretability.

AI.tldr: FDN is a spatiotemporal forecasting model that emphasizes interpretability via classification, latent activity pattern discovery, and competitive accuracy with reduced memory/runtime costs.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in the literature between state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy and interpretability in spatiotemporal systems. The paper aims to provide interpretable predictions and insight into latent dynamics while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce the Future Decomposition Network (FDN) that (a) delivers interpretable predictions through classification, (b) uncovers latent activity patterns in the target time series, and (c) achieves forecasts competitive with SOTA methods at a fraction of memory and runtime cost. The evaluation covers datasets from hydrologic, traffic, and energy domains.
AI.result: FDN achieves improved interpretability and competitive accuracy with significantly reduced memory and runtime requirements across multiple datasets (hydrologic, traffic, energy).
AI.conclusion: FDN offers a practical framework for interpretable spatiotemporal forecasting with latent pattern discovery and computational efficiency, demonstrated across diverse real-world domains.

title: What Does the Brain See? Multiview Neural Representations to Demystify the Brain-Visual Alignment
summary: Zero-shot visual decoding from electroencephalography (EEG) aims to infer visual semantics from non-invasive neural recordings, but remains challenging due to the low signal-to-noise ratio, non-stationarity, and limited spatial resolution of EEG. Existing EEG-vision alignment methods often rely on holistic EEG embeddings, which can obscure the complementary temporal, spectral, and spatial structure underlying visual perception. We introduce a unified multiview EEG representation learning framework for aligning brain responses with visual semantic embeddings. Our method builds an EEG encoder that jointly models three complementary views: input-conditioned state-space temporal dynamics, learnable wavelet-based spectral decomposition for sample-adaptive frequency modeling, and attention-modulated graph learning for structured electrode interactions. The resulting multiview EEG embeddings are fused and aligned with pretrained visual representations in a shared semantic space using contrastive learning with EEG-specific regularization, enabling 200-way zero-shot visual classification. Experiments on THINGS-EEG benchmark show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 54.8% Top-1 and 85.6% Top-5 accuracy in the within-subject setting and 15.3% Top-1 and 45.4% Top-5 accuracy in the cross-subject setting. We further present the first systematic cross-session EEG-image decoding evaluation, achieving 40.8% Top-1 and 78.0% Top-5 accuracy. These results suggest that explicitly modeling multiview neural structure improves both semantic alignment and generalization in EEG-based visual decoding.

AI.tldr: A multiview EEG representation learning framework aligns EEG with visual semantics via three complementary views, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot 200-way visual decoding on THINGS-EEG with strong within-subject and cross-subject performance and cross-session evaluation.
AI.motivation: EEG-based visual decoding suffers from low SNR, non-stationarity, and limited spatial resolution; holistic EEG embeddings obscure temporal, spectral, and spatial structure; a multiview approach can preserve complementary information and improve alignment and generalization.
AI.method: EEG encoder with three views: 1) input-conditioned state-space temporal dynamics; 2) learnable wavelet-based spectral decomposition for adaptive frequency modeling; 3) attention-modulated graph learning for structured electrode interactions. Multiview embeddings fused and aligned with pretrained visual embeddings via contrastive learning with EEG-specific regularization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on THINGS-EEG: within-subject Top-1 54.8%, Top-5 85.6%; cross-subject Top-1 15.3%, Top-5 45.4%; cross-session Top-1 40.8%, Top-5 78.0%. Demonstrates improved semantic alignment and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Explicit multiview neural structure modeling enhances EEG-based visual decoding and generalization to unseen subjects, sessions, and possibly domains.

title: Learning Action Priors for Cross-embodiment Robot Manipulation
summary: Most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models build on a Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbone by attaching an action module and optimizing the full policy jointly. This design inherits strong visual and linguistic priors from the VLM, but leaves the action module to learn physical motion almost from scratch. As a result, the policy lacks an explicit motion prior, forcing early optimization to simultaneously discover temporal action dynamics and cross-modal alignment, a challenge further amplified in cross-embodiment settings. In this work, we propose to pretrain the action module with motion priors before cross-modal VLA alignment. Specifically, we introduce a two-stage training framework that equips the action module with cross-embodiment temporal motion structure before VLA training begins. In Stage~1, a lightweight flow-matching-based encoder-decoder action module efficiently learns temporal motion structure solely from unconditioned action trajectories, without processing visual or language tokens. In Stage~2, this learned prior is transferred to VLA training through decoder reuse and early-stage latent distillation, aligning visual-language features with the action embedding space while still allowing end-to-end policy refinement. In addition, the trained encoder serves as a compact history compressor, summarizing state-action histories into a single temporal context token for history-aware modeling at negligible cost. Extensive experiments across 13 diverse cross-embodiment tasks on both simulated and real-world platforms validate the effectiveness of our approach. Compared with VLA training without action priors, our model achieves faster convergence, higher success rates, and substantially stronger performance on data-scarce real-world tasks. Moreover, scaling up the action data in Stage~1 yields a more generalizable action prior that directly improves downstream VLA performance.

AI.tldr: Two-stage motion-prior pretraining for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Stage 1 learns temporal motion priors from unconditioned action trajectories via a lightweight flow-matching encoder-decoder; Stage 2 transfers the prior to VLA training through decoder reuse and early-stage latent distillation, while the encoder also serves as a compact history compressor. This yields faster convergence and stronger performance, especially in data-scarce tasks, and scales with more action data.
AI.motivation: Current VLA training relies on strong visual and linguistic priors from the VLM, but the action module must learn physical motion largely from scratch. This leads to inefficiencies in early optimization and difficulties in cross-embodiment settings. Introducing explicit motion priors can decouple motion structure from cross-modal alignment and improve data efficiency.
AI.method: Stage 1: train a lightweight flow-matching-based encoder-decoder action module using only unconditioned action trajectories (no visual or language tokens) to learn temporal motion structure. Stage 2: transfer the learned prior to VLA training by reusing the decoder and applying early-stage latent distillation to align visual-language features with the action embedding space, while allowing end-to-end policy refinement. The encoder also functions as a compact history compressor, summarizing state-action histories into a single temporal context token.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 13 cross-embodiment tasks on both simulated and real-world platforms show faster convergence, higher success rates, and notably stronger performance on data-scarce real-world tasks. Increasing action data in Stage 1 yields a more generalizable action prior, directly benefiting downstream VLA performance.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining the action module with motion priors effectively injects temporal dynamics into VLA models, improving data efficiency, generalization, and convergence. The approach scales with more action data and provides a compact history representation that adds negligible cost.

title: The Gentle Collapse: Distributional Metrics for Continual Learning
summary: Accuracy degradation is the standard metric for Catastrophic Forgetting (CF), however, it records only whether forgetting occurred or not. It saturates at the extremes and collapses discretely at task boundaries, hiding the internal structure of what is being forgotten. We introduce six softmax-derived metrics spanning true-label rank (TLR), predictive confidence, and distributional divergence that characterize forgetting continuously, each normalized to [0, 1] with no modification to training. On CIFAR-100, these metrics carry information where accuracy does not: at 0% accuracy, the Confusion Margin spans an IQR of [0.32, 0.50] across classes that accuracy treats identically. We demonstrate that this richer signal is actionable in mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Per-sample metric scores used as loss weights reduce forgetting by 1.3 percentage points over uniform experience replay (ER) on CIFAR-100. Furthermore, the slope of a metric over a small window provides a stable sampling criterion: at a small-window size (e.g. 3 epochs), accuracy-trend degrades to 34.79% (std. = 2.32) while log-TLR achieves 41.07% (std. = 0.57). This gap is structural since reliable small-window trend estimation requires a continuous signal. On TinyImageNet, log-TLR trend sampling reduces forgetting by 7.7 percentage points over the ER baseline.

AI.tldr: Six softmax-derived metrics (spanning true-label rank, predictive confidence, and distributional divergence) offer a continuous forgetting signal to characterize and mitigate catastrophic forgetting (CF). When used as loss weights and for small-window trend sampling, they yield measurable improvements over standard experience replay (ER) on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet.
AI.motivation: Accuracy degradation as the sole CF metric hides the internal structure of forgetting. A continuous, discriminative signal is needed to better characterize CF and guide mitigation without modifying training.
AI.method: Introduce six softmax-derived metrics normalized to [0,1] that quantify forgetting via true-label rank (TLR), predictive confidence, and distributional divergence. Use per-sample metric scores as loss weights to reduce CF relative to uniform ER. Use the slope of a metric over a small window as a sampling criterion. Evaluate on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet against ER baseline.
AI.result: Metrics reveal information not captured by accuracy (e.g., Confusion Margin across classes). Per-sample weighting reduces forgetting by ~1.3 percentage points over ER on CIFAR-100. In small windows, log-TLR achieves a higher trend signal (e.g., 41.07% vs 34.79% for accuracy-trend, stds 0.57 vs 2.32). On TinyImageNet, log-TLR trend sampling reduces forgetting by ~7.7 percentage points over ER.
AI.conclusion: Continuous softmax-derived forgetting metrics provide actionable signals that improve CF mitigation; trend-based sampling (log-TLR) and per-sample weighting offer robust gains, suggesting a promising direction beyond accuracy-based metrics for continual learning.

title: Efficient Real-World Dehazing via Physics-Inspired Global-Local Decoupling
summary: Real-world single image dehazing is highly ill-posed due to spatially and spectrally varying scattering, while practical deployment demands lightweight and low-latency models. Existing approaches either rely on fragile physical inversion under simplified assumptions or adopt heavy blind architectures unsuitable for edge deployment. To overcome these limitations, we propose PGL-Net (Physics-Inspired Global-Local Decoupling Network), a lightweight framework that incorporates physical inductive biases via operator-level emulation, avoiding explicit parameter estimation. It decouples dehazing into global distribution rectification and local structural refinement. A Physics-Inspired Affine Fusion (PAF) module performs globally conditioned alignment across hierarchical skip connections to compensate for haze-induced bias, while a compact Degradation-Aware Modulation (DAM) block adaptively restores spatially and spectrally variant details through dynamic feature modulation. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that PGL-Net achieves state-of-the-art restoration quality with significantly reduced complexity. Compared with the recent SOTA SGDN, the Tiny variant (PGL-Net-T) improves PSNR by up to 2.6dB and consistently enhances downstream object detection accuracy, while achieving over a 10x reduction in inference latency. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/sc-30-bit/PGL-Net.

AI.tldr: A lightweight physics-inspired dehazing network (PGL-Net) decouples global haze correction from local refinement using PAf and DAM, achieving state-of-the-art restoration with much lower complexity; PGL-Net-T offers up to 2.6dB PSNR gain over SGDN and >10x faster latency; code released.
AI.motivation: Real-world single-image dehazing is highly ill-posed due to spatially and spectrally varying scattering. Existing methods are fragile (physical inversion under simplified assumptions) or heavy (blind architectures unsuitable for edge devices). A lightweight, physics-informed model with inductive biases is needed.
AI.method: Introduce Physics-Inspired Global-Local Decoupling Network (PGL-Net) that uses operator-level physics emulation to avoid explicit parameter estimation. Decouples dehazing into global distribution rectification and local structural refinement. A Physics-Inspired Affine Fusion (PAF) module performs globally conditioned alignment across hierarchical skip connections to compensate haze bias. A compact Degradation-Aware Modulation (DAM) block adaptively restores spatially and spectrally variant details via dynamic feature modulation. Lightweight design with no explicit transmission map estimation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks show state-of-the-art restoration quality with reduced complexity. Compared with the recent SOTA SGDN, the Tiny variant (PGL-Net-T) improves PSNR by up to 2.6 dB and consistently enhances downstream object detection accuracy, while achieving over a 10x reduction in inference latency. Code is publicly available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: PGL-Net demonstrates that physics-inspired, operator-level biases with a global-local decoupling strategy can deliver high-quality dehazing under real-world conditions with low computational cost, suitable for edge deployment. The PAf and DAM components are central to performance gains, suggesting further potential in extending physical priors to related low-level vision tasks.

title: An iterative energy-based multimodal transformer for joint retrieval of wheat soil moisture, leaf area index, and plant height from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 time series
summary: Field-scale retrieval of surface soil moisture (SM), leaf area index (LAI), and plant height (PH) is essential for precision agriculture, yet it remains an ill-posed inverse problem. Concurrent variations in soil moisture and canopy density generate substantial ambiguities in radar backscatter and spectral responses, which reduces the effectiveness of traditional feedforward regression models in heterogeneous smallholder cropping systems. This study presents the Iterative Energy-Based Transformer (iEBT) for the joint retrieval of coupled soil-canopy states from Sentinel-1 C-band SAR and Sentinel-2 multispectral time series. Instead of direct regression, iEBT embeds multi-modal predictors within a shared sequence, produces an initial state estimate, and iteratively updates the target [SM, LAI, PH] vector through normalized gradient descent to minimize a learned scalar compatibility energy function. Using 700 quality-controlled field measurements from Varanasi, India, iEBT achieved the highest learned-model performance on the random test split, with a four-seed mean R^2 of 0.854 \pm 0.012 (R_SM^2 = 0.841, R_LAI^2 = 0.905, R_PH^2 = 0.821). WCM and PROSAIL were retained as physically interpretable SAR and optical reference models for comparison. Modality ablations confirmed that Sentinel-1 drives SM retrieval, while Sentinel-2 dominates LAI, whereas PH relies on combined structural-phenological signatures. Crucially, the model's terminal energy functions as an uncalibrated post-retrieval quality diagnostic; screening the 10% highest-energy samples markedly reduced target level root-mean-square errors. While leave-one-campaign-out validation highlights persistent cross-season domain shift challenges due to localized management variations, compatibility-guided multimodal fusion offers a structured self-diagnostic path toward reliable biophysical parameter estimation

AI.tldr: Iterative Energy-Based Transformer (iEBT) for joint retrieval of soil moisture (SM), leaf area index (LAI), and plant height (PH) from Sentinel-1/2 time series; uses a learned energy function and normalized gradient descent; shows strong random-split performance and self-diagnostic energy, but exhibits cross-season domain shift.
AI.motivation: Address the ill-posed, multimodal inverse problem of retrieving coupled soil-canopy states from heterogeneous field data, where variations in soil moisture and canopy density confound radar and optical signals, limiting traditional regression models.
AI.method: iEBT embeds multi-modal predictors in a shared sequence, generates an initial state estimate, then iteratively updates [SM, LAI, PH] by normalized gradient descent to minimize a learned compatibility energy function. Uses Sentinel-1 C-band SAR and Sentinel-2 MS time series, validated on 700 field measurements from Varanasi, India; compares against WCM and PROSAIL; modality ablations identify which sensors drive each parameter; the terminal energy serves as a post-retrieval quality diagnostic.
AI.result: On random test split, four-seed mean R^2 = 0.854 ± 0.012 (R_SM^2 = 0.841, R_LAI^2 = 0.905, R_PH^2 = 0.821). Screening the top 10% high-energy samples reduces target RMSE.
