Compared with existing surgical video-language datasets, SurgAtlas provides one of the most diverse annotation schemas, combining segment-level captions, step- and phase-level descriptions, video-level surgical descriptions, and reasoning-oriented question-answer pairs organized within a hierarchical taxonomy. These annotations are constructed through an automated multi-tier pipeline with LLM-based enrichment and a staged VQA generation framework with explicit groundedness verification. The scale and diversity of SurgAtlas enable training surgical foundation models with broad procedural coverage: we finetune Qwen3-VL-8B through a two-stage captioning-then-instruction pipeline and achieve competitive or state-of-the-art results on multiple established surgical benchmarks, including phase recognition, triplet detection, and reasoning question answering. More broadly, SurgAtlas provides a large native public video corpus that can support future large-scale pretraining of multimodal surgical AI systems and contribute to the development of next-generation foundation models for surgery.

AI.tldr: SurgAtlas is a large, diverse surgical video-language dataset sourced from YouTube, comprising 15,291 videos (2,391 hours) across 18 specialties and 5,000+ procedures, including 6,182 open-surgery videos. It establishes standardized open-surgery benchmarks, an expert-validated VQA subset, and a broad, multi-tier annotation schema (segment-, step-, phase-level captions; video-level descriptions; and reasoning Q&As). Built via an automated, LLM-enriched pipeline with grounded VQA verification, it enables finetuning of surgical foundation models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B) that achieve competitive to state-of-the-art results on phase recognition, triplet detection, and reasoning QA, providing a substantial public corpus for future pretraining of multimodal surgical AI models.
AI.motivation: Fill data gaps in surgical video understanding, especially for open surgery, and create diverse, hierarchical annotations and standardized benchmarks to advance surgical AI and foundation-model pretraining.
AI.method: Public YouTube data collection; automated multi-tier annotation pipeline with LLM enrichment; staged VQA generation with groundedness verification; hierarchical taxonomy including segment/step/phase descriptions and Q&A; two-stage finetuning (captioning then instruction) of Qwen3-VL-8B.
AI.result: SurgAtlas offers diverse annotation schemas, open-surgery benchmarks, and an expert-validated VQA subset; enables training of surgical foundation models and competitive performance on established benchmarks; provides a large native public corpus for pretraining multimodal AI in surgery.
AI.conclusion: SurgAtlas enables scalable pretraining and benchmarking for next-generation multimodal surgical AI, driving broader procedural coverage and grounded reasoning capabilities.

title: FactorLibrary: From Polynomials to Circuits via Recursive Subgoals
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures, in 3rd AI for Math Workshop (ICML 2026)
summary: Finding minimal arithmetic circuits for polynomials over finite fields is a combinatorially hard problem central to algebraic complexity theory. We formulate it as a reinforcement learning problem in two directions, bottom-up and top-down. To address the challenge of a fast-growing combinatorial search space, we introduce FactorLibrary, which stores factorizable subexpressions that serve as reusable subgoals across training episodes. We trained a bottom-up agent with Gumbel-PPO-MCTS and two top-down agents with PPO+MCTS and SAC. The PPO+MCTS top-down agent exhibited the most stable performance, finding certified optimal circuits up to complexity $8$ with a success rate of $91.8\%$.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning to find minimal arithmetic circuits for polynomials over finite fields; introduces FactorLibrary; bottom-up and two top-down agents; PPO+MCTS top-down yields best results with 91.8% success up to complexity 8.
AI.motivation: Algebraic complexity presents a combinatorially hard search for minimal circuits; need scalable, reusable subgoals to guide learning.
AI.method: Formulated as RL in bottom-up and top-down directions; FactorLibrary stores reusable factorizable subexpressions; agents trained: bottom-up with Gumbel-PPO-MCTS; top-down with PPO+MCTS and SAC; evaluation on certified optimality up to complexity 8.
AI.result: PPO+MCTS top-down agent achieved stable performance and high success (91.8%), finding certified optimal circuits up to complexity 8.
AI.conclusion: FactorLibrary plus RL-guided search is effective for minimal circuit synthesis; among studied methods, top-down PPO+MCTS performs best; potential for scalable algebraic circuit synthesis.

title: OracleAnalyser: Analysing Implicit Semantics of Oracle Bone Scripts through MLLMs with Post-training
summary: With the advancement of artificial intelligence, research on oracle bone scripts has entered a new era. However, existing methods and benchmarks remain largely confined to recognition tasks, overlooking the equally crucial aspect of oracle bone analysis. To address this gap, we propose OracleAnalyser, a reasoning framework for oracle bone analysis based on post-training techniques. Specifically, we fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct through multiple post-training stages and introduce a new preference optimization algorithm, Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO), tailored to the characteristics of oracle bone datasets. In addition, we release both an oracle bone reasoning dataset and an oracle bone preference dataset, and further construct a new benchmark to evaluate models' analytical capabilities for oracle bone scripts. Extensive experiments validate the superior analytical performance of OracleAnalyser, which achieves remarkable results with only 3B parameters, surpassing models with substantially larger scales.

AI.tldr: OracleAnalyser is a post-training fine-tuned 3B model (Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct) with SFPO for oracle bone analysis, introducing new datasets and a benchmark; achieves strong reasoning with smaller models.
AI.motivation: To fill a gap in oracle bone research where analysis beyond recognition is needed, leveraging post-training to tailor models to oracle bone reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct via multiple post-training stages; introduce Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO); release dataset and benchmark; evaluate on new analytical tasks.
AI.result: OracleAnalyser achieves superior analytical performance, surpassing larger models with only 3B parameters on oracle bone reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Post-training with SFPO enables effective analytic capabilities in small to mid-sized models for specialized scripts, validating the approach and datasets.

title: Brevity is the Soul of Inference Efficiency: Inducing Concision in VLMs via Data Curation
comment: 36 pages, see https://datologyai.com for more information
summary: Inference efficiency is typically pursued by shrinking the model: distillation, pruning, quantization, and sparse routing each lower per-token cost while treating token count as fixed. But output length has been inflating, and it is precisely the component the standard toolkit leaves untouched. Here, we argue that brevity is the missing inference-efficiency lever, and that pretraining data curation is a practical way to pull it: a model trained on concise, correct data learns to answer in fewer tokens; i.e. it has a lower Cost-of-Pass. We apply our VLM curation pipeline to the MAmmoTH-VL single-image subset, and compare models trained on our curated data, the standard MAmmoTH-VL data, and external open-weight frontier VLMs. On a controlled 20-evaluation set and 14 VLMs at 1B-4B activated parameters, we hold output length fixed with a per-model regression, separating brevity from quality, and price models in FLOPs per correct answer. Curation buys a 35x Cost-of-Pass advantage over the most verbose 4B comparator (Qwen3.5-4B) within $\sim$1 pp of accuracy (0.41 vs 14.58 TFLOPs per correct answer; 0.691 vs 0.704 mean accuracy). Curation also buys a +17.55-percentage-point matched-length accuracy gain over the uncurated baseline that grows with model scale (from +16.7 pp at 1B to +21.2 pp at 4B). This brevity improvement concedes no quality: generic verbosity buys no accuracy at any capability or scale, and the window where reasoning-structured verbosity still earns its tokens shrinks from 4 of 8 capability groups at 2B to 1 of 8 at 4B. Per example, the concise model even reaches correct answers the verbose reasoning model misses, marking reasoning as a distinct curation target rather than something brevity gives up. Inference efficiency in this regime is a tokens-per-correct problem, and brevity is the lever that targets it directly.

AI.tldr: Data curation for brevity boosts VLM inference efficiency; training on concise, correct data reduces tokens per answer (Cost-of-Pass) without sacrificing accuracy; achieves large FLOPs savings and accuracy gains, especially as models scale.
AI.motivation: Per-token cost reduction methods (distillation, pruning, quantization, sparse routing) leave output length unchanged; increasing output length has become the primary efficiency frontier, so brevity should be targeted via data curation.
AI.method: Apply VLM curation pipeline to the MAmmoTH-VL single-image subset; compare models trained on curated data, standard data, and frontier open-weight VLMs; evaluate on 20 evaluation set across 14 VLMs with 1B–4B parameters; fix output length via per-model regression; measure FLOPs per correct answer and accuracy.
AI.result: Curated data yields 35x Cost-of-Pass advantage over verbose 4B model (Qwen3.5-4B) near parity in accuracy (0.41 vs 14.58 TFLOPs per correct answer; 0.691 vs 0.704 accuracy). Also yields +17.6 percentage points accuracy over uncurated baseline, increasing with model size (16.7 pp at 1B to 21.2 pp at 4B). Brevity does not sacrifice quality; verbose reasoning harms accuracy; the window where reasoning-structured verbosity still earns its tokens shrinks from 4 of 8 capability groups at 2B to 1 of 8 at 4B.
AI.conclusion: Inference efficiency for LLMs-VLMs in this setting is dominated by tokens-per-correct-answer; data-driven brevity is an effective lever that complements existing per-token efficiency techniques and scales with model size; brevity windows shrink as models grow, but curated brevity yields tangible gains without compromising reasoning in most capability groups.

title: In-context Region-based Drag: Drag Any Region to Any Shape
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026. Dataset, code, and model are available at https://github.com/bcmi/ICRDrag-Region-Drag-Editing
summary: Diffusion models have shown promise in drag-style editing. Previous works mainly focus on point-based drag, which is inherently ambiguous. This paper focuses on region-based drag and introduces a novel In-Context Region-based Drag (ICRDrag) method. Under the in-context learning framework, ICRDrag consumes a source image, a source region mask, and a target region mask, producing the target dragged image. Built upon the basic in-context learning model, we introduce two novel attention regularization: 1) image-mask attention consistency to ensure that a target region attends to similar source regions for image and mask modalities; 2) source-target attention correspondence to ensure the mutual correspondence between source and target regions. To facilitate region-based drag, we also construct Paired Region Dataset (PRD), a large-scale dataset with paired masks and images. Extensive experiments show that ICRDrag significantly outperforms existing methods in both quantitative metrics and user studies, achieving superior editing accuracy and visual fidelity. The dataset, code, and model are available at https://github.com/bcmi/ICRDrag-Region-Drag-Editing.

AI.tldr: Introduces In-Context Region-based Drag (ICRDrag) for diffusion-based image editing, leveraging source/target region masks to perform region-based dragging within an in-context learning framework, with two attention regularizations and a new Paired Region Dataset (PRD); achieves superior editing accuracy and visual fidelity; code, dataset, and model available.
AI.motivation: Point-based drag in diffusion models is ambiguous and limits controllability. There is a need for explicit, region-based editing that uses masks to guide edits and align modalities, enabling more precise and user-intuitive manipulation.
AI.method: In-context learning framework that takes a source image, a source region mask, and a target region mask to produce a target-dragged image. Introduces two attention regularizations: (1) image–mask attention consistency to align attended regions across image and mask modalities, and (2) source–target attention correspondence to enforce mutual region correspondence. Builds Paired Region Dataset (PRD), a large-scale dataset of paired masks and images to support region-based dragging.
AI.result: ICRDrag significantly outperforms existing methods on quantitative metrics and user studies, delivering higher editing accuracy and better visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: ICRDrag advances region-based drag for diffusion models by integrating in-context learning with targeted attention regularizations and a dedicated paired-region dataset; the work is complemented by released code, dataset, and model.

title: Stagnant Neuron: Towards Understanding the Plasticity Loss in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Value Factorization Methods
summary: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) value factorization methods can suffer from a loss of plasticity, gradually failing to adapt when transferring to new task instances. We trace this issue to stagnant neurons, units whose gradient updates become negligibly small relative to their weights, thereby hindering learning. While existing plasticity injection methods exist, they prove ineffective for such neurons. To address this, we propose Knowledge-retentive Neuron-level PlastIcity Focusing InjEction (KNIFE), a novel method that directly targets stagnant neurons. KNIFE replaces each stagnant neuron with a composite unit comprising three specialized components: a frozen knowledge neuron to preserve acquired knowledge, a re-initialized active neuron to restore learning capacity, and a compensation neuron to ensure the combined output matches the original, thus maintaining previous learned cooperation knowledge. Extensive experiments on SMACv2, predator-prey, and matrix games demonstrate that KNIFE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art plasticity injection methods.

AI.tldr: KNIFE mitigates stagnation in MARL value factorization by substituting stagnant neurons with a composite unit (frozen knowledge neuron + re-initialized active neuron + compensation neuron) to preserve learned cooperation while restoring learning capacity, outperforming prior plasticity injections.
AI.motivation: MARL value factorization can suffer plasticity loss due to stagnant neurons, hindering transfer to new task instances; existing plasticity injection methods are ineffective for these neurons.
AI.method: Identify stagnant neurons (negligible gradient updates relative to weights) and replace each with a composite unit: a frozen knowledge neuron to retain prior knowledge, a re-initialized active neuron to restore plasticity, and a compensation neuron to ensure the composite output matches the original, preserving cooperation knowledge.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on SMACv2, predator-prey, and matrix games shows KNIFE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art plasticity injection methods.
AI.conclusion: KNIFE offers a targeted neuron-level plasticity mechanism that preserves previous cooperative knowledge while restoring learning capacity, enabling better transfer across MARL tasks.

title: Interpretable Concept-Guided Polynomial Tabular Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for EEG-Based Mild Cognitive Impairment Detection
summary: Early and scalable detection of mild cognitive impairment (MCI) remains an unresolved clinical challenge. Existing EEG-based screening approaches are constrained by handcrafted feature pipelines that discard neurophysiologically meaningful domain structure and deep learning classifiers that sacrifice interpretability for performance. No existing work unifies physiologically organized concept encoders, cross-concept interaction modeling, and nonlinear tabular classification in a sleep EEG-based MCI detection framework. This study proposes Concept-guided Polynomial-transformed Tabular learning using Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (CPTabKAN), which maps heterogeneous EEG-derived features into domain-informed concept representations, expands them via degree-2 polynomial transformation to expose first- and second-order interactions, and applies a Fourier-parameterized TabKAN classifier to learn nonlinear decision boundaries. CPTabKAN was evaluated on the Study of Osteoporotic Fractures cohort (372 subjects, overnight polysomnography), using 1,379 features organized into ten physiologically motivated concept groups. Under 10-fold cross-validation, CPTabKAN-Second Order achieved a weighted F1-score of 0.9038 (SD 0.034), outperforming GradientBoosting by 5.65 percentage points (t(9)=1.934,p=0.043, one-sided paired test), with advantages persisting under SMOTE-based balancing. Ablation analysis confirmed independent contributions from each component. Concept importance analysis revealed that power spectral density, multi-scale entropy, and Hjorth parameters dominated first-order weights, while cross-concept interactions involving Lempel-Ziv-Welch complexity, statistics, demographics, and slow oscillations exceeded all first-order scores. These results demonstrate that concept-structured, interaction-aware tabular learning surfaces physiologically coherent reasoning, supporting clinical trust.

AI.tldr: Concept-guided Polynomial-transformed Tabular learning with Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (CPTabKAN) for sleep EEG-based MCI detection; achieves high predictive performance with interpretable, concept-structured features and cross-concept interactions.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of handcrafted EEG features that miss domain structure and black-box deep models lacking interpretability, by unifying physiologically informed concept encoders, cross-concept interactions, and nonlinear tabular classification for sleep EEG MCI detection.
AI.method: Map heterogeneous EEG features into ten physiologically motivated concept groups, apply degree-2 polynomial expansion to expose first- and second-order interactions, and use a Fourier-parameterized TabKAN classifier for nonlinear decision boundaries; evaluate on overnight PSG data with 1,379 features, 372 subjects (Study of Osteoporotic Fractures). Perform 10-fold cross-validation and SMOTE-based balancing; conduct ablation and concept-importance analyses.
AI.result: CPTabKAN-Second Order achieved weighted F1 = 0.9038 (SD 0.034) under 10-fold CV, outperforming GradientBoosting by 5.65 percentage points (t(9)=1.934, p=0.043, one-sided paired). Results persisted under SMOTE balancing. Ablation confirmed contributions of all components. Concept-importance analysis: first-order weights dominated by PSD, multi-scale entropy, and Hjorth; cross-concept interactions involving LZW complexity, statistics, demographics, and slow oscillations exceeded all first-order scores.
AI.conclusion: Concept-structured, interaction-aware tabular learning reveals physiologically coherent reasoning and improves clinical trust in sleep EEG-based MCI detection; CPTabKAN provides scalable, interpretable performance gains over traditional models.

title: FunPiQ: A New Benchmark for Pixel-Level Quality Assessment in Fundus Images
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026 main conference. Our code, weights, and dataset are available at https://github.com/penway/FunPiQ
summary: Color fundus photography (CFP) is the most common ophthalmic imaging modality for large-scale screening. However, it is highly susceptible to degradations, making robust fundus image quality assessment (FIQA) crucial. The criteria for what constitutes high-quality at the image level vary across clinical tasks, making FIQA dependent on expert knowledge. This motivated the development of automated methods and datasets. While existing datasets aim to standardize image-level quality, their criteria often differ. Furthermore, image-level labels preclude the quantitative evaluation of localized degradations, which is essential for trustworthy FIQA. We argue that pixel-level FIQA based on anatomical visibility represents a more task-agnostic, explainable approach. In this work, we introduce FunPiQ, the first FIQA benchmark to provide pixel-level quality annotations. In addition, we propose EFIQA-CP, an explainable-by-design (EBD) method that uses quality pseudo-labels based on anatomical visibility to train a CNN via Non-Negative Positive-Unlabeled learning. Extensive evaluations of classification methods with post-hoc explanations, anomaly detection methods, and EBD methods demonstrate the superior performance of the last and, particularly, of EFIQA-CP.

AI.tldr: Pixel-level FIQA for color fundus photography via FunPiQ and EFIQA-CP, using anatomical visibility and NN Positive-Unlabeled learning; shows improved performance and explainability over image-level FIQA.
AI.motivation: Address variability in image-level quality criteria and the lack of localized degradation evaluation. Provide a task-agnostic, explainable FIQA by annotating pixel-level anatomical visibility to enable precise quality assessment.
AI.method: Introduce FunPiQ, a pixel-level FIQA benchmark with pixel-level quality annotations. Propose EFIQA-CP, an explainable-by-design method that trains a CNN using pseudo-labels derived from anatomical visibility via Non-Negative Positive-Unlabeled learning. Evaluate using post-hoc explanations, anomaly detection baselines, and EBD performance.
AI.result: EFIQA-CP demonstrates superior performance compared to baselines, with improved reliability in recognizing degraded regions and providing explanations. Pixel-level FIQA improves localization of degradations and supports trustworthy QA.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-level FIQA based on anatomical visibility offers a task-agnostic, explainable framework for FIQA in fundus imaging. The FunPiQ benchmark and EFIQA-CP method enable more precise, locally interpretable quality assessment and set a new standard for FIQA evaluation.

title: Lifelong In-Context Learning with Transformers Requires Parametric Forms of Attention
summary: Lifelong continual learning remains an obstacle on the path to human-like intelligence. Modern transformers show sparks of intelligence with in-context learning. The quadratic nature of attention, however, prohibits transformers from performing this process on arbitrarily long sequences. In this work, we argue that extending in-context learning to lifelong settings is a practical solution for continual learning in AI agents. In particular, we argue that \emph{parametric forms of attention} are needed to understand a lifetime of context with transformers on a fixed hardware budget. These attention mechanisms learn the relationship between keys and their associated values at test-time with parametric regression. Our generalization of parametric approaches (linear attention, state-space models, fast weight programmers, and test-time training layers) contrasts with nonparametric counterparts like softmax attention. They replace the ever-growing key-value cache with an online-trainable neural network, maintaining a constant memory footprint. We highlight how parametric attention currently fall short of lifelong learning due to limited memory capacity or costly online updates. To address these issues, we pose a set of open questions with novel insights to guide the field toward long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes parametric attention to enable lifelong in-context learning in transformers under fixed memory by replacing nonparametric softmax with online-trainable parametric regression, outlining open problems.
AI.motivation: Lifelong continual learning remains hard; attention’s quadratic cost limits long sequences; need memory-efficient, test-time adaptable mechanisms on fixed hardware.
AI.method: Frame attention as parametric models: linear attention, state-space models, fast weight programmers, test-time training layers; online training of attention mapping between keys and values; maintains constant memory footprint; contrasts with softmax-based nonparametric approach.
AI.result: Provides a conceptual framework and identifies current limitations (memory capacity, costly online updates) rather than presenting empirical results; proposes open questions and insights.
AI.conclusion: Parametric attention is a promising path to long-horizon agents but requires research on memory-efficient updates and scalable online training; outlines a research agenda.

title: TopoCast: A Topological Fidelity Framework for Evaluating Transformer-Based Time Series Forecasting
summary: Deep learning-based models have achieved state-of-the-art performance in Time Series Forecasting (TSF), yet their evaluation remains dominated by pointwise error metrics such as Mean Squared Error (MSE), which quantify numerical accuracy but overlook structural properties of the forecast signal, including recurrent dynamics, oscillatory behavior, and phase alignment. As a result, forecasts exhibiting over-smoothing, phase shifts, or frequency distortions may achieve favorable error scores despite substantial structural degradation. To address this limitation, we propose TopoCast, a topology-driven framework for evaluating structural fidelity in TSF. TopoCast reconstructs phase-space representations of forecast and ground-truth sequences using Takens delay embedding and applies persistent homology to characterize their intrinsic dynamics. We derive four complementary topological fidelity measures from persistence diagrams and aggregate them into a Topological Fidelity Score (TFS). We further introduce dominant cycle overlap, a novel metric that maps persistent topological features to the temporal domain to assess whether dominant oscillatory patterns occur at the correct time points. Combined with TFS, this yields the Localized Topological Fidelity Score (LTFS), a phase-aware measure that captures temporal localization errors invisible to existing evaluation metrics. Experiments on five Transformer architectures across three real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that models with similar forecasting errors can exhibit markedly different structural fidelity profiles, revealing failure modes overlooked by conventional evaluation and highlighting the value of topology-aware forecast assessment.

AI.tldr: TopoCast introduces topology-based evaluation for time series forecasting, using Takens embedding and persistent homology to define a Topological Fidelity Score (TFS) and an LTFS with dominant cycle overlap; demonstrates that similar MSE can hide structural degradation; validated on five Transformer architectures across three real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Pointwise metrics like MSE miss structural dynamics (recurrent dynamics, oscillations, phase alignment) in forecasts. A topology-informed evaluation can quantify and compare structural fidelity.
AI.method: Reconstruct phase spaces of forecast and ground-truth via Takens delay embedding; compute persistent diagrams; derive four complementary topological fidelity measures; aggregate into TFS. Introduce dominant cycle overlap to map persistent features to the time domain. Combine with TFS to yield LTFS (localized phase-aware metric). Evaluate across five Transformer models on three real-world datasets.
AI.result: Experiments show that models with similar forecasting errors can have markedly different topological fidelity profiles, revealing failure modes unseen by conventional metrics and highlighting the value of topology-aware forecast assessment.
AI.conclusion: Topological fidelity measures provide phase-aware, localized evaluation of TSF outputs, complementing traditional error metrics and aiding detection of structural degradation in forecasts.

title: Pulmonary Embolism Risk Stratification from CTPA and Medical Records: Vascular Graphs Are Not All You Need
comment: 8 1/2 pages + 2 pages of references. Accepted for MICCAI 2026. This preprint has not undergone peer review or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this contribution is published in, and available online at, the external reference provided below
summary: Risk stratification for pulmonary embolism (PE) is critical for clinical decision-making. Stratification guidelines are based on patient medical records, parameters measured from computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA), and blood tests. However, blood tests are often missing in routine practice. This work studies whether state-of-the-art models can accurately classify risk stratification from only medical records and biomarkers extracted from CTPA images. We benchmark different approaches to combine medical records and cardiac biomarkers with rich pulmonary vascular information; we add vascular biomarkers to tabular models and apply graph neural networks (GNNs) on the vascular tree's intrinsic graph representation. We use a private dataset (n=353) with uniquely complete data for PE risk stratification. Our results show that, among global features, medical records and cardiac biomarkers are the most significant predictors, while vascular biomarkers do not further improve stratification. Even more surprising, even GNNs on vascular graphs fail to outperform strong tabular baseline on global features. We consider hypotheses, on both models and data, that could explain this suboptimal performance. Our investigation suggests that, counter-intuitively, vascular graphs might hold no discriminative information for PE risk stratification. Code is available from https://github.com/creatis-myriad/GENESIS.

AI.tldr: Medical-records + cardiac biomarkers suffice for PE risk stratification; adding vascular biomarkers or GNNs on vascular trees does not improve performance. Private dataset (n=353) with complete data. Code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether imaging-derived vascular biomarkers and graph-based representations add predictive value for PE risk stratification when blood tests are missing, by comparing tabular models to graph-based approaches on a complete dataset.
AI.method: Benchmark multiple approaches: (i) tabular models using medical records + cardiac biomarkers; (ii) augment with vascular biomarkers; (iii) apply graph neural networks on the vascular tree as an intrinsic graph representation. Use a private dataset of 353 patients with complete data. Compare against strong baselines and analyze why vascular features underperform. Provide code for replication.
AI.result: Global (medical records + cardiac biomarkers) features are the strongest predictors of PE risk. Vascular biomarkers do not improve stratification. Graph neural networks on vascular graphs fail to surpass the strong tabular baseline. The authors discuss potential data/model reasons for this suboptimal performance and propose hypotheses. Code is available.
AI.conclusion: Vascular graphs may not hold discriminative information for PE risk stratification in this setting; encourages reevaluation of vascular-graph representations for this task and more emphasis on robust tabular clinical features. Future work should address data quality, feature extraction, and external validation.

title: Geometry-Anchored Transport Framework for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 17 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Code: https://github.com/HXuSz11/GATF_ECCV2026
summary: Exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL) requires stable decision boundaries within a shifting feature space. While maintaining class-conditional Gaussian statistics provides a principled classification strategy, these parametric summaries remain sensitive to anisotropic representation drift. Existing methods often transport these statistics across tasks using a decoupled, post-hoc paradigm: optimizing a backbone without explicit geometric constraints can distort the legacy manifold, limiting the precision of retroactive alignment. In this paper, we formulate feature transport as an endogenous training constraint rather than a separate post-task step, presenting the Geometry-Anchored Transport Framework. First, we derive an Analytic Geometric Anchor via Mahalanobis-aligned regression to mitigate macroscopic anisotropic drift. Second, we introduce a Topology-Aware Evolution objective that regularizes localized manifold degradation while calibrating a residual network against the analytic prior. By coupling manifold evolution with transport constraints during the primary training phase, our framework mitigates evaluation errors without requiring decoupled fine-tuning. Experiments across CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, and ImageNet-100 demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently improves upon existing post-hoc alternatives under strict exemplar-free constraints.

AI.tldr: Exemplar-free CIL improved by integrating geometry-aware transport into training: an analytic Mahalanobis-based anchor plus topology-aware regularization to constrain manifold drift, outperforming post-hoc transport methods.
AI.motivation: In EFCIL, without exemplars, decision boundaries must be stable as feature space shifts. Class-conditional Gaussians are sensitive to anisotropic drift, and post-hoc transport of statistics can distort the legacy manifold. An endogenous training constraint that couples geometry and transport is needed to reduce retroactive misalignment.
AI.method: Introduce Analytic Geometric Anchor via Mahalanobis-aligned regression to counter macroscopic drift and a Topology-Aware Evolution objective to regularize local manifold degradation while calibrating a residual network to the analytic prior. These constraints are enforced during the main training phase, not as a separate fine-tuning step.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, and ImageNet-100 shows consistent improvements over traditional post-hoc statistics transport approaches under strict exemplar-free constraints.
AI.conclusion: Coupling manifold evolution with transport constraints during primary training mitigates evaluation errors due to drift and yields more accurate cross-task alignment than decoupled methods.

title: A Benchmark for Heterogeneous Stereo Deblurring with Physically- and Epipolar-constrained Cross Attention
summary: Modern stereo-capable smartphones enable immersive XR content capture. However, hardware heterogeneity across camera modules often causes severe asymmetric blur artifacts. Existing methods and benchmarks largely assume homogeneous stereo setups and therefore do not explicitly address such asymmetric degradation. To bridge this gap, we present a dedicated framework for heterogeneous stereo deblurring. First, we introduce the heterogeneous stereo deblurring (HSD) dataset, constructed from real smartphone stereo captures via multi-frame integration. Second, we propose physically- and epipolar-constrained cross attention (PECA), a lightweight module that restricts cross-view matching to an epipolar search window bounded by a optics-derived disparity upper bound. By enforcing physically valid disparity constraints, PECA enables efficient and reliable cross-view feature fusion. Moreover, our confidence-weighted attention with residual fusion emphasizes cross-guided deblurring when correspondences are reliable, while naturally falling back to self-deblurring in occluded or unreliable regions. PECA is architecture-agnostic and consistently improves CNN-, Transformer-, and NAFNet-based baselines. Extensive experiments on HSD show that PECA-enhanced models achieve improved restoration performance with favorable efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces HSD, a dataset for heterogeneous stereo deblurring, and PECA, a physically- and epipolar-constrained cross-attention module that improves cross-view fusion for deblurring across CNN/Transformer/NAFNet baselines, yielding better restoration with similar or lower cost.
AI.motivation: Hardware-induced asymmetric blur in real heterogeneous stereo setups is not well-addressed by existing homogeneous-stereo benchmarks. A dedicated dataset and a cross-view module with physical and epipolar constraints are needed to enable reliable deblurring improvements.
AI.method: Propose PECA: a lightweight cross-attention module that confines cross-view matching to an epipolar search window bounded by a disparity upper bound derived from optical properties; use confidence-weighted attention with residual fusion to emphasize reliable correspondences and fall back to self-deblurring in occluded/unreliable regions. Dataset HSD created from real smartphone captures via multi-frame integration; assesses architecture-agnostic performance improvements across CNN, Transformer, and NAFNet baselines.
AI.result: PECA-enhanced models achieve improved restoration performance on the HSD benchmark while maintaining favorable efficiency; benefits observed across CNN-, Transformer-, and NAFNet-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: PECA provides a general, efficient solution for heterogeneous stereo deblurring, leveraging physically valid disparity constraints to enable robust cross-view fusion; the HSD dataset offers a realistic evaluation platform for this problem and supports architecture-agnostic gains.

title: Learning with a Single Rollout via Monte Carlo Pass@k Critic
summary: Estimating token-level advantages in reinforcement learning (RL) for language models remains challenging because scaling up episodic experience collection is expensive. The difficulty intensifies for baseline advantage estimation methods, where repeated sampling causes trajectories to diverge into substantially different reasoning prefixes. In this context, RL algorithms such as GRPO prove limited: an outcome reward is too sparse to be attributed to specific actions like intermediate steps, and comparisons across sampled traces are non-trivial because they are heterogeneous. To mitigate both the computational cost of repeated sampling and the difficulty of credit assignment, we study single-rollout proximal policy optimization (SR-PPO) featuring token-level credit assignment in RL for language models. Instead of estimating advantages by normalizing episodic returns within the candidate group, we train a calibrated token-level credit critic using Monte Carlo outcomes from one rollout per prompt. Specifically, we use the critic to predict the Pass@k success probability at the prompt prefix, which is derived from a Pass@1 attempt. This choice yields a more selective learning signal than Pass@1: it discounts easily solved prefixes while prioritizing hard ones whose success probability remains marginal. We show that as $k$ increases, Pass@k converges to a reachability indicator, reflecting whether a prefix can lead to at least one successful continuation. In an explicit state graph, the limit ($k \rightarrow \infty$) can be computed in $O(|V|+|E|)$ time, offering a promising surrogate for direct credit assignment without the need to sample contrastive traces. As an initial validation, SR-PPO exhibits stable learning dynamics, along with consistent gains in Pass@128 success rates on mathematical reasoning benchmarks such as HMMT26 and AIME24.

AI.tldr: SR-PPO with a token-level credit critic uses a single rollout per prompt to predict Pass@k for the prompt prefix, enabling scalable credit assignment for language-model RL. As k grows, Pass@k approaches a reachability indicator, which can be computed in linear time on a state graph, offering an efficient surrogate for credit assignment without sampling contrasts. Preliminary results show stable learning and improved Pass@128 on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Estimating token-level advantages in RL for LMs is costly due to repeated sampling and divergent reasoning prefixes; baseline methods have sparse outcomes and difficult cross-trace comparisons. There is a need for scalable, informative credit signals that reduce data collection while maintaining effective credit assignment.
AI.method: Propose single-rollout PPO (SR-PPO) with token-level credit assignment. Train a calibrated token-level credit critic using Monte Carlo outcomes from one rollout per prompt. The critic predicts Pass@k success probability at the prompt prefix, derived from Pass@1. This yields a selective learning signal; as k increases, Pass@k converges to a reachability indicator. In an explicit state graph, the limit (k→∞) can be computed in O(|V|+|E|) time, avoiding contrastive trace sampling.
AI.result: SR-PPO yields stable learning dynamics and yields consistent gains in Pass@128 on mathematical reasoning benchmarks such as HMMT26 and AIME24.
AI.conclusion: A scalable surrogate for token-level credit assignment is achieved via a token-level credit critic predicting Pass@k, with theoretical convergence to a reachability indicator and efficient graph-based computation. Early results indicate stable training and performance gains on challenging math benchmarks.

title: Taxonomy-aware deep learning for hierarchical marine species classification in underwater imagery
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Presented at SPIE Defense + Security 2026 (Machine Learning from Challenging Data conference), National Harbor, MD, April 2026
summary: Automated classification of marine species from underwater imagery is essential for scalable ocean biodiversity monitoring and conservation policy. Existing approaches struggle with severe domain shift across collection platforms, fine-grained visual similarity between closely related species, and uneven annotation granularity, where many specimens can only be identified to genus or a coarser taxonomic rank. We present a taxonomy-aware deep learning framework that aligns both the training loss and the inference rule with the hierarchical structure of biological classification, combining a taxonomy-weighted loss, minimum-risk Bayesian inference, multi-scale feature encoding, and independent per-rank classification heads. Evaluated on the FathomNet 2025 dataset1 (79 marine classes across seven taxonomic ranks), the system achieves a mean taxonomic distance of 1.581, within 3% of the 1st-place solution (1.535), with the largest gains from metric-aligned inference and simple, decoupled components that generalize better than learned dependencies under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Taxonomy-aware deep learning for automated marine species classification that aligns loss and inference with biological taxonomy, improving robustness to domain shift and fine-grained distinctions.
AI.motivation: Addresses major challenges in automated marine biodiversity monitoring: severe domain shift across collection platforms, fine-grained visual similarity among related species, and uneven annotation granularity that often restricts identification to genus or higher ranks.
AI.method: Integrates a taxonomy-weighted loss, minimum-risk Bayesian inference, multi-scale feature encoding, and independent per-rank classification heads to enforce hierarchical structure during training and inference.
AI.result: On the FathomNet 2025 dataset (79 marine classes across seven taxonomic ranks), achieves mean taxonomic distance of 1.581, within 3% of the top solution (1.535); gains primarily from metric-aligned inference; components are simple, decoupled, and generalize better under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: The taxonomy-aware framework delivers competitive performance and robustness to distribution shift and annotation granularity by leveraging hierarchical structure through decoupled components and metric-aligned inference.

title: From Sparse and Imperfect 2D Anchors to Consistent 3D Gaussian Street Scenes: Support-Aware Appearance
summary: Image priors can synthesize target conditions for 3D Gaussian street scenes, but independently edited views do not define a coherent 3D target. Direct fitting can propagate view-specific noise, while existing pipelines do not jointly handle imperfect sparse anchors and standard-rasterizer deployment. To address this gap, teacher-relative appearance residual distillation is introduced for appearance baking. A structured space for frequency decomposition, confidence estimation, and primitive-level lifting is formed by residuals between teacher anchors and original renders. The direct optimization signal is supplied by renderer-space matching, while primitive assignment is regularized by support-aware Gaussian-space aggregation. Supported detail is admitted and unsupported noise is suppressed through confidence-gated coarse-to-fine optimization, after which all residuals are baked into fixed-geometry spherical-harmonic coefficients. The teacher and auxiliary training modules are discarded at inference. Evaluation across Waymo street assets, Tanks and Temples scenes, and multiple target conditions shows a favorable overall balance of target alignment, content preservation, artifact suppression, and cross-view consistency over editing-based baselines. Ablations confirm the effectiveness of the main components. Code will be released at https://github.com/Cagares/Baking-for-3D-Gaussian.

AI.tldr: Introduces teacher-relative appearance residual distillation to bake appearance for 3D Gaussian street scenes, delivering coherent target views by distilling residuals between teacher anchors and renders into fixed-geometry spherical-harmonic coefficients.
AI.motivation: Independent edited views in 3D Gaussian representations lead to incoherence and noise; direct fitting propagates view-specific artifacts; current pipelines fail to jointly handle imperfect sparse anchors and standard rasterizers.
AI.method: Propose teacher-relative appearance residual distillation with a structured space for frequency decomposition, confidence estimation, and primitive-level lifting. Use residuals between teacher anchors and original renders. Optimize via renderer-space matching; regularize primitive assignment with support-aware Gaussian-space aggregation; apply confidence-gated coarse-to-fine optimization to admit details and suppress noise; bake all residuals into fixed-geometry spherical-harmonic coefficients; discard teacher/aux modules at inference.
AI.result: Evaluations on Waymo street assets and Tanks and Temples across multiple target conditions show improved target alignment, content preservation, artifact suppression, and cross-view consistency compared with editing-based baselines. Ablation studies validate key components.
AI.conclusion: The method yields coherent, high-quality target appearances with efficient inference by baking residuals into SH coefficients; demonstrates robustness to imperfect anchors and rasterization constraints; code will be released.

title: DFMU: Data-Frugal Machine Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning is an emerging domain that ensures the safe removal of elements (includes concepts, attributes, entity and class) from the trained model along with least drop in model performance. The domain of machine unlearning brings its own indigenous challenges since the removal of pre-trained elements from model will always degrade the model performance on remaining elements. The existing methods basically rely on retraining for removal of elements from the pre-trained model, which is compute extensive. In this work, we propose a machine unlearning method which helps to reduce the computational requirement for faster retain-dataset accuracy convergence which also does not require extensive retraining of the pre-trained model. The proposed method, Data-Frugal Machine Unlearning (DFMU) requires only a single forward and backward pass for computing the importance score of various computational blocks of a model. The importance score computation is based on knowledge preserving pruning which helps to converge faster and requires far less data as compared to the existing methods. Experimentally, it achieves 40% more retain-accuracy with just 13% of data samples in comparison with SOTA method on various public datasets and also averages 88% faster processing time for forgetting a given class.

AI.tldr: Data-Frugal Machine Unlearning (DFMU) offers a single-pass, data-efficient approach to unlearning by pruning model blocks via knowledge-preserving criteria, reducing retraining needs.
AI.motivation: To enable rapid, resource-efficient unlearning in ML models without extensive retraining, addressing performance degradation on remaining data.
AI.method: Compute importance scores for model blocks with a single forward and backward pass using knowledge-preserving pruning; select blocks to facilitate forgetting with minimal data; target retain-accuracy convergence.
AI.result: Reportedly achieves 40% higher retain-accuracy using only 13% of data compared to SOTA, and ~88% faster processing for forgetting a class across public datasets.
AI.conclusion: DF MU provides a data- and compute-efficient unlearning paradigm that maintains performance on retained data while significantly speeding up forgetting operations.

title: MIMFlow: Integrating Masked Image Modeling with Normalizing Flows for End-to-End Image Generation
summary: Normalizing Flows (NFs) are powerful generative models capable of exact density estimation and sampling. However, their strict invertibility often forces the model to exhaust its capacity on low-level pixel details, hindering the capture of high-level semantic structures. While Masked Image Modeling (MIM) has excelled in representation learning, its integration into generative pipelines has remained largely modular and disjointed. In this paper, we propose MIMFlow, a unified end-to-end framework that jointly optimizes latent semantics, pixel reconstruction, and generative flow. By employing a VAE encoder to infer semantic latent from masked images, MIMFlow achieves a principled decoupling of the generative task: the Normalizing Flow focuses on modeling a simplified, low-frequency semantic manifold, while a specialized decoder handles high-frequency synthesis. This design effectively resolves the inherent capacity bottleneck of NFs, allowing the model to prioritize global structural coherence over redundant noise. Empirical results on ImageNet 256$\times$256 show that MIMFlow-L reaches 71.3\% linear probing accuracy and an FID of 2.50. Despite using only 128 tokens (50\% fewer than standard models), it yields a 32.8\% performance gain over similar-scale NF baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/MCG-NJU/MIMFlow.

AI.tldr: MIMFlow unifies masked image modeling with normalizing flows by decoupling semantic latent extraction from high-frequency detail synthesis using a VAE, letting the NF model low-frequency semantics while a decoder handles details, yielding strong ImageNet 256 results with token efficiency.
AI.motivation: Normalizing flows are powerful but constrained by strict invertibility, which leads them to spend capacity on low-level pixels rather than high-level semantics. Masked Image Modeling offers semantic-rich representations but has been poorly integrated into generative pipelines. A unified end-to-end framework is needed to leverage MIM for improved generative modeling.
AI.method: Use a VAE encoder to infer semantic latent from masked images, decoupling semantic modeling from pixel synthesis. The normalizing flow models a simplified, low-frequency semantic manifold, while a specialized decoder handles high-frequency reconstruction. The model jointly optimizes latent semantics, pixel reconstruction, and the flow. MIMFlow-L uses 128 tokens (50% fewer).
AI.result: On ImageNet 256×256, MIMFlow-L achieves 71.3% linear probing accuracy and an FID of 2.50, with a 32.8% performance gain over similar-scale NF baselines using 128 tokens.
AI.conclusion: The approach resolves the capacity bottleneck of normalizing flows by separating semantic (low-frequency) modeling from high-frequency synthesis, enabling better global coherence and efficient generation within an end-to-end MIM-guided framework.

title: Low-Complexity Policy Tessellations in Structured Markov Decision Processes
summary: We study optimal-policy geometry in structured Markov decision processes. While approximate dynamic programming and reinforcement learning typically approximate high-dimensional value functions, we show that optimal policies induce simpler decision tessellations. We propose boundary-based policy approximations that learn policy regions directly. A policy-loss decomposition links performance degradation to action margins and explains why errors concentrate near indifference boundaries. Inventory control and queue admission experiments show lower policy error, smaller value gaps, faster error decay, and stability than reinforcement learning baselines.

AI.tldr: Policy geometry in structured MDPs yields simpler decision tessellations; boundary-based policy approximations learn policy regions directly, with a policy-loss decomposition showing degradation tied to action margins and concentration of errors near indifference boundaries; empirical results on inventory control and queue admission show advantages over RL baselines.
AI.motivation: To understand whether optimal policies in structured MDPs induce simpler decision boundaries than value-function approximators reveal, and to exploit this geometry for efficient policy learning.
AI.method: Develop boundary-based policy approximations that partition the state space into policy regions (tessellations). Introduce a policy-loss decomposition that relates performance loss to action margins, explaining error concentration near indifference boundaries. Validate on inventory control and queue admission experiments comparing to RL baselines.
AI.result: Policy-based regions achieve lower policy error, smaller value gaps, faster error decay, and greater stability than reinforcement learning baselines in the tested tasks.
AI.conclusion: Optimal policies in structured MDPS exhibit simpler geometries; boundary-based region learning provides efficient, stable policy approximations and clarifies where and why RL methods incur errors, especially near indifference boundaries.

title: TriViewBench: Controlled Complexity Scaling for Multi-View Structural Reasoning in MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong performance on standard visual question answering benchmarks, yet their scalability under controlled structural complexity remains poorly understood. We introduce TriViewBench, a controlled three-view visual reasoning benchmark constructed from synthetic 3D scenes with explicitly parameterized object count and occlusion. The benchmark contains 1,923 scenes and over 14K Question-Answer (QA) pairs organized into four complexity levels and three reasoning categories: Local Decision, Object Counting, and Global Recovery. We evaluate 18 open- and closed-source MLLMs under a unified prompting protocol. All 18 models exhibit an identical capability hierarchy without exception (Local Decision > Object Counting > Global Recovery), and performance degrades monotonically with complexity: Local Decision tasks decline modestly (12.11% relative drop), while Object Counting degrades substantially (59.14%) and Global Recovery collapses severely (80.02%). Error analysis on Object Counting reveals two mechanistically independent failure modes: single-view tasks are dominated by undercounting due to occlusion blindness, whereas the multi-view task reverses to overcounting due to cross-view identity confusion. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting yields near-zero overall benefit ($Δ= -0.16\%$) and its effect on Global Recovery is strongly capability-gated, suggesting that the bottleneck lies in cross-view spatial representation rather than reasoning strategy. These findings reveal fundamental scalability limitations in current MLLMs and position TriViewBench as a controlled diagnostic framework for analyzing structural reasoning failures.

AI.tldr: TriViewBench provides a controlled, synthetic 3D-vision benchmark for multimodal LLMs to assess structural reasoning under varying object count and occlusion. All models show a consistent performance hierarchy (Local Decision > Object Counting > Global Recovery) with performance collapsing as complexity rises; CoT offers little to no benefit, highlighting cross-view spatial representation bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: To systematically probe scalability and structural reasoning of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) under controlled, parameterized visual complexity and occlusion, addressing a gap where existing benchmarks may not separate reasoning from perception.
AI.method: Construct TriViewBench from synthetic 3D scenes with explicit parameters for object count and occlusion. The dataset contains 1,923 scenes and over 14K QA pairs, organized into four complexity levels and three reasoning categories (Local Decision, Object Counting, Global Recovery). Eighteen MLLMs (open- and closed-source) are evaluated under a unified prompting protocol. Analysis includes error modes and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting effects.
AI.result: All models exhibit identical capability hierarchy: Local Decision > Object Counting > Global Recovery. Performance degrades monotonically with complexity: Local Decision has a modest relative drop (12.11%), Object Counting a substantial drop (59.14%), and Global Recovery a severe drop (80.02%). Object Counting errors split into two independent modes: single-view undercount due to occlusion blindness, and multi-view overcount due to cross-view identity confusion. CoT prompting yields near-zero overall benefit (Δ = -0.16%), with Global Recovery especially gated by cross-view spatial representation, indicating the bottleneck lies in spatial understanding rather than reasoning strategy.
AI.conclusion: TriViewBench reveals fundamental scalability limitations in current MLLMs for structured, multi-view reasoning. It provides a controlled diagnostic framework to analyze and disentangle perception, counting, and cross-view spatial reasoning failures.

title: How Robust is OCR-Reasoning? Evaluating OCR-Reasoning Robustness of Vision-Language Models under Visual Perturbations
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved strong performance on OCR-based benchmarks and increasingly focused on text-rich understanding, but their robustness under controlled visual degradation remains insufficiently understood. This gap is critical for OCR reasoning, where visual corruption can induce OCR errors and structural distortions, thereby introducing uncertainty into the reasoning task. To systematically study this problem, we introduce OCR-Robust, a benchmark designed for evaluating OCR reasoning robustness under visual perturbations. It contains 812 samples across two complementary subsets: OCR1.0, covering documents, scene text, receipts, handwriting, and mathematical content, and OCR2.0, focusing on charts, geometry diagrams, and tables. To enable efficient yet informative evaluation, we conduct a pilot study over 18 candidate perturbations and select 5 representative types at 3 severity levels each based on their impact and cross-model discriminability. We evaluate robustness using clean accuracy, Relative Corruption Retention (RCR), Worst-Case Retention (WCR), and a composite Corruption Robustness Index (CRI), and benchmark 18 models spanning proprietary systems, open-source VLMs, and OCR+LLM pipelines. Our results show that higher clean accuracy does not necessarily imply stronger robustness, and that models can suffer pronounced degradation in the worst case on OCR tasks that are sensitive to structure, and charts and tables are substantially more fragile than document-like inputs under perturbation.

AI.tldr: A benchmark, OCR-Robust, assesses OCR reasoning robustness of vision-language models under visual perturbations using 812 samples across two subsets (OCR1.0 and OCR2.0) and 5 perturbation types at 3 severities. It employs clean accuracy, Relative Corruption Retention (RCR), Worst-Case Retention (WCR), and a Corruption Robustness Index (CRI) across 18 models. Key finding: clean accuracy does not imply robustness; charts/tables inputs are especially fragile under perturbations.
AI.motivation: OCR-based reasoning systems degrade under visual corruption, leading to OCR errors and distorted structure which propagate to reasoning tasks. There is a lack of systematic evaluation of robustness of vision-language models on text-rich inputs under degradation.
AI.method: Construct OCR-Robust benchmark with 812 samples split into OCR1.0 (documents, scene text, receipts, handwriting, math) and OCR2.0 (charts, geometry diagrams, tables). Conduct a pilot study over 18 perturbations and select 5 representative perturbation types at 3 severity levels based on impact and cross-model discriminability. Evaluate 18 models (proprietary, open-source VLMs, OCR+LLM pipelines). Metrics: clean accuracy, Relative Corruption Retention (RCR), Worst-Case Retention (WCR), Corruption Robustness Index (CRI).
AI.result: The study finds that higher clean accuracy does not guarantee robustness; models can experience significant worst-case degradation on OCR tasks that depend on structural cues. Charts and tables are substantially more fragile than document-like inputs under perturbation.
AI.conclusion: OCR-Robust reveals systematic robustness gaps in current OCR-reliant VLMs and OCR-LLM pipelines. The benchmark provides a framework for evaluating robustness under visual degradation and guiding improvements for OCR reasoning under noisy conditions.

title: TL++: Accuracy and Privacy Preserving Traversal Learning for Distributed Intelligent Systems
summary: Distributed intelligent systems increasingly need to train across data silos without centralizing raw data. Federated learning keeps data local but can suffer under heterogeneous partitions and requires repeated full-model exchange. Split learning reduces communication through cut-layer activations, but standard protocols generally do not recover centralized mini-batch gradient behavior and may expose activations and gradients in plaintext. We present TL++, a two-mode traversal-learning framework that constructs virtual batches across nodes to recover centralized mini-batch gradient behavior under explicit synchronization assumptions. Base mode exchanges cut-layer activations and gradients rather than full models. Secure mode secret-shares each cut-layer activation and gradient between an orchestrator and a non-colluding helper, preventing either server from observing plaintext cut-layer tensors. This protection is limited to a semi-honest two-server setting; labels and loss-related outputs remain visible to the orchestrator. In the lightweight secure path evaluated here, exactness requires a linear or affine server path, while nonlinear operations require nonlinear MPC or approximation. We formalize TL++, analyze communication and computation costs, and evaluate it against federated and split-learning baselines on CIFAR-10 and BioGPT/PubMedQA using full fine-tuning and LoRA. On CIFAR-10, TL++ base cut 1 and exact secure cut 3 achieve accuracies of 91.41% (SD 0.19) and 90.93% (SD 0.17), respectively, exceeding the strongest measured non-TL++ baseline by more than 12 percentage points. TL++ base cut 1 also reduces per-step communication by 13.1-fold relative to full-model synchronization. PubMedQA results similarly favor TL++. Overall, TL++ approaches centralized-training performance while reducing communication and providing activation-level secret sharing.

AI.tldr: TL++ is a two-mode traversal-learning framework for distributed training across data silos that recovers centralized mini-batch gradient behavior using a base mode that exchanges cut-layer activations/gradients and a secure mode that secret-shares them between an orchestrator and a non-colluding helper. It offers near-centralized accuracy with substantial communication reductions, but security is limited to a semi-honest two-server setting and exactness depends on linear/affine server paths for the secure path.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between split learning gradient behavior and centralized mini-batch updates, while reducing communication and exposing fewer plaintext activations/gradients.
AI.method: Two modes: base mode exchanges cut-layer activations and gradients; secure mode secret-shares cut-layer activations/gradients between an orchestrator and a non-colluding helper to protect plaintext data. Formalizes TL++, analyzes costs, and evaluates against federated and split-learning baselines on CIFAR-10 and BioGPT/PubMedQA with full fine-tuning and LoRA.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10, base-cut 1 achieves 91.41% (SD 0.19) and exact-secure-cut 3 achieves 90.93% (SD 0.17), outperforming the strongest non-TL++ baselines by >12 percentage points. TL++ base cut 1 reduces per-step communication by 13.1x vs full-model sync. PubMedQA results favor TL++.
AI.conclusion: TL++ approaches centralized-training performance while reducing communication and providing activation-level secret sharing; exactness in secure mode requires linear/affine server paths; nonlinear operations require nonlinear MPC or approximation.

title: DomainShuttle: Freeform Open Domain Subject-driven Text-to-video Generation
summary: Open domain subject-driven text-to-video (S2V) generation has drawn significant interest in academia and industry. Open domain S2V mainly involves two scenarios: in-domain, which requires retaining the reference subject features as much as possible, and cross-domain, which preserves the intrinsic features of the subject while allowing subject-irrelevant properties to vary flexibly according to the text prompt. Existing methods primarily focus on maximizing subject fidelity in in-domain scenarios, which limits their editability and adaptability in cross-domain scenarios, such as novel styles, semantic combinations, or domain attributes. In this study, we propose that an ideal S2V method should flexibly shuttle between different domains, achieving strong performance in both in-domain and cross-domain scenarios. To this end, we propose DomainShuttle, which could achieve high fidelity and generative flexibility for open domain video personalization. Specifically, we introduce Domain-MoT, which decouples videos and reference features and introduces the domain-aware AdaLN for domain-specific modeling of reference images. We then introduce the Video-Reference DualRoPE scheme, which places reference image tokens and video tokens in separate RoPE spaces to enable precise subject-level spatial modeling, and Cross-Pair Consistent Loss, which aims to extract intrinsic subject features unaffected by irrelevant features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DomainShuttle achieves significant performance improvements over existing methods, exhibiting high subject fidelity and generative flexibility across diverse open domain application scenarios.

AI.tldr: DomainShuttle enables flexible open-domain video personalization by jointly achieving high subject fidelity and adaptability across in-domain and cross-domain settings using Domain-MoT with AdaLN, Video-Reference DualRoPE, and Cross-Pair Consistent Loss.
AI.motivation: Current open-domain S2V methods overemphasize in-domain fidelity, restricting cross-domain editability (novel styles, combinations, or domain attributes). A unified method that supports both high fidelity and flexible generation is needed.
AI.method: DomainShuttle introduces: (1) Domain-MoT that decouples video content from reference features and uses domain-aware AdaLN for domain-specific modeling of reference images; (2) Video-Reference DualRoPE which allocates reference image tokens and video tokens to separate RoPE spaces for precise subject-level spatial modeling; (3) Cross-Pair Consistent Loss to extract intrinsic subject features while suppressing irrelevant variability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show DomainShuttle outperforms existing methods, achieving high subject fidelity and generative flexibility across diverse open-domain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DomainShuttle provides a unified framework that bridges in-domain fidelity and cross-domain adaptability for open-domain S2V, enabling robust video personalization with strong subject consistency and flexible styling.

title: The Interplay of Harness Design and Post-Training in LLM Agents
summary: Tool-integrated LLM agents are often wrapped within a harness: the scaffolding that determines which tools are exposed, how they are described, and what auxiliary information accompanies each per-step observation. While agents are routinely post-trained, this scaffolding is typically treated as a fixed engineering detail, with design effort limited to the training-free regime. Moreover, existing post-training algorithms assume a static environment, even though tool environments and tasks often shift upon deployment. To address this gap, we extend $\texttt{ALFWorld}$ (i) to treat the harness as a controllable design dimension and (ii) to support evaluation under task and tool environment shifts. Building on this, we systematically analyze how the harness design influences post-training in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) settings. We empirically show that harness-aware post-training not only improves in-distribution performance but also enables agents to robustly adapt to OOD settings. Under a harness with minimal design effort, post-training suffers a drastic performance drop under stronger tool environment shifts, further highlighting the importance of harness-aware post-training under such shifts.

AI.tldr: Harness design is a tunable dimension; post-training with harness-aware setup improves ID and OOD robustness for tool-integrated LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap that scaffolding (harness) is often fixed and post-training assumes static environments; deployment involves evolving tool environments and tasks requiring evaluation of harness design impact.
AI.method: Extend ALFWorld to treat the harness as a controllable design dimension and evaluate post-training under task and tool environment shifts; systematically analyze harness design influence on post-training in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings; conduct empirical studies varying harness complexity and environment shifts.
AI.result: Harness-aware post-training yields better in-distribution performance and robust adaptation to OOD; with minimal harness design, post-training performance collapses under stronger tool environment shifts, underscoring the need for harness-aware training with dynamic environments.
AI.conclusion: Designing the tool harness is a critical factor for the effectiveness of post-training in tool-enabled LLM agents; results motivate design guidelines and further exploration of automated harness optimization under distribution shifts.

title: A welding penetration prediction model for laser welding process based on self-supervised learning using physics-informed neural networks
summary: The laser welding full-penetration is of critical importance, as it constitutes one of the fundamental factors in achieving defect-free welded joints. Accurate prediction of the penetration state is therefore essential for ensuring weld quality. To this end, this paper introduces SimPhysNet, a novel algorithm that achieves high classification accuracy in laser welding penetration prediction using only a limited number of labelled images. This approach effectively overcomes the limitations of supervised learning classification algorithms, which are hindered in industrial applications by their dependence on extensive, high-quality labelled data. The core of SimPhysNet is a unique self-supervised learning paradigm that embeds physical priors into a contrastive learning framework. By incorporating a physics-informed neural network (PINN), the model is guided to extract physically meaningful features of the molten pool and keyhole from a large set of unlabelled data, while three image augmentation tasks further enhance its generalization capabilities. Subsequently, a few-shot learning strategy, based on prototypical networks, enables robust classification by constructing class representations from a minimal set of labelled images. Experimental results demonstrate that SimPhysNet achieves a classification accuracy of 96.06% using only 200 labelled images (approximately 5% of the total labelled dataset), which is comparable to the performance of conventional supervised learning algorithms that utilize the entire labelled dataset. This work presents a new, efficient, and highly accurate method, providing the way for the intelligent automation of laser welding.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed, self-supervised, few-shot learning pipeline (SimPhysNet) for laser welding penetration state classification, achieving 96.06% accuracy with only ~200 labeled images (~5% of labeled data), comparable to full-supervised methods.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on large labeled datasets in industrial laser welding for accurate penetration prediction and weld quality control; enable automated, data-efficient monitoring.
AI.method: Integrates self-supervised contrastive learning with a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to extract physically meaningful features of the molten pool and keyhole from unlabeled data. Employs three image augmentation tasks to improve generalization. Uses prototypical networks for few-shot classification, forming class prototypes from a tiny labeled set.
AI.result: Achieves 96.06% classification accuracy using 200 labeled images (~5% of the labeled dataset), matching the performance of conventional supervised learning that uses the full labeled set.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a data-efficient, accurate framework for laser welding penetration prediction, enabling intelligent automation by reducing labeling requirements through physics-guided SSL and few-shot learning.

title: The Generalization Spectrum: A Chromatographic Approach to Evaluating Learning Algorithms
summary: Traditional evaluations measure a learning algorithm's final performance on an i.i.d. test set, reducing learning to a single aggregate score. This approach obscures a fundamental question: to what extent does learning from a specific example generalize to others? Such per-sample generalization, akin to learning by analogy in human cognition, captures how far the knowledge extracted from one example can transfer, yet remains invisible to standard benchmarks. We introduce the Generalization Spectrum, an evaluation framework designed to expose this hidden dimension. For each training example, we construct a controlled suite of test variants arranged by increasing transfer distance, from exact recall to implementation transfer across languages, context transfer under complete narrative re-framing, category-matched in-domain problems, and an unpaired baseline. By tracking performance across these distances, we reveal not just whether an algorithm learns, but how far that learning extends. We instantiate this framework on competitive programming, using a selection-and-synthesis pipeline seeded with recent problems to mitigate contamination. We first compare three canonical learning paradigms under matched memorization. RL converts memorization into near-transfer more efficiently than SFT-family baselines, while ICL exhibits strong but correspondence-dependent transfer. We then use the Spectrum to diagnose within-family variants. The resulting profiles show that local gains need not expand the generalization radius: abstractions and hints mainly lift local transfer, RFT preserves a stronger far-transfer tail than reference SFT, and self-distillation or hint-assisted RL can reduce far transfer even when local transfer or optimization improves.

AI.tldr: Introduces Generalization Spectrum to quantify per-sample transfer of learned knowledge by testing each training example across a spectrum of transfer distances; demonstrates in competitive programming that memorization alone doesn't guarantee broad generalization; RL improves near-transfer, ICL is strong but variable, and various strategies can affect far-transfer.
AI.motivation: Standard final-performance metrics miss how learning generalizes from one example to others; a per-sample generalization metric capturing transfer radius is needed to understand learning dynamics beyond aggregate accuracy.
AI.method: For each training example, construct a hierarchy of test variants arranged by increasing transfer distance (from exact recall to cross-language implementation transfer, narrative reframing, in-domain category matches, and unpaired baselines). Use a selection-and-synthesis pipeline seeded with recent problems to mitigate contamination. Compare three canonical learning paradigms under matched memorization (RL, SFT-family, ICL). Then diagnose within-family variants with the Spectrum to chart transfer profiles.
AI.result: RL converts memorization into near-transfer more efficiently than SFT baselines; ICL shows strong but correspondence-dependent transfer. Local gains (abstractions, hints) lift local transfer; RFT maintains a stronger far-transfer tail than reference SFT. Self-distillation or hint-assisted RL can reduce far transfer even when local transfer or optimization improves.
AI.conclusion: The Generalization Spectrum reveals a hidden dimension of learning—the transfer radius from each training example. It enables diagnostics of how design choices affect not just local performance but broader generalization, informing evaluation and model-building strategies beyond final accuracy in standard benchmarks.

title: A cross-process welding penetration status prediction algorithm based on unsupervised domain adaptation in laser and TIG welding
summary: Supervised deep learning has been widely used for weld penetration state classification; however, its performance often degrades significantly under domain shift, such as when transferring models between welding processes with distinct physical mechanisms:for instance, from arc-dominated tungsten inert gas (TIG) welding to keyhole-based laser welding. To overcome this limitation, we propose an unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) framework integrated with a gradual source domain expansion (GSDE) strategy. Evaluated on dedicated TIG and laser welding datasets, our approach achieves high accuracy in both same-process and cross-process transfer tasks. Specifically, it attains average accuracies of 90.65% on TIGFH and 90.72% on LSPS in same-process settings, surpassing a supervised baseline by 35.83% and 38.87%, respectively. More notably, in cross-process scenarios, it reaches 80.48% for TIG to Laser and 81.13% for Laser to TIG, improving upon the baseline by 43.39% and 43.40%. UMAP visualizations verify that the model learns domain-invariant features while maintaining discriminative class boundaries. This method considerably lowers the relabeling cost for new welding processes and enhances the versatility of intelligent monitoring across different welding systems.
