AI.tldr: Unsupervised domain adaptation with gradual source domain expansion (GSDE) improves weld penetration state classification under cross-domain shifts between TIG and laser welding, delivering strong same-process performance and notable cross-process transfer with reduced relabeling needs.
AI.motivation: Domain shift between welding processes (arc-dominated TIG vs keyhole laser welding) degrades supervised models; reducing labeling effort and enabling cross-system monitoring are desirable.
AI.method: Proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation framework integrated with a gradual source domain expansion strategy. Evaluated on TIG and laser welding datasets; uses domain-invariant feature learning (via UDA) and validates with UMAP visualizations to confirm discriminative class boundaries.
AI.result: Same-process accuracies: ~90.65% (TIGFH) and ~90.72% (LSPS); improvements over supervised baseline by ~35.83% and ~38.87%. Cross-process: ~80.48% (TIG→Laser) and ~81.13% (Laser→TIG); improvements over baseline by ~43.39% and ~43.40%. UMAPs show domain-invariant features with maintained class separability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GSDE-based UDA framework reduces relabeling costs for new welding processes and enhances transferability and versatility of intelligent weld monitoring across TIG and laser systems.

title: MVTrack4Gen: Multi-View Point Tracking as Geometric Supervision for 4D Video Generation
summary: Synthesizing a novel-view video from a monocular reference video along a target camera trajectory requires both geometric consistency and motion fidelity with respect to the reference video. Existing methods based on explicit 3D representations are limited by the accuracy of off-the-shelf reconstruction modules, which often produce inaccurate geometry for dynamic objects in monocular videos. In contrast, camera-conditioning-only methods can achieve high visual quality but often struggle to preserve geometric and motion consistency. In this work, we introduce MVTrack4Gen (Multi-View point Tracking for Novel-View Generation), a motion-aware training framework that leverages multi-view point tracking as an additional geometric and motion supervision signal for camera-conditioning-only novel-view video diffusion models. Our key finding is that specific attention layers encode strong correspondence cues, where query features attend to key features at geometrically corresponding locations across views and over time, and the misalignment of these correspondences causes motion inconsistency. Based on this observation, we route these features into an auxiliary multi-view tracking head and jointly train the diffusion model with a point-tracking objective. By explicitly strengthening these motion-aware correspondences, MVTrack4Gen improves existing models to better follow the motion in the reference view and maintain cross-view geometric consistency. Across diverse benchmarks, our method achieves state-of-the-art geometric consistency and competitive camera accuracy.

AI.tldr: Introduces MVTrack4Gen, a motion-aware training framework that uses a multi-view point-tracking head to enforce geometric and motion consistency in camera-conditioning diffusion models for monocular-to-novel-view video synthesis.
AI.motivation: Explicit 3D geometry methods struggle with inaccurate monocular reconstructions for dynamic scenes; camera-conditioning methods preserve visuals but lack geometry/motion consistency.
AI.method: Extracts attention-based correspondence cues in certain layers; routes these features to an auxiliary multi-view tracking head; jointly trains diffusion model with a point-tracking objective to strengthen motion-aware correspondences.
AI.result: Improved geometric consistency; achieves state-of-the-art in geometric consistency and competitive camera accuracy on diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging motion-aware correspondences via a tracking head improves cross-view consistency in diffusion-based novel-view video generation, offering a robust alternative to 3D-reconstruction-based methods.

title: Towards Robust EEG Decoding Based on Riemannian Self-Attention
summary: Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) based on electroencephalography (EEG) enables direct interaction between the brain and external environments and has significant applications in assistive technologies, medical rehabilitation, and entertainment. Recently, EEG decoding methods based on Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) learning have demonstrated superior performance. However, these methods typically employ basic network architectures and do not explicitly capture local relationships between EEG signals. This limitation is problematic for EEG signals due to their inherently low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). Moreover, most existing Riemannian manifold-based methods are restricted to specific metrics. The most widely used is the Affine-Invariant Metric (AIM). However, it has a quadratic dependency on the SPD matrices and cannot handle ill-conditioned SPD matrices, which hinders the effectiveness of networks. In contrast, the Bures-Wasserstein Metric (BWM) exhibits linear dependence on SPD matrices and demonstrates superior performance for ill conditioning. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Riemannian self-attention network based on the BWM. Additionally, the recently introduced power-deformed generalized Bures-Wasserstein metric reveals a nonlinear relationship between SPD matrices and matrix power deformation. This metric provides a more nuanced representation of the geometric structure of the SPD manifold. Consequently, we extend our model to a learnable version. For simplicity, we refer to it as GBWAtt. Experimental results on three EEG benchmarking datasets validate the robustness and effectiveness of our proposed method. The code is available at https://github.com/jissc/GBWAtt.

AI.tldr: A Riemannian self-attention network (GBWAtt) for EEG decoding using the Bures-Wasserstein metric on SPD matrices; introduces a learnable extension via a power-deformed generalized Bures-Wasserstein metric to better capture SPD geometry and local signal relations; demonstrated robustness and effectiveness on three EEG benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: EEG signals have low SNR and require models that respect SPD geometry; existing SPD learning methods use basic architectures and often rely on the AIM metric, which is ill-suited for ill-conditioned SPD matrices and has quadratic cost; a metric with linear dependence and the ability to model nonlinear SPD relationships could improve performance.
AI.method: Propose GBWAtt: a Riemannian self-attention network operating on SPD matrices with Bures-Wasserstein metric; extend to a learnable version using the power-deformed generalized Bures-Wasserstein metric to capture nonlinear relationships on the SPD manifold; validate on three EEG benchmarking datasets; publicly available code.
AI.result: Experiments on three EEG benchmarks show that GBWAtt is robust and effective for SPD-based EEG decoding, with improved handling of ill-conditioned SPD matrices and improved modeling of local signal relations; code is provided online.
AI.conclusion: GBWAtt offers a geometry-aware, robust approach for EEG decoding on SPD manifolds, leveraging BWM and its learnable, nonlinear extension to better exploit local signal structure and handle ill-conditioning; suitable for practical BCI deployment.

title: Gradient-based inverse lithography for EUV masks via the waveguide method and a physics-informed neural operator
summary: Gradient-based inverse lithography technology~(ILT) for extreme ultraviolet~(EUV) masks is presented. A novel framework treats the differentiable waveguide method and the recently proposed waveguide neural operator~(WGNO) as end-to-end physics engines, recovering the permittivity of the absorber of the mask through automatic differentiation of the full forward diffraction model. Numerical experiments on realistic 2D and 3D absorbers of the mask (TaBN, La, U) at $λ{=}11.2$~nm show that the considered ILT methods make it possible to obtain a mask structure that achieves the desired field on the wafer.

AI.tldr: A differentiable ILT framework combines the differentiable waveguide method and WGNO into an end-to-end physics engine to recover the absorber permittivity of EUV masks, enabling designs that achieve a target wafer field at 11.2 nm.
AI.motivation: To solve the inverse lithography problem for EUV masks by learning the absorber permittivity within a differentiable forward model, achieving precise control of the wafer-field through end-to-end optimization.
AI.method: Treat the differentiable waveguide method and WGNO as end-to-end physics engines; perform automatic differentiation through the full forward diffraction model to recover the absorber permittivity; validate on realistic 2D and 3D absorber geometries (TaBN, La, U) at λ = 11.2 nm.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show that the ILT methods enable the construction of mask structures that produce the desired field on the wafer.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end differentiable ILT with physics-based engines is viable for EUV mask design, enabling accurate recovery of absorber permittivity and realization of target wafer fields.

title: TryOnCrafter: Unleashing Camera Trajectories for Realistic Video Virtual Try-on via a Renderable 4D Try-on Proxy
summary: While Video Virtual Try-on (VVT) has achieved remarkable progress in synthesizing realistic garment overlays on dynamic subjects, existing paradigms remains fundamentally constrained by a passive dependency on source camera trajectories, failing to accommodate the requisite interactive freedom for omnidirectional viewpoint exploration. To address this limitation, we define a pioneering research frontier: Camera-controllable Video Virtual Try-on (CaM-VVT). Unlike conventional VVT, CaM-VVT not only necessitates viewpoint-agnostic texture hallucination but also strict structural synchronization between non-rigid human dynamics and background contexts under arbitrary, unconstrained camera movements. To tackle these challenges, we present TryOnCrafter, the first unified DiT-based framework specifically architected for the CaM-VVT task. Departing from implicit pixel-space manipulation, we introduce a Renderable 4D Try-on Proxy that explicitly decouples the human subject from the environment. This is achieved by distilling high-fidelity 2D try-on priors into a clothed 3DGS-based avatar, which is subsequently animated via SMPL-X sequences and metric-aligned into a reconstructed background point cloud. This proxy establishes a robust structural foundation with superior texture density and motion integrity. Our Proxy-Anchored Video DiT leverages this robust structural foundation as a primary geometric anchor, ensuring that the synthesized photorealistic videos are strictly constrained by prescribed trajectories and physically plausible deformations. Benefiting from the inherent editability of the 4D proxy, TryOnCrafter facilitates diverse downstream applications, including human relocalization, ``bullet time'' effects, and $360$-degree orbital viewing.

AI.tldr: CaM-VVT enables interactive, camera-controllable video virtual try-on using TryOnCrafter, a DiT-based framework with a 4D renderable proxy that decouples subject from environment and enforces trajectory-constrained rendering.
AI.motivation: Conventional VVT relies on source camera motion and lacks interactive omnidirectional viewpoint control. There is a need for texture-faithful, structurally synchronized rendering under arbitrary camera movements.
AI.method: Introduce a Renderable 4D Try-on Proxy that distills 2D try-on priors into a clothed 3DGS-based avatar, animated via SMPL-X sequences and aligned into a reconstructed background point cloud. Use the proxy as a geometric anchor in a Proxy-Anchored Video DiT for photorealistic, trajectory-constrained video synthesis, enabling 4D editability and downstream tasks.
AI.result: First unified DiT-based framework for CaM-VVT. Demonstrates robust texture density and motion integrity, producing photorealistic videos that adhere to prescribed trajectories and physically plausible deformations; enables applications like human relocalization, bullet time, and 360-degree orbital viewing.
AI.conclusion: CaM-VVT with a proxy-anchored DiT is a viable direction to achieve interactive, photorealistic video synthesis under arbitrary camera motion by explicitly decoupling subject from environment via a 4D proxy.

title: Distill on a Diet: Efficient Knowledge Distillation via Learnable Data Pruning
summary: Knowledge Distillation (KD) is widely used to obtain compact models for efficient inference in resource-constrained environments. Yet the computational overhead of the distillation process itself is often overlooked, raising the question of whether a better student model can be obtained with less data and less compute via data pruning. However, existing data pruning methods are not designed for KD: some introduce substantial overhead, such as obtaining training dynamics through retraining, while others rely on heuristic selection rules that fail to capture what KD actually requires, often resulting in suboptimal subsets. To address these issues, we propose IF-Beta, an efficient data pruning framework that combines influence functions with a learnable sampling policy. Empirically, we first demonstrate that influence functions can serve as an effective and efficient estimator of sample impact in KD settings, where only a pretrained teacher is available. Building on this, our sampling policy is specifically parameterized by a Beta distribution, whose highly flexible two-parameter family allows the policy to adapt to diverse pruning regimes rather than being tied to fixed heuristic forms. Next, we formulate KD pruning as optimizing this policy through a bilevel objective, where the inner loop operates in the teacher feature space with a KD-aligned objective, enabling fast proxy training, while the outer loop updates the policy parameters to maximize distillation performance. This design ensures that IF-Beta is both computationally efficient and inherently aligned with the goals of KD. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet show that IF-Beta consistently outperforms other baselines across a wide range of pruning ratios. Remarkably, IF-Beta enables students trained on less data and less compute to surpass the performance of students distilled on the full dataset.

AI.tldr: IF-Beta introduces an efficient data pruning framework for knowledge distillation (KD) that uses influence functions to estimate sample impact with a pretrained teacher and a learnable Beta-distributed sampling policy. The bilevel optimization tunes the policy to maximize KD performance, enabling training with less data and compute while achieving state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet.
AI.motivation: Knowledge distillation often incurs substantial computational overhead via data usage and training; existing pruning methods either add overhead (e.g., retraining) or rely on heuristic rules that fail to capture KD requirements. There is a need for data pruning that is aligned with KD objectives and efficient to compute.
AI.method: 1) Use influence functions as an efficient estimator of sample impact in KD settings with only a pretrained teacher. 2) Parameterize the sampling policy with a Beta distribution to provide flexible pruning behavior. 3) Cast KD pruning as a bilevel optimization: inner loop trains in the teacher feature space with a KD-aligned objective for proxy training; outer loop updates Beta parameters to maximize distillation performance.
AI.result: IF-Beta consistently outperforms baselines across pruning ratios on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet. Notably, students trained on less data and with less compute surpass those distilled on the full dataset.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves computational efficiency and KD-alignment, enabling effective data pruning for KD. It demonstrates broad applicability across standard benchmarks, reducing data and compute requirements without sacrificing distillation performance.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Computer-Use Agents: A Benchmark across Vision-Language Models and GUI Grounding Datasets
summary: Computer-use agents turn vision-language model (VLM) predictions into executable GUI clicks, so reliable uncertainty estimates are essential for rejection, calibration, miss-severity ranking, and spatial safety regions. Yet evidence on post-hoc uncertainty quantification (UQ) for these agents is fragmented across isolated model and dataset pairs, leaving it unclear whether UQ rankings stay stable when the agent, benchmark, or observable interface changes. We present Argus, a cross-regime benchmark for post-hoc UQ in single-step executable GUI grounding: a 27-method open-weight matrix over 4 VLM agents and 4 datasets, plus an 8-method closed-source matrix across 3 frontier vendors where logits, hidden states, and attention maps are unavailable. Evaluated methods span logit-based scores, sampling and consistency measures, hidden-state and density estimators (Mahalanobis, SAPLMA), attention-based scores, P(True) and verbalised-confidence prompting, and split-conformal prediction. The main finding is selective transfer: UQ rankings are stable across datasets for a fixed model, but degrade across model classes and observable interfaces. Hidden-state and density methods are the most stable open-weight family, while CoCoA-1MCA, Focus, sampling-based scores, and verbalised self-assessment win in specific regimes. Within-model ranking transfer is strong (Spearman rho up to 0.969), but cross-tier transfer to closed-source vendors averages only +0.08, so closed-source UQ should be reranked on the target rather than extrapolated. Conformal click regions show score-level discrimination is not enough for deployment: locally weighted disks shrink radii by 40-60% when the plug-in UQ is calibrated, but coverage degrades under calibration-test or interface mismatch. We release per-item records, calibration/test splits, UQ scores, and analysis scripts for regime-aware UQ selection in GUI agents.

AI.tldr: Argus provides a cross-regime benchmark for post-hoc uncertainty quantification (UQ) in single-step executable GUI grounding, revealing regime-dependent transfer: UQ rankings are stable across datasets for a fixed model but degrade across model classes and interfaces. Hidden-state and density-based methods are most stable in open-weight settings; certain methods excel only in specific regimes; cross-tier transfer to closed-source vendors is weak, suggesting target-specific re-ranking. Calibrated conformal regions improve discriminative power but can shrink coverage, especially under interface mismatch. Per-item data and scripts are released for regime-aware UQ selection.
AI.motivation: To address fragmented evidence on post-hoc UQ for vision-language grounded GUI agents, by creating a unified cross-regime benchmark (Argus) that tests multiple UQ methods across diverse models, datasets, and observable interfaces, to understand transferability and calibration behavior.
AI.method: Construct Argus with a 27-method open-weight matrix spanning 4 VLM agents and 4 datasets, plus an 8-method closed-source matrix across 3 frontier vendors. Evaluate a broad suite of UQ signals: logit-based scores, sampling and consistency measures, hidden-state and density estimators (e.g., Mahalanobis, SAPLMA), attention-based scores, P(True) and verbalised-confidence prompting, and split-conformal prediction. Assess ranking stability with Spearman correlation, calibration effects, and interface mismatch effects. Release per-item records, splits, scores, and scripts for regime-aware UQ selection.
AI.result: Key findings: UQ rankings are stable across datasets for a fixed model but degrade when changing model classes or interfaces. Hidden-state and density estimators are the most stable open-weight methods; CoCoA-1MCA, Focus, sampling-based scores, and verbalised self-assessment show strengths in specific regimes. Within-model ranking transfer is strong (Spearman ρ up to 0.969), but cross-tier transfer to closed vendors is modest (~+0.08), implying re-ranking on target. Conformal click regions provide discrimination beyond score-level, but calibration can reduce coverage, especially under calibration-test or interface mismatch. The authors release data and scripts to enable regime-aware UQ selection for GUI agents.
AI.conclusion: Regime-aware UQ selection is essential for GUI-grounded agents. Transferability of UQ signals is high within a fixed model/dataset but not across models or interfaces; practitioners should revalidate UQ on target interfaces (especially closed-source) and consider calibration-aware approaches that balance region size and coverage.

title: Low Variance Trust Region Optimization with Independent Actors and Sequential Updates in Cooperative Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning assumes each agent shares the same reward function and can be trained effectively using the Trust Region framework of single-agent. Instead of relying on other agents' actions, the independent actors setting considers each agent to act based only on its local information, thus having more flexible applications. However, in the sequential update framework, it is required to re-estimate the joint advantage function after each individual agent's policy step. Despite the practical success of importance sampling, the updated advantage function suffers from exponentially high variance problems, which likely result in unstable convergence. In this work, we first analyze the high variance advantage both empirically and theoretically. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a clipping objective to control the upper bounds of the advantage fluctuation in sequential updates. With the proposed objective, we provide a monotonic bound with sub-linear convergence to $ε$-Nash Equilibria. We further derive two new practical algorithms using our clipping objective. The experiment results on three popular multi-agent reinforcement learning benchmarks show that our proposed method outperforms the tested baselines in most environments. By carefully analyzing different training settings, our proposed method is highlighted with both stable convergence properties and the desired low advantage variance estimation. For reproducibility purposes, our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/giangbang/Low-Variance-Trust-Region-MARL.

AI.tldr: Proposes a clipping objective for sequential updates in independent-actor MARL to bound advantage variance, yielding a monotonic convergence bound to ε-Nash equilibria and two practical algorithms; experiments show improved stability and performance; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the high variance of importance-weighted advantage estimates in sequential updates of independent actors in MARL, which impedes stable convergence.
AI.method: Introduce a clipping objective that bounds fluctuations of the advantage; derive a monotonic convergence bound with sub-linear rate to ε-Nash equilibria; develop two practical algorithms leveraging the clipping objective.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three MARL benchmarks shows the proposed method outperforms baselines in most environments, with stable convergence and low-variance advantage estimates.
AI.conclusion: Clipping the advantage fluctuations mitigates variance in sequential MARL updates, enabling stable learning and convergence toward ε-Nash equilibria; provides practical algorithms and reproducible code.

title: OncoSynth: Synthetic data generation for treatment effect estimation in oncology
summary: In oncology, access to patient-level data is often restricted. Synthetic data provides an alternative for analyzing treatment effectiveness, but existing methods for synthetic data generation fail to preserve the causal relationships between covariates, treatments, and outcomes, thereby leading to biased estimates of treatment effects. Here, we introduce OncoSynth, a generative, causally-aware machine learning framework designed to produce synthetic cohorts that enable accurate estimation of population- and patient-level treatment effects. OncoSynth uses a diffusion-based sequential approach to model how covariates influence treatment assignment and how treatment affects survival. We evaluate OncoSynth using large lung (N = 37,128) and breast cancer (N = 17,046) cohorts. Our results show that OncoSynth generates high-fidelity synthetic patient cohorts that preserve real-world patient, treatment, and outcome distributions. Notably, OncoSynth improves treatment effect estimation over existing approaches, by reducing population-level treatment effect error by up to 66%, and patient-level treatment effect error by up to 58%. Thereby, OncoSynth supports reliable evidence generation for precision oncology in settings where data sharing is restricted.

AI.tldr: OncoSynth is a diffusion-based, causally-aware generative framework that creates synthetic cancer cohorts preserving covariate-treatment-outcome relationships to enable accurate population- and patient-level treatment-effect estimation when real patient data are restricted.
AI.motivation: Access to patient-level oncology data is often restricted; existing synthetic data methods fail to preserve causal structure, leading to biased treatment-effect estimates.
AI.method: A diffusion-based sequential model that captures how covariates influence treatment assignment and how treatment affects survival; generates synthetic cohorts for lung and breast cancer using large real-world datasets.
AI.result: Evaluated on two large cohorts (lung n=37,128; breast n=17,046); synthetic data preserves real distributions; improves treatment-effect estimation by up to 66% at population level and 58% at patient level relative to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: OncoSynth enables reliable evidence generation for precision oncology under data-sharing constraints by producing high-fidelity, causally-consistent synthetic cohorts.

title: Beyond One-Size-Fits-All: Diagnosis-Driven Online Reinforcement Learning with Offline Priors
summary: Online reinforcement learning (RL) agents increasingly depend on knowledge acquired offline to achieve practical efficiency. Originally studied in offline-to-online RL, this paradigm now spans foundation model post-training and embodied intelligence, with prior types expanding from offline datasets and pre-trained policies to increasingly diverse knowledge sources such as multimodal foundation models and generative world models. Offline priors have become central to how deep RL is developed and deployed. However, this reliance introduces a challenge that the prevailing benchmark-driven paradigm cannot resolve: because prior validity varies across deployments and shifts during training, no single approach to managing it is universally optimal, and benchmark rankings offer limited guidance for real-world deployments. Rather than pursuing universal solutions, we argue that the field should shift to diagnosis-driven tension management, in which deployment-specific evidence guides how the learner relates to its priors throughout training, enabling both flexible and adaptive deployment. We support this position with a framework characterizing how priors reshape online optimization through three functional roles, controlled experiments demonstrating help-or-hurt reversals, cross-domain evidence from foundation model post-training to embodied intelligence, and engagement with five substantive counterarguments.

AI.tldr: Argues for diagnosis-driven tension management of offline priors in online RL rather than universal strategies; introduces a three-role framework for priors, with experiments showing help-or-hurt reversals and cross-domain evidence across foundation models and embodied intelligence.
AI.motivation: Offline priors are increasingly central to online RL, but their validity varies by deployment and training phase; benchmark rankings are inadequate for guiding real-world deployments.
AI.method: Propose a framework that characterizes three functional roles of priors in online optimization; conduct controlled experiments showing help-or-hurt reversals; provide cross-domain evidence from foundation model post-training to embodied intelligence; engage five substantive counterarguments.
AI.result: Shows that priors can both help and hurt depending on context, supporting a diagnosis-driven approach and offering deployment-specific guidance on how to relate to priors across domains.
AI.conclusion: Advocate shifting the RL community toward diagnosis-driven tension management, where deployment-specific evidence guides how learners leverage priors during training, enabling flexible and adaptive deployment instead of universal solutions.

title: MiniOpt: Reasoning to Model and Solve General Optimization Problems with Limited Resources
summary: Achieving strong optimization generalization across diverse optimization problems while requiring limited training resources remains a challenging problem for optimization-oriented large language models (LLMs). Existing approaches typically rely on large-scale supervised datasets, costly reasoning annotations, and expensive intermediate step verification, resulting in substantial training overhead. To address these challenges, we propose MiniOpt, a reinforcement learning framework that learns to solve optimization problems through an "reasoning-to-model-and-solve" paradigm. MiniOpt decomposes optimization reasoning into structured optimization modeling and executable solver generation. Building upon this paradigm, we introduce OptReward, a reward function with hierarchical score structure that jointly evaluates formulation and solution, enabling effective policy learning without expert demonstrations. We further develop an optimization-oriented policy optimization strategy that improves exploration efficiency and stabilizes reinforcement learning for compact models. Extensive experiments show that MiniOpt-3B exhibits strong optimization generalization across various optimization types, problem scenarios, and task domains. For models with fewer than 10B parameters, MiniOpt series achieves the highest average solving accuracy (SA). For models with more than 10B parameters, MiniOpt still shows competitive performance. These results suggest that optimization-oriented reward design and reinforcement learning provide an effective pathway for developing compact optimization-specialized language models with strong optimization generalization capabilities. The code is available at https://github.com/Hsiang-1/MiniOpt.

AI.tldr: MiniOpt is a reinforcement learning framework for optimization-oriented LLMs that uses a reasoning-to-model-and-solve paradigm, with OptReward reward shaping and a specialized policy optimization strategy to achieve strong optimization generalization using compact models; results show leading performance for sub-10B models and competitive results otherwise.
AI.motivation: Reduce training resources and reliance on large-scale supervised datasets and annotations by enabling compact optimization-specialized LLMs to generalize across diverse optimization problems.
AI.method: Introduce MiniOpt that splits reasoning into optimization modeling and solver generation; define OptReward with hierarchical scores for formulation and solution; develop optimization-oriented policy optimization to improve exploration and stabilization for compact models.
AI.result: MiniOpt-3B achieves strong optimization generalization across problem types and domains; sub-10B models achieve highest average solving accuracy; >10B models remain competitive; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Optimization-oriented reward design coupled with reinforcement learning enables development of compact optimization-specialized LLMs with strong generalization capabilities.

title: Leaking Circuit Secrets: Gradient Leakage Attacks on Graph Neural Networks
summary: As graph neural networks (GNNs) become standard tools for critical tasks in circuit design and analysis, their security and privacy risks require careful attention. Here, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of gradient leakage attacks (GLAs) on GNNs in circuit-design and hardware-security tasks, a practical threat that has been largely overlooked. We assess state-of-the-art (SOTA) GNNs, including GraphSAGE, GCN, GIN, and GAT, trained on standard netlist benchmarks (ISCAS'85, EPFL, and TrustHub), for their fundamental vulnerability to GLAs. We find that GLAs can expose sensitive information, such as gate types and distinctive properties of hardware Trojans, which may assist adversaries in analyzing logic locking schemes or evading Trojan detection mechanisms. Our analysis shows that these risks are influenced by architectural features, with attention mechanisms (GAT) exacerbating leakage, while injective aggregation (GIN) provides comparatively stronger resilience. We further evaluate several SOTA defense techniques, including differential privacy, gradient clipping, secure aggregation, model compression with quantization, and adversarial training. We find that these techniques improve resilience only in specific settings and can also compromise model performance. Overall, our work provides key insights toward privacy-preserving GNNs and highlights the need for more robust and efficient defenses. We release our full methodology and artifacts.

AI.tldr: First systematic evaluation of gradient leakage attacks on GNNs in circuit-design tasks; reveals gate types and Trojan properties; GAT leakage higher, GIN more robust; defenses offer limited resilience with trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Address the overlooked privacy risks of GNNs in hardware-security tasks by assessing gradient leakage attacks (GLAs) on GNNs trained for circuit design benchmarks.
AI.method: Empirically evaluate SOTA GNNs (GraphSAGE, GCN, GIN, GAT) on standard netlist benchmarks (ISCAS'85, EPFL, TrustHub) under gradient leakage attacks; test defenses (differential privacy, gradient clipping, secure aggregation, quantization, adversarial training).
AI.result: GLAs can reveal sensitive information such as gate types and Trojan properties; architectural features influence leakage, with attention (GAT) exacerbating leakage and injective aggregation (GIN) providing stronger resilience; defenses improve resilience only in certain settings and may reduce model performance.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need for robust privacy-preserving GNNs in circuit-design contexts and provides guidance on when existing defenses help; releases full methodology and artifacts.

title: Semantic Consistency Policy Optimization for Reinforcement Learning of LLM Agents
comment: 16 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables. Under review at EMNLP 2026
summary: Group-based reinforcement learning effectively post-trains LLM agents for long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks by deriving step-level credit from trajectory outcomes. However, this ties a step's credit to its rollout's final outcome: semantically near-identical intermediate steps receive opposite credit depending on whether their trajectory eventually succeeded or failed. Such semantic credit inconsistency sends conflicting gradients to similar actions and wastes the partially-correct progress inside failed rollouts. Motivated by this, we propose Semantic Consistency Policy Optimization (SCPO), a value-free reward-shaping method that mitigates this inconsistency by recovering step-level credit from successful siblings in the same rollout group. Concretely, SCPO scores each failed step against a successful sibling and adds positive step-level credit for new progress along that sibling. On ALFWorld and WebShop, SCPO matches or exceeds strong group-based baselines, reaching 93.7+/-4.1 percent success on ALFWorld and 74.8+/-2.0 percent on WebShop at 1.5B parameters, with gains concentrated on the hardest multi-step tasks.

AI.tldr: SCPO introduces a value-free reward shaping method to fix semantic credit inconsistency in group-based reinforcement learning for long-horizon tasks. By comparing failed steps to successful siblings within the same rollout group, it re-allocates step credit to capture partial progress, improving performance on ALFWorld and WebShop at 1.5B params.
AI.motivation: Existing group-based RL links a step's credit to final rollout outcome, causing semantically similar intermediate steps to receive opposite credit depending on success or failure. This misalignment leads to conflicting gradients and wasted progress in failed rollouts.
AI.method: SCPO computes step-level credit by contrasting failed steps with successful siblings within the same rollout group. It uses positive credit for progress aligned with a successful sibling, without relying on a value function (value-free).
AI.result: SCPO matches or surpasses strong baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop, achieving 93.7±4.1% success on ALFWorld and 74.8±2.0% on WebShop at 1.5B parameters, with most gains on hardest multi-step tasks.
AI.conclusion: Semantic credit inconsistency in group-based RL can be mitigated through consistency-aware, value-free reward shaping. SCPO effectively recovers step-level credit from successful siblings, improving long-horizon task performance.

title: Learning Subset-Shared Invariances for Domain Generalization with Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Domain generalization (DG) aims to learn a model from one or more source domains that generalizes to an unseen target domain without accessing target data during training. A common approach enforces invariance of representations across all source domains, assuming predictive structure is globally shared. However, we demonstrate that enforcing invariance across more domains gradually restricts the feasible representation space, discarding transferable predictive factors that are not universally shared. To address this limitation, we propose subset-shared invariance, where predictive structure is assumed stable only within domain subsets. We implement this principle with a mixture-of-experts architecture, where each expert aligns the specific domains it serves and a routing mechanism composes subset-invariant components for prediction. This creates a routing-conditioned invariance, jointly learned with the representation. To facilitate effective decomposition, we develop training objectives that encourage selective alignment, confident and balanced routing, and diverse expert specialization. Experiments on DomainBed benchmarks demonstrate improved out-of-domain generalization and greater robustness under increasing domain heterogeneity. Our results suggest that DG should move beyond enforcing a single global invariance and instead model invariance through partially shared structure across domain subsets.

AI.tldr: Proposes subset-shared invariance for domain generalization using a mixture-of-experts with routing-conditioned invariance; shows improved generalization on DomainBed.
AI.motivation: Global invariance across all source domains can overly constrain representations and discard transferable factors that are not universally shared; partial invariance within domain subsets may better capture transferable structure.
AI.method: Mixture-of-experts where each expert aligns to specific domains it serves; a routing mechanism composes subset-invariant components for prediction. Training objectives promote selective alignment, confident and balanced routing, and diverse expert specialization; routing-conditioned invariance is learned jointly with representations.
AI.result: Experiments on DomainBed benchmarks demonstrate improved out-of-domain generalization and robustness under increasing domain heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: Domain Generalization should move beyond a single global invariance and model invariance through partially shared structure across domain subsets via a routing-enabled mixture of experts.

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Neural Network Compression (HiReLC): Pruning and Quantization
summary: We present HiReLC, a hierarchical ensemble-reinforcement learning framework for automated joint quantization and structured pruning of deep neural networks. The framework decomposes the compression search across two levels of abstraction: low-level agents (LLAs) operate independently per block, selecting per-kernel configurations over a multi-discrete action space spanning bitwidth, pruning keep-ratio, quantization type, and granularity, while high-level agents (HLAs) coordinate global budget allocation via ensemble voting guided by Fisher Information-based sensitivity estimates. To mitigate the computational cost of policy evaluation, an iterative active learning loop interleaves surrogate-guided RL optimization with post-compression fine-tuning, using a lightweight MLP surrogate to amortize expensive evaluations and a logit-MSE proxy during cold-start. The surrogate is used for reward shaping rather than as a replacement for final post-compression evaluation. The controller is architecture-agnostic by design, with a modular layer abstraction decoupling the RL environment from the underlying network topology. Experiments across Vision Transformer and CNN benchmarks demonstrate effective parameter-storage compression ratios of 5.99 - 6.72$\times$ with a 3.83 % gain in one setting and 0.55 - 5.62 % accuracy drops elsewhere, supporting hierarchical policy decomposition and sensitivity-aware guidance as practical design choices for joint neural network compression.

AI.tldr: HiReLC is a hierarchical ensemble RL framework for joint per-kernel quantization and structured pruning, using low-level per-block agents and high-level budget-coordinating agents, with a surrogate-guided iterative loop; architecture-agnostic; achieves ~6x compression with modest accuracy loss on ViT and CNNs.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost of joint compression search while maintaining performance; enable architecture-agnostic deployment with sensitivity-aware budgeting.
AI.method: Two-tier agents (LLAs per block, HLAs coordinating budgets via ensemble voting with Fisher Information estimates); multi-discrete actions (bitwidth, prune ratio, quantization type, granularity); iterative active learning with a lightweight MLP surrogate for reward shaping, logit-MSE cold-start; post-compression fine-tuning; modular layer abstraction.
AI.result: Reported parameter-storage compression 5.99–6.72x; 3.83% gain in one setting; accuracy drop 0.55–5.62% across other settings; demonstrated on Vision Transformer and CNN benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical policy decomposition and sensitivity-guided budgeting are effective for joint compression; surrogate-assisted RL reduces evaluation cost while maintaining final performance; architecture-agnostic controller broadens applicability.

title: Cellular Predictions on the Move: What about Data?
summary: Mobile cellular load forecasting is native to network resource optimization and delivery of services with reliability, latency and quality guarantees. The mainstream of machine learning research in the area is focused primarily on developing powerful learning structures for improved prediction accuracy. The data used for forecasting traditionally belong to the cellular domain and at most contain exogenous information about the surroundings of the base stations. We approach the prediction task from the perspective of data as a vital component of any data learning process. We hypothesize that substantial improvements could be achieved when the data inform on the processes that create the cellular load. Specifically, we propose to characterize the population dynamics -- the potential number of cellular traffic sources and their mobility -- in addition to employing historical time series of mobile data traffic. We validate our hypothesis for the rarely examined highway scenario. Comprehensive experiments show forecasting improvements on the order of $60\%$ due to the use of these data alone.

AI.tldr: Incorporating population dynamics and mobility data with historical traffic dramatically boosts highway cellular load forecasting (~60% improvement) beyond exogenous information alone.
AI.motivation: Existing ML work relies on powerful predictors but treats data as secondary. The authors argue that data reflecting the generating processes (population dynamics and traffic source mobility) are essential to improve predictions.
AI.method: Characterize population dynamics (potential number of traffic sources and their mobility) in addition to using historical time series of mobile data traffic; validate on a highway scenario with comprehensive experiments.
AI.result: Forecasting performance improves by about 60% using these data sources alone.
AI.conclusion: Integrating data that encode the generating processes of cellular load substantially enhances forecast accuracy; demonstrated in a highway context and suggests broader applicability.

title: Black-Box Assisted Regression: Phase Transitions and Minimax Optimality
comment: 23 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Foundation models are often used as fixed black-box predictors for downstream tasks with limited labeled data, but their predictions may be biased and unsafe to trust blindly. We study this setting through black-box assisted nonparametric regression: a learner observes labeled samples and can query a fixed predictor $f_0$, while the target $f^*$ is close to $f_0$ in $L_2(P_X)$ up to an unknown radius $δ$. We give a finite-sample minimax characterization showing a phase transition at $δ_c(n) \asymp n^{-β/(2β+d)}$, with leading risk $\min\{δ^2, n^{-2β/(2β+d)}\}$. We then analyze a Safe Residual Estimator: it learns a correction around $f_0$, initializes the residual head at zero so the initial predictor equals $f_0$, and uses holdout selection to revert to $f_0$ when the learned correction is not supported by validation data. Here, "safe" means avoiding negative transfer, i.e., performing worse than the black-box predictor alone. The estimator matches the leading minimax term up to an additive validation-selection cost. Synthetic regression experiments verify the predicted phase transition, while CIFAR-100 with CLIP and AG News with Qwen3-8B provide practice-facing evidence that the same residual-correction tradeoff is useful beyond the formal squared-loss regression setting.

AI.tldr: Black-box-assisted nonparametric regression: characterization of when a fixed predictor f0 can be safely improved upon by learning a residual, revealing a phase transition in minimax risk, and proposing a Safe Residual Estimator that reverts to f0 if the learned correction is unsupported. Empirical results support the theory and show practical utility beyond squared loss.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are often used as fixed black-box predictors with limited labeled data; they may be biased or unsafe to rely on blindly. The work aims to quantify when and how one can safely improve upon a black-box predictor and to design algorithms that minimize risk while avoiding negative transfer.
AI.method: Finite-sample minimax analysis for black-box assisted nonparametric regression: assume the target f* is within L2 distance δ of the fixed predictor f0; derive a phase transition at δ_c(n) ~ n^{-β/(2β+d)} with leading risk min{δ^2, n^{-2β/(2β+d)}}. Propose the Safe Residual Estimator: learn a correction around f0, initialize residual head at zero so predictor equals f0, and use holdout validation to revert to f0 when the learned correction is unsupported. Prove the estimator matches the leading minimax term up to additive validation cost; validate via synthetic experiments and real-data settings (CIFAR-100 with CLIP, AG News with Qwen3-8B).
AI.result: Identification of a phase transition in the usefulness of learning a residual on top of a fixed black-box predictor, with risk scaling min{δ^2, n^{-2β/(2β+d)}}. The Safe Residual Estimator achieves near-minimax performance by balancing correction learning and safety via validation-based fallback to f0, with empirical support of the phase transition and residual-correction tradeoffs extending beyond squared loss.
AI.conclusion: Safe, residual-based corrections can meaningfully augment black-box predictors in nonparametric regression, achieving near-minimax risk for a broad regime and avoiding negative transfer; the residual-correction tradeoff persists across synthetic and real-world tasks, suggesting practical applicability beyond the formal setting.

title: Natural Ungrokking: Asymmetric Control of Which Rules Survive Pretraining
comment: Foundations of Deep Generative Models (FoGen) Workshop at ICML 2026. 23 pages (5-page main text plus appendices), 5 figures. Code: https://github.com/lijuliana/Natural-Ungrokking
summary:
Midway through an ordinary pretraining run, a small language model learns the pronoun-gender rule: cued with a girl's name ("Sue cried because"), it resolves the next pronoun to she, generalizing to held-out probes (0.94 by step 925). By step 3,500 the same model scores near zero on the same probes, although the rule's evidence is still in the training data. We call this within-run reversal natural ungrokking: the corpus decides, with no trace in the loss curve, which learned rules a model keeps.
  Which rules survive is predictable from one corpus statistic: how often the training stream shows the rule winning. Across un-intervened runs (two corpora, three budgets, three seeds), support frequency decides a rule's fate; the data-to-parameter ratio only modulates how deeply a doomed rule falls. The same emerge-then-collapse dynamics appear in public Pythia checkpoints, collapse depth ordered by model scale as predicted. The forgetting is a displacement: a competing surface pattern out-competes the rule, and the log-probability margin between them crosses zero within 100 training steps of the behavioral collapse.
  Control over this fate is asymmetric: the same edit that destroys a rule on demand cannot restore it. Flipping support to counter-evidence in place kills the rule with monotone dose-response in two unrelated rules; but injecting support back, even to 450 times the level that naturally sustains it, buys no recovery. Every confirmatory threshold and prediction was pre-registered before the data it governed was read.

AI.tldr: Within a single pretraining run, a small language model first learns a gender-pronoun rule and then forgets it, a phenomenon dubbed "ungrokking". Rule survival is governed by corpus-level support frequency rather than data quantity alone; more data can deepen a doomed rule's collapse. The dynamics replicate in public checkpoints and scale with model size; rule elimination is asymmetric and not reversible by simply increasing supportive evidence. All claims were pre-registered before data access.
AI.motivation: Understand how learnable linguistic rules emerge, persist, or are lost during standard LM pretraining, and what corpus statistics and model scaling reveal about plasticity vs. stability in learned heuristics.
AI.method: Track a small LM during ordinary pretraining; probe pronoun resolution (e.g., Sue cried because ___ → she) and its generalization to held-out probes; vary corpora, training budgets, and seeds; examine public Pythia checkpoints; quantify log-probability margins between competing patterns; perform deliberate edits to data to flip evidence and assess retrievability; pre-register thresholds and predictions.
AI.result: Rule survival correlates with how often the rule is favored by the corpus; the data-to-parameter ratio modulates the depth of the doomed rule's collapse. Recurrent dynamics observed across corpora and seeds; model-scale influences collapse depth in public checkpoints. Forgetting is a displacement by a rival pattern; the log-probability margin crosses zero within ~100 steps of behavioral collapse. Rule suppression is asymmetric: destroying a rule is easy, restoring it even with massively increased supportive evidence is not. All confirmatory thresholds and predictions were pre-registered.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals predictable, corpus-statistics–driven dynamics for the retention or loss of learned rules during pretraining. It demonstrates a plasticity-stability tradeoff where rules can emerge and then be irrevocably suppressed by competing patterns, with suppression being easier than recovery. The results generalize across corpora, budgets, seeds, and model scales and have implications for interpretability, data curation, and the design of robust training protocols.

title: Bridging Spherical Black-Box Optimizers
summary: When gradient information is unavailable, black-box optimization (BBO) methods provide a practical alternative. While Evolution Strategies (ES), Consensus-Based Optimization (CBO), Optimization via Integration (OVI), and related methods have each been studied independently, their connections remain underexplored. We unify these approaches within a common theoretical framework, revealing that they differ primarily in two design choices: fitness aggregation (controlling sharpness preference) and consensus scope (controlling modality). Leveraging these insights, we introduce hybrid optimizers that interpolate between existing methods. Our ES-OVI hybrid allows explicit control over the preference for flat minima, enabling a trade-off between performance and robustness in continuous control tasks. Our CBO-OVI hybrids combine the higher-dimensional efficiency of parametric methods with the multimodal capabilities of particle-based approaches, achieving competitive results on language model merging under limited evaluation budgets. We validate our methods on standard BBO benchmarks and higher-dimensional locomotion tasks, demonstrating that the hybrid methods can outperform their constituent algorithms.

AI.tldr: The work unifies ES, CBO, and OVI under a single theoretical framework, identifies two key design choices (fitness aggregation and consensus scope), and develops hybrid optimizers (ES-OVI and CBO-OVI). These hybrids enable controlled flat-minima preference and enhanced multimodal/search efficiency, respectively, and show competitive or superior performance on standard BBO benchmarks and high-dimensional tasks.
AI.motivation: To reconcile independently studied black-box optimization methods, reveal their structural commonalities, and provide a principled way to combine them to balance performance, robustness, and multimodality.
AI.method: A unified theoretical framework parameterizes methods by two axes: fitness aggregation (controlling sharpness of minima) and consensus scope (controlling modality). Hybrid optimizers interpolate between existing methods along these axes. The ES-OVI hybrid adds explicit control over flat-minima preference; CBO-OVI hybrids merge parametric efficiency with particle-based multimodality for tasks like language-model merging under limited evaluation budgets.
AI.result: Empirical validation on standard BBO benchmarks and high-dimensional locomotion tasks shows that the hybrids can outperform their constituent algorithms; the ES-OVI variant offers a tunable trade-off between performance and robustness in continuous control; CBO-OVI hybrids achieve competitive results given evaluation constraints.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework clarifies how these BBO methods differ and how to combine them to achieve better performance across budgets and dimensionalities. The proposed hybrids extend the practical toolbox for black-box optimization, with demonstrated benefits in both low- and high-dimensional settings.

title: Model Forensics: Investigating Whether Concerning Behavior Reflects Misalignment
summary: A central goal of safety research is determining whether a model is misaligned. Prior work has largely focused on detecting concerning behavior. But behavior alone does not establish misalignment: a concerning action can arise from benign causes such as confusion. This motivates model forensics: investigating whether the action was driven by malign intent. In this paper, we propose a baseline protocol for model forensics consisting of two steps, iterated as needed. First, we read the chain of thought (CoT) to generate hypotheses about what drives model behavior. Second, we make edits to the prompt or environment to test these hypotheses. While the CoT is not always faithful, it is a rich source of unsupervised insight that can guide the collection of more rigorous evidence. To evaluate our protocol, we create a suite of six agentic environments where models exhibit concerning behavior, and apply it to each. We establish that Kimi K2 Thinking takes shortcuts due to a genuine disposition towards low-effort actions, by showing this hypothesis successfully predicts its behavior. Through counterfactual experiments, we show DeepSeek R1 deceives out of a desire to be consistent with a previous instance of itself. Our methods nonetheless leave significant room for refinement. For example, when we test whether Kimi K2 Thinking believes it is violating user intent, we find no evidence of such a belief, but without positive controls we cannot confirm our tests would detect it. Overall, we find our simple protocol provides a strong baseline that we hope future work will improve upon. More broadly, our work is a concrete step in developing the growing field of model forensics.

AI.tldr: Proposes a baseline two-step protocol for model forensics that uses chain-of-thought to generate hypotheses about model behavior and then tests them by prompt/environment edits; validated on six agentic environments, yielding insights but with noted limitations; aims to advance the field of model forensics as a baseline.
AI.motivation: To move beyond detecting concerning behavior toward diagnosing underlying malign intent (misalignment) in AI systems, by developing a forensic protocol that separates symptom from intent.
AI.method: Two-step protocol: (1) read the chain of thought (CoT) to generate hypotheses about drivers of model behavior; (2) edit the prompt or environment to test these hypotheses. Applied to six agentic environments with models exhibiting concerning behavior; use counterfactual tests to probe hypotheses (e.g., consistency, intent).
AI.result: The protocol yields interpretable hypotheses explaining behavior (e.g., Kimi K2 Thinking acts via low-effort disposition; DeepSeek R1 deceives to stay consistent). CoT may be unfaithful but can guide evidence collection; experiments reveal current protocol has gaps such as lack of positive controls, and substantial room for refinement.
AI.conclusion: The proposed baseline offers a concrete starting point for model forensics, with potential for refinement and broader applicability as the field matures.

title: Deep Neural Networks with Ordinal Loss for Medical Applications
summary: In many prediction problems in medical applications, target labels exhibit an inherent ordinal structure, where class ordering reflects clinically meaningful severity levels. The cost associated with misclassification is often non-uniform and asymmetric, as errors between distant ordinal categories may have substantially more severe consequences than errors between adjacent ones, and overestimating disease severity may have different clinical implications than underestimating it. Traditional loss functions such as multi-class cross-entropy treat all misclassifications equally and fail to incorporate this ordering information. Recent advances in ordinal regression aim to address this limitation by integrating rank-based structures into deep learning models. In this work, we introduce the \textbf{Ordinal Cross-Entropy (OCE)} framework, a general and architecture-independent approach for learning from ordinal data. The proposed method extends the standard cross-entropy formulation to account for misclassification severity through an ordinal cost matrix while preserving the probabilistic interpretation and optimization benefits of the conventional loss. We provide a theoretical analysis of the OCE gradient behavior and show that it yields smoother optimization dynamics and improved ordinal consistency. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method achieves lower prediction error costs and better calibration compared to existing state-of-the-art ordinal approaches, establishing OCE as a simple yet effective solution for ordinal regression in deep neural networks.

AI.tldr: Introduce Ordinal Cross-Entropy (OCE), an architecture-agnostic loss that extends cross-entropy with an ordinal cost matrix to capture misclassification severity in ordinal targets, yielding smoother gradients, better ordinal consistency, and improved calibration and lower error costs in deep neural networks.
AI.motivation: Standard cross-entropy treats all misclassifications equally, ignoring ordinal structure and asymmetric costs of misclassification in medical ordinal labels; there is a need for a principled loss that encodes ordinal information.
AI.method: Extend cross-entropy with an ordinal cost matrix reflecting misclassification severity; retain probabilistic interpretation; perform gradient analysis showing smoother optimization; architecture-independent so applicable to various DNNs.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show lower prediction error costs and better calibration compared to existing state-of-the-art ordinal approaches.
AI.conclusion: OCE provides a simple yet effective framework for ordinal regression in deep networks, balancing probabilistic training with ordinal-aware optimization to improve prediction quality and calibration.

title: Neglected Free Lunch from Post-training: Progress Advantage for LLM Agents
summary: Process reward models enable fine-grained, step-level evaluation of LLMs, yet building them for agentic settings remains prohibitively difficult: long-horizon interactions, irreversible actions, and stochastic environment feedback make both human annotation and Monte Carlo estimation infeasible at scale. In this work, we show that reinforcement learning (RL) post-training already provides the ingredients for effective step-level scoring, eliminating the need for dedicated reward model training altogether. Concretely, we derive an implicit advantage under a general stochastic Markov decision process, which we term progress advantage -- log-probability ratio between the RL-trained policy and its reference policy exactly recovers the optimal advantage function. This formulation makes the resulting signal annotation-free, domain-agnostic, and available as a byproduct of the standard RL post-training pipeline. We validate the effectiveness of the progress advantage across three different applications: test-time scaling, uncertainty quantification, and failure attribution on five benchmarks and four model families. Across all settings, it consistently outperforms confidence-based baselines and, despite requiring no task-specific training, surpasses dedicated trained reward models. We complement these results with deeper analyses on characteristics of progress advantage, offering practical guidance for adoption in real-world agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Annotation-free step-level scoring for agentic LLMs via progress advantage derived from RL post-training; outperforms baselines and many reward models without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Building robust reward models for long-horizon, stochastic agentic interactions is hard due to annotation bottlenecks and infeasible Monte Carlo estimates; a scalable, domain-agnostic scoring signal is needed.
AI.method: Define progress advantage as the log-probability ratio between an RL-trained policy and a reference policy under a stochastic MDP. This ratio recovers the optimal advantage function, enabling an annotation-free signal as a byproduct of standard RL post-training. No task-specific reward model training required.
AI.result: Empirical validation across test-time scaling, uncertainty quantification, and failure attribution on five benchmarks and four model families. Progress advantage consistently beats confidence-based baselines and, without task-specific training, surpasses many dedicated reward models.
AI.conclusion: Progress advantage provides a practical, scalable, and annotation-free signal for evaluating agentic LLMs, leveraging existing RL post-training pipelines; offers guidance for real-world adoption.

title: Re-mixing Embeddings for Patient Augmentation in Data Scarce Multiple Instance Learning
comment: Accepted for publication at the 29th International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention - MICCAI 2026
summary: Data scarcity is a major bottleneck in medical Multiple Instance Learning (MIL), especially for rare diseases or expensive modalities. We introduce a statistically grounded patient augmentation approach that generates realistic patients directly in embedding space. Using Gaussian Mixture Models as a probabilistic clustering approach on pooled instance embeddings from all patients, our method learns disease-specific "recipes"-statistical distributions of instances across unsupervised clusters. New patients are then generated by sampling embeddings from clusters based on learned recipes. Unlike existing methods that require examples from all categories, our method can generate patients offline by re-mixing pooled embeddings. Generated patients are further selected based on uncertainty quantification to improve MIL performance. We evaluate our method across three clinically relevant scarcity scenarios: (i) cross-dataset transfer, where an entirely missing "healthy" class is generated using statistics from an external cohort; (ii) low-data regimes, where class sizes are extremely limited; and (iii) small-cohort non-image tasks, including single-cell RNA-seq and flow cytometry. Across all experiments, our method improves performance over baseline, often outperforming other bag-mixing strategies. Notably, in the missing-class scenario, a performance comparable to full-dataset training is achieved, demonstrating its potential for rare disease diagnostic and privacy-preserving patient augmentation. The code is available at https://github.com/marrlab/RECIPE

AI.tldr: A statistically grounded MIL augmentation method (RECIPE) generates synthetic patients in embedding space using Gaussian Mixture Models to learn disease-specific embedding distributions from pooled instances. New patients are sampled from these distributions (recipes) and optionally filtered by uncertainty; aims to alleviate data scarcity, including missing classes, across cross-dataset, low-data, and non-image single-cell modalities, with reported performance gains and potential for privacy-preserving augmentation.
AI.motivation: Medical MIL suffers from data scarcity, especially for rare diseases and expensive modalities. Traditional augmentation is limited when some classes are missing or data is extremely limited. A method that can generate realistic patients offline by remixing pooled embeddings could boost MIL performance while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Pool instance embeddings across patients and fit Gaussian Mixture Models to form disease-specific clusters. Define recipes as distributions of instances across unsupervised clusters. Generate new patients by sampling embeddings from chosen cluster distributions according to the recipes. Generate offline by remixing pooled embeddings without requiring all categories. Optionally select synthetic patients using uncertainty quantification to improve MIL.
AI.result: Evaluated on three scarcity scenarios: (i) cross-dataset transfer with an unseen healthy class generated from external data; (ii) low-data regimes with very small class sizes; (iii) small-cohort non-image tasks (single-cell RNA-seq, flow cytometry). Across experiments, RECIPE improves baseline MIL and often surpasses other bag-mixing strategies; in the missing-class scenario, performance approaches that of full-dataset training.
AI.conclusion: RECIPE demonstrates effective, privacy-friendly augmentation for MIL under data scarcity, including missing classes, with cross-domain applicability and publicly available code (GitHub).

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation with Sampled Demonstrations Reduces Output Diversity
summary: On-policy self-distillation achieves strong pass@1 accuracy by using a single model as both teacher and student, with the teacher conditioned on a correct demonstration to provide dense token-level feedback. We show that this could come at a hidden cost: rollout diversity decreases and pass@k curves flatten (i.e., generating more rollouts fails to improve accuracy). We trace this to compounding biases in the design of self-distillation with sampled demonstrations. The teacher scores each student rollout while conditioned on a sampled correct rollout, channeling its feedback through the model's own biases. We theoretically analyze the optimal self-distillation policy and show that it tilts the base distribution by a pointwise conditional mutual information score between the student's rollout and the correct rollout used as context. Unlike the ideal optimal on-policy reinforcement learning (RL), which preserves probability ratios among equally correct rollouts, self-distillation can amplify existing probability gaps, concentrating mass on already-dominant modes. On a controlled graph path-finding task and science question-answering benchmarks, self-distilled models match or exceed RL on average performance but exhibit substantially lower functional and semantic diversity, failing on out-of-distribution settings that require diverse strategies.

AI.tldr: Self-distillation can boost pass@1 but biases feedback to its own dominant modes, reducing rollout diversity and flattening pass@k curves; the optimal self-distillation policy shifts the base distribution via a pointwise conditional mutual information between student and the context (correct rollout). Unlike ideal RL, it amplifies existing gaps, concentrating mass on dominant modes.
AI.motivation: Examine on-policy self-distillation where a single model acts as both teacher and student. Analyze how conditioning on a correct demonstration affects feedback, rollout diversity, and performance robustness, and compare to reinforcement learning (RL).
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the optimal self-distillation policy; empirical evaluation on a controlled graph path-finding task and science QA benchmarks; comparison against RL and assessment of diversity and OOD robustness.
AI.result: Self-distilled models can match or exceed RL on average performance but exhibit substantially lower functional and semantic diversity. They show flattening of pass@k curves (diminished benefit from more rollouts) and fail on out-of-distribution tasks requiring diverse strategies. The analysis attributes this to a compounding bias from conditioning feedback on a sampled correct rollout, which biases the teacher’s scores toward the model's own biases and concentrates probability mass on already-dominant modes.
AI.conclusion: Self-distillation offers strong average performance but at the expense of diversity and out-of-distribution robustness. To deploy safely, one should mitigate the conditioning bias (e.g., diversify demonstrations, mix with external teachers) or integrate diversity-preserving objectives.

title: Tensorion: A Tensor-Aware Generalization of the Muon Optimizer
summary: Common first-order optimizers, such as Adam, implicitly treat each parameter block as an unstructured vector, which disregards the multilinear weight structure present in many modern machine learning models. Recent work has shown that exploiting matrix structure can improve optimization dynamics. A notable example is Muon, which performs steepest descent under the spectral norm constraint. We take the next step and introduce Tensorion, a tensor-aware optimizer that extends Muon's constrained optimization perspective from matrices to higher-order tensors. Tensorion is built around a linear minimization oracle (LMO) over a tensor norm ball. The norm is carefully chosen to balance two objectives: tightly bounding the tensor spectral norm, while still keeping the LMO tractable. This LMO becomes computable because it reduces to operations on adaptively selected unfolding matrices. Notably, when restricted to order-2 tensors (i.e., matrices), Tensorion recovers Muon exactly. Experiments on tensor-based computer vision problems suggest that Tensorion can offer improved convergence behavior and more stable gradient updates compared with Adam-based and existing tensor-aware baselines in the evaluated settings.

AI.tldr: Tensorion extends Muon to higher-order tensors by introducing a tensor-aware optimizer built around a linear minimization oracle (LMO) on a tensor norm ball. It recovers Muon for matrices, and under tensor structure it offers improved convergence and gradient stability over Adam in tensor-based vision tasks.
AI.motivation: First-order optimizers typically treat parameter blocks as unstructured vectors, ignoring multilinear tensor structure present in modern models. Exploiting tensor structure can improve optimization dynamics beyond matrix-based methods like Muon.
AI.method: Tensorion formulates a constrained optimization step with an LMO over a tensor norm ball chosen to tightly bound the tensor spectral norm while keeping the LMO tractable. The LMO reduces to operations on adaptively selected unfolding matrices, enabling practical computation. The approach generalizes Muon; when restricted to order-2 tensors, it exactly recovers Muon.
AI.result: Experiments on tensor-based computer vision problems indicate Tensorion achieves improved convergence behavior and more stable gradient updates compared with Adam and existing tensor-aware baselines in the evaluated settings.
AI.conclusion: Tensorion successfully generalizes the constrained optimization perspective from matrices to higher-order tensors, preserving Muon’s favorable properties while leveraging tensor structure. It demonstrates potential for improved optimization dynamics in tensorized models and broader applicability beyond the tested CV tasks.

title: An Analysis of Posterior Collapse, Parameterization and Initialization in Variational Deep Gaussian Processes
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Machine Learning Research
summary: DGPs are probabilistic models with remarkable prediction performance that concatenate GPs across several layers. Exact inference in DGPs is intractable, and variational inference is often used to approximate the posterior with a parametric distribution tuned by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence. Moreover, finding a good VI approximation is challenging. In particular, a problem of VI is posterior collapse, where VI converges to a variational posterior that matches the prior. In variational DGPs, this implies explaining the data as noise. This work studies posterior collapse in DGPs and identifies its connection to the DSVI algorithm and the widely used linear prior mean function employed in all but the last layer. We show that the benefit of the linear prior mean does not arise from avoiding the non-injective pathology in very deep DGPs, as previously believed, but from improving the conditioning of the optimization problem at initialization. Thus, we propose an alternative initialization of a zero prior mean DGP that mimics a DGP with a linear prior mean at initialization. This enables successful training of DGPs without imposing optimization-driven constraints on the prior, allowing to choose the prior based on modeling assumptions rather than optimization convenience. Our analysis considers three common parameterizations of DGPs and shows that not all of them benefit from a linear prior mean. We also explain why a whitened parameterization of the \DGP provides more stable convergence, something often assumed from experience, but lacking a rigorous analysis. Furthermore, we show that this stability is also beneficial to avoid the posterior collapse problem. Extensive experiments validate our findings: the proposed initialization prevents posterior collapse, improves stability, and achieves performance comparable to (and sometimes better than) DGPs with a linear prior mean.

AI.tldr: Posterior collapse in variational deep Gaussian processes (DGPs) is analyzed; linear prior mean improves initialization, not addressing non-injective pathology; proposes zero-mean initialization to mimic linear prior mean; whitened parameterization enhances stability and helps avoid collapse; three DGP parameterizations examined; experiments show prevention of collapse and competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Understand why VI collapses to the prior in DGPs, disentangle the role of linear prior means, and develop initialization/parameterization strategies to enable robust training without prior-imposed biases.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis linking VI in DGPs to DSVI; examine three common DGP parameterizations; derive effect of linear prior mean on optimization conditioning; propose an initialization that mimics linear prior mean for zero-mean DGPs; analyze whitening; empirical validation across models.
AI.result: Initialization that mimics linear prior mean prevents posterior collapse and improves stability; whitening improves convergence and reduces collapse tendency; some parameterizations do not benefit from linear prior mean; performance comparable to or better than DGPs with linear prior mean.
AI.conclusion: Prior choice should reflect modeling assumptions rather than optimization-by-design constraints; zero-mean initialization can substitute linear prior mean; whitening is beneficial for stability and avoiding collapse; practitioners can train DGPs without restricting priors while maintaining performance.

title: Variational Autoencoder Layer
summary: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) belong to a family of autoencoders with probabilistic properties, making them well suited for generating data by producing a smooth and continuous latent space. Despite being introduced over a decade ago, the method continues to be widely adopted in both research and industry for diverse applications. While VAEs are typically used as standalone models, this paper introduces a novel approach to integrate them as a neural network layer. Furthermore, a new training strategy is proposed for models incorporating these layers, and their performance is thoroughly analyzed.

AI.tldr: Proposes treating Variational Autoencoders as a differentiable neural network layer and introduces a training strategy for models using such layers; includes performance analysis.
AI.motivation: To enable modular, end-to-end integration of probabilistic latent representations via VAE components, improving reusability and optimization in complex architectures.
AI.method: Wrap VAE components (encoder, reparameterization, decoder) as a neural layer/module; propose a specialized training strategy for networks incorporating these VAE layers (e.g., joint objectives, scheduling, and regularization to balance reconstruction and KL terms).
AI.result: Abstract asserts thorough performance analysis but provides no concrete empirical results or metrics.
AI.conclusion: Integration of VAE layers with a dedicated training strategy appears viable and beneficial for modular probabilistic modeling; empirical validation remains to be demonstrated.
