title: $\text{DT}^2$: Decision-Targeted Digital Twins
summary: A digital twin (DT) is a virtual model of a real-world system that can assist decision-making by simulating scenarios induced by different policies. However, typical machine learning-based DTs do not optimise for this use case. We prove that, when model capacity is limited, training DTs to minimise one-step transition errors can produce suboptimal models for ranking sets of policies according to a reward function. We further show that this holds empirically, even with expressive model classes. To address this, we introduce $\text{DT}^2$, a decision-targeted DT training paradigm. Firstly, $\text{DT}^2$ uses fitted Q-evaluation to estimate values of candidate policies from offline data. A DT is then trained to generate rollouts that preserve pairwise policy rankings derived from these proxy ground-truth values with an architecture-agnostic loss function. We empirically demonstrate the efficacy of our method across a range of settings and architectures. $\text{DT}^2$ consistently improves policy ranking and reduces decision regret during policy selection relative to conventional DT training, both for policies used during training and for unseen policies, while maintaining a good level of raw simulation fidelity.

AI.tldr: DT training that optimizes policy ranking rather than one-step fidelity; introduces DT^2 using fitted Q-evaluation to preserve policy rankings in rollout data, improving policy ranking and reducing regret while maintaining fidelity.
AI.motivation: Standard ML-based digital twins trained to minimize one-step transition errors can produce suboptimal models for ranking policies by their expected reward, especially with limited model capacity.
AI.method: Compute proxy policy values from offline data using fitted Q-evaluation; train the DT to generate rollouts that preserve pairwise policy rankings derived from these proxy values, using an architecture-agnostic loss function.
AI.result: DT^2 consistently improves policy ranking and reduces decision regret during policy selection relative to conventional DT training for both in-training and unseen policies, while retaining good raw simulation fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Decision-targeted DT training aligns the DT's behavior with decision quality, yielding better ranking performance and robustness without sacrificing fidelity.

title: Improving Neural Network Training by Decoupling the Magnitude and Direction of Weight Vectors
summary: Modern neural network training relies on optimizers such as Adam and Muon which act on each weight matrix as a single object. Yet every weight matrix carries two distinct quantities -- a \emph{magnitude} and a \emph{direction} -- and all optimizers stepping in the matrix as a whole couple their dynamics: the directional change from an update depends on the current magnitude, while the magnitude drifts as a byproduct of learning the direction, so neither is governed directly by the learning rate. Typical training therefore leans on surrounding recipes such as weight decay and warmup to keep learning stable at scale, though these regulate the coupling only indirectly; other recent methods instead constrain the weight to a fixed-norm sphere, but add no learnable magnitude, leaving scale control to normalization layers alone. We propose \emph{Magnitude--Direction (MD) Decoupling}, an optimizer modification that factorizes each weight into a fixed-norm direction on a hypersphere and learnable per-row and per-column magnitude gains, updated at separate learning rates, all while the model still sees a single fused weight tensor. The method is agnostic to the base optimizer and removes the need for weight decay and warmup. Across both Adam and Muon, MD Decoupling improves on well-tuned baselines, transfers the optimal LR across model width without retuning, and continues to help at scale on large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models. Treating magnitude and direction as separately controlled quantities thus yields more predictable training dynamics and a simple, broadly applicable improvement to modern optimizers.

AI.tldr: Magnitude–Direction (MD) Decoupling factors each weight into a fixed-norm direction and learnable magnitude gains, enabling separate learning-rate control and improving optimizer performance without weight decay or warmup.
AI.motivation: Modern optimizers treat weight matrices as single objects, coupling magnitude and direction and making learning-rate effects indirect. Existing fixes (weight decay/warmup, or fixed-norm spheres) either indirectly regulate scale or do not learn magnitude. Decoupling aims for predictable dynamics by separating magnitude and direction.
AI.method: Decompose each weight into a fixed-norm direction on a hypersphere and learnable per-row and per-column magnitude gains. Update these gains with separate learning rates while the model still uses a single fused weight tensor. The approach is optimizer-agnostic and removes the need for weight decay and warmup.
AI.result: MD Decoupling improves baselines for both Adam and Muon, transfers the optimal learning rate across model widths without retuning, and remains beneficial at scale for large Mixture-of-Experts models.
AI.conclusion: Separating magnitude and direction yields more predictable training dynamics and provides a simple, broadly applicable enhancement to modern optimizers.

title: The Inference-Compute Frontier and a Latency-Efficient Architecture for Limit Order Book Prediction
summary:
We study whether a scaling-law-style inference-compute frontier appears in limit order book prediction. Using FI-2010 and a suite of models ranging from small decision trees to neural LOB architectures, we find that the realized empirical frontier of predictive loss versus structural forward work is well summarized by a power law. In particular, with MLPLOB held out as an architecture family, a power-law fit to the low- and mid-compute non-MLPLOB frontier extrapolates across multiple orders of magnitude and attains $R^2=0.941$ on the excluded high-compute MLPLOB target frontier.
  A similar exercise in latency space gives substantially weaker results, showing that latency is not merely noisy compute. We use this gap to motivate FastBiNLOB, a dense axis-separable LOB mixer built from hardware-friendly temporal and feature mixing operations. In a five-seed experiment, FastBiNLOB exceeds the published $y_{10}$ and $y_{100}$ macro-F1 targets at notably lower latency than existing published SOTA architectures.

AI.tldr: A scaling-law frontier governs LOB prediction: predictive loss vs forward compute follows a power-law, with the MLPLOB frontier fitting well and extrapolating across compute regimes; latency behavior is weaker; a hardware-friendly model (FastBiNLOB) achieves lower-latency SOTA macro-F1 in five seeds.
AI.motivation: To test whether a scaling-law style relation exists between compute (and latency) and predictive loss in limit-order-book prediction, and to develop efficient architectures aligned with this frontier.
AI.method: Evaluate FI-2010 using models from simple decision trees to neural LOBs, treating MLPLOB as an architecture family. Fit a power-law to the low- and mid-compute frontier and extrapolate to high compute; assess latency frontier; introduce FastBiNLOB, a dense axis-separable LOB mixer. Conduct a five-seed experiment comparing macro-F1 targets y10 and y100 against published SOTA.
AI.result: Empirically, the realized predictive-loss vs compute frontier is well described by a power law, with R^2 = 0.941 on the excluded high-compute MLPLOB frontier. Latency-based frontier shows weaker structure. FastBiNLOB exceeds published y10 and y100 macro-F1 targets at notably lower latency than existing SOTA architectures in a five-seed study.
AI.conclusion: There is a scaling-law frontier in compute for LOB prediction; latency reveals non-trivial structure beyond compute noise. Hardware-friendly architectures like FastBiNLOB can achieve superior latency performance while meeting end-task targets, as suggested by multi-seed results and frontier extrapolation.

title: Is Variational Monte Carlo Robust? Sharp Moment Thresholds and Heavy-tailed Stochastic Optimization
summary: Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) is a central algorithm in electronic structure theory and has gained renewed importance through modern neural-network ansätze such as FermiNet. At its core, VMC seeks ground states by minimizing the Rayleigh quotient by stochastic optimization. In this work, we show that the resulting stochastic optimization problem is intrinsically governed by the nodal geometry of the underlying wave function. More precisely, we establish that properties of the nodal set determine the integrability of the local energy and gradient estimators that drive VMC. For broad and practically relevant ansatz classes, including Slater-Jastrow wave functions with variable-exponent Slater-type orbitals, we prove that these estimators are generically heavy-tailed and fail to admit higher moments. At the same time, for general analytic ansätze, we prove weak moment bounds for the relevant estimators and identify precise low-moment regimes, showing how generic and degenerate nodal structures lead to different integrability thresholds. Building on this analysis, we introduce a new robust variant of VMC $\unicode{x2013}$ coined PS-Clip-VMC $\unicode{x2013}$ which is based on clipping both the local energy and the gradient random variable. We prove that PS-Clip-VMC converges both in expectation and with high probability in the weak moment regime of VMC. Preliminary experiments for training FermiNet on Atoms with up to 18 electrons suggest that PS-Clip-VMC is significantly more robust than standard methods.

AI.tldr: Nodal geometry governs the stochastic estimators in variational Monte Carlo (VMC); estimators can be heavy-tailed for broad ansatzes, yet analytic ansätze admit weak moment bounds. A robust PS-Clip-VMC method with clipping of local energy and gradient is proposed, with proven convergence and favorable empirical performance up to 18-electron atoms.
AI.motivation: To understand the integrability properties of local-energy and gradient estimators in VMC and the effect of nodal geometry on convergence, and to develop a robust algorithm with guaranteed convergence under realistic tail behaviors.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze the nodal-set geometry to characterize the moment behavior of VMC estimators across ansatz classes (e.g., Slater–Jastrow with variable-exponent Slater orbitals) and prove heavy-tailedness or weak moment bounds. They then introduce PS-Clip-VMC, a clipping-based variant that bounds both the local energy and gradient estimators, and prove convergence in expectation and with high probability under the weak-moment regime. Preliminary experiments train FermiNet on atoms up to 18 electrons.
AI.result: Estimation routines for broad, non-analytic ansätze exhibit heavy tails and lack higher moments, while analytic ansätze display weak moment bounds with identifiable low-moment regimes. PS-Clip-VMC converges in expectation and with high probability in the weak moment regime. Empirically, PS-Clip-VMC shows robustness improvements over standard VMC on FermiNet models for up to 18 electrons.
AI.conclusion: Nodal geometry is a driving factor for estimator integrability in VMC. A clipping-based robust variant (PS-Clip-VMC) provides convergence guarantees and improved practical robustness, bridging theoretical tail behavior and empirical performance across representative electronic-structure ansätze.

title: RevengeBench: Reverse Engineering Code-Space Policies from Behavioral Experiments
summary: For most of scientific history, researchers studying behavior could only infer hidden mechanisms from outward actions: an inverse problem that becomes more tractable when observation is augmented by targeted intervention. We pose a computational analogue: given only behavioral traces of an agent in a game environment, can a learner reconstruct the underlying decision program as executable code, and how much does this reconstruction improve with the ability to design controlled experiments? We introduce RevengeBench, a benchmark of 75 LLM generated, Elo-calibrated policies across five game environments, drawn from CodeClash tournament trajectories. The learner observes the hidden target policy play against sampled opponents and designs behavioral probes in the form of custom opponent policies that elicit informative behavior. It then submits an executable hypothesis, which is evaluated using continuous action-distance metrics. We further validate that recovered code carries informative signal in downstream player-versus-player tournaments. Across twelve frontier LLMs, recovery quality varies substantially (34 to 72% of initial distance closed), with reconstructed policies yielding measurable competitive advantage, particularly for weaker models that otherwise struggle to design effective counter-strategies. Our benchmark positions behavioral recovery of programmatic policies as a tractable inverse problem in code-space, opening a path to opponent modeling, policy interpretability, and the broader question of inferring latent mechanisms from observations.

AI.tldr: RevengeBench benchmarks recovering executable policies from behavioral traces. Across 12 frontier LLMs, 34–72% of initial behavior distance is closed, enabling competitive advantage and demonstrating that latent decision programs can be inferred from traces and probes.
AI.motivation: Addresses an inverse problem: can we recover a policymaking program from behavior? Adding targeted interventions (probes) makes the reconstruction more informative. This links behavioral data to interpretable, executable policies, with applications in opponent modeling and policy interpretability.
AI.method: RevengeBench comprises 75 LLM-generated, Elo-calibrated policies across five game environments from CodeClash trajectories. A learner observes a target policy playing against sampled opponents, designs behavioral probes via custom opponent policies, then submits an executable hypothesis. Evaluation uses continuous action-distance metrics; downstream PvP tournaments validate informative signal of recovered code.
AI.result: Recovery quality varies notably across twelve frontier LLMs, closing 34–72% of the initial policy distance. Reconstructed policies confer measurable competitive advantage, especially for weaker models that struggle to counter-strategize.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral recovery of programmatic policies is a tractable inverse problem in code-space, enabling advances in opponent modeling, policy interpretability, and the broader goal of inferring latent mechanisms from observations.

title: TurboMPC: Fast, Scalable, and Differentiable Model Predictive Control on the GPU
summary: Robotics increasingly relies on GPUs for parallel simulation, large-scale learning, and neural-network inference. For model predictive control (MPC) to scale with this paradigm, solvers must run efficiently on this hardware while remaining fast, differentiable, and compatible with expressive MPC formulations used in robotics. We present TurboMPC, a differentiable MPC solver that runs entirely on the GPU and supports state and control inequality constraints, implicit integrators, cross-time-coupled costs, and slack variables. TurboMPC combines sequential quadratic programming (SQP), an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) inner solver, implicit differentiation, and a co-designed JAX-CUDA implementation for efficiency and ease of use. In simulation, we validate TurboMPC on constrained planning, humanoid imitation learning, and reinforcement learning with neural-network cost function tasks, achieving up to $15\times$ and $58\times$ speedups over state-of-the-art CPU and GPU differentiable solvers, respectively. We deploy TurboMPC on a full-scale car for minimum-time racing and find that batched, GPU-accelerated tuning of MPC parameters via Bayesian optimization yields significantly faster driving than a hand-tuned baseline. TurboMPC also scales to planning horizons of over $8000$ knot points while maintaining control of the vehicle. We open-source TurboMPC at: https://github.com/ToyotaResearchInstitute/turbompc

AI.tldr: A GPU-based, differentiable MPC solver (TurboMPC) that integrates SQP outer iterations with an ADMM inner solver, implicit differentiation, and a JAX-CUDA implementation. It supports full state/control inequality constraints, implicit integrators, cross-time costs, and slack variables, enabling long-horizon planning on GPUs with high speed and differentiability.
AI.motivation: To enable fast, differentiable MPC for robotics on GPUs, handling expressive (constrained, cross-time) formulations and large horizons, while maintaining differentiability for learning and optimization.
AI.method: A hybrid solver that combines sequential quadratic programming (SQP) with an inner ADMM solver, uses implicit differentiation, and is co-designed with a JAX-CUDA implementation. Supports state and control inequality constraints, implicit integrators, cross-time-coupled costs, and slack variables; open-source release.
AI.result: Achieves up to 15x speedups over CPU differentiable solvers and 58x speedups over GPU differentiable solvers. Validated on constrained planning, humanoid imitation learning, and reinforcement learning with neural-network costs. Deployed on full-scale car for minimum-time racing; Bayesian optimization of MPC parameters accelerates driving versus hand-tuned baseline. Scales to horizons over 8000 knot points.
AI.conclusion: TurboMPC is a scalable, efficient, differentiable MPC solution running entirely on GPU, enabling large, expressive MPC formulations in robotics and providing substantial speedups and practical deployment; open-source.

title: BluTrain: A C++/CUDA Framework for AI Systems
summary: Progress in deep learning is, at scale, more a matter of systems engineering than of modelling: the behaviour of a model in training (its throughput, its memory footprint, and the numerical fidelity of the result) is determined less by the architecture itself than by how that architecture is expressed on the hardware. To achieve absolute control over this hardware expression while abstracting away systems complexity to make modelling seamless and eliminating the need for repetitive orchestration logic, BluTrain was architected from first principles as a robust, lightweight, and architecture-general training framework in standard C++ and the core CUDA programming model. Every layer is implemented natively: a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler. In formal evaluations training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system, BluTrain outperforms industry-standard baselines in both throughput (sustaining an average of 407K tokens/s versus PyTorch's 395K tokens/s) and memory efficiency (achieving up to a 22% footprint reduction), while strictly preserving numerical fidelity and converging to a marginally lower final validation loss. With every layer explicitly open to native tuning, the performance ceiling is the framework's own to raise.

AI.tldr: BluTrain is a C++/CUDA-based, architecture-general DL training framework designed to exercise direct control over hardware expression, delivering higher throughput and lower memory footprint than PyTorch on a 124M GPT-2 baseline while preserving numerical fidelity.
AI.motivation: To achieve absolute, low-level control over hardware expression and eliminate repetitive orchestration logic, addressing systems-engineering bottlenecks that dominate training performance at scale.
AI.method: Native-layer implementation in standard C++/CUDA: a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler. The framework targets architecture generality and provides end-to-end path from model to hardware with minimal orchestration. Evaluation conducted on a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline trained in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system.
AI.result: In formal evaluations, BluTrain achieves 407K tokens/s throughput (vs PyTorch 395K), up to 22% memory footprint reduction, and preserves numerical fidelity with a marginally lower final validation loss compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: With explicit native-tuning at every layer, BluTrain’s performance ceiling is set by the framework itself, promising room for further gains as the hardware-exposure and compiler stack evolve.
